topics.title: Introduction to Renewable Energy Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Renewable Energy Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Renewable Energy Sources (RES)** are energy sources that are naturally replenished at a rate faster than or equal to the rate at which they are consumed. They are derived from ongoing natural processes such as sunlight, wind, flowing water, biomass, geothermal heat, and tidal forces, and are essentially inexhaustible on a human timescale.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT864)** syllabus, an energy source is classified as *renewable* if it satisfies three fundamental criteria:
1. **Natural Flux Continuity** — The source is continuously replenished by a planetary or solar-terrestrial process.
2. **Low Net Carbon Footprint** — Net greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions over the lifecycle are negligible compared to fossil fuels.
3. **Sustainable Yield** — The extraction rate does not exceed the natural regeneration rate of the resource.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Renewable ≠ Unlimited. While renewable sources are inexhaustible *in principle*, they are subject to **localized intermittency** (e.g., solar at night, calm winds) and **spatial variability** (e.g., geothermal only in tectonically active zones).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Salary vs. Savings Account":**
> Think of energy the way a person manages money. A **non-renewable source (fossil fuel)** is like a **fixed savings account (FD)** — you can withdraw, but the principal keeps shrinking, and one day it hits zero. A **renewable source (solar, wind)** is like a **monthly salary** — the money (energy) comes in continuously as long as the sun shines or wind blows, and as long as you don't spend it faster than it arrives, you never run out. **Sustainable energy** is simply "living within your salary" — matching consumption to replenishment.

### 1.3 Classification of Energy Sources

Energy sources are broadly classified into two categories:

| Category | Description | Examples | Reserves |
|----------|-------------|----------|----------|
| **Conventional / Non-Renewable** | Finite stock, depleting rapidly | Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium) | Limited (50–150 years for fossil fuels) |
| **Non-Conventional / Renewable** | Continuously replenished, sustainable | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC | Practically inexhaustible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydroelectric power (large dams)** sits in a unique position — although water is renewable, the *dam capacity* is fixed, so it is often categorized separately as a "mature renewable."

### 1.4 Primary vs. Secondary Energy Sources

- **Primary Energy Sources:** Found in nature *directly* (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium).
- **Secondary Energy Sources:** Derived from primary sources through conversion (electricity from coal, gasoline from crude oil, hydrogen from water electrolysis).

### 1.5 Standard Units and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Energy Units (KTU-accepted):**
> - **1 Joule (J)** = 1 N·m (SI base unit of energy)
> - **1 kiloWatt-hour (kWh)** = $3.6 \times 10^6$ J
> - **1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)** = $41.868 \times 10^9$ J
> - **1 Calorie (cal)** = **4.186 J**
> - **1 BTU (British Thermal Unit)** = **1055.06 J**

- **Solar Constant (G\_sc):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$ (energy flux at the top of Earth's atmosphere on a surface perpendicular to the solar rays at mean Earth–Sun distance).
- **Global average solar irradiance at Earth's surface (G):** $G \approx 1000 \, \text{W/m}^2$ at noon (peak sun hours).
- **Earth's rotational energy budget** = $1.74 \times 10^{17}$ W of solar power intercepted (≈ 10,000× global electricity demand).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Global Energy Dilemma — Why Renewables?

The transition to renewable energy is driven by **four converging crises** mapped directly to KTU Module 1 learning outcomes:

**1. Resource Depletion**
- Proven oil reserves (BP Statistical Review, 2023): **~1700 billion barrels**, current consumption **~100 million barrels/day**.
- Reserves-to-Production (R/P) ratio for crude oil ≈ **50 years**.
- Coal R/P ratio ≈ **130 years**; Natural gas ≈ **49 years**.

**2. Environmental Degradation**
- $CO_2$ emissions in 2023: **~37.4 GtCO₂** (global, from energy sector alone).
- Atmospheric $CO_2$ concentration: **~421 ppm** (2023, NOAA) — highest in at least 800,000 years.
- IPCC 1.5°C target: Net-zero $CO_2$ by **~2050**.

**3. Energy Security & Geopolitics**
- Oil import dependence (India 2023): **~85%** of crude oil requirements.
- Energy access deficit: **~770 million people** globally without electricity (IEA, 2023).

**4. Climate Change Mitigation**
- Paris Agreement (2015): Limit global warming to **1.5°C above pre-industrial levels**.

### 2.2 The Global Energy Scenario (KTU High-Yield Data)

| Region | Primary Energy Mix (approx., 2023) | Renewable Share (electricity) |
|--------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **World Average** | Oil 31%, Coal 27%, Gas 24%, Renewables+Hydro+Nuclear 18% | ~30% |
| **European Union** | Heavy gas + rising renewables | ~44% |
| **United States** | Oil 38%, Gas 32%, Coal 10%, Renewables 13% | ~22% |
| **China** | Coal 55%, Oil 19%, Gas 11%, Renewables 15% | ~31% |
| **India** | Coal 50%, Oil 25%, Gas 6%, Renewables + Hydro 12% | ~25% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot-Question Data Point (Frequently Asked):** As of **December 2023**, India ranks **4th globally** in renewable energy installed capacity (~175 GW including large hydro). Target: **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030** (Panchamrit commitment, COP26, Glasgow 2021).

### 2.3 The Indian Energy Scenario (PEMUT864 — Mandatory)

- **Installed Power Capacity (India, Dec 2023):** ~430 GW
  - Coal: ~50%
  - Renewables (incl. large hydro): ~33%
  - Gas: ~6%
  - Nuclear: ~2%
  - Diesel + Others: ~9%
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (India):** ~**0.8 kW** (vs. world average ~3.2 kW, USA ~12 kW).
- **Energy import bill (FY 2022–23):** ~$160 billion.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-----------------|
| **Energy from fuel combustion** | $E = m \times CV$ | J (m in kg, CV in J/kg) | Calorific value of biomass, coal |
| **Power (rate of energy use)** | $P = \frac{E}{t} = \frac{dE}{dt}$ | W (J/s) | Solar/wind turbine rating |
| **Energy conversion efficiency** | $\eta = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | PV panel, turbine efficiency |
| **Capacity factor (CF)** | $CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760} \times 100\%$ | % | Plant performance over 1 year |
| **Solar constant** | $G_{sc} = 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$ | W/m² | Top-of-atmosphere solar flux |
| **Energy on collector** | $E = A \times G \times \tau \times \eta$ | Wh (A in m², G in W/m², t in h) | Solar collector design |
| **Cumulative installed capacity** | $C_{cum}(t) = C_0 + \sum \Delta C_i$ | GW or MW | Project growth tracking |
| **Specific energy yield** | $Y = \frac{E_{annual}}{P_{installed}}$ | kWh/kWp | PV plant benchmarking |
| **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** | $LCOE = \frac{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | ₹/kWh or $/kWh | Techno-economic comparison |
| **Carbon emission factor (fossil)** | $E_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times EF$ | kg CO₂ | Grid emission accounting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Units Reminder:** $1 \, \text{kWp}$ (kilowatt-peak) refers to PV output under **Standard Test Conditions (STC): 1000 W/m² irradiance, 25°C cell temperature, AM 1.5 spectrum**. Always check units — KTU examiners deduct marks for **kW vs kWh** confusion.

### 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages of Renewable Energy

| Aspect | Renewable Energy | Non-Renewable Energy |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Availability** | Inexhaustible, location-dependent | Finite, depleting |
| **Emissions** | Negligible operational, low lifecycle | High $CO_2$, $SO_x$, $NO_x$ |
| **Operating cost** | Low (sunlight is free) | High (fuel cost) |
| **Capital cost** | High initial | Moderate initial |
| **Reliability** | Intermittent (needs storage) | Dispatchable (on-demand) |
| **Land footprint** | Large (solar/wind farms) | Concentrated |
| **Energy density** | Low (solar ~1 kW/m²) | High (oil ~10,000 kWh/m³) |
| **Sustainability** | Yes | No |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Solar Energy Reaching Earth's Surface

The Sun radiates isotropically with a total luminosity $L_\odot \approx 3.828 \times 10^{26}$ W. The Earth, at mean distance $d = 1 \, \text{AU} \approx 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m, intercepts this radiation over a disk of area $A_{disk} = \pi R_E^2$ (where $R_E \approx 6.371 \times 10^6$ m is Earth's radius).

**Step 1: Compute solar flux at Earth's orbital distance (Solar Constant).**

The total solar power is distributed uniformly over a sphere of radius $d$. The flux at distance $d$ is:

$$G_{sc} = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi d^2}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$G_{sc} = \frac{3.828 \times 10^{26}}{4 \pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2}$$

**Step 2: Expand the denominator.**

$$4 \pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2 = 4 \pi (2.238 \times 10^{22}) = 2.812 \times 10^{23}$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio.**

$$G_{sc} = \frac{3.828 \times 10^{26}}{2.812 \times 10^{23}} \approx 1361 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

This matches the accepted value $G_{sc} = 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$ (the small difference comes from Earth's elliptical orbit; modern accepted mean value ≈ 1361 W/m² per WMO 1981).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The factor of $4\pi d^2$ arises from the surface area of a sphere, because isotropic radiation spreads uniformly in all directions. The Earth, being much smaller than the orbital sphere, only "catches" energy on its cross-sectional disk ($\pi R_E^2$), not the full sphere.

**Step 4: Total power intercepted by Earth.**

The Earth intercepts power over its cross-sectional disk area:

$$P_{intercepted} = G_{sc} \times \pi R_E^2$$

$$P_{intercepted} = 1367 \times \pi (6.371 \times 10^6)^2$$

$$P_{intercepted} = 1367 \times 1.275 \times 10^{14} \approx 1.743 \times 10^{17} \, \text{W}$$

**Step 5: Average flux over entire Earth surface.**

Since Earth is a sphere, the intercepted power is spread over $4\pi R_E^2$:

$$\bar{G} = \frac{P_{intercepted}}{4\pi R_E^2} = \frac{G_{sc}}{4} \approx 342 \, \text{W/m}^2}$$

This is the **global average solar irradiance at the top of the atmosphere**. About **30% is reflected** (planetary albedo $\alpha \approx 0.30$), so the average absorbed flux is:

$$\bar{G}_{abs} = \frac{G_{sc}(1-\alpha)}{4} \approx 240 \, \text{W/m}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Trick:** If the question gives $G_{sc} = 1.367 \, \text{kW/m}^2$ and asks for *peak* surface irradiance at noon on the equator, the answer is **~1000 W/m²** (after atmospheric attenuation of ~25–30% plus cosine losses). If it asks for *global average*, the answer is **~240 W/m²** absorbed.

### 3.2 Derivation: Capacity Factor for a Solar PV Plant

**Step 1: Define the actual annual energy output.**

A 1 MWp PV plant produces $E_{actual}$ kWh in a year. This is the *measured* energy at the AC side of the inverter, summed monthly.

**Step 2: Define the maximum possible (theoretical) energy.**

If the plant operated at its rated peak power $P_{rated}$ continuously for 8760 hours (the full year):

$$E_{max} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \, \text{h}$$

For a 1 MWp plant:

$$E_{max} = 1 \, \text{MW} \times 8760 \, \text{h} = 8.76 \times 10^6 \, \text{kWh}$$

**Step 3: Compute the capacity factor.**

$$CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{max}} = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$$

**Step 4: Example calculation.**

A 1 MWp solar plant in Kerala generates $E_{actual} = 1.5 \times 10^6$ kWh per year.

$$CF = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^3 \times 8760} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{8.76 \times 10^6}$$

$$CF \approx 0.1712 = 17.12\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical CF ranges (KTU reference data):**
> - Solar PV: **15–22%** (India average ≈ 19%)
> - Onshore Wind: **25–40%**
> - Offshore Wind: **35–55%**
> - Coal plant: **60–80%**
> - Hydro: **30–60%**
> - Nuclear: **85–92%**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Renewables Are Not Yet Primary Globally

| Barrier | Description | Mitigation Strategy |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Intermittency** | Solar/wind availability varies with weather & time | Battery storage, pumped hydro, green hydrogen |
| **Grid stability** | Variable generation affects frequency/voltage | Smart grids, inverters with grid-forming capability |
| **High upfront cost** | Solar PV CAPEX ≈ ₹4–5 Cr/MW (2023) | Subsidies, falling module prices (~₹15/Wp) |
| **Land requirement** | 1 MW solar ≈ 4–5 acres | Rooftop, agrivoltaics, floating PV |
| **Storage cost** | Li-ion BESS ≈ ₹6–8 Cr/MWh | Research in solid-state, flow batteries |
| **Policy uncertainty** | Tariff changes, subsidy cuts | Long-term PPAs, RE100 commitments |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define renewable energy sources. List any four examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Renewable energy sources are naturally replenished energy resources that are not depleted when used, with replenishment rates equal to or faster than consumption rates on a human timescale. They are sustainable, low-carbon, and derived from ongoing planetary processes.

Four examples:
1. **Solar Energy** (sunlight)
2. **Wind Energy** (atmospheric circulation)
3. **Hydropower** (water cycle)
4. **Biomass** (photosynthesis-derived organic matter)

Other valid: Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for listing (½ mark each × 4 = 2, but cap at 1 mark as per KTU pattern).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between conventional and non-conventional energy sources with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Conventional Energy | Non-Conventional (Renewable) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Source type | Finite stock | Flow resource |
| Examples | Coal, Petroleum | Solar, Wind |
| Emissions | High ($CO_2$, $SO_2$) | Negligible |
| Reserve life | 50–150 years | Practically unlimited |
| Cost structure | Fuel-dominant | Capital-dominant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark tabular distinction + 1 mark examples + 1 mark for key observation (e.g., reserve life or emissions).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the global and Indian energy scenario with relevant statistics. Discuss the share of renewable energy in total installed capacity.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With neat diagrams, classify renewable energy sources into solar, non-solar, and indirect-solar categories. Give at least two examples for each.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Global Energy Scenario:**
- Total primary energy consumption (2023): **~606 EJ** (~168,000 TWh).
- Fossil fuels dominate: **~82%** of primary energy (Coal 27%, Oil 31%, Gas 24%).
- Renewables (incl. hydro): **~14%**, Nuclear: **~4%**.
- Per capita consumption: World ≈ 3.2 kW; USA ≈ 12 kW; EU ≈ 5.5 kW; India ≈ 0.8 kW.
- $CO_2$ emissions from energy: **~37.4 GtCO₂** (2023).
- Renewable installed capacity (world, 2023): **~3700 GW** (solar ~1400 GW, wind ~1000 GW, hydro ~1400 GW).

**Indian Energy Scenario:**
- Installed capacity (Dec 2023): **~430 GW**.
- Coal: ~50% (~205 GW); Renewables + Large Hydro: ~33% (~142 GW); Gas: ~6%; Nuclear: ~8 GW.
- Per capita consumption: ~**1250 kWh/year** (one of the lowest among major economies).
- Energy import bill: **~$160 billion** (2022–23), ~85% of crude oil imported.
- **Panchamrit targets (COP26, 2021):** 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030; 50% energy from non-fossil sources; net-zero by 2070.

**Conclusion:** India is rapidly scaling renewables but still heavily coal-dependent. Renewables are the fastest-growing segment (~15% CAGR over the last decade).

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Classification of Renewable Energy Sources:**

**1. Direct Solar (sunlight captured at Earth's surface):**
- Solar Photovoltaic (PV) cells
- Solar Thermal collectors
- Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)
- Solar architecture (passive design)

**2. Indirect Solar (solar-driven natural cycles):**
- **Wind Energy** — atmospheric circulation driven by uneven solar heating
- **Hydropower** — water cycle (evaporation → precipitation → runoff)
- **Biomass** — photosynthesis
- **Wave Energy** — wind-induced surface gravity waves
- **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** — differential heating of ocean layers
- **Tidal Energy** — gravitational interaction (technically solar-system origin)

**3. Non-Solar Renewable:**
- **Geothermal Energy** — radioactive decay in Earth's core, accessible in tectonically active zones

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by:
> 1. **Misclassifying tidal energy** as non-solar. Tides arise from the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun, so tidal is *not* solar-driven. Geothermal is the *only* truly non-solar renewable.
> 2. **Conflating primary and secondary energy** — saying "electricity is a renewable source" is wrong; electricity is a secondary carrier.
> 3. **Forgetting units or omitting per-capita data** when asked for "statistics." Always quantify.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the expression for the solar constant at Earth's orbit. A solar PV plant of 5 MWp capacity has a measured annual energy output of $9.5 \times 10^6$ kWh. Calculate its capacity factor.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the environmental impacts of conventional energy sources. Compare the levelized cost of energy (LCOE) trends of solar PV, wind, and coal-based thermal plants over the last decade.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Solar constant derivation (reproduced from Section 3.1).**

The Sun emits total luminosity $L_\odot = 3.828 \times 10^{26}$ W isotropically. At Earth's orbital distance $d = 1.496 \times 10^{11}$ m, the flux spreads over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$:

$$G_{sc} = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi d^2} = \frac{3.828 \times 10^{26}}{4\pi (1.496 \times 10^{11})^2}$$

$$G_{sc} \approx 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Capacity factor calculation.**

Given: $P_{rated} = 5 \, \text{MWp} = 5000 \, \text{kWp}$, $E_{actual} = 9.5 \times 10^6 \, \text{kWh/year}$.

$$CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760} = \frac{9.5 \times 10^6}{5000 \times 8760}$$

$$CF = \frac{9.5 \times 10^6}{4.38 \times 10^7} = 0.2169$$

$$\boxed{CF \approx 21.69\%}$$

This is consistent with typical Indian solar PV plants (range 18–24%).

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Environmental Impacts of Conventional Energy Sources:**

1. **Atmospheric Pollution:**
   - $CO_2$ (main GHG): coal ≈ 1000 kg CO₂/MWh, oil ≈ 700 kg CO₂/MWh, gas ≈ 400 kg CO₂/MWh.
   - $SO_2$ → acid rain, respiratory illness.
   - $NO_x$ → smog, ground-level ozone.
   - Particulate Matter (PM2.5, PM10) → lung disease, cardiovascular mortality.

2. **Water Pollution:**
   - Oil spills, thermal discharge from cooling water, acid mine drainage.
   - Coal ash ponds (e.g., ash pond failures in India).

3. **Land Degradation:**
   - Mining displaces communities, deforestation, soil erosion.
   - Subsidence from underground mining.

4. **Biodiversity Loss:**
   - Habitat fragmentation, bird mortality from wind farms (less than fossil fuel infrastructure).

5. **Climate Change:**
   - Cumulative GHG emissions drive global warming → sea-level rise, extreme weather.

6. **Health Impacts:**
   - WHO estimates air pollution causes **~7 million premature deaths/year** globally.

**LCOE Trend Comparison (USD/MWh, global weighted average, Lazard's LCOE+ versions):**

| Source | 2010 | 2015 | 2020 | 2023 |
|--------|------|------|------|------|
| **Utility Solar PV** | ~$380 | ~$100 | ~$60 | ~$45 |
| **Onshore Wind** | ~$120 | ~$60 | ~$45 | ~$35 |
| **Coal (unsubsidized)** | ~$110 | ~$100 | ~$109 | ~$117 |
| **Natural Gas (CCGT)** | ~$80 | ~$70 | ~$60 | ~$65 |

**Key observation:** Solar PV LCOE has fallen by **~88%** in 13 years — the steepest learning curve of any energy technology. Solar and onshore wind are now **cheaper than new coal plants in most markets** (India, Australia, USA, China).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes in (b):
> 1. **Confusing LCOE with total cost.** LCOE is *per unit of energy delivered* (₹/kWh or $/MWh), not absolute project cost.
> 2. **Failing to state units explicitly** in LCOE comparison — always write "₹/kWh" or "$/MWh."
> 3. **Omitting the year of data** — LCOE is time-sensitive; KTU expects 2023 / latest figures.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Recall — KTU Module 1**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** RES = naturally replenished, sustainable, low-carbon energy sources.
> 
> ✅ **Classification:** Non-renewable (fossil + nuclear) vs Renewable (solar direct, solar indirect, geothermal).
> 
> ✅ **Solar Constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367 \, \text{W/m}^2$ at top of atmosphere.
> 
> ✅ **Earth's average absorbed solar flux:** $\bar{G}_{abs} \approx 240 \, \text{W/m}^2$ (after albedo).
> 
> ✅ **Total solar power intercepted by Earth:** $\approx 1.74 \times 10^{17} \, \text{W} \approx 174,000 \, \text{TW}$.
> 
> ✅ **Human primary energy demand:** $\approx 18 \, \text{TW}$ — solar provides ~10,000× our needs in principle.
> 
> ✅ **Capacity Factor (CF):** $CF = E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \times 8760)$ — typical solar 15–22%, wind 25–40%, coal 60–80%.
> 
> ✅ **Per capita electricity (India):** ~1250 kWh/year (very low globally; KTU often asks this).
> 
> ✅ **India's RE target:** 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030; Net-zero by **2070**.
> 
> ✅ **LCOE order (2023):** Solar ≈ Wind < Gas < Coal (new builds) — renewables are now cheapest.
> 
> ✅ **Tidal is NOT solar-driven** (gravitational). **Geothermal is the only non-solar renewable.**
> 
> ✅ **Units to memorize:** $1 \, \text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \, \text{J}$, $1 \, \text{TOE} = 41.868 \, \text{GJ}$, $1 \, \text{kWp}$ (STC conditions).
> 
> ✅ **Energy hierarchy:** Primary sources (sun, coal) → Secondary (electricity, hydrogen).
> 
> ✅ **Three pillars of RE transition:** Technological maturity, Economic competitiveness, Policy support.
> 
> ✅ **Common pitfalls to avoid in KTU exam:** Mixing kW and kWh, omitting albedo (30%) in solar flux calcs, misclassifying tidal/geothermal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Energy Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Energy Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Classification of Energy Resources — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** syllabus for the course *PEMUT864 – Renewable Energy Sources*, **Energy Resources** are defined as the naturally occurring or artificially created substances, phenomena, or systems from which useful *mechanical work*, *thermal energy*, or *electrical energy* can be extracted, converted, and utilized to serve human industrial, domestic, and agricultural needs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)**
> Energy resources are the primary reserves or flows of energy in nature that can be harnessed by mankind to perform useful work. They are broadly classified based on their origin, replenishment rate, usability, and commercial viability.

### The Five Principal Classification Axes (KTU 2024 Module 1)

A KTU 2024 Scheme student is expected to classify any energy source along **five independent axes**:

1. **Origin-based Classification** → Primary vs. Secondary
2. **Replenishment-based Classification** → Renewable vs. Non-Renewable
3. **Conventionality-based Classification** → Conventional vs. Non-Conventional
4. **Commercial Viability Classification** → Commercial vs. Non-Commercial
5. **Environmental Impact Classification** → Clean / Green vs. Polluting

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tank and The River" 💧

Imagine you are thirsty and need water:

- **Non-Renewable Energy** is like a **water tank** that has a **fixed amount of water**. Every time you use some, the level drops. If you use it too fast, the tank runs dry forever. **Coal, petroleum, natural gas, and uranium** are like this tank — they took **millions of years** to form and cannot be replenished within a human lifetime.

- **Renewable Energy** is like a **continuously flowing river**. As long as the sun shines and rain falls, the river keeps flowing. You can draw water from it daily, and it refills naturally. **Solar, wind, hydro, biomass, tidal, and geothermal** energy behave like this river — they are replenished by natural processes on a **human timescale (hours to years)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sun is the "Master Source":**
> Almost all renewable energy on Earth — whether it is wind, biomass, hydropower, or even wave energy — is ultimately a **derivative of solar radiation**. The single exception is **geothermal energy** (from Earth's core heat) and **tidal energy** (from Earth–Moon gravitational interaction). This "Sun as master source" concept is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard physical constants** and **energy units** are used throughout this module and are critical for KTU numerical problems:

| Constant / Unit | Symbol | Value / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant | $G_{SC}$ | **1.361 kW/m²** (or 1361 W/m²) |
| Standard Calorific Value of Coal | $CV_{coal}$ | **~25 MJ/kg** (varies 20–30 MJ/kg) |
| Energy Unit (SI) | J | **1 J = 1 N·m** |
| Power Unit (SI) | W | **1 W = 1 J/s** |
| 1 kWh (commercial) | — | **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3600 kJ** |
| 1 Toe (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) | — | **1 Toe = 41.868 GJ** |
| 1 BTU (British Thermal Unit) | — | **1 BTU = 1055.06 J** |

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the conversion **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** — it appears in **almost every KTU numerical on energy economics**.

---

## 1.4 Quick-View: The Master Classification Tree

```
                            ENERGY RESOURCES
                                  │
        ┌─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐
   PRIMARY (Nature)          SECONDARY (Derived)        WASTE / RECOVERED
        │                         │                         │
   Coal, Oil, Wind,         Electricity, Hydrogen,     Biogas from waste,
   Sun, Uranium             Steam, Petrol, Diesel      Refuse-derived fuel
```

This hierarchy will be elaborated with full theoretical depth in **Section 2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Axis 1 — Primary vs. Secondary Energy Resources

### 2.1.1 Primary Energy

**Definition:** Energy resources that occur in their **natural, unconverted form** and have **not been subjected to any human-engineered transformation process**.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Found in nature without any human intervention.
- Used as input to conversion systems.
- Can be either renewable (sunlight) or non-renewable (coal).

**Examples:**
- **Non-Renrenewable primary sources:** Coal, crude oil, natural gas, uranium (nuclear fuel).
- **Renewable primary sources:** Solar radiation, wind, geothermal heat, falling water (hydel head), tides, waves, biomass in its raw form.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Energy

**Definition:** Energy resources that are **derived from primary sources** through a **conversion process** (combustion, refining,发电, electrolysis, etc.).

**Key Characteristics:**
- Do **not** exist freely in nature in their usable form.
- Always require a primary energy input.
- Typically more convenient, portable, or efficient for end-use.

**Examples:**
- **Electricity** (from coal, sun, wind, or water)
- **Hydrogen gas** (from electrolysis of water)
- **Refined petroleum products:** Petrol, diesel, kerosene, LPG
- **Coke** (from coal)
- **Producer gas, biogas** (from biomass)
- **Steam** (from boiler heated by any primary fuel)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electricity is ALWAYS a secondary energy form** — it must be generated from a primary source. This is a recurring conceptual question in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Axis 2 — Renewable vs. Non-Renewable Energy

### 2.2.1 Renewable Energy Sources

A renewable energy source is one whose **replenishment rate (by natural cycles)** is **comparable to or greater than its rate of consumption by humans**.

**Mathematical Criterion (Replenishment Index):**

$$
R_{idx} = \frac{\text{Rate of Natural Replenishment}}{\text{Rate of Human Consumption}}
$$

If $R_{idx} \geq 1$, the resource is renewable. If $R_{idx} < 1$, it is non-renewable.

**The Renewables (KTU Module 1 List):**
1. **Solar Energy** — Photovoltaic + Solar Thermal
2. **Wind Energy**
3. **Hydel / Hydro Energy** (small, mini, micro, large)
4. **Biomass Energy** (wood, agricultural residue, biogas, biodiesel)
5. **Geothermal Energy**
6. **Tidal Energy**
7. **Wave / Ocean Energy**
8. **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)**

### 2.2.2 Non-Renewable Energy Sources

A non-renewable energy source is one whose **replenishment rate is negligibly slow** (geological timescales: **millions of years**) compared to human consumption.

**Key Non-Renewables:**
1. **Coal** (Anthracite, Bituminous, Lignite, Peat)
2. **Petroleum / Crude Oil**
3. **Natural Gas**
4. **Nuclear Fuels** — Uranium ($^{235}U$, $^{238}U$), Thorium ($^{232}Th$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Nuclear fuels are classified as non-renewable** even though they release enormous energy per unit mass — because the fissile isotope $^{235}U$ is finite and the breeder cycle has not been commercially scaled to make nuclear "renewable" in the practical KTU sense.

---

## 2.3 Axis 3 — Conventional vs. Non-Conventional

| Aspect | Conventional | Non-Conventional |
|---|---|---|
| **Also called** | Traditional / Established | Modern / Alternative |
| **Era of dominance** | Pre-1970s (Industrial age) | Post-1970s oil crisis |
| **Examples** | Coal, oil, natural gas, large hydro, fire-wood | Solar PV, wind, geothermal, OTEC, tidal, biomass, fuel cells |
| **Adoption status** | High penetration, mature grids | Growing penetration, R&D stage |
| **Carbon footprint** | Generally high | Generally low / zero |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classification into "conventional" and "non-conventional" is **historical and adoption-based**, not scientific. As renewables scale, this distinction is blurring globally.

---

## 2.4 Axis 4 — Commercial vs. Non-Commercial Energy

This classification is critical for **developing nations like India** and appears frequently in KTU Module 1 questions.

| Type | Commercial Energy | Non-Commercial Energy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Bought/sold in the market at a price | Not bought/sold; collected free from nature |
| **Examples** | Electricity, LPG, kerosene, petrol, diesel | Firewood, cow-dung, agricultural waste, straw, charcoal (rural) |
| **Accounting** | Counted in national energy statistics (GDP) | Often unaccounted; "Energy Ladder" issue |
| **KTU relevance** | High — energy security & economy | High — rural development, indoor pollution |

> [!WARNING]
> Non-commercial energy is **statistically invisible** but **biophysically massive** in India. About **25–30%** of rural household energy is still non-commercial (TERI/MNRE data). Examiners love asking about this.

---

## 2.5 Axis 5 — Environmental / Sustainability Classification

| Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Clean / Green Energy** | Low / zero emissions during operation; minimal ecological footprint | Solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, tidal |
| **Polluting Energy** | High $CO_2$, $SO_x$, $NO_x$, particulates | Coal, oil, natural gas (relatively cleaner but still fossil) |
| **Sustainable Energy** | Meets present demand without compromising future generations | All renewables + nuclear (debatable) |
| **Non-Sustainable Energy** | Depletes finite reserves; adds to greenhouse burden | All fossil fuels |

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** for all KTU numericals in Module 1.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy from fuel (by mass) | $E = m \times CV$ | Joules (J) | Heating value calculation |
| 2 | Energy from fuel (by volume) | $E = V \times \rho \times CV$ | Joules (J) | Liquid fuel calorific value |
| 3 | Power–Energy relation | $E = P \times t$ | J = W × s | kWh billing, plant output |
| 4 | kWh to MJ conversion | $E_{MJ} = E_{kWh} \times 3.6$ | MJ | Unit conversion in KTU problems |
| 5 | Toe conversion | $1 \text{ Toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$ | GJ | National energy accounting |
| 6 | Solar Constant | $G_{SC} = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ | W/m² | Solar flux at top of atmosphere |
| 7 | Calorific Value of Coal (typical) | $CV \approx 25 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | MJ/kg | Energy content of bituminous coal |
| 8 | Calorific Value of Petrol | $CV \approx 44 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | MJ/kg | Refined fuel energy density |
| 9 | Calorific Value of Natural Gas | $CV \approx 55 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | MJ/kg | Methane-rich gas energy |
| 10 | Calorific Value of Biogas (~60% CH₄) | $CV \approx 22 \text{ MJ/m}^3$ | MJ/m³ | Biomass energy estimation |
| 11 | Air-Fuel Ratio (theoretical, coal) | $A/F = \frac{11.6 \cdot C + 34.8 \cdot (H_2 - O_2/8) + 4.35 \cdot S}{100}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Combustion stoichiometry |
| 12 | Efficiency of conversion | $\eta = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Rankine, Brayton, PV efficiency |

> **Note on Table Notation:** All absolute-value or "divided by" notations inside math expressions are written as `\vert ... \vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plant Engineers** use the Primary/Secondary axis to plan **fuel logistics** (coal vs. electricity at the grid).
- **Policy Makers** use the Renewable/Non-Renewable axis to plan **decarbonization roadmaps** (e.g., India's **Panchamrit** target of 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030).
- **Rural Development Officers** use the Commercial/Non-Commercial axis to plan **clean cooking fuel transitions** (UJALA / Saubhagya / Ujjwala schemes).
- **Energy Auditors** use the Calorific Value formulas to compute **boiler efficiency**, **heat balance**, and **specific fuel consumption** of thermal plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Content from Mass and Calorific Value

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style)
A thermal power plant consumes **$m = 500$ tonnes of coal per day**. The calorific value of the coal is **$CV = 24$ MJ/kg**. Determine the total thermal energy input per day in (a) MJ, (b) kWh, and (c) Toe.

### Given Data
$$
m = 500 \text{ tonnes/day} = 500 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/day}
$$
$$
CV = 24 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

### Part (a): Energy in MJ

**Step 1 — State the fundamental relationship.**

$$
E = m \times CV
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
E = (500 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/day}) \times (24 \text{ MJ/kg})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product.**

$$
E = 12{,}000 \times 10^{3} \text{ MJ/day} = 1.2 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 1.2 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ/day}}
$$

### Part (b): Energy in kWh

**Step 1 — Use the conversion factor** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.

$$
E_{kWh} = \frac{E_{MJ}}{3.6}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
E_{kWh} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{7}}{3.6} \text{ kWh/day}
$$

$$
E_{kWh} = 3.333 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 3.33 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/day} \; (\text{or} \; 3.33 \text{ GWh/day})}
$$

### Part (c): Energy in Toe

**Step 1 — Use the conversion factor** $1 \text{ Toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 41.868 \times 10^{3} \text{ MJ}$.

$$
E_{Toe} = \frac{E_{MJ}}{41.868 \times 10^{3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
E_{Toe} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{7}}{41.868 \times 10^{3}}
$$

$$
E_{Toe} = 286.6 \text{ Toe/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 286.6 \text{ Toe/day}}
$$

### Interpretation
This single 500-tonne/day plant consumes thermal energy equivalent to the **annual energy of ~860 average Indian households** (at 1 kWh/day/household). It also explains why a single percentage point of **plant efficiency** can save thousands of tonnes of coal annually.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Replenishment Index $R_{idx}$ for Coal vs. Solar

This is an **analytical derivation** that combines both classification axes.

### Step 1 — Define Replenishment Index

$$
R_{idx} = \frac{\dot{E}_{replenish}}{\dot{E}_{consume}}
$$

where $\dot{E}$ denotes an energy *rate* (energy per unit time).

### Step 2 — For Coal (Non-Renewable)

Earth's coal reserves: $E_{coal} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{12}$ tonnes × $24$ MJ/kg.

$$
E_{coal,total} = 1.07 \times 10^{12} \times 10^{3} \text{ kg} \times 24 \text{ MJ/kg} \approx 2.57 \times 10^{16} \text{ MJ}
$$

Annual human consumption: $\approx 8 \times 10^{15}$ MJ/year (IEA 2022 data).

Time to deplete: $t = E_{coal,total} / \dot{E}_{consume} \approx 320$ years (reserves-to-production ratio).

$$
R_{idx}^{coal} = \frac{0}{8 \times 10^{15}} = 0 \ll 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \text{Non-Renewable}
$$

(The replenishment rate is **zero** on human timescales.)

### Step 3 — For Solar (Renewable)

Solar power intercepted by Earth:

$$
P_{solar} = G_{SC} \times \pi R_{Earth}^{2}
$$

$$
P_{solar} = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2 \times \pi \times (6.371 \times 10^{6} \text{ m})^{2}
$$

$$
P_{solar} = 1361 \times \pi \times 4.059 \times 10^{13} \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{solar} \approx 1.74 \times 10^{17} \text{ W} = 1.74 \times 10^{11} \text{ GW}
$$

Total human power consumption: $\approx 2.3 \times 10^{4}$ GW (2023 world electricity demand).

$$
R_{idx}^{solar} = \frac{1.74 \times 10^{11}}{2.3 \times 10^{4}} \approx 7.56 \times 10^{6} \gg 1
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Renewable (the sun provides ~7.5 million times our current need)}
$$

### Step 4 — Comparative Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\frac{R_{idx}^{solar}}{R_{idx}^{coal}} \to \infty}
$$

This single comparison is the **engineering justification** for transitioning to renewables — a 14-mark KTU question has been asked on this.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — The Complete KTU Classification Grid

| Source | Primary/Secondary | Renewable/Non-Renewable | Conventional/Non-Conventional | Commercial/Non-Commercial | Clean/Polluting |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coal** | Primary | Non-Renewable | Conventional | Commercial | Polluting |
| **Crude Oil** | Primary | Non-Renewable | Conventional | Commercial | Polluting |
| **Natural Gas** | Primary | Non-Renewable | Conventional | Commercial | Moderate |
| **Uranium (Nuclear)** | Primary | Non-Renewable | Conventional | Commercial | Low GHG, but waste issue |
| **Firewood** | Primary | Renewable | Conventional | Non-Commercial | Polluting (indoor) |
| **Cow-dung cake** | Primary | Renewable | Conventional | Non-Commercial | Polluting (indoor) |
| **Solar (radiation)** | Primary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial (grid-tied) | Clean |
| **Wind** | Primary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Clean |
| **Large Hydro** | Primary | Renewable | Conventional | Commercial | Clean (with dam impacts) |
| **Small / Micro Hydro** | Primary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Clean |
| **Biomass (modern)** | Primary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Mostly clean |
| **Biogas** | Secondary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Clean |
| **Biodiesel** | Secondary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Clean |
| **Geothermal** | Primary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Clean (with H₂S) |
| **Tidal** | Primary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Clean |
| **Wave / OTEC** | Primary | Renewable | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Clean |
| **Electricity** | Secondary | Depends on source | — | Commercial | Depends on source |
| **Hydrogen (green)** | Secondary | Renewable (if from RE) | Non-Conventional | Commercial | Clean (zero tailpipe) |
| **Coke** | Secondary | Non-Renewable | Conventional | Commercial | Polluting |
| **Petrol / Diesel** | Secondary | Non-Renewable | Conventional | Commercial | Polluting |
| **LPG** | Secondary | Non-Renewable | Conventional | Commercial | Cleanest fossil |

> [!NOTE]
> This single matrix is the **most important exam summary** in Module 1. KTU examiners routinely set a **"classify the following 10 sources"** question worth 7 marks directly from this table.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ENERGY RESOURCES] --> B[PRIMARY]
    A --> C[SECONDARY]
    A --> D[RESIDUE & WASTE]

    B --> B1[CONVENTIONAL]
    B --> B2[NON-CONVENTIONAL]

    B1 --> B1A[NON RENEWABLE]
    B1 --> B1B[RENEWABLE]

    B1A --> N1[COAL]
    B1A --> N2[CRUDE OIL]
    B1A --> N3[NATURAL GAS]
    B1A --> N4[URANIUM NUCLEAR]

    B1B --> R1[LARGE HYDRO]
    B1B --> R2[FIREWOOD]
    B1B --> R3[COW DUNG]

    B2 --> B2A[RENEWABLE]
    B2A --> S1[SOLAR PV AND THERMAL]
    B2A --> S2[WIND]
    B2A --> S3[SMALL AND MICRO HYDRO]
    B2A --> S4[BIOMASS AND BIOGAS]
    B2A --> S5[GEOTHERMAL]
    B2A --> S6[TIDAL]
    B2A --> S7[WAVE AND OTEC]

    C --> C1[ELECTRICITY]
    C --> C2[REFINED FUELS]
    C --> C3[HYDROGEN GREEN]
    C --> C4[COKE PRODUCER GAS]

    D --> D1[MUNICIPAL SOLID WASTE]
    D --> D2[INDUSTRIAL WASTE HEAT]
    D --> D3[AGRICULTURAL RESIDUE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram follows the KTU Module 1 textbook flow — **Primary → Conventional/Non-Conventional → Renewable/Non-Renewable** — which is the most exam-friendly structure for writing 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Energy Resource-to-Application Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    SR[SUN MASTER SOURCE] --> SE[SOLAR DIRECT]
    SR --> WIND1[WIND INDIRECT]
    SR --> BIO1[BIOMASS VIA PHOTOSYNTHESIS]
    SR --> HYDRO1[HYDRO VIA EVAPORATION CYCLE]

    GEO[EARTH CORE HEAT] --> GEO1[GEOTHERMAL]
    GRAV[EARTH MOON GRAVITY] --> TIDAL1[TIDAL]

    SE --> CONV1[CONVERSION TECHNOLOGIES]
    WIND1 --> CONV1
    BIO1 --> CONV1
    HYDRO1 --> CONV1
    GEO1 --> CONV1
    TIDAL1 --> CONV1

    CONV1 --> PV[PV PANELS]
    CONV1 --> WT[WIND TURBINES]
    CONV1 --> BIOG[BIOREACTORS]
    CONV1 --> TURB[HYDRO TURBINES]
    CONV1 --> GTURB[GEOTHERMAL PLANTS]
    CONV1 --> TTURB[TIDAL BARRAGES]

    PV --> GRID[GRID ELECTRICITY]
    WT --> GRID
    TURB --> GRID
    GTURB --> GRID
    TTURB --> GRID

    GRID --> USE1[INDUSTRIAL LOAD]
    GRID --> USE2[DOMESTIC LOAD]
    GRID --> USE3[TRANSPORT EV]
    GRID --> USE4[AGRICULTURAL PUMPS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **SUN → Master Source** link in the leftmost column is the KTU high-yield concept. Memorize that *all* renewables except geothermal and tidal are derivatives of solar radiation.

---

## 4.3 Replenishment Index Comparison (Block Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[REPLENISHMENT INDEX RIDX] --> SIDE1[RIDX IS LESS THAN 1]
    R --> SIDE2[RIDX IS GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO 1]

    SIDE1 --> LBL1[NON RENEWABLE]
    LBL1 --> LBL1A[COAL OIL GAS NUCLEAR]

    SIDE2 --> LBL2[RENEWABLE]
    LBL2 --> LBL2A[SOLAR WIND HYDRO BIOMASS GEO TIDAL]
```

This diagram directly supports **Section 3.2 derivation** and is the cleanest visual for a 7-mark "explain replenishment" answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **Q: Differentiate between Primary and Secondary energy sources. Give two examples of each.**

### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Aspect | Primary Energy | Secondary Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Found in nature directly | Derived from primary via conversion |
| Human intervention | None | Required (refining,发电, etc.) |
| Examples | Coal, sunlight, crude oil, wind, uranium | Electricity, petrol, hydrogen, coke |

**Examples (KTU-expected phrasing):**
- **Primary:** Coal, Crude oil, Natural gas, Solar radiation, Wind, Geothermal heat.
- **Secondary:** Electricity, Petrol, Diesel, LPG, Hydrogen, Biogas, Steam.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1m) + Comparison table (1m) + Examples (1m).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **Q: What is meant by a "renewable" energy source? State any four examples of renewable energy sources with one application of each.**

### Model Answer (3 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A renewable energy source is one whose **rate of replenishment by natural cycles is comparable to or greater than the rate of human consumption**, making it effectively inexhaustible on a human timescale.

**Examples with applications (2 marks):**

| Renewable Source | Application |
|---|---|
| Solar Photovoltaic | Rooftop PV panels for domestic electricity |
| Wind | Grid-connected wind farms (e.g., Muppandal, Tamil Nadu) |
| Hydel | Large dams (e.g., Idukki, Kerala) |
| Biomass / Biogas | Rural cooking fuel and organic fertilizer slurry |
| Geothermal | Space heating and electricity in volcanic regions |
| Tidal | Tidal barrage power plants |

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1m) + 4 correct examples with applications (2m — 0.5 each).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice Model)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **Option A1**
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Modified Model Paper]**

> **Q: (a)** Classify energy resources in detail along **five different classification axes**, giving two examples for each sub-category.
>     **(b)** A thermal power plant consumes **600 tonnes of coal per day** with a calorific value of **22 MJ/kg**. Calculate the daily thermal energy input in (i) MJ, (ii) kWh, and (iii) Toe. If the plant's electrical efficiency is **30%**, find the **daily electrical energy generated in MWh**.

---

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

The five classification axes are:

**Axis 1 — Primary vs. Secondary [1.5 marks]**
- **Primary:** Found in nature, e.g., coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind.
- **Secondary:** Derived, e.g., electricity, hydrogen, petrol.

**Axis 2 — Renewable vs. Non-Renewable [1.5 marks]**
- **Renewable:** Replenished naturally, e.g., solar, wind, hydro, biomass.
- **Non-Renewable:** Finite reserves, e.g., coal, oil, gas, uranium.

**Axis 3 — Conventional vs. Non-Conventional [1 mark]**
- **Conventional:** Traditional, e.g., coal, oil, large hydro, firewood.
- **Non-Conventional:** Modern, e.g., solar PV, wind, geothermal, OTEC.

**Axis 4 — Commercial vs. Non-Commercial [1.5 marks]**
- **Commercial:** Traded in markets, e.g., LPG, electricity, kerosene.
- **Non-Commercial:** Free / untraded, e.g., firewood, cow-dung, agricultural residue.

**Axis 5 — Clean / Polluting [1.5 marks]**
- **Clean:** Low / zero emissions, e.g., solar, wind, hydro, geothermal.
- **Polluting:** High emissions, e.g., coal, oil, biomass (traditional).

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
$$
m = 600 \text{ tonnes/day} = 6 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg/day}, \quad CV = 22 \text{ MJ/kg}, \quad \eta = 30\%
$$

**Step 1 — Thermal Energy Input in MJ [1.5 marks]:**

$$
E_{th} = m \times CV = 6 \times 10^{5} \times 22 = 1.32 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to kWh [1.5 marks]:**

$$
E_{th,kWh} = \frac{1.32 \times 10^{7}}{3.6} = 3.667 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Toe [1.5 marks]:**

$$
E_{th,Toe} = \frac{1.32 \times 10^{7}}{41.868 \times 10^{3}} = 315.3 \text{ Toe/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Electrical Energy Output in MWh [2.5 marks]:**

$$
E_{el} = \eta \times E_{th,kWh} = 0.30 \times 3.667 \times 10^{6} = 1.10 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/day}
$$

Converting to MWh (divide by 1000):

$$
E_{el} = 1100 \text{ MWh/day}
$$

**Final Results:**
$$
\boxed{E_{th} = 1.32 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ/day} \quad ; \quad E_{th} = 3.667 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/day} \quad ; \quad E_{th} = 315.3 \text{ Toe/day} \quad ; \quad E_{el} = 1100 \text{ MWh/day}}
$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **Option B1 (Internal Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

> **Q: (a)** Explain the difference between **renewable and non-renewable** energy sources with at least **five examples each**. Discuss why the **Sun is considered the master source** of all renewable energy.
>     **(b)** A household uses **3 units (kWh) of electricity per day**. The electricity is generated by a coal-based plant with an overall efficiency of **25%** and coal calorific value of **24 MJ/kg**. Calculate: (i) the daily thermal energy required at the plant, (ii) the mass of coal consumed per day, and (iii) the **CO₂ emissions per day**, given that burning 1 kg of coal releases **2.42 kg CO₂**.

---

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Renewable vs. Non-Renewable Comparison Table [3 marks]:**

| Aspect | Renewable | Non-Renewable |
|---|---|---|
| Replenishment | Natural cycle rate ≥ consumption rate | Replenishment negligible |
| Reserves | Effectively unlimited | Finite, depleting |
| Carbon footprint | Low / zero | High |
| Examples | Solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal, wave | Coal, oil, natural gas, uranium |

**Five examples each (2 marks — 0.5 each pair):**
- **Renewable:** Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal
- **Non-Renewable:** Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium), Lignite/Peat

**Sun as Master Source [2 marks]:**
- All energy on Earth originates either from the **Sun (solar radiation)** or from the **Earth's interior (geothermal)** or **Earth–Moon gravitational interaction (tidal)**.
- Solar radiation drives **wind** (via differential heating of air), **hydel** (via evaporation–precipitation cycle), **biomass** (via photosynthesis), and **wave energy** (via wind-driven sea surface).
- Only **geothermal** and **tidal** are *not* solar-derived.
- The solar constant $G_{SC} = 1361$ W/m² means the Sun delivers $\approx 1.74 \times 10^{17}$ W to Earth — about **7.5 million times** current human energy demand.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
$$
E_{el} = 3 \text{ kWh/day}, \quad \eta_{plant} = 0.25, \quad CV_{coal} = 24 \text{ MJ/kg}, \quad e_{CO_2} = 2.42 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg coal}
$$

**Step 1 — Daily Thermal Energy Required at Plant [2 marks]:**

$$
E_{th} = \frac{E_{el}}{\eta} = \frac{3 \text{ kWh}}{0.25} = 12 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

Converting to MJ:

$$
E_{th} = 12 \times 3.6 = 43.2 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Coal Consumed per Day [2.5 marks]:**

$$
m_{coal} = \frac{E_{th}}{CV} = \frac{43.2 \text{ MJ/day}}{24 \text{ MJ/kg}} = 1.8 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily CO₂ Emissions [2.5 marks]:**

$$
m_{CO_2} = m_{coal} \times e_{CO_2} = 1.8 \text{ kg/day} \times 2.42 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg coal}
$$

$$
m_{CO_2} = 4.356 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{day}
$$

**Final Results:**
$$
\boxed{E_{th} = 12 \text{ kWh/day} = 43.2 \text{ MJ/day} \quad ; \quad m_{coal} = 1.8 \text{ kg/day} \quad ; \quad m_{CO_2} = 4.36 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{day}}
$$

**Real-world significance:** For a typical urban household, this is the **per-capita carbon shadow** of electricity. Across India, ~300 million households → this scales to **~478,000 tonnes of CO₂ per day** from domestic electricity alone.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Classification Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating "renewable" with "non-conventional"** — they are *NOT* synonymous. Firewood is **renewable** but **conventional**. Large hydro is **renewable** but **conventional**. Always use the correct axis label.
> 2. **Calling nuclear energy "renewable"** — for KTU 2024, nuclear ($^{235}U$) is **non-renewable** because reserves are finite on human timescales. (Breeder reactors are not yet commercial at scale.)
> 3. **Forgetting the unit conversion $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$** — losing 1–2 marks in numericals.
> 4. **Calling electricity "primary"** — always mark electricity as **secondary**, irrespective of source.
> 5. **Skipping the efficiency step in numericals** — if the question mentions "electrical energy" with a plant efficiency, students often forget to divide by $\eta$ and lose 2.5 marks.
> 6. **Writing examples without applications** — KTU Part A (3 marks) requires **example + application**, not just names. Memorize one application per source.
> 7. **Confusing Toe with BOE** — $1$ Toe $= 41.868$ GJ; $1$ BOE (barrel of oil equivalent) $= 5.8$ MMBTU $\approx 6.12$ GJ. Use Toe for KTU unless specified.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Primary = Nature's gift**; **Secondary = human-converted** (electricity, petrol, hydrogen, coke).
- ⭐ **Renewable = replenishable on human timescale**; **Non-Renewable = finite** (fossil + nuclear).
- ⭐ **Conventional = traditional** (coal, oil, large hydro); **Non-Conventional = modern** (solar PV, wind, OTEC, tidal).
- ⭐ **Commercial = bought/sold** (LPG, kerosene); **Non-Commercial = free/traditional** (firewood, cow-dung).
- ⭐ **Clean = low/zero emissions** (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal); **Polluting = high emissions** (coal, oil, traditional biomass).
- ⭐ **Sun is the master source** of all renewables **except** geothermal and tidal.
- ⭐ **Solar constant** $G_{SC} = 1361$ W/m² — the incoming flux at the top of Earth's atmosphere.
- ⭐ **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** — the most-tested conversion in KTU numericals.
- ⭐ **1 Toe = 41.868 GJ** — used in national energy accounting.
- ⭐ **Replenishment index** $R_{idx} = \dot{E}_{replenish} / \dot{E}_{consume}$: $\geq 1$ for renewables, $\ll 1$ for non-renewables.
- ⭐ **Coal calorific value** $\approx 24$–$25$ MJ/kg; **Petrol** $\approx 44$ MJ/kg; **Natural gas** $\approx 55$ MJ/kg; **Biogas (60% methane)** $\approx 22$ MJ/m³.
- ⭐ **Nuclear = non-renewable** in KTU 2024 context.
- ⭐ **Electricity is ALWAYS secondary** — never classify as primary.
- ⭐ **Plant efficiency $\eta$** must be applied to convert *thermal input* → *electrical output*.
- ⭐ **CO₂ emission factor of coal** $\approx 2.42$ kg CO₂/kg coal (this factor appears in 14-mark KTU environmental numericals).
- ⭐ **India context:** Non-commercial energy is still ~25–30% of rural household energy — examiners love this statistic.
- ⭐ **Master classification grid** in Section 3.3 is the single most important visual summary to memorize for KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Social Implications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Social Implications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌍 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Social Implications of Renewable Energy** refer to the comprehensive set of direct and indirect effects, both tangible and intangible, that the transition from conventional fossil-fuel-based energy systems to renewable energy systems (RES) exerts upon human societies, communities, livelihoods, cultural values, public health, and equity structures.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is examined under **Course Outcome CO1 / CO2** of *Renewable Energy Sources*, where students must demonstrate the ability to *"understand the environmental, economic, and social dimensions of renewable energy adoption."*

The social dimension is often referred to as the **"Third Pillar"** of sustainable energy development, alongside the **environmental (1st pillar)** and **economic (2nd pillar)** dimensions. Without addressing the social pillar, even technologically superior and economically viable renewable projects can fail due to public opposition, social inequity, or cultural misalignment.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Sustainable Energy

| Pillar | Focus | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Environmental** | Emissions, ecology | $\text{CO}_2$ avoided (tonnes/year) |
| **Economic** | Cost, jobs, GDP | LCOE (₹/kWh) |
| **Social** | Equity, health, acceptance | Social Acceptance Index (SAI) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Village Well → Solar Microgrid Transition**

> Imagine a village in Kerala that has relied for generations on a single community well (representing **fossil fuels**). The well is reliable, but it is far from some homes, the water is sometimes polluted, the elderly struggle to fetch it, and disputes arise over usage rights. When a **solar microgrid with rooftop panels** is proposed, the reaction is mixed:
>
> - **Homes near the well** (incumbent fossil users) may resist — *"Why change what works?"* (Resistance to change)
> - **Homes far from the well** (energy-poor) welcome it enthusiastically — *"Finally, electricity at home!"* (Energy equity gain)
> - **Traditional well-drawers** (fossil-sector workers) fear losing their livelihood — (Just transition concerns)
> - **The village temple committee** worries about panels altering the skyline — (Cultural/aesthetic concerns)
> - **The local school** sees an opportunity for evening study hours — (Human development gain)
>
> This single transformation captures **all five major social implications**: equity, livelihood, culture, acceptance, and development.

## 1.4 Key Social Metrics Used Globally

The following standard metrics are referenced by the **International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA)** and the **International Energy Agency (IEA)** when quantifying social implications:

- **Jobs-to-MW Ratio:** $J_{MW}$ = number of direct jobs created per MW installed
- **Energy Access Rate:** % of population with reliable electricity
- **Displacement Factor:** persons displaced per $\text{km}^2$ of project footprint
- **PM2.5 Health Cost Avoided:** monetary value of avoided respiratory disease
- **Social Acceptance Index (SAI):** composite score on a 0–100 scale

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 expects students to articulate *at least five* distinct social implications and provide one real-world example (preferably Indian) for each.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Social Implications — The "HEAL-PEACE" Framework

For systematic analysis, KTU examiners expect a structured decomposition. The eight major social implications can be remembered using the mnemonic **HEAL-PEACE**:

### H — Health & Well-being
- **Mechanism:** Replacement of indoor biomass combustion (chulha) and kerosene lamps eliminates **Particulate Matter (PM2.5)**, carbon monoxide, and nitrogen oxides.
- **WHO Burden:** ~3.8 million premature deaths/year from household air pollution (HAP).
- **Kerala Specific:** LPG and electric cooking transitions have reduced respiratory OPD visits by an estimated **15–20%** in rural districts.

### E — Employment & Livelihoods
- **Net Job Creation:** IRENA reports that the renewable sector created **16.2 million jobs globally in 2022** (vs. 18 million in coal + oil + gas combined).
- **Job Quality Shift:** Manual extractive jobs → skilled technical/ manufacturing jobs.
- **Just Transition:** Workers in coal belts (e.g., Jharkhand, Odisha) need reskilling.

### A — Access & Affordability (Energy Equity)
- **Definition:** Universal access to affordable, reliable, and modern energy services — **SDG 7**.
- **Mechanism:** Decentralized renewables (solar home systems, mini-grids) leapfrog grid extension in remote areas.
- **Kerala Success:** 100% household electrification (KSEB 2023), but quality of supply and affordability remain issues for below-poverty-line (BPL) families.

### L — Land Use & Displacement
- **Footprint Concern:** Utility-scale solar/wind farms require large contiguous land parcels.
- **Conflict Triggers:** Displacement of farmers, loss of grazing land, deforestation.
- **Mitigation:** Agrivoltaics, floating solar, rooftop优先.

### P — Public Participation & Acceptance
- **NIMBY Effect:** *"Not In My BackYard"* — local opposition despite national-level support.
- **Social Acceptance Theory (Wüstenhagen et al., 2007):**
  - **Socio-political acceptance** (public opinion)
  - **Community acceptance** (local siting)
  - **Market acceptance** (consumer adoption)

### E — Education & Skill Development
- **Multiplier Effect:** Each renewable project creates demand for technicians, electricians, project managers.
- **Kerala Initiative:** ANERT (Agency for New and Renewable Energy Research and Technology) skill training programs.

### A — Aesthetic & Cultural Impact
- **Wind-Solar Hybrid Visual:** Alters rural landscape character.
- **Sacred Groves & Heritage Sites:** RE projects near culturally sensitive zones (e.g., temple hills in Kerala) face resistance.
- **Indigenous Knowledge:** Traditional ecological knowledge must be integrated.

### C — Community Empowerment & Energy Democracy
- **Prosumer Model:** Households become both producers and consumers of energy.
- **Energy Cooperatives:** Village-level solar cooperatives (e.g., Dhule, Maharashtra model).
- **Gender Equity:** Women spend 3–6 hours/day on biomass collection; electrification frees this time.

### E — Economic Distributional Effects
- **Tariff Equity:** Cross-subsidy in electricity tariffs — industrial consumers subsidize domestic.
- **RE Impact on Tariffs:** Initial high cost, but long-term deflationary due to zero fuel cost.

## 2.2 The Three Layers of Social Acceptance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wüstenhagen, Wolsink & Bürer (2007) Model — A KTU High-Yield Concept**

Social acceptance of renewable energy operates at **three distinct layers**:

1. **Socio-political Acceptance:** Society-wide approval, policy support, and media discourse.
2. **Market Acceptance:** Consumer willingness to purchase/adopt renewable-based products (e.g., rooftop solar, EVs).
3. **Community Acceptance:** Local-level acceptance at the specific project site — the most fragile layer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Jobs per MW (Solar PV) | $J_{MW} = \dfrac{N_{jobs}}{P_{installed}}$ | jobs/MW | Employment forecasting |
| 2 | Energy Access Rate | $EAR = \dfrac{H_{electrified}}{H_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | SDG-7 monitoring |
| 3 | Avoided Health Cost | $AHC = C_{PM2.5} \times E_{avoided}$ | ₹/year | Cost-benefit analysis |
| 4 | Land Footprint Intensity | $LFI = \dfrac{A_{land}}{E_{annual}}$ | $\text{ha}/\text{GWh}$ | Land-use planning |
| 5 | Social Acceptance Index | $SAI = \dfrac{\sum w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum w_i}$ | 0–100 | Public perception studies |
| 6 | Payback Social Period | $PSP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{B_{social,annual}}$ | years | Social ROI |
| 7 | Displacement Factor | $DF = \dfrac{N_{displaced}}{A_{project}}$ | persons/ha | Siting ethics |
| 8 | Energy Poverty Gap | $EPG = E_{req} - E_{available}$ | $\text{kWh/yr}$ | Equity analysis |

> **Notation Key:** $N_{jobs}$ = number of jobs, $P_{installed}$ = installed capacity in MW, $C_{PM2.5}$ = health cost per kg of PM2.5, $E_{avoided}$ = emissions avoided, $A_{land}$ = land area used, $E_{annual}$ = annual energy generated, $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = sub-score, $C_{investment}$ = total cost, $B_{social,annual}$ = annual social benefit.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding social implications is not "soft" — it is **mission-critical engineering** because:

- A **rejected wind farm** (e.g., the Satara wind project protests in Maharashtra) wastes crores in sunk cost.
- A **socially-blind solar park** can trigger 5–10 year legal delays.
- **World Bank/ADB** funding for RE projects now mandates **Social Impact Assessment (SIA)** as a loan condition.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, 2013** in India makes SIA legally binding for projects above **100 acres (rural) / 50 acres (urban)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Quantitative Models

## 3.1 Derivation: Social Return on Investment (SROI) for a Rooftop Solar Project

> This derivation shows how a *social* return is quantified in monetary terms — a frequent KTU numerical question.

**Given:**
- A 5 kW rooftop solar system installed in a Kerala rural household.
- Investment: ₹3,50,000 (after MNRE subsidy).
- Annual electricity bill saved: ₹18,000/year.
- Annual health cost avoided (reduced kerosene use): ₹4,500/year.
- Annual productive hours gained (lighting for evening study): ₹6,000/year.
- Project lifetime: 25 years.
- Discount rate: $r = 8\%$.

**Step 1 — Define Total Social Benefit Stream**

The annual social benefit is the sum of *direct* (electricity bill) and *indirect* (health + productive time) benefits:

$$B_{annual} = B_{direct} + B_{health} + B_{productive}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$B_{annual} = 18{,}000 + 4{,}500 + 6{,}000 = 28{,}500 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Present Value of Total Benefits (PVB)**

Using the present value of annuity formula:

$$PVB = B_{annual} \times \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Annuity Factor**

$$\frac{1 - (1 + 0.08)^{-25}}{0.08} = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-25}}{0.08}$$

$$(1.08)^{25} = 6.8485 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1.08)^{-25} = \frac{1}{6.8485} = 0.1460$$

$$\text{Annuity factor} = \frac{1 - 0.1460}{0.08} = \frac{0.8540}{0.08} = 10.675$$

**Step 5 — Compute PVB**

$$PVB = 28{,}500 \times 10.675 = 3{,}04{,}237.5 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Net Present Social Value (NPSV)**

$$NPSV = PVB - C_{investment}$$

$$NPSV = 3{,}04{,}237.5 - 3{,}50{,}000 = -45{,}762.5 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**

The NPSV is **negative in pure monetary terms**, indicating the household does not recover the full investment through quantifiable social benefits alone. However, this calculation **excludes** unquantified co-benefits (climate contribution, energy security, dignity, women's time saved). KTU examiners expect students to **critically evaluate** this gap.

## 3.2 Derivation: Land-Use Conflict Index for a Wind Farm

**Given:**
- Proposed wind farm: 50 MW capacity.
- Total land footprint: 200 hectares (hill-top ridgeline).
- Houses within 500 m buffer: 45 households.
- Tribal hamlets affected: 2 (120 persons).

**Step 1 — Compute Displacement Factor (DF)**

$$DF = \frac{N_{displaced}}{A_{project}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$DF = \frac{120}{200} = 0.6 \text{ persons/ha}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Land Footprint Intensity (LFI)**

Assuming Capacity Factor $CF = 0.30$:

$$E_{annual} = P \times 8760 \times CF = 50 \times 8760 \times 0.30$$

$$E_{annual} = 1{,}31{,}400 \text{ MWh/yr} = 131.4 \text{ GWh/yr}$$

$$LFI = \frac{200}{131.4} = 1.52 \text{ ha/GWh}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret Against Benchmarks**

| RE Technology | Typical LFI ($\text{ha}/\text{GWh}$) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Rooftop Solar | 0.0 | Best |
| Offshore Wind | ~0.5 | Excellent |
| Onshore Wind (this case) | 1.52 | Moderate |
| Utility Solar | 4.0–8.0 | High impact |
| Bioenergy (crop-based) | 50–200 | Severe |

**Step 5 — Conclude**

The wind farm has a **moderate LFI**, but the presence of **tribal displacement** elevates the project to **high social-risk category**, triggering mandatory SIA under the 2013 Act.

## 3.3 Comparative Quantitative Analysis: Social Cost of Coal vs. Solar

| Parameter | Coal-based Power | Solar PV (Rooftop) |
|---|---|---|
| PM2.5 emissions | $0.05 \text{ kg/kWh}$ | $\approx 0$ |
| Health cost (Kerala) | ₹0.80 / kWh externalized | ₹0 |
| Water consumption | $1.2 \text{ L/kWh}$ | $0.02 \text{ L/kWh}$ (cleaning only) |
| Displacement (lifecycle) | High (mining) | Negligible |
| Local employment (per MW) | 0.7 jobs | 5–7 jobs (manufacturing) |

> **Engineering Insight:** Even without monetizing climate damage, the **avoided health cost alone** (~₹0.80/kWh for coal) makes rooftop solar socially optimal in high-pollution urban Kerala districts (Eloor, Kochi industrial belt).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stakeholder Map of a Renewable Energy Project

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Renewable Energy Project] --> B[Primary Stakeholders]
    A --> C[Secondary Stakeholders]
    A --> D[Tertiary Stakeholders]
    B --> B1[Local Residents]
    B --> B2[Farmers and Land Owners]
    B --> B3[Indigenous Communities]
    B --> B4[Local Panchayat]
    C --> C1[State DISCOM e.g. KSEB]
    C --> C2[Central Agencies MNRE ANERT]
    C --> C3[Project Developer]
    C --> C4[Financial Institutions]
    D --> D1[NGOs and Civil Society]
    D --> D2[Academic Institutions]
    D --> D3[Media]
    D --> D4[Future Generations]
    B1 --> E[Social Acceptance]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
```

## 4.2 Social Impact Flow — Causal Chain Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    RE[Renewable Energy Deployment] --> EMP[Job Creation]
    RE --> HE[Health Improvement]
    RE --> EQ[Energy Equity]
    RE --> LU[Land Use Change]
    RE --> CU[Cultural Shift]
    EMP --> LIV[Improved Livelihoods]
    EMP --> SK[Skill Upgradation]
    HE --> PROD[Higher Productivity]
    HE --> HOSP[Lower Hospital Visits]
    EQ --> ACC[Universal Access]
    EQ --> GEN[Women Empowerment]
    LU --> DISP[Possible Displacement]
    LU --> AGR[Agrivoltaic Opportunity]
```
