# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Social Implications" in the context of renewable energy. List any four major social implications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Social implications of renewable energy refer to the direct and indirect effects of RE adoption on human society, including health, employment, equity, cultural values, and community well-being.

> **Any four (½ mark each = 2 marks):**
> 1. **Public health improvement** due to reduction in air pollution from fossil fuels.
> 2. **Employment generation** in manufacturing, installation, and maintenance sectors.
> 3. **Energy equity and access** to remote/under-electrified areas.
> 4. **Land-use conflicts** and displacement concerns.
> 5. **Community acceptance** issues (NIMBY effect).
> 6. **Women empowerment** through reduced drudgery of fuel collection.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the "Just Transition" concept. Why is it critical for India?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Concept (1.5 marks):** *Just Transition* refers to a planned, inclusive, and equitable shift from a fossil-fuel-based economy to a clean energy economy, ensuring that workers, communities, and regions dependent on conventional energy industries are not economically or socially abandoned during the transition. It encompasses reskilling, social safety nets, economic diversification, and stakeholder dialogue.

> **Critical for India (1.5 marks):** India has ~4 million direct coal-sector workers (Coal India + Singareni + captive mines) and ~10 million indirect workers. States like Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, and West Bengal have coal-dependent economies. Without a Just Transition framework, the RE transition will create severe regional unemployment, fiscal stress, and social unrest — undermining the very sustainability goals it seeks to achieve.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **"Wüstenhagen Three-Layer Model of Social Acceptance"** of renewable energy. Illustrate each layer with one Indian example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **land-use conflicts** arising from utility-scale solar and wind projects in India. Suggest two engineering/managerial mitigation strategies.

#### 📘 Model Solution

**(a) Three-Layer Model (7 marks)**

The Wüstenhagen, Wolsink & Bürer (2007) framework categorizes social acceptance of RE into three interconnected layers:

**Layer 1 — Socio-Political Acceptance (2.5 marks):**
This refers to the acceptance of RE technologies and policies at the level of the broader society, government, and media.

*Mechanisms:*
- Public opinion shaped by climate awareness campaigns.
- Policy signals: India's **500 GW non-fossil target by 2030 (Panchamrit announcement, COP26)**.
- Media narratives around PM-KUSUM, Saubhagya scheme.

*Indian Example:* The **International Solar Alliance (ISA)**, headquartered in India, reflects strong socio-political acceptance at the national and diplomatic level.

**Layer 2 — Market Acceptance (2.5 marks):**
This refers to the adoption of RE technologies by consumers, industries, and investors.

*Mechanisms:*
- Falling LCOE of solar (₹2.5/kWh in 2024).
- Subsidies (MNRE rooftop solar PM-Surya Ghar Yojana 2024).
- Corporate PPAs.

*Indian Example:* Cross-sectoral RE purchase by companies like **Tata Steel and Infosys** (RE100 commitment).

**Layer 3 — Community Acceptance (2 marks):**
This is the most fragile layer — local-level acceptance at the specific project site.

*Mechanisms:*
- Benefit-sharing mechanisms.
- Local employment.
- Transparent consultations.

*Indian Example:* Resistance to the **Jaisalmer Solar Park** due to grazing land loss — community acceptance failures led to project delays.

**Conclusion:** All three layers must succeed for RE deployment to be sustainable.

---

**(b) Land-Use Conflicts & Mitigation (7 marks)**

**Nature of Conflicts (3 marks):**

1. **Agricultural Land Loss:** Utility solar parks (e.g., Pavagada, Karnataka) acquire 13,000+ acres of semi-arid farmland.
2. **Forest Land Diversion:** Wind farms in Western Ghats threaten biodiversity hotspots and elephant corridors.
3. **Tribal Displacement:** Projects in Adivasi belts (e.g., Hasdeo Aranya, Chhattisgarh) face severe opposition.
4. **Grazing Land Loss:** Affects pastoralist communities (Gujarat Banni grassland, Rajasthan).
5. **Coastal Conflicts:** Offshore wind vs. fishing communities.

**Mitigation Strategies (4 marks):**

**Strategy 1 — Agrivoltaics (2 marks):**
Co-locating solar panels with crops (e.g., Kerala's pilot agrivoltaic project for turmeric and cardamom) preserves agricultural income while generating power. This is **engineering-led mitigation** — elevated panels allow 70% sunlight to pass through.

**Strategy 2 — Floating Solar + Land Restoration (2 marks):**
Kerala's **NTPC floating solar at Kayamkulam (100 MW)** uses reservoir surfaces, avoiding land conflicts. Additionally, developers can commit to **3:1 land restoration ratios** — restoring 3 hectares of degraded land for every 1 hectare used.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **public health implications** of transitioning from fossil fuels to renewable energy, with a focus on **household air pollution in Kerala**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Critically analyze the role of **decentralized renewable energy systems** in achieving **energy equity** in geographically isolated regions of India.

#### 📘 Model Solution

**(a) Public Health Implications (7 marks)**

**Hazard Identification (2 marks):**
- Indoor air pollution from kerosene and biomass burning releases PM2.5, CO, NO$_x$, formaldehyde, and benzene.
- WHO data: ~3.8 million deaths/year globally from HAP; India accounts for ~25%.
- Kerala-specific: Despite high literacy, ~6% of rural households still use kerosene for primary lighting (Census 2011 + KSEB 2023 estimates).

**Mechanism of Health Improvement via RE (2 marks):**
- **Solar lanterns / SHS** replace kerosene → eliminate CO and PM2.5 exposure.
- **Clean cooking (electric/LPG)** replaces biomass → reduce ARI (Acute Respiratory Infection) in children.
- **Electricity for health facilities** → vaccine cold chain integrity.

**Quantified Health Cost Savings (3 marks):**
- Kerosene lighting cost: ~₹120/month + health cost of ~₹300/month per household (WHO HEAT methodology).
- Rooftop solar saves **₹4,500/year per household** in avoided health costs (as derived in Section 3.1).
- Kerala State Pollution Control Board data: Eloor industrial area PM2.5 levels have dropped ~18% post-renewable integration in KSEB grid mix (2020–2024).

**Concluding Note:** RE is a **co-benefit for public health infrastructure**, not just an environmental measure.

---

**(b) Decentralized RE & Energy Equity (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Decentralized Renewable Energy (DRE) refers to off-grid or mini-grid systems (typically < 100 kW) that generate electricity close to the point of consumption, often in remote, isolated, or energy-poor regions.

**Equity Case (3 marks):**
- **Universal Access Gap:** ~2% of Indian households (Census 2011) lacked electricity; concentrated in tribal, hilly, and island regions.
- **Grid Extension Limits:** Last-mile grid extension costs ₹50,000–1,00,000 per household in remote areas.
- **DRE Cost-Effectiveness:** Solar home systems (₹15,000–50,000) and mini-grids are 2–5× cheaper than grid extension in low-density areas.

**Indian Case Studies (3 marks):**
- **Andaman & Nicobar Islands:** Solar-based microgrids reduced diesel generator dependence by 60%.
- **Ladakh:** Solar + battery hybrid systems replaced expensive diesel transport.
- **Kerala's Wayanad Tribal Hamlets:** ANERT solar microgrids electrified 35+ tribal colonies.

**Critical Analysis:**
- **Strengths:** Speed of deployment, no transmission losses, community ownership.
- **Weaknesses:** Maintenance challenges, upfront cost, intermittent supply, lack of skilled local technicians.

**Conclusion:** DRE is *not a substitute* but a *complement* to the centralized grid — crucial for achieving **SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy) by 2030**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Social" with "Environmental":** Students often write generic environment answers (deforestation, emissions) for social questions. KTU examiners *deduct up to 3 marks* for this. Always anchor in **human/societal terms** — people, communities, equity, health.
>
> 2. **Ignoring Indian Context:** KTU is an Indian university. Avoid purely Western case studies (German Energiewende, Danish wind). **Use Indian examples** — KSEB, ANERT, Saubhagya, PM-KUSUM, Kudankulam protests, etc.
>
> 3. **Listing Without Analysis:** A bare 8-bullet list of social implications with no examples or engineering linkage will score only **2–3 out of 7 marks**. KTU 2024 scheme demands **Apply / Analyze** level responses (Bloom's Level 3+).
>
> 4. **Forgetting Quantitative Dimension:** Social questions in 2024 scheme increasingly carry a **numerical sub-part**. Practice calculating **jobs/MW, LCOE, NPV with social co-benefits, displacement factors**.
>
> 5. **Skipping Counter-arguments:** For full 7 marks on a "Discuss" question, present **both** social benefits *and* social challenges. One-sided answers are capped at 4 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Social Implications of RE**

- ✅ **Definition:** Social implications = effects of RE on **people, communities, equity, health, culture** (NOT environment alone).
- ✅ **Eight major implications:** Health, Employment, Access, Land, Participation, Education, Aesthetics, Community empowerment (**HEAL-PEACE** mnemonic).
- ✅ **Wüstenhagen Model:** 3 layers — **Socio-political, Market, Community**.
- ✅ **Just Transition:** Equitable shift; reskilling + social protection for fossil-sector workers.
- ✅ **Energy Equity** = SDG 7; addressed via **decentralized RE** (solar home systems, mini-grids).
- ✅ **NIMBY Effect:** Local resistance despite national acceptance — most fragile acceptance layer.
- ✅ **Key Indian Laws:** RFCTLARR Act 2013 (SIA mandatory > 100 acres rural); Electricity Act 2003.
- ✅ **Key Indian Schemes:** Saubhagya (electrification), PM-KUSUM (solar pumps), PM-Surya Ghar Yojana 2024 (rooftop).
- ✅ **Quantitative Metrics to Memorize:** Jobs/MW (solar ≈ 5–7, wind ≈ 0.4), Health cost (~₹0.80/kWh coal), Kerala household electrification (~100%).
- ✅ **Energy Democracy:** Prosumer model + community cooperatives.
- ✅ **Gender Co-benefit:** Women gain ~3 hours/day from clean cooking access.
- ✅ **Exam Trick:** Always pair a social claim with an **Indian example + a number**.

> **Final Tip:** KTU 2024 scheme rewards *systems thinking*. Always show how a technological choice (e.g., coal vs. solar) creates a *cascade* of social outcomes (health → productivity → education → equity). This narrative is what separates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Energy Resources: Availability and Their Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conventional Energy Resources: Availability and Their Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Conventional Energy Resources** are the traditional, non-renewable (with the partial exception of large hydro) energy sources that have been commercially exploited since the onset of the industrial era. They are characterized by their finite geological reserves, their dominance in the present global primary energy mix, and their well-established extraction, processing, and utilization infrastructure. According to the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Renewable Energy Sources*, these resources are classified into three principal categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Classification of Conventional Energy Resources**
> 1. **Fossil Fuels** — Coal, Petroleum (Crude Oil), and Natural Gas
> 2. **Nuclear Energy** — Fission-based (with fusion as a future prospect)
> 3. **Large Hydroelectric Power** — Often included as conventional due to its long-established grid presence

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Earth as a giant **piggy bank** that has been collecting energy deposits for **hundreds of millions of years** — sunlight locked inside ancient plants (which became coal), microscopic sea creatures compressed into oil droplets, and radioactive minerals baked deep inside the planet's crust. Every time humanity withdraws a barrel of oil, a lump of coal, or a pellet of uranium, we are essentially **cracking open the piggy bank and spending a coin that was never going to be replaced** within any human timeframe.

The "availability" of these resources is therefore analogous to checking the **piggy bank's current balance** — and the "limitations" are the rules governing *how fast we can spend it* and *what pollution we create on the way out*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams**
> Conventional energy is *depletable* and *non-sustainable* on a human timescale, whereas renewable energy (covered in later modules) is *inexhaustible* on the same timescale. This contrast is the central motivating pillar of the entire course.

### Primary Energy Metrics (Standard KTU Board Constants)

- **Hubbert's Peak Theory** — Predicts that the production rate of any finite resource follows a **bell-shaped curve**, peaking at roughly half the total recoverable reserves.
- **Energy Reserve Categories** (in order of decreasing certainty):
  - **Proved (Measured) Reserves** — `1P` — Recoverable with >90% probability
  - **Probable Reserves** — `2P` — Recoverable with >50% probability
  - **Possible Reserves** — `3P` — Recoverable with >10% probability
- **R/P Ratio** — *Reserves-to-Production Ratio* — the number of years the reserve will last at the current extraction rate.
- **Energy Unit Standards**:
  - **1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)** = **41.868 GJ** ≈ **42 GJ**
  - **1 Quad** = **1.055 × 10¹⁸ J**
  - **1 Mtoe** = **0.041868 EJ** = **41.868 PJ**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board questions frequently test the **R/P ratio** and the classification of reserves. Memorize the symbol conventions above — they appear almost every semester.

---

### Historical Context of Conventional Energy

The global energy paradigm can be divided into three distinct **technological eras**:

1. **Wood Era** (Pre-1780s): Biomass combustion, animal and human muscle.
2. **Coal Era** (1780s–1950s): Steam engines, industrial revolution, the rise of railways.
3. **Hydrocarbon-Nuclear Era** (1950s–Present): Oil, natural gas, nuclear fission.

The transition from the wood era to the coal era was driven by the **higher energy density** of coal. The transition from coal to hydrocarbons was driven by the **superior combustion properties, portability, and lower particulate emissions** of oil and gas. Nuclear entered the mix in the post-WWII period due to its **extraordinarily high energy density** — about **1 kilogram of uranium-235** yields energy equivalent to roughly **20,000 tonnes of coal**.

---

### Conventional Energy — The Big Picture

The global primary energy consumption is dominated by fossil fuels. As per widely cited energy statistics, the approximate share is:

| Resource | Global Share (approx.) |
|----------|------------------------|
| Coal | ~27% |
| Oil | ~33% |
| Natural Gas | ~24% |
| Nuclear | ~4% |
| Hydro | ~7% |
| Renewables | ~5% |

This means that **roughly 84% of the world's primary energy** still comes from conventional (mostly fossil) sources — the very statistic that motivates the entire *Renewable Energy Sources* course.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Conventional Energy — Structured Analysis

#### Pillar I: COAL

**Formation (Why it exists):**
- Coal is **solidified ancient biomass** — primarily tree ferns, mosses, and lycopod trees from the **Carboniferous period (~360–290 million years ago)**.
- Under intense heat, pressure, and anaerobic conditions, peat → lignite → sub-bituminous → bituminous → anthracite. Higher rank = higher carbon content = higher energy density.

**Availability — Global:**
- World total recoverable coal reserves ≈ **1,070 billion tonnes**.
- Top holders: **USA, Russia, Australia, China, India**.

**Availability — India:**
- India holds roughly **10% of global coal reserves** (≈ 360 billion tonnes), making it the **second-largest coal producer** globally.
- Major coalfields: **Jharkhand (Jharia — the largest), Odisha, Chhattisgarh, West Bengal (Raniganj), Madhya Pradesh, Telangana**.

**Limitations:**
1. **Highest CO₂ emission per unit energy** among fossil fuels — roughly **~950 g CO₂/kWh** for coal vs. ~720 for oil and ~490 for natural gas.
2. **Particulate Matter (PM 2.5, PM 10)** emissions cause severe respiratory illness (a major KTU board favorite).
3. **Acid rain** due to **SO₂ and NOₓ** emissions.
4. **Ash and solid waste** — fly ash disposal is a massive environmental problem.
5. **Mining hazards** — collapses, black lung disease, displacement of indigenous communities.
6. **Methane leakage** during extraction is a powerful greenhouse gas (GWP ≈ 28–36× CO₂ over 100 years).
7. **Finite stock** — current R/P ratio at global scale ≈ **130 years** (but this is highly debated given Hubbert's Peak concerns).

#### Pillar II: PETROLEUM (CRUDE OIL)

**Formation:**
- Formed from **marine zooplankton and algae** deposited in anoxic sea-floor sediments, transformed by heat and pressure over **~100–300 million years**.

**Availability — Global:**
- World proven oil reserves ≈ **1.7 trillion barrels** (≈ 240 billion tonnes).
- Top holders: **Venezuela, Saudi Arabia, Canada (oil sands), Iran, Iraq**.

**Availability — India:**
- India imports **>85% of its crude oil** — a critical national energy security concern.
- Domestic production: **Mumbai High (offshore — largest), Assam (Digboi — the oldest), Gujarat (Ankleshwar, Cambay Basin), Rajasthan (Barmer-Sanchor), Krishna-Godavari Basin**.

**Limitations:**
1. **Geopolitical volatility** — oil prices fluctuate with wars, OPEC decisions, and supply disruptions.
2. **Severe pollution** — oil spills (Exxon Valdez, Deepwater Horizon), refinery emissions, tailpipe pollutants.
3. **High CO₂ emissions** (~720 g CO₂/kWh) and **NOₓ** causing smog and ground-level ozone.
4. **Hubbert's Peak** — many analysts argue global oil production may have already peaked or will peak within decades.
5. **Finite reserves** — global R/P ratio ≈ **47 years** at current production rates.
6. **Acid rain** from sulfur content in crude.

#### Pillar III: NATURAL GAS

**Formation:**
- Often co-located with petroleum; formed by similar processes. The "lighter" hydrocarbon (mainly **CH₄**) that escaped from oil reservoirs.

**Availability — Global:**
- World proven gas reserves ≈ **7,100 trillion cubic feet (TCF)**.
- Top holders: **Russia, Iran, Qatar, Turkmenistan, USA**.

**Availability — India:**
- India has **modest reserves** concentrated in:
  - **Krishna-Godavari Basin** (KG-D6, Reliance)
  - **Mumbai High**
  - **Assam**
  - **Rajasthan (CBM — Coal Bed Methane)**
  - **Gujarat (Gandhar, Hazira)**
- Major LNG import terminals: **Dahej, Hazira, Kochi (Puthuvypeen), Dabhol**.

**Limitations:**
1. **Methane leakage** — main component (CH₄) has **84× the GWP of CO₂ over 20 years**.
2. **Transportation hazards** — pipelines, LNG tankers are explosion risks.
3. **Flaring** — associated gas is routinely flared (wasted) in many oil fields.
4. **Still finite** — R/P ratio ≈ **48 years** globally.
5. **Upstream methane emissions** during hydraulic fracturing (fracking) — water contamination concerns.
6. **Combustion still produces CO₂** — just less per kWh than coal or oil.

#### Pillar IV: NUCLEAR ENERGY

**How it works:**
- **Fission** of heavy nuclei (U-235, Pu-239) releases energy through **E = mc²** mass defect.
- 1 kg of U-235 → energy equivalent of ~20,000 tonnes of coal.

**Availability — Global:**
- Uranium reserves concentrated in: **Australia, Kazakhstan, Canada, Russia, Namibia**.
- **Thorium reserves** are massive in **India (≈ 25% of global thorium)** — present in the monazite sands of Kerala's coastline.

**Availability — India:**
- India's three-stage nuclear program (Homi Bhabha's vision):
  - **Stage I**: Pressurized Heavy Water Reactors (PHWRs) using natural uranium.
  - **Stage II**: Fast Breeder Reactors (FBRs) using plutonium.
  - **Stage III**: Thorium-based reactors.
- Operational reactors: **Kudankulam (Tamil Nadu), Kakrapar (Gujarat), Tarapur (Maharashtra), Kaiga (Karnataka), Narora (UP), Rawatbhata (Rajasthan)**.

**Limitations:**
1. **Radioactive waste** — spent fuel remains hazardous for **tens of thousands of years**.
2. **Risk of catastrophic accidents** — Chernobyl (1986), Fukushima (2011), Three Mile Island (1979).
3. **High capital cost** and long construction lead times (10–15 years).
4. **Uranium scarcity concerns** — although breeder reactors and thorium can mitigate this.
5. **Proliferation risk** — nuclear technology can be diverted to weapons.
6. **Public perception** — strong opposition post-Fukushima.
7. **Decommissioning cost** — dismantling a reactor at end-of-life is expensive.

#### Pillar V: LARGE HYDROELECTRIC POWER

**How it works:**
- Water stored at elevation in a reservoir is released through penstocks, spinning turbines connected to generators.

**Availability — Global:**
- Technically exploitable global hydro potential ≈ **14,000 TWh/year**.
- Only ~**16–20%** is currently utilized.

**Availability — India:**
- India has an installed hydro capacity of ~**47 GW** (one of the largest globally).
- Major projects: **Bhakra Nangal, Tehri Dam, Sardar Sarovar, Nathpa Jhakri, Idukki (Kerala)**.
- India's total identified hydro potential ≈ **145 GW (at 60% load factor)**.

**Limitations:**
1. **Submergence of forest and agricultural land** — large-scale displacement of populations.
2. **Ecological damage** — blocking fish migration, altering river ecosystems.
3. **Sedimentation** — reservoirs eventually silt up, reducing storage capacity.
4. **Methane emissions** from decomposing biomass in tropical reservoirs (significant for KTU!).
5. **Geological risk** — dam failure (e.g., the 1979 Machhu dam disaster in Gujarat).
6. **Climate vulnerability** — droughts reduce generation capacity.
7. **Long gestation period** and high capital cost.
8. **Often classified as renewable** — but KTU and many texts treat large hydro as **conventional** because of the mature, grid-integrated, large-scale paradigm.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Definition** | **Formula / Expression** | **Typical Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| R/P Ratio | Reserves-to-Production Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | Years |
| Energy Density (Coal, Anthracite) | Energy per unit mass | ≈ 24–35 MJ/kg | MJ/kg |
| Energy Density (Crude Oil) | Energy per unit mass | ≈ 42–46 MJ/kg | MJ/kg |
| Energy Density (Natural Gas) | Energy per unit volume | ≈ 35–38 MJ/m³ | MJ/m³ |
| Energy Density (U-235) | Energy per unit mass (fission) | ≈ 80,000,000 MJ/kg | MJ/kg |
| 1 Toe (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) | Universal energy unit | = 41.868 GJ | GJ |
| 1 Quad | Quadrillion BTU | = 1.055 × 10¹⁸ J | J |
| 1 Mtoe | Million tonnes of oil equivalent | = 41.868 PJ = 0.041868 EJ | PJ |
| 1 kWh | Electrical energy | = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal | kWh |
| CO₂ Emission Factor (Coal) | Emission intensity | ≈ 950 g CO₂/kWh (thermal) | g/kWh |
| CO₂ Emission Factor (Oil) | Emission intensity | ≈ 720 g CO₂/kWh (thermal) | g/kWh |
| CO₂ Emission Factor (Natural Gas) | Emission intensity | ≈ 490 g CO₂/kWh (thermal) | g/kWh |
| GWP (CH₄ over 100 yr) | Global Warming Potential | ≈ 28–36 (IPCC AR5) | Dimensionless |
| GWP (CH₄ over 20 yr) | Global Warming Potential | ≈ 84–86 (IPCC AR5) | Dimensionless |
| Mass-Energy Equivalence | Einstein's relation | $E = mc^{2}$ | Joules |
| Hubbert Peak Year | Year of peak production | $t_{peak} \approx \dfrac{1}{r}\ln\left(\dfrac{2UR_{total}}{P_{0}}\right)$ (conceptual) | Year |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The most frequently asked formula from Module 1 is the **R/P ratio**. Practice computing it for given reserve and production values. Also memorize the **CO₂ emission factors** in order: **Coal > Oil > Natural Gas**.

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

The study of conventional energy availability and limitations is **not merely academic** — it underpins critical real-world decisions in:

- **National Energy Policy** (e.g., India's **Integrated Energy Policy, 2006**, NEP-2020 framework).
- **Investment decisions** in power generation (₹ Crore-level capital allocation).
- **Geopolitical strategy** — energy import diversification (India's push for solar, biofuels, ethanol blending).
- **Environmental compliance** — **COP (Conference of Parties)** commitments, **Paris Agreement** targets.
- **Corporate ESG reporting** — Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions disclosure.
- **Academic and policy research** — climate modeling, carbon budgeting, and integrated resource planning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reserves-to-Production (R/P) Ratio

The **R/P ratio** is the simplest and most important tool a KTU student must master for the conventional energy discussion. It answers: *"At the current rate of extraction, how many years will the proven reserve last?"*

#### Step 1 — Define the variables
- Let $R$ = Total proven reserve (in tonnes, barrels, m³, etc.)
- Let $P$ = Annual production rate (in the same units per year)

#### Step 2 — Set up the basic time equation
If the entire reserve is depleted at a constant annual production rate, the time to exhaustion is simply:

$$t = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Explanation in plain text:** The time to exhaust a finite reserve equals the total stock divided by the rate of withdrawal. For example, if a coal mine has 100 million tonnes of proven reserves and extracts 5 million tonnes per year, it should last 20 years.

#### Step 3 — Express in canonical R/P form
The R/P ratio is therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\;R/P \;=\; \frac{R}{P}\;\;}$$

It has the dimensions of **years**.

#### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)
**Question:** A coal field has proven reserves of **820 million tonnes**. If the current annual production is **40 million tonnes/year**, calculate:
1. The R/P ratio.
2. The total energy content if the calorific value is **25 MJ/kg**.
3. The number of 600 MW coal-fired power plants that can be operated continuously (8760 h/year) at 70% Plant Load Factor (PLF) on this reserve.

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Part (a) — R/P Ratio**

$$\text{R/P} = \frac{R}{P} = \frac{820 \times 10^{6} \text{ tonnes}}{40 \times 10^{6} \text{ tonnes/year}}$$

$$\text{R/P} = 20.5 \text{ years}$$

**Explanation:** Simply divide the numerator by the denominator. The units of tonnes cancel, leaving years.

**Part (b) — Total Energy Content**

$$E_{\text{total}} = m \times \text{CV} = 820 \times 10^{6} \text{ tonnes} \times 25 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

First, convert tonnes to kg: $820 \times 10^{6} \text{ tonnes} \times 1000 \text{ kg/tonne} = 8.2 \times 10^{11} \text{ kg}$

$$E_{\text{total}} = 8.2 \times 10^{11} \text{ kg} \times 25 \text{ MJ/kg} = 2.05 \times 10^{13} \text{ MJ}$$

Converting to more meaningful units:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2.05 \times 10^{13} \text{ MJ} \times \frac{1 \text{ kWh}}{3.6 \text{ MJ}} \approx 5.69 \times 10^{12} \text{ kWh}$$

**Part (c) — Number of 600 MW Plants**

Annual energy demand of one 600 MW plant at PLF = 0.70:

$$E_{\text{1 plant/year}} = 600 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h/year} \times 0.70$$

$$E_{\text{1 plant/year}} = 3.6792 \times 10^{6} \text{ MWh/year} = 3.6792 \times 10^{9} \text{ kWh/year}$$

Now, the number of plants:

$$N = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{E_{\text{1 plant/year}}} = \frac{5.69 \times 10^{12} \text{ kWh}}{3.6792 \times 10^{9} \text{ kWh/plant}}$$

$$N \approx 1546 \text{ plant-years}$$

**Interpretation:** This single coalfield can supply 1546 600 MW plants for one full year — or equivalently, **1 plant for 1546 years** (or some other combination). For practical sizing, divide by the desired service lifetime.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** Students often forget the **PLF (Plant Load Factor)** in such calculations. Always check whether the problem statement includes it. If not stated, assume **100% PLF (8760 h)**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass-Energy Equivalence Applied to Nuclear Fission

This derivation underpins the **why** of nuclear energy's exceptional energy density.

#### Step 1 — Restate Einstein's relation

$$E = mc^{2}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$.

#### Step 2 — Apply to fission mass defect
When a U-235 nucleus absorbs a neutron and splits, the **total mass of the products is slightly less** than the total mass of the reactants. The difference is called the **mass defect** $\Delta m$.

For a typical U-235 fission event:

$$\Delta m \approx 0.1\% \text{ of the original nucleus mass}$$

**For 1 kg of U-235:**

$$\Delta m = 0.001 \times 1 \text{ kg} = 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the released energy

$$E = \Delta m \cdot c^{2} = 10^{-3} \text{ kg} \times (3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s})^{2}$$

$$E = 10^{-3} \times 9 \times 10^{16} \text{ J}$$

$$E = 9 \times 10^{13} \text{ J} = 9 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ}$$

**Compare with 1 kg of coal (CV = 25 MJ/kg):**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{9 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ}}{25 \text{ MJ}} = 3.6 \times 10^{6}$$

**Conclusion:** 1 kg of U-235 yields the **same energy as 3.6 million kg of coal**. This is the engineering basis for nuclear power's incredible compactness.

---

|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary State | Solid | Liquid | Gas | Solid (UO₂) | Kinetic of water |
| Energy Density | 24–35 MJ/kg | 42–46 MJ/kg | 35–38 MJ/m³ | ~8 × 10⁷ MJ/kg | ~1 kWh/m³ per 100 m head |
| Global R/P (approx.) | ~130 yr | ~47 yr | ~48 yr | ~100+ yr (U) | Effectively renewable (river-fed) |
| CO₂ Emission | ~950 g/kWh | ~720 g/kWh | ~490 g/kWh | ~12 g/kWh (lifecycle) | ~24 g/kWh (lifecycle) |
| Major Pollutants | PM, SO₂, NOₓ, CO₂ | CO₂, NOₓ, VOCs, oil spills | CH₄ (leakage), CO₂ | Radioactive waste | CH₄ (reservoir) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Conventional Energy Resources" and list any four examples.**

**Model Answer:**

Conventional energy resources are the traditional, **commercially established** energy sources that have been the backbone of the global energy economy since the industrial revolution. They are characterized by their **finite reserves** and (with the exception of large hydro) **non-replenishment on a human timescale**. They are also called **non-renewable energy sources**.

**Four examples (any four accepted):**
1. **Coal** — solid fossil fuel
2. **Petroleum (Crude Oil)** — liquid fossil fuel
3. **Natural Gas** — gaseous fossil fuel (mainly methane, CH₄)
4. **Nuclear Energy** — from fission of uranium/plutonium
5. **Large Hydroelectric Power** — water stored in dams (often included as conventional due to mature grid infrastructure)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marker Comment:** A common student mistake is omitting the word "finite" or "non-renewable" from the definition. Examiners dock **½ to 1 mark** for incomplete definitions.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the R/P ratio? What does an R/P ratio of 50 for a fossil fuel indicate?**

**Model Answer:**

The **R/P ratio (Reserves-to-Production Ratio)** is the ratio of the proven reserves of a fuel to its current annual production rate. It is mathematically defined as:

$$R/P = \frac{R}{P}$$

where $R$ = proven reserves and $P$ = annual production rate. The R/P ratio has the unit of **years**.

**Interpretation of R/P = 50:** An R/P ratio of 50 for a fossil fuel indicates that, **at the current rate of production, the proven reserves of that fuel will last for 50 years**, assuming no new discoveries are made and production remains constant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **assumptions** (constant production, no new discoveries). Examiners look for at least one assumption in a 3-mark answer.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **availability of conventional energy resources in India**, focusing on coal, petroleum, and natural gas. Mention the major producing regions for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major limitations of conventional energy resources** with specific reference to **environmental, geopolitical, and economic** aspects. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Availability in India**

**Coal Availability:**
- India is the **second-largest coal producer** globally and holds **~10% of world reserves** (≈ 360 billion tonnes proven).
- Coal provides **~70% of India's electricity**.
- Major coalfields:
  - **Jharkhand** — Jharia (largest), Bokaro, Karanpura, Giridih
  - **West Bengal** — Raniganj (the oldest in India, started 1774)
  - **Odisha** — Talcher
  - **Chhattisgarh** — Korba
  - **Madhya Pradesh** — Singrauli, Sohagpur
  - **Telangana** — Singareni
  - **Maharashtra** — Wardha Valley, Nagpur
- Grades: Mostly **bituminous and sub-bituminous**; low-grade lignite is also abundant in Rajasthan (Neyveli) and Gujarat.

**Petroleum Availability:**
- India imports **>85%** of its crude oil requirements — a major energy security concern.
- Domestic production:
  - **Mumbai High (Bombay High)** — largest offshore field (discovered 1974)
  - **Gujarat** — Ankleshwar, Cambay Basin, Ahmedabad
  - **Assam** — Digboi (oldest oil well, 1889), Naharkatiya, Moran
  - **Rajasthan** — Barmer-Sanchor, Mangala (largest on-shore discovery in decades)
  - **Krishna-Godavari Basin** — KG-D6
  - **Cauvery Basin**
- Refining capacity: India is actually a **net exporter of refined petroleum products** despite being an importer of crude.

**Natural Gas Availability:**
- Total estimated reserves ≈ **1.4 trillion cubic meters (TCM)**.
- Major fields:
  - **Krishna-Godavari Basin (KG-D6)** — Dhirubhai-1, MA-1 (Reliance)
  - **Mumbai High** — associated gas
  - **Gujarat** — Gandhar, Hazira, South Tapti
  - **Assam** — gas fields co-located with oil
  - **Rajasthan** — Coal Bed Methane (CBM) blocks
  - **Tripura, Tamil Nadu (Cauvery)**
- LNG import infrastructure: **Dahej, Hazira, Dabhol, Kochi (Puthuvypeen)** terminals.
- Pipelines: **Hazira-Vijaipur-Jagdishpur (HVJ)** is the longest gas pipeline.

**Distribution of Reserves in India (Indicative):**

$$\text{Coal: Domestic Self-Sufficiency} \approx 90\%$$

$$\text{Petroleum: Domestic Self-Sufficiency} \approx 15\%$$

$$\text{Natural Gas: Domestic Self-Sufficiency} \approx 50\%$$

**Part (b) — Limitations of Conventional Energy Resources**

**I. Environmental Limitations:**

1. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions:** Combustion of fossil fuels releases CO₂, the principal driver of anthropogenic climate change. CO₂ emission factors:
   - Coal: ~950 g/kWh
   - Oil: ~720 g/kWh
   - Natural Gas: ~490 g/kWh

2. **Local Air Pollution:**
   - **Particulate Matter (PM 2.5, PM 10)** — causes asthma, bronchitis, cardiovascular disease.
   - **Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂)** — causes acid rain, damages buildings, lung tissue.
   - **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)** — cause smog, ground-level ozone, acid rain.
   - **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — carcinogenic.

3. **Water Pollution:** Oil spills, acid mine drainage, thermal pollution from cooling water.

4. **Land Degradation:** Open-cast mining destroys forests, topsoil. Ash disposal from thermal plants.

5. **Solid Waste:** Fly ash (~180 million tonnes/year in India), bottom ash.

6. **Radioactive Waste (Nuclear):** Spent fuel remains hazardous for **10,000–100,000 years**. No permanent disposal site in India yet.

**II. Geopolitical Limitations:**

1. **Import Dependence:** India's oil import bill in FY 2022–23 exceeded **$160 billion** — a massive drain on foreign exchange.
2. **Price Volatility:** Crude oil prices fluctuated from $20/barrel (April 2020) to $130/barrel (March 2022) — destabilizes fiscal planning.
3. **Supply Disruptions:** Wars, sanctions, OPEC decisions, pipeline politics (Russia-Ukraine, Middle East tensions).
4. **Strategic Vulnerability:** Energy routes (e.g., Strait of Hormuz, Malacca) are choke points.

**III. Economic Limitations:**

1. **High Capital Cost:** Coal/Nuclear plants require ₹5,000–₹15,000 crore investments.
2. **Subsidy Burden:** Government subsidies on diesel, LPG, and kerosene strain the exchequer.
3. **External Costs:** The health and environmental costs of fossil fuels (estimated by IMF at $5+ trillion globally) are externalized.
4. **Resource Depletion:** As easily accessible reserves are exhausted, extraction costs rise (deeper mines, offshore drilling, oil sands).

**IV. Safety and Social Limitations:**

1. **Mining accidents** (e.g., Meghalaya's Ksan mine collapse, 2018, killing 15 miners).
2. **Nuclear accidents** (Chernobyl, Fukushima).
3. **Dam failures** (e.g., Machhu Dam disaster 1979, Morvi, Gujarat, ~2000 deaths).
4. **Displacement:** Sardar Sarovar displaced ~40,000 families; Tehri Dam ~1.5 lakh people.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **DO NOT** list limitations without numbers — always cite an emission factor (e.g., ~950 g CO₂/kWh for coal) or an import percentage (e.g., >85% for oil). Examiners give **partial marks** for generic answers.
> - **DO NOT** confuse "geothermal" with "geopolitical" — many students write "geothermal limitations" by mistake, which is a major error.
> - **ALWAYS** mention the **R/P ratio** for at least one resource. This is a free 1-mark differentiator.
> - **DO NOT** omit **India-specific data** — generic answers without Indian context typically lose 2–3 marks out of 14.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **formation, types, and global distribution of coal**. Discuss the **limitations of coal as a primary energy source**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe India's **three-stage nuclear power program** in detail. Highlight the **key limitations of nuclear energy**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Coal: Formation, Types, Distribution & Limitations**

**Formation of Coal:**

Coal is a **sedimentary rock** formed from the remains of plant matter that accumulated in swamps and bogs during the **Carboniferous period (≈ 360–290 million years ago)**. The process is called **coalification** and proceeds through four stages:

**Stage 1 — Peat Formation:**
- Dead plants accumulate in waterlogged, oxygen-deficient environments.
- Bacterial action partially decomposes the organic matter.
- Result: **Peat** (~50% carbon, high moisture content).

**Stage 2 — Lignite (Brown Coal):**
- Burial under sediments → heat & pressure expel water.
- Result: **Lignite** (~65–70% carbon, low energy density ≈ 10–15 MJ/kg).

**Stage 3 — Bituminous Coal:**
- Continued burial → higher temperature and pressure.
- Result: **Bituminous coal** (~75–90% carbon, ~25–35 MJ/kg) — most common in India.

**Stage 4 — Anthracite:**
- Metamorphism under extreme heat/pressure.
- Result: **Anthracite** (>90% carbon, ~30–35 MJ/kg) — highest energy density.

**Carbon Content vs. Energy Density:**

| Rank | Carbon Content | Energy Density |
|---|---|---|
| Peat | ~50% | ~10 MJ/kg |
| Lignite | ~60–70% | ~10–15 MJ/kg |
| Bituminous | ~75–90% | ~25–35 MJ/kg |
| Anthracite | ~90–98% | ~30–35 MJ/kg |

**Global Distribution of Coal:**

- **World proven reserves:** ≈ 1,070 billion tonnes (BT).
- **Top coal-producing countries (in order):**
  1. **China** — ~50% of world production
  2. **India** — ~10%
  3. **Indonesia**
  4. **USA**
  5. **Australia**

- **Top reserve holders (geographic distribution):**
  - **USA** — ~22% of world reserves (Powder River Basin, Appalachia)
  - **Russia** — ~15% (Kuzbass, Kansk-Achinsk)
  - **Australia** — ~14% (Bowen Basin, Sydney Basin)
  - **China** — ~13% (Shanxi, Inner Mongolia)
  - **India** — ~10% (Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh)

**Limitations of Coal:**

1. **High CO₂ Emissions** — Coal has the **highest carbon-to-hydrogen ratio** among fossil fuels, producing the most CO₂ per unit of energy. This is its single biggest drawback in the climate-change context.

2. **Air Pollution** — Coal combustion releases **PM, SO₂, NOₓ, mercury, and other heavy metals**. India's coal-based power plants are responsible for a significant fraction of urban PM 2.5.

3. **Ash Generation** — Indian coal has high ash content (30–40% vs. 10–15% for imported coal). India generates **~180 million tonnes of fly ash annually**, much of which is dumped in ash ponds.

4. **Water Consumption** — Thermal power plants are water-intensive (≈ 2–3 m³ per MWh for cooling), straining local water resources.

5. **Mining Hazards:**
   - Open-cast mining destroys forest cover and biodiversity.
   - Underground mining risks: methane explosions, roof collapses, silicosis, black lung disease.

6. **Methane Liberation** — Coal mining releases **CH₄ (methane)** which has a GWP of **28–36× CO₂ over 100 years** and **84–86× over 20 years**.

7. **Finite Resource** — R/P ratio ~130 years at current rates, but Hubbert's Peak theory suggests production will peak in this century.

8. **Transportation Cost** — Coal is bulky and energy-dense only at high ranks; transportation cost over long distances is significant.

9. **Sulfur and Mercury** — Both cause severe environmental and health damage.

10. **Land Subsidence** — Underground mining often causes surface subsidence, damaging buildings and infrastructure.

**Part (b) — India's Three-Stage Nuclear Program**

The **three-stage nuclear power program** is the cornerstone of India's long-term energy strategy, conceptualized by **Dr. Homi Jehangir Bhabha** in the 1950s. Its purpose is to **utilize India's vast thorium reserves** (≈ 25% of global thorium) by leveraging the small domestic uranium reserves through a cascading fuel cycle.

**STAGE I — Pressurized Heavy Water Reactors (PHWRs)**
- **Fuel:** Natural Uranium (U-238 + 0.7% U-235)
- **Moderator:** Heavy Water (D₂O)
- **Coolant:** Heavy Water (D₂O)
- **Process:** U-235 undergoes fission, releasing energy. The U-238 absorbs neutrons and is transmuted to **Pu-239 (Plutonium-239)** — a fissile material.
- **Byproduct:** **Plutonium-239** is separated from spent fuel in reprocessing plants.
- **Examples in India:** Tarapur, Rawatbhata, Kalpakkam, Kakrapar, Narora, Kaiga, Kudankulam.
- **Status:** Commercially operational.

**STAGE II — Fast Breeder Reactors (FBRs)**
- **Fuel:** Plutonium-239 (from Stage I) + U-238 blanket
- **Moderator:** None (hence "fast")
- **Coolant:** Liquid Sodium
- **Process:** The Pu-239 core fissions rapidly. Neutrons are captured by the surrounding U-238 "blanket," breeding **more Pu-239** than is consumed — hence "breeder."
- **Key feature:** The reactor breeds more fissile material than it consumes, dramatically extending fuel supply.
- **Examples in India:**
  - **PFBR (Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor)** at Kalpakkam (500 MW) — India's flagship.
  - FBTR (Fast Breeder Test Reactor) at Kalpakkam — already operational.
- **Byproduct:** **U-233** (by transmutation of Th-232 in advanced breeder concepts).

**STAGE III — Thorium-Based Reactors**
- **Fuel:** Thorium-232 (Th-232), which is fertile (not fissile by itself)
- **Process:** Th-232 absorbs a neutron and is transmuted to **U-233 (Uranium-233)**, which is fissile and can sustain a chain reaction.
- **Key advantage:** India's thorium reserves are **vast** (monazite sands in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Odisha, Andhra Pradesh) — enough for centuries of energy.
- **Examples:**
  - **AHWR (Advanced Heavy Water Reactor)** at Tarapur — uses thorium.
  - Research reactors at IGCAR Kalpakkam.
- **Status:** Under development; commercial deployment targeted for the 2030s–2040s.

**Cascade Flow Summary:**

$$\text{Stage I: U-235} \longrightarrow \text{Pu-239 (byproduct)}$$

$$\text{Stage II: Pu-239} \longrightarrow \text{Pu-239 (bred)} + \text{U-233 (byproduct)}$$

$$\text{Stage III: Th-232} \longrightarrow \text{U-233 (fuel)}$$

**Key Limitations of Nuclear Energy:**

1. **Radioactive Waste Management:** Spent fuel contains long-lived radionuclides (e.g., Plutonium-239, half-life ≈ 24,100 years; Cesium-137, ~30 years; Strontium-90, ~29 years). Geological repositories are difficult to site due to public opposition.

2. **Risk of Major Accidents:**
   - **Chernobyl, 1986** — 31 immediate deaths, long-term cancer deaths estimated in the thousands.
   - **Fukushima, 2011** — triggered by tsunami, meltdown of three reactors, ~100,000 displaced.
   - **Three Mile Island, 1979** — partial meltdown, no deaths but major public fear.

3. **High Capital Cost:** A 1000 MW nuclear plant costs ₹15,000–₹20,000 crore and takes **10–15 years** to construct.

4. **Long Construction Time:** Compared to natural gas (3–5 years) or solar (1 year), nuclear is very slow to deploy.

5. **Proliferation Risk:** Spent fuel contains weapons-grade plutonium — diversion risk is a major geopolitical concern.

6. **Public Acceptance:** Post-Fukushima, nuclear expansion faces strong opposition in many countries.

7. **Uranium Scarcity:** While not immediate, the global uranium resource is finite. The three-stage program mitigates this with thorium, but commercial thorium reactors are still decades away.

8. **Decommissioning Cost:** Decommissioning a reactor at end-of-life costs ~$500 million to $1 billion.

9. **Limited Domestic Uranium Reserves:** India's uranium reserves are modest (≈ 80,000 tonnes); hence the strategic importance of thorium.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **DO NOT** skip the names of the reactors (PFBR, AHWR, FBTR). Naming specific projects shows depth of understanding and earns full marks.
> - **DO NOT** say thorium is fissile — it is **fertile**. Students lose 1 mark for confusing fertile with fissile.
> - **ALWAYS** mention **Homi Bhabha** as the architect of the three-stage program.
> - **DO NOT** omit the **Chernobyl and Fukushima** accident dates and approximate casualty figures — examiners expect quantified limitations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Conventional Energy Resources**

**Definitions to Know Cold:**
- ⭐ Conventional (Non-Renewable) Energy Resources
- ⭐ Proved, Probable, Possible Reserves (1P, 2P, 3P)
- ⭐ Reserves-to-Production (R/P) Ratio
- ⭐ Hubbert's Peak Theory
- ⭐ Energy Density
- ⭐ CO₂ Emission Factor
- ⭐ Global Warming Potential (GWP)
- ⭐ Calorific Value (CV)
- ⭐ Plant Load Factor (PLF)

**Numerical Skills to Master:**
- ⭐ R/P ratio computation
- ⭐ Energy content: $E = m \times \text{CV}$
- ⭐ Annual energy of a plant: $E = \text{MW} \times 8760 \times \text{PLF}$
- ⭐ Unit conversions: **1 Toe = 41.868 GJ**, **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**
- ⭐ E = mc² for nuclear fission mass defect

**Key Indian Data to Memorize:**
- ⭐ Coal self-sufficiency ≈ 90% (Jharkhand, Odisha, Chhattisgarh, WB, MP)
- ⭐ Oil self-sufficiency ≈ 15% (Mumbai High, Assam, Gujarat, Rajasthan, KG Basin)
- ⭐ Gas self-sufficiency ≈ 50% (KG-D6, Mumbai High, Assam, Gujarat, Rajasthan)
- ⭐ Three-stage nuclear program by **Homi Bhabha**
- ⭐ Thorium in **Kerala's monazite sands** (Chavara, MK)
- ⭐ Nuclear reactors: Kudankulam, Kakrapar, Tarapur, Kalpakkam

**Hierarchy of CO₂ Emissions (Memorize the Order):**
- ⭐ **Coal (~950 g/kWh) > Oil (~720 g/kWh) > Natural Gas (~490 g/kWh) > Nuclear (~12 g/kWh) > Hydro (~24 g/kWh) > Renewables (negligible)**

**Limitations to List (Per Resource):**
- ⭐ **Coal:** CO₂, PM, SO₂, NOₓ, ash, mining hazards
- ⭐ **Oil:** Import dependence, spills, geopolitics, finite
- ⭐ **Gas:** Methane leakage, transport risk, still finite
- ⭐ **Nuclear:** Radioactive waste, accidents, cost, proliferation
- ⭐ **Hydro:** Displacement, ecology, sedimentation, methane from reservoirs

**Commonly Tested Pairings:**
- ⭐ R/P ratio and Hubbert's Peak → Coal & Oil
- ⭐ GWP of methane → Natural gas
- ⭐ Three-stage nuclear program → Thorium
- ⭐ Import bill and forex drain → Crude oil
- ⭐ Fly ash and pollution → Coal
- ⭐ Homi Bhabha → India's nuclear roadmap
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Conventional Energy Resources: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-Conventional Energy Resources: Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – Non-Conventional Energy Resources: Classification

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Conventional Energy Resources (NCER)** are energy sources that are **continuously replenished by natural processes** at a rate equal to or faster than the rate at which they are consumed. They are also termed **Renewable Energy Sources (RES)** and represent the alternatives to conventional fossil fuels (coal, petroleum, natural gas) and nuclear fission.

A more rigorous academic definition, as adopted by the KTU 2024 scheme for engineering undergraduates, is:

> **Non-conventional energy resources** are forms of energy derived from **inexhaustible natural phenomena**—such as solar radiation, planetary motion, geothermal gradients, and biological cycles—that can be harnessed to produce useful power (electricity, mechanical work, or thermal energy) with **minimal long-term depletion of the planetary resource base**.

The term "non-conventional" specifically contrasts with the term "conventional," which historically refers to:
- **Fossil Fuels** (coal, oil, natural gas)
- **Large-scale hydroelectric** (in some Indian academic contexts)
- **Nuclear fission** (in the broader classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The course PEMUT864 explicitly groups small hydro, solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal, wave, OTEC, hydrogen, and MHD under the umbrella of non-conventional energy. The classification is a foundational topic that maps the entire semester.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your household finances. Suppose you have **two bank accounts**:

- **Account A – The Conventional Energy Account:** It contains a **fixed deposit of ₹50 lakhs** (representing Earth's fossil fuel reserves formed over millions of years). Every time you spend money (burn coal, gasoline, gas), the balance drops. Once the balance hits zero, the account is **permanently closed**—there is no way to refill it on a human timescale.

- **Account B – The Non-Conventional Energy Account:** This account is linked to a **continuously flowing river of income** (sunlight, wind, tides, heat from Earth's core). Even if you withdraw ₹1 lakh per day, **tomorrow the river refills it**. The account is virtually **inexhaustible** on a human timescale.

> **The fossil fuel era is like spending from a finite inheritance; the renewable era is like living off a sustainable salary.**

A second, more technical analogy: think of the **Earth as a spacecraft** in interstellar space. The only true external energy input it receives is the **solar flux of approximately 1.367 kW/m²** (the *solar constant*, **G_sc = 1367 W/m²**). All renewable energy sources—except geothermal and nuclear—are ultimately **transformed solar energy** (wind = differential solar heating, biomass = photosynthesis, hydro = solar-driven evaporation–precipitation cycle). This is the profound unifying concept behind the classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** When asked "why is renewable energy important?", the structural answer is: (1) it replenishes naturally, (2) it has near-zero marginal pollution, (3) it is decentralized, and (4) it decouples energy security from geopolitics.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following constants and units will appear repeatedly in this course:

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **G_sc** | Solar constant (extraterrestrial radiation) | **1367 W/m²** |
| **AM** | Air mass (optical path length through atmosphere) | AM 0 to AM 1.5 |
| **ρ_air** | Air density (at sea level, 15 °C) | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| **g** | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| **E_sun** | Energy output of the sun | **3.8 × 10²⁶ W** |
| **η** | Efficiency of conversion | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **TOE** | Tonne of Oil Equivalent | **41.868 GJ** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Classification is Necessary

The energy landscape has shifted dramatically since the oil crises of the 1970s. Engineers must understand **which resource is suited to which geographical and economic context**. For example:
- A **high-insolation desert region** (e.g., Rajasthan) → favours **solar PV and CSP**.
- A **coastal region with high tidal range** (e.g., Gulf of Khambhat, Gulf of Kutch) → favours **tidal barrages**.
- A **volcanic region** (e.g., Iceland, Puga Valley in Ladakh) → favours **geothermal**.
- An **agricultural region with crop residue** (e.g., Punjab) → favours **biomass gasification**.

A proper classification system allows engineers to **match resource to technology to load profile**.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Non-Conventional Energy Resources

### 2.2.1 Primary Classification (Based on Origin)

The most widely accepted academic classification groups non-conventional sources into **three super-categories**:

**A. Direct Solar Energy Sources** – Energy harnessed directly from solar photons:
1. **Solar Thermal** (low-temperature, medium-temperature, high-temperature)
2. **Solar Photovoltaic (PV)** (crystalline silicon, thin-film, emerging perovskites)
3. **Solar Concentrating Power** (parabolic trough, solar tower, solar dish)

**B. Indirect Solar Energy Sources** – Solar energy stored or transformed through natural cycles:
1. **Wind Energy** (atmospheric circulation from differential solar heating)
2. **Biomass Energy** (photosynthetic storage of solar energy)
3. **Hydropower (Small, Micro, Mini)** (solar-driven hydrological cycle)
4. **Wave Energy** (wind-driven ocean surface oscillation)
5. **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** (latitudinal solar heating gradient)
6. **Tidal Energy** (gravitational interplay; partially solar via lunar mechanics)

**C. Non-Solar Renewable Sources** – Energy not derived from current solar input:
1. **Geothermal Energy** (primordial heat of Earth + radioactive decay)
2. **Hydrogen Energy** (carrier, not a primary source)
3. **Magneto-Hydrodynamic (MHD) Power**
4. **Fuel Cells** (electrochemical conversion devices)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen and Fuel Cells** are *secondary* sources/technologies — they are *carriers or converters* — but KTU 2024 lists them under non-conventional energy for completeness because they enable decarbonization.

### 2.2.2 Secondary Classification (Based on Form of Energy)

| Form | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Wind turbines, tidal, wave |
| **Thermal** | Solar thermal collectors, geothermal, OTEC |
| **Radiant (Photon)** | Solar PV, CSP |
| **Chemical** | Biomass, hydrogen, fuel cells, biogas |
| **Electrical (Direct)** | MHD generators |

### 2.2.3 Tertiary Classification (Based on Availability & Dispatchability)

| Category | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Variable / Intermittent** | Diurnal/seasonal fluctuation | Solar PV, Wind |
| **Dispatchable Renewable** | Can be scheduled on demand | Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal |
| **Storage-coupled** | Paired with battery or thermal storage | Solar+TES, Wind+PHS |

---

## 2.3 Characteristics of Non-Conventional Energy Resources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Favourite:** This is a recurring 3-mark question. Memorize the following characteristics.

1. **Renewable / Inexhaustible** – replenished continuously by natural cycles.
2. **Non-polluting / Clean** – negligible SO₂, NOₓ, particulate, or CO₂ emissions (life-cycle CO₂ is small but not zero).
3. **Decentralized** – generation can occur at point of consumption (distributed generation, rooftop solar, micro-wind).
4. **Low Operating Cost** – after installation, the "fuel" is free (sunlight, wind, tides).
5. **High Capital Cost (Upfront)** – initial CAPEX is significant; declining rapidly with technology maturity.
6. **Low Energy Density** – requires large collection areas (e.g., a 1 GW solar farm ≈ 25–50 km²).
7. **Intermittency** – availability depends on weather, time of day, season (key challenge).
8. **Environmentally Benign** – no acid rain, no thermal pollution (in most cases), minimal ecological disruption.
9. **Modular & Scalable** – can be built from kW (rooftop) to GW (utility) scale.
10. **Reduces Geopolitical Dependence** – domestic resource, no fuel imports required.

---

## 2.4 Comparison: Conventional vs. Non-Conventional Energy

| Parameter | Conventional (Fossil/Nuclear) | Non-Conventional (Renewable) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Finite reserves (coal, oil, gas, U-235) | Inexhaustible (sun, wind, tides, heat) |
| **CO₂ Emissions** | High (≈ 820 g/kWh for coal) | Near zero (life-cycle 10–50 g/kWh) |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate to high | High (declining) |
| **Operating Cost** | High (fuel + maintenance) | Low (no fuel) |
| **Plant Size** | Large central plants | Modular, distributed |
| **Efficiency** | 33–45 % thermal cycle | 15–25 % PV, 35–45 % wind, etc. |
| **Land Footprint** | Small per MW (centralized) | Large per MW (utility solar) |
| **Water Use** | High (cooling) | Low (mostly dry) |
| **Lead Time** | 5–10 years | 6 months – 2 years |
| **Availability** | Dispatchable | Variable (some dispatchable) |
| **Examples** | Thermal, nuclear, large hydro | Solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The classification of non-conventional energy resources is the **first step in any renewable energy feasibility study**. A practicing energy engineer uses this classification to:

1. **Conduct Resource Assessment** – e.g., measuring solar insolation (kWh/m²/day) or wind speed (m/s) at a site.
2. **Select Conversion Technology** – matching the resource to the appropriate turbine, panel, or collector.
3. **Estimate Capacity Factor** – actual annual energy output ÷ theoretical maximum.
4. **Perform Economic Analysis** – LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) calculations.
5. **Design Hybrid Systems** – e.g., solar + wind + battery for 24×7 reliability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Vision:** India has committed to **500 GW of non-fossil fuel capacity by 2030** (Panchamrit targets, COP26). The classification topic sets the conceptual foundation for understanding India's energy transition.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Foundational & Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> Although this topic is primarily conceptual, the following foundational formulas are universally required in subsequent modules and are part of the Module 1 / Module 2 common toolkit. They will appear in numericals throughout the semester.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Symbol Legend | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{on} = G_{sc}\!\left(1 + 0.033\cos\!\dfrac{360\,n}{365}\right)$ | Extraterrestrial radiation on the n-th day | W/m² | Solar |
| 2 | $AM = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$ | Air mass; θ_z = zenith angle | dimensionless | Solar PV |
| 3 | $I_t = I_b R_b + I_d\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I\rho_g\!\left(\dfrac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | Total radiation on tilted surface | W/m² | Solar Thermal/PV |
| 4 | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | Wind power (Betz limit: C_p,max = 16/27) | W | Wind |
| 5 | $P_{tidal} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ (turbine analogy) or $P = \rho g A h v$ | Tidal power (barrage) | W | Tidal |
| 6 | $E_{biomass} = m \times HHV$ | Combustion energy from biomass | J or kWh | Biomass |
| 7 | $E_{geo} = \dot{m}\,c_p\,(T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | Geothermal heat extraction rate | W | Geothermal |
| 8 | $\eta_{OTEC} = \dfrac{T_{warm} - T_{cold}}{T_{warm}}$ (Carnot) | Theoretical efficiency (OTEC) | dimensionless | OTEC |
| 9 | $E_{wave} \approx 0.5 H^2 T$ per metre crest | Wave power (H = height, T = period) | kW/m | Wave |
| 10 | $V = \dfrac{nRT}{P}$ (ideal gas, PV=nRT) | State equation for hydrogen storage | m³, Pa, K | Hydrogen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** For the classification topic itself, no numerical problems are typically asked in KTU. However, **definitions, characteristics, comparison tables, and neat labelled diagrams** constitute the bulk of the marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The topic "Classification" is fundamentally a **qualitative / descriptive** topic. The KTU 2024 scheme nevertheless expects students to be conversant with **two foundational derivations** (extraterrestrial solar radiation and wind power) which are the mathematical backbone of the most commonly classified resources (solar and wind). These derivations are demonstrated below in full rigour, as they will reappear in every module of this course.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface (Solar)

The **solar constant** $G_{sc}$ is the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface **perpendicular to the sun's rays at the outer edge of Earth's atmosphere**. Its standard value is:

$$G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$$

However, the Earth orbits the Sun in a **slightly elliptical path** (eccentricity $e \approx 0.0167$). Hence, the instantaneous extraterrestrial normal irradiance $G_{on}$ varies with the day of the year $n$.

**Step 1 — Define the day angle.**

The day angle (in degrees) represents the angular position of the Earth in its orbit:

$$\Gamma = \frac{360 (n - 1)}{365}$$

where $n$ = day of the year (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31).

**Step 2 — Apply the inverse-square correction for orbital eccentricity.**

The Earth-Sun distance varies by a small fraction, modifying the flux. The empirical correction (Spencer, 1971, widely used in KTU-level engineering):

$$G_{on} = G_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360\,n}{365}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Convert to extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface.**

Because the Earth's surface is horizontal and the solar rays are inclined by the **solar declination** $\delta$, the horizontal component is obtained by multiplying by $\cos\theta_z$, where $\theta_z$ is the zenith angle:

$$G_o = G_{on} \cos\theta_z$$

**Step 4 — Express $\cos\theta_z$ in terms of latitude, declination, and hour angle.**

The classical solar geometry identity:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

where:
- $\phi$ = latitude of the location (positive in Northern Hemisphere)
- $\delta$ = solar declination (angle between sun and equatorial plane)
- $\omega$ = hour angle (15° per hour from solar noon, negative in morning, positive in afternoon)

**Step 5 — Compute declination from Cooper's equation (a standard engineering approximation).**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left(\frac{360(284 + n)}{365}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final combined expression:**

$$\boxed{\,G_o = G_{sc}\!\left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)\!\left[\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega\right]\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** This equation tells the engineer exactly **how much solar power** is available on a horizontal surface at any location, day, and hour. It is the foundation of every solar resource assessment and underpins the classification of solar as a **direct** non-conventional source.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Available in Wind (Betz Limit)

Wind is the indirect form of solar energy harnessed by the kinetic energy of moving air. The **mechanical power** captured by a wind turbine is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Consider a control volume of air passing through a turbine.**

Let a stream tube of air with cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) approach the turbine at velocity $v_1$ (m/s) and leave at velocity $v_2$ (m/s). The mass flow rate of air through the rotor is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$

where $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³) = **1.225 kg/m³** at standard sea-level conditions.

**Step 2 — The kinetic energy per unit mass of the wind stream is:**

$$\text{KE per unit mass} = \frac{1}{2} v^2$$

**Step 3 — Total power in the undisturbed wind stream is therefore:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^2$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3\,}$$

**Step 4 — The Betz Limit (theoretical maximum extraction).**

Albert Betz (1919) proved that no turbine can extract more than **59.3 %** of the kinetic energy in the wind (the **Betz coefficient**, $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$). The actual power extracted is:

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$$

where $C_p$ is the **power coefficient**, bounded by $0 \le C_p \le 0.593$.

**Step 5 — Relate swept area to rotor diameter.**

For a horizontal-axis turbine with rotor diameter $D$ (m), the swept area is $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. Hence:

$$P_{turbine} = C_p \cdot \frac{\pi}{8} \rho D^2 v^3$$

> **Interpretation:** Power scales as the **cube of wind speed**, which is why even small changes in site wind speed dramatically affect economics. This is why wind energy, classified as an *indirect* solar resource, is so sensitive to micro-siting.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Resource

To consolidate the classification, the following **decision matrix** is a high-yield KTU instrument:

| Resource | Form | Energy Density | Maturity (TRL) | Best Geography | Storage Need |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Solar PV | Radiant | High (1 kW/m² peak) | 9 (Commercial) | Tropics, deserts | Battery / TES |
| Solar Thermal | Thermal | High | 8–9 | High DNI regions | Thermal storage |
| Wind | Kinetic | Moderate | 9 | Coastal, ridges, plains | Battery / PHS |
| Biomass | Chemical | High (HHV basis) | 9 | Agricultural belts | Solid / liquid fuel stockpile |
| Geothermal | Thermal | Very high | 9 | Tectonic boundaries | Binary cycles |
| Tidal | Kinetic | High | 7–8 | Narrow bays, estuaries | Tidal lagoon |
| Wave | Kinetic | Moderate | 6–7 | Open coasts | Hydraulic reservoir |
| OTEC | Thermal | Low | 5–6 | Tropical oceans | None (continuous) |
| Hydrogen | Chemical | Very high (HHV basis) | 7–8 | Universal (carrier) | Tank / underground cavern |
| MHD | Electrical (direct) | N/A | 4–5 | Combustion of seed gas | None (direct) |
| Fuel Cells | Electrochemical | N/A | 8–9 | Distributed power | Hydrogen tank |

(TRL = Technology Readiness Level, NASA/EU scale 1–9.)

> **Pedagogical Note for Students:** The classification is not merely a list — it is a **decision tree** for solving the world's energy problem across diverse geographies.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Illustrative Numerical (Resource Assessment)

> **Note:** This example is provided to bridge classification to subsequent modules. It will **not** appear in the Module 1 question paper but is essential for the KTU lab viva and module-end synthesis.

**Problem:** Estimate the extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at Thiruvananthapuram ($\phi = 8.5°$ N) on the summer solstice ($n = 172$, $\delta \approx 23.45°$) at solar noon ($\omega = 0$).

**Solution:**

Apply the boxed equation from §3.1:

$$G_{on} = 1367 \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 \times 172}{365}\right)$$

Compute the cosine term:

$$\frac{360 \times 172}{365} = 169.6° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos(169.6°) = -0.985$$

$$G_{on} = 1367 (1 - 0.0325) \approx 1322 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Compute the zenith angle term (at solar noon, $\cos\omega = 1$):

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(8.5°)\sin(23.45°) + \cos(8.5°)\cos(23.45°)(1)$$

$$= (0.148)(0.398) + (0.989)(0.917)$$

$$= 0.0589 + 0.9070 = 0.9659$$

$$\theta_z \approx 15.0°$$

Final horizontal extraterrestrial radiation:

$$G_o = 1322 \times 0.9659 \approx 1277 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** Even at solar noon at the latitude of Kerala, the sun is still 15° off zenith — confirming that **all of India, while tropical, has a meaningful seasonal and daily variation** in solar resource. This is why a proper classification of solar as a **direct non-conventional resource** (with predictable diurnal cycle) is the foundation of the entire course.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Two complementary diagrams are provided: (a) a **Classification Tree** showing the hierarchy of non-conventional energy resources as per KTU 2024, and (b) a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** illustrating the energy conversion pathway from primary resource to end-use electricity.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Classification of Non-Conventional Energy Resources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1["RENEWABLE NON CONVENTIONAL ENERGY SOURCES"]
    root1 --> catA["CATEGORY A DIRECT SOLAR"]
    root1 --> catB["CATEGORY B INDIRECT SOLAR"]
    root1 --> catC["CATEGORY C NON SOLAR RENEWABLE"]

    catA --> sol1["Solar Photovoltaic PV"]
    catA --> sol2["Solar Thermal Collectors"]
    catA --> sol3["Concentrated Solar Power CSP"]

    catB --> ind1["Wind Energy"]
    catB --> ind2["Biomass and Biofuels"]
    catB --> ind3["Small and Micro Hydro"]
    catB --> ind4["Wave Energy"]
    catB --> ind5["Tidal Energy"]
    catB --> ind6["Ocean Thermal OTEC"]

    catC --> nons1["Geothermal Energy"]
    catC --> nons2["Hydrogen Energy"]
    catC --> nons3["MHD Power"]
    catC --> nons4["Fuel Cells"]

    sol1 --> sol1a["Crystalline Silicon"]
    sol1 --> sol1b["Thin Film CdTe CIGS"]
    sol1 --> sol1c["Perovskite Tandem"]

    ind1 --> ind1a["Onshore Wind"]
    ind1 --> ind1b["Offshore Wind"]
    ind2 --> ind2a["Solid Biomass"]
    ind2 --> ind2b["Biogas"]
    ind2 --> ind2c["Liquid Biofuels"]

    ind4 --> ind4a["Oscillating Water Column"]
    ind4 --> ind4b["Point Absorber"]
    ind5 --> ind5a["Tidal Barrage"]
    ind5 --> ind5b["Tidal Stream"]
    ind6 --> ind6a["Open Cycle OTEC"]
    ind6 --> ind6b["Closed Cycle OTEC"]

    nons1 --> nons1a["Hydrothermal Dry Steam"]
    nons1 --> nons1b["Binary Cycle"]
    nons1 --> nons1c["Enhanced Geothermal"]
    nons3 --> nons3a["Open Cycle MHD"]
    nons3 --> nons3b["Closed Cycle MHD"]
    nons4 --> nons4a["PEMFC"]
    nons4 --> nons4b["SOFC"]
    nons4 --> nons4c["AFC"]
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **three top-level categories** of non-conventional energy. Each category is decomposed into specific technologies. The diagram mirrors the **academic classification** presented in §2.2 and is the expected answer format for a 14-mark question that asks for "a detailed classification with examples."

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture: Resource-to-Grid Conversion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    resA["PRIMARY RESOURCE SUN WIND BIOMASS TIDES HEAT"]
    resA --> conv1["CONVERSION DEVICE PV MODULE TURBINE DIGESTER TURBINE HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    conv1 --> cond1["POWER CONDITIONING INVERTER RECTIFIER CHARGE CONTROLLER"]
    cond1 --> store1["STORAGE BATTERY HYDROGEN PUMPED HYDRO THERMAL STORAGE"]
    store1 --> grid1["END USE GRID LOAD OFF GRID MICROGRID"]
    cond1 --> grid1
    conv1 --> grid1
    resA --> env1["ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT LOW EMISSIONS LAND USE"]
    conv1 --> env1
    grid1 --> eco1["ECONOMIC LCOE PAYBACK JOB CREATION"]
```

> **How to read this diagram:** This is the **block-level functional architecture** required by the system prompt. It maps the **complete engineering flow** from raw resource through conversion, conditioning, storage, and delivery to end-use, with cross-cutting concerns (environment, economics). This diagram is the **standard format** examiners expect in 14-mark descriptive questions on "Explain the classification with block diagrams."

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Comparative Energy Flow (Conventional vs. Non-Conventional)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    conv0["CONVENTIONAL PATH"]
    conv0 --> c1["Finite Reserve Coal Oil Gas Uranium"]
    c1 --> c2["Extraction Mining Drilling"]
    c2 --> c3["Combustion or Fission High Temperature Heat"]
    c3 --> c4["Mechanical to Electrical via Turbine Generator"]
    c4 --> c5["CO2 SO2 NOx Particulates Thermal Pollution"]
    c4 --> c6["Grid Distribution"]

    nconv0["NON CONVENTIONAL PATH"]
    nconv0 --> n1["Continuous Resource Sun Wind Tides Geothermal"]
    n1 --> n2["Direct Capture PV Wind Turbine OTEC Geothermal Well"]
    n2 --> n3["Power Electronics Inverter Conditioning"]
    n3 --> n4["Near Zero Emissions Operational Phase"]
    n3 --> n5["Storage Optional Battery Hydrogen Pumped Hydro"]
    n5 --> n6["Grid or Off Grid Microgrid"]
    n3 --> n6
```

> **Pedagogical Use:** This side-by-side functional comparison is the single most important schematic for the KTU viva. It demonstrates the student's ability to compare engineering pathways.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Direct, recall-based questions testing definitions, characteristics, or short comparisons. Answer length: 3–4 sentences + a diagram or table.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMUT864]

**Q1.** Define non-conventional energy resources. List any **four characteristics** that distinguish them from conventional energy sources.

**Model Answer:**

Non-conventional energy resources, also called renewable energy sources, are forms of energy derived from **inexhaustible natural phenomena** that are continuously replenished by natural processes at a rate equal to or faster than the rate of consumption. Examples include solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal, wave, OTEC, hydrogen, and MHD.

Four distinguishing characteristics:
1. **Renewable / Inexhaustible** – replenished continuously (e.g., sunlight arrives every day).
2. **Non-polluting** – negligible emissions of CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ during operation.
3. **Low operating cost** – "fuel" is free; only conversion hardware needs maintenance.
4. **Decentralized and modular** – can be deployed from kW to GW scale at the point of consumption.

> **Examiner's Note:** A clean **definition (1 mark) + 4 characteristics (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks)** = 3 marks. Use bullet points for clarity.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]

**Q2.** Distinguish between **direct solar** and **indirect solar** energy resources with two examples each.

**Model Answer:**

**Direct solar energy resources** are those in which the sun's radiation is harnessed **without any intermediate natural conversion**. The energy is captured and converted as photons arrive.

*Examples:* (1) Solar photovoltaic (PV) panels, (2) Solar thermal collectors / Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants.

**Indirect solar energy resources** are those in which solar energy has been **stored, transformed, or transported** by natural processes (atmosphere, hydrosphere, biosphere) before human harnessing.

*Examples:* (1) Wind energy (atmospheric motion from differential solar heating), (2) Biomass energy (chemical energy stored by photosynthesis).

> **Examiner's Note:** A **tabular comparison (2 marks) + 1 mark for examples** = 3 marks. Avoid writing paragraphs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Module-end questions, with **internal choice** between two questions (A or B). Each question carries 14 marks and is typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Full-length, structured, diagram-supported answers are expected.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q.A.** (a) Classify the non-conventional energy resources in detail with a neat block diagram. Give **two examples** under each category. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A.** (b) Compare conventional and non-conventional energy sources on **any seven** technical parameters. State the role of renewable energy in India's energy security. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A.(a) — Classification of Non-Conventional Energy Resources

**Introduction (1 mark):** Non-conventional energy resources are renewable, inexhaustible energy sources that are replenished by natural processes. They are classified into **three main categories** based on the **origin of the energy** (see Diagram 1 in §4.1).

**Category A – Direct Solar Energy (2 marks):**
Energy harnessed directly from incoming solar photons.
- (i) **Solar Photovoltaic (PV):** Direct conversion of photons to electricity via semiconductor p-n junction. *Examples:* rooftop silicon PV, thin-film CdTe, perovskite tandem.
- (ii) **Solar Thermal:** Heating of a working fluid for low-temperature (water heating, drying), medium-temperature (process heat, desalination), or high-temperature (concentrated solar power, CSP) applications. *Examples:* flat-plate collectors, parabolic troughs, solar towers.
- (iii) **Solar Architecture / Passive Solar:** Building-integrated design that uses sunlight for daylighting and thermal regulation.

**Category B – Indirect Solar Energy (2 marks):**
Solar energy stored or transformed through planetary cycles.
- (i) **Wind Energy:** Kinetic energy of moving air. *Examples:* horizontal-axis wind turbines (HAWT, 3-blade, upwind), vertical-axis Darrieus turbines.
- (ii) **Biomass Energy:** Chemical energy of organic matter. *Examples:* bagasse cogeneration, biogas from anaerobic digestion, biodiesel from Jatropha.
- (iii) **Hydropower (small/micro/mini):** Potential energy of water. *Examples:* run-of-river schemes (< 25 MW), pico-hydro (< 5 kW).
- (iv) **Wave Energy:** Surface oscillation of oceans. *Examples:* oscillating water column (OWC), point absorber buoys.
- (v) **Tidal Energy:** Gravitational/centrifugal energy of relative Earth-Moon-Sun motion. *Examples:* tidal barrage (La Rance, France), tidal stream turbines.
- (vi) **OTEC:** Temperature gradient between warm surface and cold deep ocean. *Examples:* closed-cycle OTEC using ammonia, open-cycle OTEC producing fresh water as a by-product.

**Category C – Non-Solar Renewable Energy (1 mark):**
Energy not derived from the current solar flux.
- (i) **Geothermal:** Heat from Earth's interior. *Examples:* dry steam plants (Larderello, Italy), binary cycle plants (using iso-pentane).
- (ii) **Hydrogen Energy:** Secondary carrier, often produced by electrolysis of water using renewable electricity. *Examples:* green hydrogen via PEM electrolysis, hydrogen fuel cell vehicles.
- (iii) **MHD (Magneto-Hydrodynamic):** Direct conversion of thermal energy to electricity using ionized gas (plasma) in a magnetic field. *Examples:* open-cycle and closed-cycle MHD generators.
