- (iv) **Fuel Cells:** Electrochemical conversion of hydrogen (or other fuels) directly to electricity. *Examples:* PEMFC for vehicles, SOFC for stationary power.

**Neat Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid classification tree in §4.1 (must be hand-drawn on the answer sheet). A hierarchical flowchart with three top-level branches and sub-branches is the standard.

> **Examiner's Note:** A clear **3-category classification + at least 2 examples per category (4 marks) + introduction (1 mark) + conclusion (1 mark) + diagram (1 mark) = 7 marks**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A.(b) — Conventional vs. Non-Conventional Energy + India's Energy Security

**Comparison Table (5 marks):** Refer to the comparison table in §2.4 of these notes. **Any seven parameters** must be covered; the table format with clean rows is recommended.

| Parameter | Conventional | Non-Conventional |
|---|---|---|
| Resource base | Finite (coal, oil, gas, U) | Inexhaustible (sun, wind, tides) |
| CO₂ emissions | High (820 g/kWh for coal) | Near zero (10–50 g/kWh life-cycle) |
| Capital cost | Moderate to high | High (declining year-on-year) |
| Operating cost | High (fuel + O&M) | Low (no fuel cost) |
| Plant size | Large, centralized | Modular, distributed |
| Energy density | High | Low to moderate |
| Land footprint | Small per MW | Large per MW (utility solar/wind) |
| Dispatchability | Dispatchable | Variable (intermittent) |

**Role of Renewable Energy in India's Energy Security (2 marks):**

1. **Reduces Import Dependence:** India imports ≈ 85 % of its crude oil and ≈ 50 % of its natural gas. Renewables are domestic, eliminating forex outflow and geopolitical vulnerability.
2. **Panchamrit Targets (COP26, Glasgow 2021):** India has committed to:
   - 500 GW of non-fossil fuel installed capacity by 2030.
   - 50 % of energy from renewable sources by 2030.
   - Net-zero emissions by 2070.
3. **Decentralized Electrification:** Off-grid solar pumps, solar home systems, and microgrids bring electricity to the **~50,000 un-electrified villages** of India's remote regions.
4. **Employment Generation:** The renewable sector creates **3× more jobs per MW** than the conventional thermal sector (TERI estimates).
5. **Climate Commitments:** Aligns with Paris Agreement NDC targets.

> **Examiner's Note:** A **comparison table (5 marks) + 5-point discussion on India's energy security (2 marks) = 7 marks**. The student's own words on energy security must be supported by data.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**Q.B.** (a) Explain the **primary, secondary, and tertiary classifications** of non-conventional energy resources. Discuss the **intermittency challenge** and the methods to overcome it. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B.** (b) With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a renewable energy conversion system** from resource to grid, and discuss the role of **storage in dispatchable renewable power**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B.(a) — Multi-level Classification + Intermittency

**Primary Classification (2 marks):** Based on the **origin of energy**, into three super-categories:
- **Direct Solar** (photons captured directly)
- **Indirect Solar** (transformed through natural cycles)
- **Non-Solar Renewable** (geothermal, hydrogen, MHD, fuel cells)

**Secondary Classification (2 marks):** Based on **form of energy**:
- Mechanical (wind, tidal, wave)
- Thermal (solar thermal, geothermal, OTEC)
- Radiant (solar PV, CSP)
- Chemical (biomass, hydrogen, fuel cells)
- Direct Electrical (MHD)

**Tertiary Classification (1 mark):** Based on **availability and dispatchability**:
- Variable / Intermittent (solar PV, wind)
- Dispatchable Renewable (biomass, geothermal, tidal)
- Storage-coupled (solar+TES, wind+PHS)

**Intermittency Challenge (1 mark):**

Intermittency is the **non-availability of a renewable source at a given time** due to:
- Diurnal cycle (solar PV produces 0 W at night)
- Weather variability (cloud cover, calm days reduce PV/wind output)
- Seasonal variation (monsoon wind vs. summer wind patterns)

This creates a **mismatch between supply and demand**, threatening grid stability and requiring **spinning reserves** in conventional power plants.

**Methods to Overcome Intermittency (1 mark):**

1. **Energy Storage Systems:**
   - **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)** – Lithium-ion, flow batteries (vanadium redox).
   - **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** – reversible turbine-pump (e.g., Kadamparai, Tamil Nadu).
   - **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** – molten salt in CSP, PCM in solar water heaters.
   - **Hydrogen Storage** – electrolyze water when surplus, fuel cell when deficit.
2. **Forecasting & AI-based dispatch.**
3. **Hybrid Systems** – e.g., solar + wind + biomass in a single microgrid.
4. **Demand Response** – shifting industrial load to match solar peak.
5. **Grid Interconnection** – Geographic smoothing of intermittency.

> **Examiner's Note:** A **3-tier classification (5 marks) + intermittency discussion (1 mark) + solutions (1 mark) = 7 marks**. Use a diagram of multi-level classification.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B.(b) — Block Diagram & Role of Storage

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in §4.2 of these notes. The four essential stages are:

1. **Primary Resource** (sun, wind, biomass, tide, geothermal heat)
2. **Conversion Device** (PV module, wind turbine, digester, OTEC heat exchanger)
3. **Power Conditioning** (inverter, rectifier, MPPT charge controller)
4. **Storage** (battery, PHS, hydrogen tank, thermal store)
5. **End Use** (grid, off-grid load, microgrid)

Each block must be drawn as a labelled rectangle with arrows showing energy/material flow.

**Role of Storage in Dispatchable Renewable Power (3 marks):**

Storage transforms an **intermittent renewable resource** into a **dispatchable one**, on par with thermal/nuclear plants:

| Storage Type | Round-trip η | Discharge Duration | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Li-ion BESS | 90–95 % | Hours | Solar + storage microgrids |
| Pumped Hydro | 75–85 % | Days | Wind + PHS at grid scale |
| Hydrogen (P2G2P) | 35–45 % | Seasonal | Long-duration storage |
| Molten Salt (TES) | 90 % | Hours | CSP plants |
| Flywheel | 85–90 % | Minutes | Frequency regulation |

Storage decouples the **time of generation** from the **time of consumption**, enabling firm renewable capacity contracts. It also provides **ancillary services** — frequency regulation, voltage support, black-start capability.

> **Examiner's Note:** A **clean block diagram (4 marks) + storage table/discussion (3 marks) = 7 marks**. The diagram must show ALL four blocks with arrows.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in the Classification topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Direct" and "Indirect" Solar.** *Pitfall:* Writing "tidal energy is direct solar." *Correction:* Tidal is indirect solar (gravitational cycle) — but it is also partially driven by lunar mechanics. State it as "indirect solar" in KTU exams.
> 2. **Listing hydrogen as a primary energy source.** *Pitfall:* Calling hydrogen a "source." *Correction:* It is a **carrier** (must be produced). Examiners dock 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the non-solar category.** *Pitfall:* Only listing solar and indirect solar. *Correction:* Geothermal, MHD, hydrogen, fuel cells are **non-solar renewables** (or non-solar in general).
> 4. **No diagram in a 7-mark question.** *Pitfall:* Pure prose. *Correction:* KTU examiners expect a **neat block diagram** (hand-drawn) for full marks. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up solar thermal and solar PV.** *Pitfall:* Using the terms interchangeably. *Correction:* Thermal = heat; PV = electricity.
> 6. **Not citing the Betz limit** when wind is discussed. *Pitfall:* Generic "wind has kinetic energy." *Correction:* Cite $C_{p,\max} = 16/27$ — this signals depth and earns brownie marks.
> 7. **Using decimal approximations of constants.** *Pitfall:* Writing G_sc = 1400. *Correction:* Use **1367 W/m²** (or 1361 W/m² per newer ASTM E490).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- **Definition:** Non-conventional energy = renewable, inexhaustible, replenished by natural cycles. **Examples:** solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal, wave, OTEC, hydrogen, MHD, fuel cells.
- **3 Top Categories:** **Direct Solar / Indirect Solar / Non-Solar Renewable**.
- **Solar Constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ (extraterrestrial, perpendicular to sun, top-of-atmosphere).
- **Extraterrestrial Daily Radiation:** $G_{on} = G_{sc}(1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365))$.
- **Air Mass:** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ (AM 1.5 = standard test condition for PV modules).
- **Declination:** $\delta = 23.45\sin(360(284+n)/365)$ degrees.
- **Zenith Angle:** $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- **Wind Power:** $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ — **cubic dependence on wind speed** is critical.
- **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ — no turbine can exceed 59.3 % efficiency.
- **Standard Air Density:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level, 15 °C.
- **Standard Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ — used in tidal and hydro calculations.
- **Hydrogen:** **Carrier, not source** — produced from water, biomass, or fossil fuels.
- **Fuel Cells:** Electrochemical converters — PEMFC (low T), SOFC (high T), AFC (alkaline).
- **MHD:** Direct thermal-to-electrical conversion using plasma in a magnetic field.
- **Characteristics:** Renewable, clean, decentralized, low O&M cost, high CAPEX, low energy density, intermittent (solar/wind), modular.
- **India's Target:** 500 GW non-fossil by 2030, Net-Zero by 2070.
- **Common Diagram:** Hierarchical classification tree with 3 branches and 2–3 examples each.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing storage (battery) with source (sun). Storage is the **bridge**, not the **fuel**.

> **Final Exam Advice for KTU 2024:** Memorize the **3-tier classification tree**, the **10 characteristics of renewable energy**, the **comparison table**, the **solar constant and Betz limit**, and the **block diagram of resource-to-grid conversion**. These four items cover **> 80 %** of marks allocated to this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Merits and Demerits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relative Merits and Demerits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Renewable Energy Sources (RES)**, the *relative merits and demerits* refers to the systematic comparative evaluation of renewable energy technologies against conventional (fossil-fuel based) energy systems, examined across four canonical engineering axes:

1. **Technical Axis** – efficiency, reliability, capacity factor, grid integration.
2. **Economic Axis** – capital cost, Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE), payback period.
3. **Environmental Axis** – greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, land use, water footprint.
4. **Social Axis** – energy access, job creation, energy security, public acceptance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** *"Relative merits and demerits is the qualitative and quantitative benchmarking of renewable energy sources (solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal) against non-renewable sources (coal, oil, natural gas, nuclear) using standardized technical, economic, environmental, and social indicators."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Two Commute Options:**
> Imagine choosing between a **bicycle** and a **petrol scooter** for your daily college commute.
> - The **bicycle** (Renewable Energy): Zero fuel cost, no exhaust, silent, healthy — but you arrive sweaty, it's slow uphill, and it's tough in heavy rain (low energy density, intermittency).
> - The **scooter** (Conventional Energy): Fast, reliable, goes anywhere — but needs costly petrol, pollutes, and breaks down the environment (emissions, depletion, climate change).
>
> *The "merits" are the bicycle's positive traits; the "demerits" are its limitations. The "relative" part means we always compare with the scooter, not judge the bicycle in isolation.*

### 1.3 Standard Energy Metrics Used in Comparison

> [!TIP]
> **Key Benchmark Constants & Units You Must Memorize for KTU 2024:**
> - **Energy Density** — measured in **MJ/kg** or **kWh/L**.
> - **Capacity Factor (CF)** — dimensionless ratio (typical wind: 0.25–0.45, solar PV: 0.15–0.25, coal: 0.70–0.85).
> - **LCOE** — measured in **₹/kWh** or **USD/MWh**.
> - **Carbon Dioxide Equivalent (CO₂e)** — measured in **gCO₂e/kWh**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Analysis of the Four Comparison Axes

#### Axis A — Technical Merits of Renewable Energy Sources
- **Inexhaustibility** — Sun delivers ~$1.74 \times 10^{17}$ W to Earth's upper atmosphere; usable terrestrial insolation is essentially unbounded on human timescales.
- **Modularity & Scalability** — From a 5 W solar lantern to a 500 MW solar park; same physics applies.
- **Low Mechanical Complexity** — Solar PV has no moving parts; wind turbines have only the rotor assembly.
- **Distributed Generation (DG) Capability** — Reduces transmission/distribution (T&D) losses (typically 15–20% in Indian grid).
- **Quick Installation** — Rooftop solar can be commissioned in 1–3 days per kW.

#### Axis B — Technical Demerits of Renewable Energy Sources
- **Intermittency & Variability** — Solar irradiance is zero at night; wind speed follows Weibull distribution, not on-demand.
- **Low Energy Density** — Solar: ~$1 \text{ kW/m}^2$ peak; wind: ~$0.5 \text{ kW/m}^2$ — fossil fuels deliver $\sim 10^4 \times$ more per unit volume.
- **Grid Integration Challenges** — Voltage/frequency fluctuations require storage or spinning reserve.
- **Site Specificity** — Hydro needs rivers, geothermal needs tectonics, wind needs corridors.
- **Low Capacity Factor** — Typical solar CF in Kerala ≈ **0.16–0.18**.

#### Axis C — Economic & Environmental Merits
- **Zero Marginal Fuel Cost** — Sunlight and wind are free at point of use.
- **Negative Carbon Lifecycle** — Solar PV lifecycle emissions: ~**40–50 gCO₂e/kWh** vs Coal: ~**820–1,000 gCO₂e/kWh**.
- **No Fuel Supply Risk** — Immunity to geopolitical price shocks (e.g., 2022 crude oil surge).
- **Job Multiplier** — IRENA reports renewables create ~**2–3× more jobs/MW** than fossil plants.

#### Axis D — Economic & Environmental Demerits
- **High Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** — Solar PV: ₹35,000–50,000 per kW installed (2024 benchmark).
- **Storage Cost** — Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) at ₹6,000–10,000 per kWh add 30–50% to project cost.
- **Land Footprint** — Utility-scale solar needs ~**2–2.5 acres/MW**; wind needs ~**0.25 acres/MW** but with exclusion zones.
- **Resource Curse Risk** — Mining of Li, Co, Ni for batteries creates new environmental liabilities.
- **End-of-Life Disposal** — Solar panel recycling is currently <10% globally; wind blade disposal is a known issue.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** that KTU 2024 examiners consistently test under "merits and demerits" topics. Memorize the unit column verbatim.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Merit/Demerit Indicator |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Capacity Factor (CF)** | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | dimensionless or % | Lower CF = greater intermittency (demerit) |
| 2 | **Levelized Cost of Energy** | $LCOE = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{E_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | ₹/kWh | Lower LCOE = economic merit |
| 3 | **Energy Payback Time (EPBT)** | $EPBT = \dfrac{E_{invested}}{E_{annual\,generated}}$ | years | Lower EPBT = manufacturing merit |
| 4 | **Carbon Payback Time (CPBT)** | $CPBT = \dfrac{EM_{manufacturing}}{EM_{avoided\,annual}}$ | years | Lower CPBT = environmental merit |
| 5 | **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** | $PLF = \dfrac{\text{Units generated}}{\text{Installed Capacity} \times \text{Hours}}$ | % | Used for dispatchable plants |
| 6 | **CUF (Capacity Utilization Factor) — Indian MNRE Term** | $CUF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{DC} \times 365 \times 24}$ | % | Standard for solar tendering |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The merits/demerits framework directly drives:
- **Project Tendering Decisions** — NTPC RE, SECI, and KSEB use LCOE + CUF to shortlist bidders.
- **Policy Design** — India's **PM-KUSUM, PM-Surya Ghar Yojana** subsidies are calibrated to overcome the CAPEX demerit.
- **Hybrid System Design** — Solar + Wind + BESS hybrids use *complementary intermittency* to convert individual demerits into a system-level merit.
- **Carbon Credit Valuation** — Lower gCO₂e/kWh → higher carbon credit price → improved project IRR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Capacity Factor as a Merit/Demerit Quantifier

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU 2024:** The Capacity Factor is the single most-tested quantitative metric in Module 1. Examiners love asking "Why is solar CF lower than coal? Justify with a numerical example."

**Given:**
- A 10 kW rooftop solar PV system installed in Kochi, Kerala.
- Annual energy generation recorded: **$E_{actual} = 14{,}600$ kWh/year**.

**Find:** Capacity Factor (CF) and interpret as a merit/demerit indicator.

**Step 1 — Identify the rated energy possible in one year**

A 10 kW system, if operating at full rated power for the entire year, would generate:

$$E_{rated} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values**

$$E_{rated} = 10 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 87{,}600 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Write the Capacity Factor formula**

$$CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{rated}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute measured generation**

$$CF = \frac{14{,}600 \text{ kWh}}{87{,}600 \text{ kWh}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the ratio**

$$CF = 0.1667 \approx 16.67\%$$

**Step 6 — Comparative Interpretation (the "Relative" part)**

| Source | Typical CF | Inference |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Coal (India) | 70–85% | Merit: dispatchable, baseload |
| Solar PV (Kerala) | 16–18% | Demerit: intermittent |
| Wind (Tamil Nadu) | 25–40% | Moderate merit |
| Hydro (large) | 35–55% | Merit: flexible storage |
| Nuclear | 85–92% | High merit (non-RES baseline) |

**Step 6a — Engineering Conclusion**

A CF of **16.67%** means the system delivers only 1/6th of its nameplate capacity on a yearly average — this is the **quantitative evidence of the intermittency demerit**, and it directly drives the need for storage or grid backup.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — LCOE Comparison: Solar PV vs Coal

**Given:**
- Solar PV: CAPEX = ₹45,000/kW, OPEX = ₹1,000/kW/yr, lifetime = 25 yr, CF = 0.18, discount rate = 8%.
- Coal: CAPEX = ₹60,000/kW, OPEX = ₹4,000/kW/yr + fuel ₹3,500/kW/yr, lifetime = 30 yr, CF = 0.80, discount rate = 8%.

**Step 1 — Annual Energy Generation (Solar PV)**

$$E_{solar} = 1 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \times 0.18 = 1{,}576.8 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Annual Energy Generation (Coal)**

$$E_{coal} = 1 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \times 0.80 = 7{,}008 \text{ kWh/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)**

$$PVAF = \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r}$$

For Solar (n=25, r=0.08):

$$PVAF_{solar} = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-25}}{0.08} = \frac{1 - 0.1460}{0.08} = 10.675$$

For Coal (n=30, r=0.08):

$$PVAF_{coal} = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-30}}{0.08} = \frac{1 - 0.0994}{0.08} = 11.258$$

**Step 4 — PV of Total Costs (Solar)**

$$PVC_{solar} = 45{,}000 + 1{,}000 \times 10.675 = 45{,}000 + 10{,}675 = ₹55{,}675$$

**Step 5 — PV of Total Costs (Coal)**

$$PVC_{coal} = 60{,}000 + (4{,}000 + 3{,}500) \times 11.258 = 60{,}000 + 7{,}500 \times 11.258 = 60{,}000 + 84{,}435 = ₹1{,}44{,}435$$

**Step 6 — PV of Total Energy (Solar)**

$$PVE_{solar} = 1{,}576.8 \times 10.675 = 16{,}832 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 7 — PV of Total Energy (Coal)**

$$PVE_{coal} = 7{,}008 \times 11.258 = 78{,}896 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 8 — LCOE Computation**

$$LCOE_{solar} = \frac{55{,}675}{16{,}832} = ₹3.31 \text{ /kWh}$$

$$LCOE_{coal} = \frac{1{,}44{,}435}{78{,}896} = ₹1.83 \text{ /kWh}$$

**Step 9 — Economic Merit/Demerit Interpretation**

| Parameter | Solar PV | Coal | Verdict |
|-----------|---------|------|---------|
| LCOE | ₹3.31 | ₹1.83 | Coal wins on raw LCOE (current demerit of RES) |
| Fuel cost exposure | ₹0 forever | Rises 4–6%/yr | Solar wins long-term (merit of RES) |
| Carbon cost (₹/tCO₂ × emissions) | ₹0 | +₹1.2–1.8/kWh externalized | Solar wins when internalized |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** A perfect exam answer notes that *without carbon pricing, coal still has lower LCOE — which is precisely why the Indian government uses subsidies and RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation) policy to overcome this economic demerit of RES.*

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Major Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Ready 4×6 Comparison Matrix** (memorize the column headers exactly as they appear below):

| Criterion | Solar PV | Wind | Hydro (Large) | Biomass | Coal (Reference) |
|-----------|---------|------|---------------|---------|------------------|
| **Renewability** | Inexhaustible | Inexhaustible | Replenishable (water cycle) | Replenishable (cultivation) | Depletable |
| **Initial Cost (₹/kW)** | 35,000–50,000 | 60,000–80,000 | 50,000–1,00,000 | 40,000–60,000 | 60,000–90,000 |
| **Capacity Factor** | 0.15–0.25 | 0.25–0.45 | 0.35–0.55 | 0.65–0.80 | 0.70–0.85 |
| **LCOE (₹/kWh, 2024)** | 2.5–3.5 | 3.0–4.5 | 2.5–5.0 | 5.0–7.0 | 3.5–5.5 (incl. damage) |
| **CO₂e (g/kWh)** | 40–50 | 10–20 | 3–10 | 20–50 (variably negative) | 820–1,000 |
| **Storage Need** | High (6–12 hr) | Moderate | None (reservoir) | Low | None |
| **Land (acres/MW)** | 2.0–2.5 | 0.25 | Variable (flooded) | 5–10 (plantation) | 1.0–1.5 (mining) |
| **Grid Stability Merit** | Low | Low–Medium | Very High | Medium | Very High |
| **Key Demerit** | Intermittency | Variability | Displaces people | Air quality | Emissions + ash |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Multi-Criteria Decision Framework for RES Merits/Demerits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source Selection] --> B{Is it Renewable?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Evaluate RES Merits]
    B -- No --> D[Use as Baseline Reference]

    C --> E[Technical Axis]
    C --> F[Economic Axis]
    C --> G[Environmental Axis]
    C --> H[Social Axis]

    E --> E1[Capacity Factor]
    E --> E2[Energy Density]
    E --> E3[Grid Stability]

    F --> F1[CAPEX]
    F --> F2[LCOE]
    F --> F3[Payback Period]

    G --> G1[GHG Emissions]
    G --> G2[Land Footprint]
    G --> G3[Water Usage]

    H --> H1[Energy Access]
    H --> H2[Job Creation]
    H --> H3[Public Acceptance]

    E1 --> I[Composite Merit Score]
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    F1 --> I
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> J{Is Merit Score > Threshold?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Deploy RES]
    J -- No --> L[Hybridize or Defer]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — RES vs Conventional Source Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Conventional[Conventional Energy Source]
        A1[Coal/Oil/Gas Stock] --> A2[Combustion Plant]
        A2 --> A3[AC Grid]
        A2 --> A4[CO2 Emissions]
        A2 --> A5[Ash + Particulates]
    end

    subgraph Renewable[Renewable Energy Source]
        B1[Sun/Wind/Water] --> B2[Conversion Tech]
        B2 --> B3[Inverter + BESS]
        B3 --> B4[AC Grid]
        B3 --> B5[Near-Zero Emissions]
        B3 --> B6[Modular Scalable]
    end

    A3 -.-> C[End User]
    B4 -.-> C
    A4 -.-> D[Atmosphere]
    B5 -.-> D
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Radar-Style Visual — Relative Strength Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Merit: Zero Fuel Cost] --> Result[Net RES Profile]
    P2[Merit: Low Emissions] --> Result
    P3[Merit: Modularity] --> Result
    P4[Merit: Energy Security] --> Result

    N1[Demerit: Intermittency] --> Result
    N2[Demerit: High CAPEX] --> Result
    N3[Demerit: Low Energy Density] --> Result
    N4[Demerit: Storage Dependency] --> Result

    Result --> Outcome{Policy + Technology Trajectory}
    Outcome --> O1[2024: Subsidies needed]
    Outcome --> O2[2030: Grid parity achieved]
    Outcome --> O3[2040: Dominant global mix]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. List any three relative merits of renewable energy sources over conventional sources.**
*(Cognitive Level: Remember/Understand | CO Mapping: CO1)*

**Model Answer (3 key points × 1 mark each):**
1. **Environmental Merit:** Renewable sources emit 95–99% less CO₂e per kWh across their lifecycle compared to coal-based thermal power (Solar PV: ~50 gCO₂e/kWh vs Coal: ~900 gCO₂e/kWh).
2. **Economic Merit:** Zero marginal fuel cost; immune to fossil-fuel price volatility and forex risk.
3. **Social/Strategic Merit:** Enhances national energy security by reducing import dependence (India currently imports ~85% of its crude oil requirements).

---

**Q2. State any three relative demerits of renewable energy sources.**
*(Cognitive Level: Remember/Understand | CO Mapping: CO1)*

**Model Answer (3 key points × 1 mark each):**
1. **Technical Demerit — Intermittency:** Solar irradiance is unavailable at night; wind speed fluctuates — Capacity Factor of solar PV in Kerala is only ~16–18% versus ~75% for coal.
2. **Economic Demerit — High CAPEX:** Utility-scale solar PV requires ₹35,000–50,000 per kW upfront, whereas coal thermal plants are financed through long-term fuel-linked PPAs.
3. **Technical Demerit — Low Energy Density:** Solar flux at Earth's surface is ~1 kW/m²; fossil fuels deliver ~10,000× more energy density per unit volume, requiring large land footprints for equivalent output.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the relative merits of renewable energy sources over conventional energy sources under technical, economic, environmental, and social categories. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A 5 MW solar PV plant in Kerala has an annual energy generation of 7,884 MWh. Compute the Capacity Factor and comment on the technical merit/demerit it reveals. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| Category | Relative Merit of RES | Quantitative Justification |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| **Technical** | Modularity, scalability, distributed generation | Rooftop solar: 1 W to MWs using identical cell technology |
| **Economic** | Zero fuel cost, predictable LCOE | Solar LCOE has dropped from ₹17/kWh (2010) to ₹3/kWh (2024) |
| **Environmental** | Lifecycle GHG reduction | 40–50 gCO₂e/kWh (Solar) vs 820–1,000 gCO₂e/kWh (Coal) |
| **Social** | Energy access, employment, security | IRENA: 12.7 million global renewable jobs in 2022 |

*(Distribute 1.75 marks per category — examiner scoring grid)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data**

$$P_{rated} = 5 \text{ MW} = 5{,}000 \text{ kW}$$

$$E_{actual} = 7{,}884 \text{ MWh} = 7{,}884{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Compute rated annual energy**

$$E_{rated} = 5{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} = 43{,}800{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Capacity Factor formula**

$$CF = \frac{7{,}884{,}000}{43{,}800{,}000} = 0.18 = 18\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

An 18% CF confirms the **intermittency demerit** of solar PV — the system delivers only about **1/5th of its installed capacity** on a yearly average. This justifies the need for:
- Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), or
- Hybridization with wind/biomass, or
- Grid backup from dispatchable sources (e.g., KSEB hydel).

*(Allocate 1.75 marks for the formula, 1.75 marks for numerical substitution, 1.5 marks for correct evaluation, 2 marks for engineering interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Pattern]**
> *(a)* Compare renewable and non-renewable energy sources using a structured table covering at least six parameters, clearly highlighting the relative merits and demerits. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A rooftop solar PV system has the following data: CAPEX = ₹4,00,000, annual OPEX = ₹8,000, annual energy = 4,500 kWh, lifetime = 25 years, discount rate = 8%. Determine the LCOE and state whether the system is economically viable at a tariff of ₹4.00/kWh. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A clean six-parameter comparison table covering: Renewability, LCOE, CO₂e emissions, CAPEX, Land Requirement, and Capacity Factor — values as enumerated in Section 3.3 of these notes.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for table format, 6 marks = 1 mark per accurate row)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)**

$$PVAF = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-25}}{0.08} = 10.675$$

**Step 2 — Present Value of CAPEX**

$$PV_{capex} = ₹4{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Present Value of OPEX stream**

$$PV_{opex} = 8{,}000 \times 10.675 = ₹85{,}400$$

**Step 4 — Total PV of Costs**

$$PVC_{total} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 85{,}400 = ₹4{,}85{,}400$$

**Step 5 — Present Value of Energy stream**

$$PVE = 4{,}500 \times 10.675 = 48{,}037.5 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 6 — LCOE Calculation**

$$LCOE = \frac{4{,}85{,}400}{48{,}037.5} = ₹10.11 \text{ /kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Economic Viability Verdict**

Since **LCOE (₹10.11/kWh) > Tariff (₹4.00/kWh)**, the project is **NOT economically viable at a ₹4/kWh tariff without subsidy**. To make it viable, the system needs:
- MNRE CFA subsidy of ~₹78,000 (PM-Surya Ghar), or
- Net-metering benefit valued at retail tariff (~₹7–8/kWh), or
- A higher PPA tariff of ≥ ₹10/kWh.

*(Mark split: 3 marks for PVAF + PV computations, 2 marks for LCOE arithmetic, 2 marks for the correct verdict and justification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students write "renewable = good, non-renewable = bad" without numbers. **Always quantify** — write CF, LCOE, gCO₂e/kWh values.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Confusing *Capacity Factor (CF)* with *CUF*. CUF is the Indian MNRE tendering term; CF is the international standard. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you use the wrong one in the wrong context.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Forgetting the **PVAF formula derivation** in LCOE problems — many students simply divide CAPEX by lifetime energy. This is wrong; the **discounted cash flow (DCF)** method is mandatory.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Writing "solar/wind are non-polluting" — this is a *relative* claim, not absolute. Solar panel manufacturing, wind blade disposal, and rare-earth mining have environmental footprints. The correct answer is *"negligible lifecycle emissions compared to fossil fuels."*
> - **Pitfall 5:** Skipping units in formula substitution. KTU 2024 strictly deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit on long-answer questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist before every KTU 2024 exam:**

- ✅ **Merits of RES** = Inexhaustible, Zero fuel cost, Low emissions (~50 gCO₂e/kWh), Modular, Energy security, Job creation, Decentralized.
- ✅ **Demerits of RES** = Intermittency (solar CF 16–18% in Kerala), High CAPEX (₹35k–50k/kW), Low energy density (~1 kW/m²), Land footprint, Storage dependency, Recycling challenges.
- ✅ **Always quote Capacity Factor** when comparing dispatchability: Coal 75%, Hydro 45%, Wind 30%, Solar 18%.
- ✅ **LCOE benchmark values (2024):** Solar ₹2.5–3.5/kWh, Wind ₹3.0–4.5/kWh, Coal ₹3.5–5.5/kWh (with damage cost).
- ✅ **LCOE Formula** must use the **discounted cash flow (DCF)** method with **PVAF**, never a simple division.
- ✅ **Capacity Factor Formula:** $CF = E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \times 8760)$ — no shortcut, no exception.
- ✅ **Energy Payback Time (EPBT)** for solar PV ≈ **1.5–2.5 years**; system lifetime = 25 years → net energy gain = **~23 years of clean energy**.
- ✅ **Carbon Payback Time (CPBT)** for solar PV ≈ **1–3 years** depending on grid carbon intensity.
- ✅ **Merit/Demerit must be "relative"** — always pair a RES claim with a non-RES comparison (e.g., "RES emits 90% less CO₂e than coal").
- ✅ **Kerala-specific data:** Average solar insolation = **4.5–5.5 kWh/m²/day**; CUF of KSEB solar plants ≈ **17–19%**.
- ✅ **India policy levers to remember:** PM-KUSUM (agricultural solar), PM-Surya Ghar (rooftop), RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation), REC (Renewable Energy Certificate).
- ✅ **Hybrid systems** (Solar + Wind + BESS) raise the combined CF to **35–45%**, converting individual demerits into a system-level merit — a high-value KTU 2024 application question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Energy: Principle of Conversion of Solar Radiation into Heat Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Solar Energy: Principle of Conversion of Solar Radiation into Heat Energy # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Solar Radiation — The Foundation of Solar-Thermal Energy **Solar radiation** is the electromagnetic energy emitted by the Sun (predominantly as black-body radiation at ~5778 K) that travels through space and reaches the Earth's atmosphere and surface. When this radiant energy is **absorbed by a material and its temperature rises**, the conversion into **heat (thermal) energy** is said to occur. This is the most direct, oldest, and most widely deployed form of renewable energy utilization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** "Solar thermal conversion is the process by which the short-wave electromagnetic energy emitted by the sun is absorbed on a receiver surface and transformed into sensible heat (temperature rise) in a working fluid or solid mass, which can then be utilized for low-, medium-, or high-temperature applications such as water heating, space heating, desalination, cooking, or power generation." ### The Two-Step Conversion Chain 1. **Radiation → Thermal (Sensible Heat):** Photons strike a dark/selective absorber surface; their energy raises vibrational energy of molecules → temperature rises. 2. **Thermal → Useful Work:** Hot fluid is piped to an application (heater, turbine, dryer, still). --- ## 1.2 The Solar Constant and Extraterrestrial Irradiance The Earth-Sun distance varies through the year because of Earth's elliptical orbit. The radiation flux at a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays, just outside the Earth's atmosphere, at the mean Earth-Sun distance of **1 AU (1.496 × 10⁸ km)**, is called the **Solar Constant**. > [!NOTE] > **Solar Constant G_sc = 1367 W/m² ≈ 1.367 kW/m²** > (Current WMO/ASA standard value: **G_sc = 1361 W/m²**; KTU textbooks use **1367 W/m²**). The extraterrestrial normal irradiance on day *n* (Jan 1 = 1) is corrected for eccentricity by the **eccentricity correction factor E₀**: $$E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\!\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)$$ $$G_{on} = G_{sc}\,E_0 \quad [\mathrm{W/m^2}]$$ > **Typical range:** 1309 W/m² (July) to 1412 W/m² (January) over the year. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sun as a Giant Kettle Heater" 🍵 Imagine you are standing on a beach at noon. Sunlight is like a **giant invisible kettle of electromagnetic waves** continuously pouring energy on the Earth's surface — about **1 kW per square meter**, equivalent to a small electric room-heater for every square meter of land! When a black painted surface (or a copper tube painted matte black) is exposed, the photon energy is "trapped" as molecular motion — this is **heat**. If you then run water through that hot tube, the water absorbs the heat and comes out warm. That is essentially what a **flat-plate solar water heater** does. A **concentrating collector** is like a magnifying glass — it focuses a large area of sunlight onto a tiny spot, raising the temperature from "lukewarm" (~80 °C) to "red-hot" (~1500 °C), enabling power generation via turbines. --- ## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (KTU Vocabulary) | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Solar Constant | G_sc | **1367 W/m²** | | Extraterrestrial radiation (horizontal) | G_o | 0 → 1412 W/m² | | Beam (direct) radiation | G_b | 0 → 1000 W/m² | | Diffuse radiation | G_d | 0 → 400 W/m² | | Ground-reflected radiation | G_r | < 100 W/m² | | Total on horizontal surface | G_T | 0 → 1100 W/m² | | Albedo (ρ_g) | ρ | 0.2–0.7 (snow) | --- ## 1.5 The Path of Sunlight Through the Atmosphere When solar radiation enters the atmosphere, it undergoes three fundamental processes: 1. **Absorption** — by ozone (UV), water vapor, CO₂ (IR). 2. **Scattering** — by air molecules (Rayleigh) and dust (Mie) → produces **diffuse radiation**. 3. **Reflection** — by clouds and ground (albedo). The fraction that reaches the ground unmodified is the **beam (direct) radiation G_b**. The scattered fraction is the **diffuse radiation G_d**. Their sum is the **global (total) radiation on a horizontal surface G**: $$G = G_b \cos\theta_z + G_d$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Beam radiation** travels in a straight line from the Sun's disk. **Diffuse radiation** arrives from the whole sky dome. A clear sunny day = high beam; a cloudy day = mostly diffuse. --- ## 1.6 What is a "Clear Day" vs a "Cloudy Day" — The Clearness Index A dimensionless number that quantifies atmospheric transparency: $$k_T = \frac{G}{G_o}$$ | Sky Condition | k_T | |---|---| | Overcast | 0.0 – 0.3 | | Partly cloudy | 0.3 – 0.6 | | Clear | 0.6 – 0.8 | | Very clear (high altitude) | 0.8 – 1.0 | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Solar Geometry — The Mathematics of Where the Sun Is To calculate the solar radiation falling on a tilted surface, we must first determine the **position of the Sun in the sky** relative to a location on Earth. This requires three angles: ### (a) Declination Angle (δ) — Seasonal Position The angle between the line joining the Earth's centre to the Sun and the equatorial plane. Varies from **+23.45°** (summer solstice, June 21) to **−23.45°** (winter solstice, Dec 21). $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$ where **n** = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365). ### (b) Hour Angle (ω) — Daily Position Zero at solar noon, negative in the morning, positive in the afternoon. **+15° per hour.** $$\omega = 15^\circ\,(t_{solar} - 12) \quad [\text{degrees}]$$ ### (c) Zenith Angle (θ_z) — Angle from Vertical The angle between the Sun's rays and the local vertical (normal to horizontal). $$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\,\sin\delta + \cos\phi\,\cos\delta\,\cos\omega$$ where **φ** = latitude of the location (°N positive in Northern hemisphere). ### (d) Altitude Angle (α_s) and Azimuth Angle (γ_s) - **Altitude:** α_s = 90° − θ_z - **Azimuth:** $$\sin\gamma_s = \frac{\cos\delta\,\sin\omega}{\cos\alpha_s}$$ --- ## 2.2 Air Mass — Path Length Through the Atmosphere The longer the path of sunlight through the atmosphere, the more it is attenuated. **Air Mass (AM)** is the ratio of the actual path to the minimum (vertical) path: $$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$ - **AM 0:** Outside the atmosphere (space) - **AM 1:** Sun directly overhead (zenith) at sea level - **AM 1.5:** Sun at 48.19° from zenith — the **KTU/IEC standard for testing PV modules**. - **AM 2:** Sun at 60° from zenith. A more accurate formula for high zenith angles (pressure-corrected): $$AM = \frac{P}{101.325}\cdot\frac{1}{\cos\theta_z + 0.5057\,(96.08^\circ - \theta_z)^{-1.636}}$$ --- ## 2.3 Attenuation of Solar Radiation — The Beer–Lambert Law When beam radiation passes through an absorbing/scattering medium, its intensity decays exponentially with the optical path length: $$I_b = I_{on}\,e^{-k_a\,m}$$ where: - **I_on** = extraterrestrial beam intensity (W/m²) - **k_a** = atmospheric extinction/turbidity coefficient (typically 0.1–0.4) - **m** = air mass This is the foundation of the **Angstrom–Prescott correlation** for estimating monthly mean daily global radiation: $$\frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_o}} = a + b\,\left(\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}}\right)$$ where: - **H̄** = monthly mean daily global radiation on horizontal surface - **H̄_o** = extraterrestrial monthly mean daily radiation - **n̄** = actual sunshine hours - **N̄** = maximum possible sunshine hours (day length) - **a, b** = Angstrom coefficients (a ≈ 0.23, b ≈ 0.48 for tropical India) --- ## 2.4 Solar Radiation on a Tilted Surface A solar collector is rarely horizontal. On a tilted surface (tilt = β, azimuth = γ), the relevant angles are: ### Angle of Incidence (θ_i): $$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta\,\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\,\cos\omega\,\cos(\phi-\beta)$$ > For a surface **facing the equator at latitude φ**, optimum tilt β = φ and **θ_i = θ_z** at solar noon. ### Beam Radiation on Tilted Surface: $$G_{b,T} = G_b \cdot \frac{\cos\theta_i}{\cos\theta_z}$$ > When θ_i > 90°, the Sun is behind the plane; the surface receives no beam radiation. ### Diffuse Radiation on Tilted Surface (Isotropic Sky — Liu & Jordan Model): $$G_{d,T} = G_d \cdot \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$$ ### Ground-Reflected Radiation: $$G_r = \rho_g \cdot G \cdot \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$$ ### Total Radiation on Tilted Surface: $$\boxed{G_T = G_b\frac{\cos\theta_i}{\cos\theta_z} + G_d\frac{1+\cos\beta}{2} + \rho_g\,G\frac{1-\cos\beta}{2}}$$ --- ## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | E₀ = 1 + 0.033 cos(360n/365) | Eccentricity correction | – | | 2 | G_on = G_sc · E₀ | Extraterrestrial normal irradiance | W/m² | | 3 | δ = 23.45° sin[360(284+n)/365] | Declination | ° | | 4 | ω = 15°(t − 12) | Hour angle | ° | | 5 | cos θ_z = sin φ sin δ + cos φ cos δ cos ω | Zenith angle | – | | 6 | AM = 1/cos θ_z | Air mass | – | | 7 | I_b = I_on · e^(−k_a m) | Beer–Lambert attenuation | W/m² | | 8 | H̄/H̄_o = a + b (n̄/N̄) | Angstrom–Prescott | – | | 9 | cos θ_i = sin δ sin(φ−β) + cos δ cos ω cos(φ−β) | Incidence angle | – | | 10 | G_b,T = G_b cos θ_i / cos θ_z | Beam on tilt | W/m² | | 11 | G_d,T = G_d (1+cos β)/2 | Diffuse (isotropic) | W/m² | | 12 | G_r = ρ_g G (1−cos β)/2 | Ground-reflected | W/m² | | 13 | H̄_o = (24/π) G_sc E₀ (cos φ cos δ sin ω_s + ω_s sin φ sin δ) | Daily ET radiation | MJ/m²/day | | 14 | ω_s = cos⁻¹(−tan φ tan δ) | Sunrise hour angle | rad | | 15 | Q_u = A_c F_R (τα)[S − U_L(T_i − T_a)] | Useful heat gain | W | --- ## 2.6 Engineering Significance > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every numerical in Module 1 reduces to "calculate radiation on a tilted surface at time *t* on day *n* at latitude *φ*". Master the geometry — you master the module. In real industry, the same formulae are used by: - **Tata Power Solar** to size plants, - **India Meteorological Department (IMD)** for solar resource atlases, - **MNRE's** "Solar Radiation Resource Assessment (SRRA)" stations. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface H̄_o This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** — full marks are awarded for the step-by-step integral. ### Step 1 — Instantaneous Beam Irradiance on a Horizontal Surface Outside the Atmosphere The Sun delivers G_on = G_sc · E₀ on a surface **perpendicular to the beam**. A horizontal surface captures only the cosine component: $$G_{oh} = G_{on}\,\cos\theta_z$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Zenith-Angle Cosine Identity $$G_{oh} = G_{sc}\,E_0\,[\sin\phi\,\sin\delta + \cos\phi\,\cos\delta\,\cos\omega]$$ ### Step 3 — Integrate Over a Day (from Sunrise −ω_s to Sunset +ω_s) $$H_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_{oh}\,\frac{dt}{d\omega}\,d\omega$$ Since ω = 15°(t − 12) ⇒ dt = dω/15° per hour, and converting degrees to radians (15°/hr = π/12 rad/hr): $$\frac{dt}{d\omega} = \frac{12}{\pi} \quad [\text{hours per radian}]$$ ### Step 4 — Write the Integral and Split It $$H_o = G_{sc}\,E_0\,\frac{12}{\pi}\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s}\!\bigl[\sin\phi\,\sin\delta + \cos\phi\,\cos\delta\,\cos\omega\bigr]\,d\omega$$ The first term is constant in ω; integrate over a symmetric interval [−ω_s, +ω_s]: $$\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s}\!\sin\phi\,\sin\delta\,d\omega = 2\omega_s\,\sin\phi\,\sin\delta$$ The second term is an even function in cos(ω): $$\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s}\!\cos\phi\,\cos\delta\,\cos\omega\,d\omega = 2\cos\phi\,\cos\delta\,\sin\omega_s$$ ### Step 5 — Final Expression $$\boxed{H_o = \frac{24}{\pi}\,G_{sc}\,E_0\,\left[\cos\phi\,\cos\delta\,\sin\omega_s + \omega_s\,\sin\phi\,\sin\delta\right]}$$ Units: **Wh/m²/day**. Multiply by 3.6 × 10⁻³ to get **MJ/m²/day**. ### Step 6 — Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle At sunrise, α_s = 0 ⇒ cos θ_z = 0 ⇒ sin φ sin δ + cos φ cos δ cos ω_s = 0: $$\boxed{\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\,\tan\delta)}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Useful Heat Gain from a Flat-Plate Collector The **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation** is the cornerstone of solar-thermal design. ### Step 1 — Energy Balance on a Flat-Plate Collector Let: - A_c = collector area (m²) - S = absorbed solar flux per unit area = G_T (τα) (W/m²) - U_L = overall top + edge + bottom loss coefficient (W/m²·K) - T_pm = mean plate temperature (K) - T_a = ambient temperature (K) - m_dot = mass flow rate (kg/s) - c_p = specific heat of fluid (J/kg·K) - T_i, T_o = inlet & outlet fluid temperatures The plate absorbs (τα)G_T per m², loses U_L(T_pm − T_a) to surroundings, and transfers the rest to the fluid. $$A_c\,S = U_L\,A_c\,(T_{pm} - T_a) + \dot{m}\,c_p\,(T_o - T_i)$$ ### Step 2 — Define the Heat Removal Factor F_R Empirically, F_R accounts for the fact that the plate temperature is not uniform and the fluid temperature increases along the flow path. $$Q_u = \dot{m}\,c_p\,(T_o - T_i) = A_c\,F_R\,[S - U_L\,(T_i - T_a)]$$ ### Step 3 — Final Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation $$\boxed{Q_u = A_c\,F_R\,(\tau\alpha)\,\bigl[G_T - U_L\,(T_i - T_a)\bigr] \quad [\mathrm{W}]}$$ This is the **single most important equation** for KTU solar-thermal problems. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Eccentricity Factor E₀ The Earth-Sun distance r varies as: $$r = r_0\,(1 - e^2)/(1 + e\cos v)$$ where **e ≈ 0.01673** is the orbital eccentricity and **v** is the true anomaly. Flux ∝ 1/r². Expanding with day number *n* and using the day-of-perihelion (Jan 3) as reference gives: $$E_0 = \left(\frac{r_0}{r}\right)^2 = 1 + 0.033\,\cos\!\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right)$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Board Pattern) > **Question (KTU 14-mark type):** A flat-plate collector of area **2.5 m²** is installed at **Trivandrum (φ = 8.5° N)** at a tilt **β = 10°** facing south. At solar noon on **March 21 (n = 80)**, the measured beam radiation on a horizontal surface is **G_b = 720 W/m²**, diffuse is **G_d = 180 W/m²**, ambient is **30 °C**, and inlet water temperature is **35 °C**. Given (τα) = 0.78, U_L = 5.5 W/m²·K, F_R = 0.78, ρ_g = 0.2, and G_sc = 1367 W/m². Calculate: > 1. The declination angle δ. > 2. The incidence angle on the collector θ_i. > 3. Total radiation on the tilted surface G_T. > 4. The useful heat gain Q_u. ### Solution: **1) Declination angle:** $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284+80)}{365}\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin(359.0^\circ) \approx 0.04^\circ \approx 0^\circ$$ (Equinox — as expected on March 21.) **2) At solar noon, ω = 0:** $$\cos\theta_i = \sin(0^\circ)\sin(8.5^\circ-10^\circ) + \cos(0^\circ)\cos(0^\circ)\cos(8.5^\circ-10^\circ)$$ $$= 0 + 1 \cdot 1 \cdot \cos(-1.5^\circ) = 0.99966$$ $$\theta_i \approx 1.58^\circ \approx \theta_z$$ **3) Total on tilted surface:** - G = 720 cos θ_z + 180 ≈ 720·0.99966 + 180 = 899.76 W/m² - Beam on tilt: G_b,T = 720 × (cos θ_i / cos θ_z) = 720 × 1.0000 = 720 W/m² - Diffuse on tilt: G_d,T = 180 × (1+cos 10°)/2 = 180 × (1+0.9848)/2 = **178.63 W/m²** - Reflected: G_r = 0.2 × 899.76 × (1−cos 10°)/2 = 0.2 × 899.76 × 0.0076 = **1.37 W/m²** $$G_T = 720 + 178.63 + 1.37 = \mathbf{900.0\ W/m^2}$$ **4) Useful heat gain:** $$Q_u = 2.5 \times 0.78 \times 0.78 \times [900.0 - 5.5 \times (35 - 30)]$$ $$= 2.5 \times 0.6084 \times [900 - 27.5] = 1.521 \times 872.5 = \mathbf{1327\ W \approx 1.33\ kW}$$ > **Efficiency:** η = Q_u / (A_c G_T) = 1327 / (2.5 × 900) = **58.9 %** # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Solar-Thermal Conversion Chain — End-to-End Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[SUN
5778 K Black Body] --> B1[Extraterrestrial
Irradiance G_on] B1 --> C1[Atmospheric
Interaction] C1 --> D1[Absorption
by O3, H2O, CO2] C1 --> D2[Scattering
Rayleigh and Mie] C1 --> D3[Reflection
by Clouds] D2 --> E1[Diffuse Radiation
G_d] B1 -.unchanged.-> E2[Beam Radiation
G_b] E1 --> F1[Horizontal Surface
G = G_b cosθz + G_d] E2 --> F1 F1 --> G1[Tilted Collector
Surface A_c] G1 --> H1[Selective Absorber
Plate black] H1 --> I1[Heat Transfer
to Fluid] I1 --> J1[Useful Thermal
Energy Q_u] J1 --> K1[Application
Heating Power Drying] style A1 fill:#ffcc33,stroke:#333 style H1 fill:#663300,stroke:#333,color:#fff style J1 fill:#ff6600,stroke:#333,color:#fff style K1 fill:#009900,stroke:#333,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Flat-Plate Collector — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB SUN[Sunlight
Beam + Diffuse] --> GLA[Glazing
Glass Cover] GLA --> ABS[Absorber Plate
Black Selective Surface] ABS --> TUB[Riser Tubes
Carry Fluid] TUB --> INS[Insulation
Back and Side] TUB --> FLU[Working Fluid
Water or Glycol] FLU --> APP[End Use
Tank or Process] ARA[Ambient
T_a] -.heat loss UL.-> ABS ARA -.convection.-> GLA style SUN fill:#ffcc33 style GLA fill:#99ccff style ABS fill:#333333,color:#fff style TUB fill:#cc6600 style INS fill:#cccc99 style FLU fill:#3399ff,color:#fff style APP fill:#009900,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Solar Radiation Components — On a Tilted Surface ```mermaid flowchart TB SUN2[Sun] -- Beam G_b --> SURF[Tilted Collector
Surface β] SKY[Hemisphere Sky
Diffuse G_d] --> SURF GRO[Ground
Reflected] --> SURF SURF --> GT[Total Radiation G_T
on Tilted Surface] SUN2 -.transmits.-> SKY GRO -.reflects from.-> SUN2 style SUN2 fill:#ffcc33 style SKY fill:#ccddff style GRO fill:#996633,color:#fff style SURF fill:#ff9933 style GT fill:#cc0000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Energy Balance Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR S_in[Input S
absorbed] --> BAL[Energy Balance
at Steady State] U_loss[Loss U_L T_pm - T_a] --> BAL BAL --> Q_use[Useful Q_u
transferred to fluid] T_pm[Plate Temp T_pm] --> U_loss T_a[Ambient T_a] --> U_loss T_pm --> Q_use T_i[Inlet Fluid T_i] --> Q_use T_o[Outlet Fluid T_o] --> Q_use style S_in fill:#ffcc33 style U_loss fill:#ff3333,color:#fff style Q_use fill:#00cc00,color:#fff style BAL fill:#ffff66 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. Define Solar Constant. Mention its standard value and the reason for its variation with day of the year.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **Solar Constant** is the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface placed perpendicular to the Sun's rays, at the mean Earth-Sun distance of **1 AU (1.496 × 10⁸ km)**, **outside the Earth's atmosphere**. **Standard value:** **G_sc = 1367 W/m² (1.367 kW/m²).** **Variation:** Although the *solar constant* is, by definition, constant in value, the **extraterrestrial irradiance on day n** varies between ~1309 W/m² (aphelion, ~July 4) and ~1412 W/m² (perihelion, ~Jan 3) because the Earth orbits the Sun in an ellipse. The variation is captured by the **eccentricity correction factor E₀**: $$E_0 = 1 + 0.033\,\cos\!\left(\frac{360\,n}{365}\right), \quad G_{on} = G_{sc}\,E_0$$ > (3 marks: definition 1, value 1, variation/reason 1) --- ### **Q2. What is Air Mass? Write the expression for Air Mass and state its value at zenith and at 60° zenith angle.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Air Mass (AM)** is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the minimum (vertical) path length when the Sun is at the zenith. **Expression:** $$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$ where **θ_z** is the solar zenith angle. - At **zenith (θ_z = 0°):** **AM = 1** - At **θ_z = 60°:** **AM = 1/cos 60° = 1/0.5 = 2** (i.e., AM 2) > (3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, numerical values 1) --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface** H̄_o. Show all integration steps. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A solar flat-plate collector of area **3 m²** is tilted at **β = 15°** (facing south) at **New Delhi (φ = 28.6° N)**. At 10:00 AM (solar time) on **March 21 (n = 80)**, the following data are given: - Beam radiation on horizontal surface: G_b = 700 W/m² - Diffuse radiation: G_d = 170 W/m² - Ambient temperature: 28 °C - Inlet water temperature: 40 °C - (τα) = 0.76, U_L = 6.0 W/m²·K, F_R = 0.80 - ρ_g = 0.2, G_sc = 1367 W/m² Compute: (i) Declination δ, (ii) Hour angle ω, (iii) Total radiation on the tilted surface G_T, (iv) Useful heat gain Q_u, and (v) Collector efficiency η. **[7 marks]** --- #### **Model Solution (Q-A):** **(a) Derivation of Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation H̄_o:** *(Full marks allocated to step-by-step work)* - Step 1: Beam irradiance on a horizontal surface outside the atmosphere is G_oh = G_sc E₀ cos θ_z. - Step 2: Substitute cos θ_z = sin φ sin δ + cos φ cos δ cos ω. - Step 3: Write hourly-to-radian conversion: dt/dω = 12/π hours per radian. - Step 4: Form integral H_o = ∫ G_oh dt from −ω_s to +ω_s. - Step 5: Integrate the constant term → 2ω_s sin φ sin δ. - Step 6: Integrate the cos ω term → 2 cos φ cos δ sin ω_s. - Step 7: Final boxed result: $$H_o = \frac{24}{\pi}\,G_{sc}\,E_0\,\left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\,\sin\omega_s + \omega_s\,\sin\phi\,\sin\delta\right]$$ - Step 8: ω_s = cos⁻¹(−tan φ tan δ). **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Declination:** $$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360(284+80)}{365}\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin(359.0^\circ) \approx 0^\circ$$ **(ii) Hour angle (10:00 AM, solar time):** $$\omega = 15^\circ\,(10 - 12) = -30^\circ \quad \text{(morning)}$$ **(iii) Total radiation on tilted surface:** First compute θ_z at 10:00 AM, δ ≈ 0: $$\cos\theta_z = \sin(28.6^\circ)\sin(0) + \cos(28.6^\circ)\cos(0)\cos(-30^\circ)$$ $$= 0 + 0.8776 \times 0.8660 = 0.7600$$ $$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.7600) = 40.54^\circ$$ Compute cos θ_i (β = 15°, φ = 28.6°): $$\cos\theta_i = \sin(0)\sin(28.6-15) + \cos(0)\cos(-30)\cos(28.6-15)$$ $$= 0 + 1 \times 0.8660 \times \cos(13.6^\circ)$$ $$= 0.8660 \times 0.9718 = 0.8416$$ $$\theta_i = 32.6^\circ$$ Global horizontal G = 700·0.7600 + 170 = 532.0 + 170 = **702.0 W/m²** - Beam on tilt: G_b,T = 700 × (0.8416 / 0.7600) = 700 × 1.1074 = **775.2 W/m²** - Diffuse on tilt: G_d,T = 170 × (1 + cos 15°)/2 = 170 × 1.9659/2 = **167.1 W/m²** - Reflected: G_r = 0.2 × 702.0 × (1 − cos 15°)/2 = 0.2 × 702.0 × 0.0171 = **2.4 W/m²** $$G_T = 775.2 + 167.1 + 2.4 = \mathbf{944.7\ W/m^2}$$ **(iv) Useful heat gain (Hottel–Whillier–Bliss):** $$Q_u = 3.0 \times 0.80 \times 0.76 \times [944.7 - 6.0 \times (40 - 28)]$$ $$= 1.824 \times [944.7 - 72.0] = 1.824 \times 872.7 = \mathbf{1591.8\ W \approx 1.59\ kW}$$ **(v) Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{Q_u}{A_c\,G_T} = \frac{1591.8}{3.0 \times 944.7} = \frac{1591.8}{2834.1} = 0.5616 = \mathbf{56.2\%}$$ --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** What is a **flat-plate solar collector**? With a neat block schematic, explain the energy balance and derive the **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation** for useful heat gain. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Using the **Angstrom–Prescott correlation**, estimate the **monthly mean daily global radiation** on a horizontal surface at a location having latitude **φ = 26° N**, if the Angstrom coefficients are **a = 0.25, b = 0.55**, the actual sunshine hours are **n̄ = 8.0 hr/day**, and the maximum possible sunshine hours for that month are **N̄ = 11.5 hr/day**. The day of year is the **15th of the month**, and the **monthly mean extraterrestrial radiation H̄_o = 28.5 MJ/m²/day**. Comment on the sky condition. **[7 marks]** --- #### **Model Solution (Q-B):** **(a) Flat-Plate Collector & Hottel–Whillier–Bliss Equation:** A **flat-plate solar collector** is a device that absorbs both beam and diffuse solar radiation on a flat absorber surface, converts it into thermal energy, and transfers the heat to a working fluid (water/air) flowing through tubes attached to the absorber. **Components (block schematic):** - Glazing (transparent cover) – reduces convection and IR loss - Absorber plate – black/selective surface (τα ≈ 0.75–0.95) - Riser tubes – carry the working fluid - Insulation (back + side) – minimizes conductive loss - Frame / Casing **Energy balance (already shown in SECTION 3.2):** The plate absorbs (τα)·G_T, loses U_L(T_pm − T_a) to surroundings, and transfers the rest to the fluid. After introducing the **heat removal factor F_R**, the final equation is: $$\boxed{Q_u = A_c\,F_R\,(\tau\alpha)\,\bigl[G_T - U_L\,(T_i - T_a)\bigr]}$$ **(b) Angstrom–Prescott Calculation:** $$\frac{\bar{H}}{\bar{H_o}} = a + b\,\left(\frac{\bar{n}}{\bar{N}}\right) = 0.25 + 0.55 \times \frac{8.0}{11.5}$$ $$= 0.25 + 0.55 \times 0.6957 = 0.25 + 0.3826 = 0.6326$$ $$\bar{H} = 0.6326 \times 28.5 = \mathbf{18.03\ MJ/m^2/day}$$ **Clearness index k_T = 0.63** → Indicates a **clear-to-partly-cloudy** sky. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students lose marks on solar radiation problems:** > 1. **Mixing up cos θ_z and cos θ_i** — Beam-on-tilt uses **θ_i**, not θ_z. Converting beam from horizontal to tilt needs the ratio **cos θ_i / cos θ_z**, NOT just cos θ_i. > 2. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in the H̄_o integration — examiners deduct 1 mark. > 3. **Wrong sign in (φ − β)** when the surface faces south. Draw a diagram; sign errors are the #1 reason students get negative beam values. > 4. **Using the average G_T instead of instantaneous** in the Hottel–Whillier equation — the equation is **instantaneous**, not daily. > 5. **Confusing solar noon with local clock noon** — KTU problems almost always use **solar time**; converting IST → solar time is rarely required, but hour angle is from solar noon. > 6. **Forgetting (τα) and F_R** in efficiency calculation. Marks lost: 2 per mistake. > 7. **Not stating units** in final answers — even if numerically correct, marks are docked for "W" vs "W/m²" confusion. --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - ☑ **Solar Constant** G_sc = 1367 W/m²; extraterrestrial flux G_on = G_sc · E₀. - ☑ **Eccentricity factor** E₀ = 1 + 0.033 cos(360n/365). - ☑ **Declination** δ = 23.45° sin[360(284+n)/365] — 0° at equinoxes, ±23.45° at solstices. - ☑ **Hour angle** ω = 15°(t_solar − 12) — negative in morning, positive in afternoon. - ☑ **Zenith** cos θ_z = sin φ sin δ + cos φ cos δ cos ω. - ☑ **Air mass** AM = 1/cos θ_z; AM 1.5 = standard test condition. - ☑ **Beam on tilt** G_b,T = G_b · (cos θ_i / cos θ_z). - ☑ **Diffuse on tilt** (isotropic) = G_d · (1+cos β)/2. - ☑ **Ground-reflected** = ρ_g · G · (1−cos β)/2. - ☑ **Daily ET radiation** H̄_o formula — derived by integrating G_oh from −ω_s to +ω_s. - ☑ **Angstrom–Prescott** H̄/H̄_o = a + b (n̄/N̄) — to estimate from sunshine data. - ☑ **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss** Q_u = A_c F_R (τα) [G_T − U_L (T_i − T_a)] — for collector heat gain. - ☑ **Clearness index** k_T = G/G_o — 0 (overcast) → 1 (clear). - ☑ **Albedo** ρ_g = 0.2 (soil/grass), 0.7 (fresh snow). - ☑ **Conversion sequence:** Sun → Atmosphere (attenuation) → Surface (beam + diffuse) → Absorber (heat) → Fluid (Q_u) → Application.

topics.title: Solar Thermal Collectors and Their Types
Estimation of Solar Radiation on Flat Plate Collectors
Solar Concentrators: Parabolic Trough and Parabolic Dish (Description and Working)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Concentrators: Parabolic Trough and Parabolic Dish (Description and Working)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Solar Concentrators** are optical devices that use reflecting surfaces (mirrors) to focus a large area of incident solar radiation onto a smaller receiver area, thereby increasing the **solar flux density** (W/m²) and elevating the working temperature of the heat-collection fluid. They form the backbone of **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** technologies, classified under KTU Module 1 of the Renewable Energy Sources syllabus.

For Module 1, two principal geometries are mandated:

| Geometry | Focus Type | Tracking |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Parabolic Trough** | Line focus (1-D) | Single-axis |
| **Parabolic Dish** | Point focus (2-D) | Dual-axis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT864):** The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically requires students to compare the *description, working principle, concentration ratio, and applications* of parabolic trough versus parabolic dish systems. Expect at least one 7-mark sub-question from this topic in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Parabolic Trough — The "Long Candle-Lighter":**
Imagine a curved, polished gutter running east-west. Sunlight falls on its entire length, but the curved mirror reflects all rays onto a single pipe (the *receiver tube*) running along the middle of the gutter. The pipe gets extremely hot, even though the mirror itself is cool. As the sun moves across the sky, the gutter slowly rotates (single-axis tracking) so the focus always lands on the pipe — like slowly turning a magnifying glass under sunlight to keep a single spot glowing.

**Parabolic Dish — The "Satellite-Dish Cooker":**
Picture a large, dish-shaped mirror shaped exactly like a satellite TV antenna. Every ray of sunlight hitting the dish reflects and converges to one tiny point in mid-air, where a small receiver is hung. The temperature at that point can exceed **1500 °C** — enough to melt steel. Because the focus is a *point*, the dish must track the sun in **two axes** (altitude + azimuth), like a telescope following stars.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Trough = line focus (1-D, cylindrical paraboloid). Dish = point focus (2-D, rotational paraboloid). This dimensional difference drives every downstream engineering parameter — temperature, efficiency, tracking, and cost.

## 1.3 Universal Physical Constants (Bolded)

The following constants are mandatory for KTU numerical problems:

- **Solar disc half-angle:** $\theta_s = 0.265^\circ \approx 4.65 \times 10^{-3}$ **rad**
- **Extraterrestrial normal irradiance (AM 0):** $G_{sc} = 1367$ **W/m²** (Solar Constant)
- **Peak terrestrial direct normal irradiance (DNI):** $G_{bn} \approx 1000$ **W/m²** at AM 1.5
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/m²K⁴**
- **Maximum geometric concentration ratio (line focus):** $C_{max} = 1/\sin\theta_s \approx 216$
- **Maximum geometric concentration ratio (point focus):** $C_{max} = 1/\sin^2\theta_s \approx 46{,}000$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parabolic Trough — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC) is a **linear-focusing**, **line-axis** concentrator formed by translating a 2-D parabolic cross-section along a straight axis.

**Geometric Anatomy:**
- **Aperture width (W):** Distance across the mirror opening.
- **Focal length (f):** Distance from the vertex of the parabola to the focal line.
- **Rim angle (φ):** Angle between the optical axis and the line joining the focus to the rim edge.
- **Receiver/absorber tube:** Coated with selective absorber (e.g., cermet), evacuated glass envelope to suppress convective losses.
- **Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF):** Synthetic oil (Therminol VP-1), molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃), or water/steam in direct-steam generation.

**Step-by-Step Working Logic:**

1. **Solar incidence** — Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) strikes the **aperture plane** of the trough. Diffuse radiation is *rejected* by the geometry and lost (this is why PTCs require high-DNI sites like Rajasthan or Atacama).
2. **Reflection** — The silvered glass mirror (reflectivity $\rho \approx 0.93$) bends every incoming parallel ray toward the **focal line** by the law of reflection.
3. **Absorption** — The absorber tube captures concentrated flux. Concentration ratio $C$ multiplies the incident flux.
4. **HTF heating** — The fluid inside the tube rises from $\sim 290$ °C (cold leg) to $\sim 400$ °C (hot leg).
5. **Single-axis tracking** — The trough pivots on a north-south horizontal axis, rotating east-to-west daily to compensate the sun's hourly motion.
6. **Power block interface** — Hot HTF enters a heat exchanger, generating superheated steam (typically 100 bar, 380 °C) that drives a Rankine-cycle turbine.
7. **Recirculation** — Cooled HTF returns via pumps to the cold leg of the trough field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Parabolic troughs power commercial-scale plants such as **SEGS (Solar Energy Generating Systems)** in the Mojave Desert, California, totaling 354 MW capacity, and India's **India One Solar Thermal Power Plant** in Abu Road (2.5 MW).

## 2.2 Parabolic Dish — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The Parabolic Dish (also called *Dish-Stirling* system when paired with a Stirling engine) is a **point-focusing**, **rotationally-symmetric** concentrator.

**Geometric Anatomy:**
- **Aperture diameter (D):** Typically 5–15 m for standalone units.
- **Focal length (f):** Distance from dish vertex to the receiver.
- **f-number (f/D):** Typically 0.5–1.0; lower f-number gives shallower dish with wider rim angle.
- **Receiver:** Cavity-type with small aperture, mounted at the focus.
- **Reflector:** Either stretched-metal membrane or back-silvered glass facets.

**Step-by-Step Working Logic:**

1. **Solar incidence** — DNI strikes the full **circular aperture** of the dish.
2. **Reflection** — Every facet of the parabolic surface (an equation of revolution) directs sunlight to the **focal point**.
3. **Dual-axis tracking** — A sun-sensor + motor assembly rotates the dish in both **azimuth** and **elevation** to maintain alignment to within $\pm 0.1^\circ$.
4. **Concentrated absorption** — The receiver cavity receives flux densities of **1000–2000 suns** (i.e., 1–2 MW/m²).
5. **Working fluid heating** — Either hydrogen/helium (Stirling engine), air (micro-turbine + Brayton), or direct thermal storage medium is heated to **500–1500 °C**.
6. **Power conversion** — A Stirling engine (closed-cycle, external combustion) or microturbine converts thermal energy directly to mechanical, then to electrical via an alternator.
7. **Waste heat rejection** — Cooling water or air-cooled radiators dissipate the rejected heat from the engine cold side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Dish-Stirling systems achieve the **highest thermal-to-electric conversion efficiency (≈ 31.25 %)** among all CSP technologies — even surpassing PV in some operating regimes when combined with thermal storage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|----------|--------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Parabola (vertex form) | y | $y = \dfrac{x^2}{4f}$ | metres |
| 2 | Aperture-depth relation | d | $d = \dfrac{W^2}{16f}$ | metres |
| 3 | Rim (edge) angle | $\phi$ | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{W}{4f}$ | degrees / radians |
| 4 | Geometric concentration ratio (line focus) | $C_{g,L}$ | $C_{g,L} = \dfrac{W}{\pi D_r}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Geometric concentration ratio (point focus) | $C_{g,P}$ | $C_{g,P} = \dfrac{A_{ap}}{\pi D_r^2/4} = \left(\dfrac{D}{D_r}\right)^2$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Max theoretical concentration (line) | $C_{max,L}$ | $C_{max,L} = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta_s}$ | $\approx 216$ |
| 7 | Max theoretical concentration (point) | $C_{max,P}$ | $C_{max,P} = \dfrac{1}{\sin^2\theta_s}$ | $\approx 46{,}000$ |
| 8 | Concentrated flux on receiver | $q_{rec}$ | $q_{rec} = C \cdot \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot G_{bn}$ | W/m² |
| 9 | Optical efficiency (PTC) | $\eta_o$ | $\eta_o = \rho \tau \alpha \gamma$ | 0.6 – 0.75 |
| 10 | Intercept factor | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{Q_{abs}}{Q_{inc}}$ | 0.95 – 0.99 |
| 11 | Useful heat gain | $Q_u$ | $Q_u = F_R \left[(\tau\alpha) G_{bn} - U_L(T_{fi}-T_a)\right] A_a$ | W |
| 12 | Heat loss coefficient | $U_L$ | $U_L = U_{conv} + U_{rad} + U_{cond}$ | W/m²K |
| 13 | Radiation loss (receiver) | $q_{rad}$ | $q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_{rec}^4 - T_{sky}^4)$ | W/m² |
| 14 | Solar disc half-angle | $\theta_s$ | constant | $0.265^\circ$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use $\vert$ (absolute value) notation in formulas like $U_L(T_{fi}-T_a)$ — KTU valuation penalises incorrect signs in heat-loss terms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Parabola Equation & Rim Angle Relation

Starting from the standard conic-section definition, a parabola is the locus of points equidistant from the **focus** and the **directrix**.

Let the focus be at $(0, f)$ and directrix at $y = -f$.

For any point $(x, y)$ on the parabola:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance to focus:} \quad & \sqrt{x^2 + (y-f)^2} \\
\text{Distance to directrix:} \quad & y + f \\
\text{Setting them equal:} \quad & \sqrt{x^2 + (y-f)^2} = y + f \\
\text{Squaring both sides:} \quad & x^2 + (y-f)^2 = (y+f)^2 \\
\text{Expanding:} \quad & x^2 + y^2 - 2fy + f^2 = y^2 + 2fy + f^2 \\
\text{Cancel } y^2 \text{ and } f^2: \quad & x^2 = 4fy \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad & \boxed{y = \dfrac{x^2}{4f}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **canonical parabola equation** used in every KTU derivation. The vertex is at the origin, the focus lies at distance $f$ on the positive y-axis, and every parallel ray entering along the y-axis reflects to the focus (law of reflection = equal angles).

**Rim Angle Derivation:**

The rim of the trough is at $x = W/2$. The line from focus $(0, f)$ to rim $(W/2, W^2/(16f))$ subtends an angle $\phi$ with the axis:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \dfrac{\text{horizontal offset}}{\text{vertical drop}} = \dfrac{W/2 - 0}{f - \dfrac{W^2}{16f}} \\
\text{But for typical troughs, } \frac{W^2}{16f} \ll f, \text{ so:} \quad & \tan\phi \approx \dfrac{W/2}{f} = \dfrac{W}{2f}
\end{aligned}$$

A common KTU-friendly simplified form treats the rim angle from focus-to-rim relative to the axis (vertex-to-focus line):

$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \dfrac{W}{4f}}$$

The **depth** (sagitta) of the parabolic trough then follows:

$$d = f - \dfrac{W^2}{16f} \quad \text{or for small rim angles:} \quad d \approx \dfrac{W^2}{16f}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Geometric Concentration Ratio (Line Focus)

The **geometric concentration ratio** is defined as the ratio of the aperture area from which radiation is collected to the absorber area onto which it is concentrated.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{g,L} &= \dfrac{\text{Aperture area per unit length}}{\text{Absorber area per unit length}} \\
\text{Aperture area per unit length (L=1 m):} \quad & A_a = W \times 1 = W \\
\text{Absorber tube perimeter per unit length:} \quad & A_r = \pi D_r \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad & \boxed{C_{g,L} = \dfrac{W}{\pi D_r}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A parabolic trough has aperture $W = 5.0$ m and absorber tube outer diameter $D_r = 0.07$ m. Find $C_{g,L}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{g,L} &= \dfrac{5.0}{\pi \times 0.07} = \dfrac{5.0}{0.2199} \\
\boxed{C_{g,L} &\approx 22.7}
\end{aligned}$$

This is well within the practical range of **15–80** for commercial PTCs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Theoretical Concentration Ratio

The Sun is **not** a point source — it subtends a half-angle $\theta_s \approx 0.265^\circ$. Therefore, the reflected cone from any point on the concentrator also subtends $\theta_s$ at the receiver. The receiver cannot be smaller than the image of the Sun.

**For line focus (2-D analysis):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{max,L} &= \dfrac{\text{Length of sun image along receiver normal}}{\text{Receiver diameter minimum}} \\
\text{A point at the rim reflects at angle } \theta_s \text{ off-axis:} \quad & \text{spread on receiver} \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad & \boxed{C_{max,L} = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta_s} \approx \dfrac{1}{0.00465} \approx 215.5}
\end{aligned}$$

**For point focus (3-D analysis):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{max,P} &= \dfrac{1}{\sin^2\theta_s} = \left(\dfrac{1}{\sin 0.265^\circ}\right)^2 \\
C_{max,P} &= (215.5)^2 \\
\boxed{C_{max,P} &\approx 46{,}442}
\end{aligned}$$

This explains why dish systems achieve **two orders of magnitude higher** concentration than troughs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Optical Efficiency & Useful Heat Gain

For a parabolic trough, the **optical efficiency** $\eta_o$ is the product of all sub-efficiencies:

$$\eta_o = \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma$$

where $\rho$ is mirror reflectivity, $\tau$ is glass envelope transmittance, $\alpha$ is absorber absorptivity, and $\gamma$ is the intercept factor.

The **useful heat gain** of the receiver per unit aperture area:

$$q_u = F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) G_{bn} \cdot C_{g,L} - U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

where:
- $F_R$ = heat-removal factor (ratio of actual useful gain to maximum possible gain)
- $U_L$ = overall heat-loss coefficient
- $T_{fi}$ = mean fluid temperature inside absorber
- $T_a$ = ambient temperature

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A PTC has $W = 4$ m, $D_r = 0.05$ m, $G_{bn} = 900$ W/m², $(\tau\alpha) = 0.85$, $\rho = 0.92$, $\gamma = 0.97$, $U_L = 4.5$ W/m²K, $T_{fi} = 350$ °C, $T_a = 30$ °C. Find useful heat flux per unit aperture area.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{g,L} &= \dfrac{4}{\pi \times 0.05} = \dfrac{4}{0.1571} = 25.46 \\
q_{rec} &= (\tau\alpha) \cdot \rho \cdot \gamma \cdot G_{bn} \cdot C_{g,L} \\
q_{rec} &= 0.85 \times 0.92 \times 0.97 \times 900 \times 25.46 \\
q_{rec} &= 0.7582 \times 22914 = 17{,}372 \text{ W/m}^2 \\
q_{loss} &= U_L (T_{fi} - T_a) = 4.5 \times (350 - 30) = 1440 \text{ W/m}^2 \\
\boxed{q_u &= 17{,}372 - 1440 = 15{,}932 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 15.93 \text{ kW/m}^2}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| Focus type | Linear (1-D) | Point (2-D) |
| Geometry | Cylindrical paraboloid | Rotational paraboloid |
| Tracking | Single-axis (E-W) | Dual-axis (E-W + N-S) |
| Concentration ratio | 15 – 80 | 600 – 2000 |
| Working temperature | 200 – 400 °C | 500 – 1500 °C |
| Receiver | Linear evacuated tube | Cavity receiver |
| Power block | Rankine cycle (steam) | Stirling engine / Brayton |
| Plant scale | 1 – 400 MW (utility) | 5 – 50 kW (modular) |
| Annual efficiency | 11 – 16 % | 23 – 31 % |
| Land use | Moderate | High (anti-tracking spacing) |
| Storage compatibility | Excellent (molten salt) | Limited (small thermal mass) |
| Commercial maturity | Mature (1980s+) | Pilot / Niche |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
