# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between line-focusing and point-focusing solar concentrators with one example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Line-focusing concentrators concentrate sunlight onto a *linear* focal line using a 2-D parabolic cross-section translated along an axis. They require only single-axis tracking. Example: **Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)**, typical concentration ratio 15–80.
Point-focusing concentrators concentrate sunlight onto a *single* point using a 3-D rotational paraboloid, requiring dual-axis tracking. Example: **Parabolic Dish**, typical concentration ratio 600–2000.
The fundamental difference arises from the dimensionality of the reflecting surface: 1-D translation vs. 2-D revolution, leading to radically different concentration ratios and operating temperatures.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Why is dual-axis tracking mandatory for a parabolic dish system but single-axis tracking sufficient for a parabolic trough? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A parabolic trough has translational symmetry along its long axis. The sun's apparent motion along that axis (north-south daily drift over the year) does not move the focal line off the receiver — only the cross-axis motion (east-west hourly motion) requires compensation via single-axis tracking.
A parabolic dish, however, is rotationally symmetric about a single axis pointing at the sun. The sun's apparent position changes in *both* azimuth and elevation throughout the day and year. To keep the reflected cone centred on the cavity receiver, the dish must rotate about **two perpendicular axes simultaneously** (azimuth + elevation), hence **dual-axis tracking** is mandatory.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Describe the construction and working principle of a **Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)** with a neat labelled sketch. List its major components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A parabolic trough has an aperture width of $5.5$ m and a focal length of $1.85$ m. The absorber tube has an outer diameter of $0.07$ m. Calculate:
   (i) the rim (edge) angle,
   (ii) the geometric concentration ratio,
   (iii) the depth of the trough.
   Take $\pi = 3.1416$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction:**
- **Reflector:** A long, curved mirror made of back-silvered low-iron glass panels (or aluminised polymer film in newer designs) bent into a parabolic cross-section. The reflectivity is typically **$\rho = 0.90$ to $0.94$**.
- **Support structure:** Steel space-frame pylons that hold the mirror contour within $\pm 2$ mm tolerance to maintain focus.
- **Receiver tube:** A steel tube coated with a *cermet* selective absorber (high $\alpha$, low $\varepsilon$), enclosed in an evacuated borosilicate glass envelope to suppress convection losses. Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF) such as Therminol VP-1 or molten salt flows inside.
- **Tracking system:** Hydraulic or motor-driven slew drive rotates the entire trough about a horizontal N-S axis to follow the sun.
- **Interconnection:** Troughs are arranged in parallel "loops" of typically 4–8 troughs connected in series via flexible bellows.

**Working Principle:**
DNI incident on the aperture is reflected by the parabolic mirror and concentrated onto the linear focal line where the receiver tube sits. The concentrated flux (typically 30–80 times ambient DNI) heats the HTF to 300–400 °C. The hot HTF is piped to a central heat exchanger where it boils water to generate steam at ~100 bar, ~380 °C. The steam drives a Rankine-cycle turbine coupled to a generator, producing electricity. Cooled HTF is pumped back to the trough field. The trough uses **single-axis tracking** to compensate the sun's hourly east-west motion.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $W = 5.5$ m, $f = 1.85$ m, $D_r = 0.07$ m.

**(i) Rim angle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tan\phi &= \dfrac{W}{4f} = \dfrac{5.5}{4 \times 1.85} = \dfrac{5.5}{7.40} \\
\tan\phi &= 0.7432 \\
\phi &= \tan^{-1}(0.7432) \\
\boxed{\phi &\approx 36.6^\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Geometric concentration ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{g,L} &= \dfrac{W}{\pi D_r} = \dfrac{5.5}{3.1416 \times 0.07} = \dfrac{5.5}{0.2199} \\
\boxed{C_{g,L} &\approx 25.0}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Depth of trough:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \dfrac{W^2}{16f} = \dfrac{(5.5)^2}{16 \times 1.85} = \dfrac{30.25}{29.6} \\
\boxed{d &\approx 1.022 \text{ m}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction, working, and applications of a **Parabolic Dish-Stirling System**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare parabolic trough and parabolic dish systems on the basis of (i) concentration ratio, (ii) tracking mechanism, (iii) operating temperature, (iv) thermal efficiency, (v) power conversion system, and (vi) commercial maturity. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Construction (refer SECTION_4 diagram):**
- **Reflector dish:** A rotationally-symmetric paraboloid of diameter 5–15 m, fabricated either from (a) silvered glass facets (e.g., SAIC/Sandia designs) or (b) stretched polymer membrane with aluminium coating (less accurate but cheap).
- **Receiver:** A small hemispherical *cavity receiver* (aperture ~ 0.2–0.3 m) positioned precisely at the focal point. Inside, working gas (H₂ or He) is heated.
- **Stirling engine:** A sealed, closed-cycle external heat engine with two pistons (displacer + power) coupled to the receiver.
- **Sun-tracking system:** Dual-axis (azimuth + altitude) drives, typically using a sun-sensor with closed-loop control (±0.1° accuracy).
- **Generator/alternator:** Directly coupled to the Stirling engine output shaft.
- **Cooling subsystem:** Air-cooled fins or water jacket at the engine's cold side.

**Working:**
Sunlight striking the dish aperture is reflected by the paraboloid surface and converges onto the cavity receiver, producing flux densities of 1000–2000 suns and temperatures of 500–1500 °C inside the cavity. The working gas is alternately compressed and expanded by the Stirling engine's pistons. The expansion phase is powered by the heat absorbed from the cavity; the compression phase rejects heat to the cooler. Mechanical reciprocating motion is converted to rotary motion and then to electrical AC via the alternator. Dish-Stirling systems reach a peak solar-to-electric efficiency of ~ 31.25 % (record-holder in CSP).

**Applications:**
- Remote village electrification and off-grid power (5–50 kW units).
- Border outpost power supplies.
- Hybrid solar-gas microgrids.
- Research and demonstration plants (e.g., SES EuroDish, Infinia SunCatcher).

#### Model Solution to (b):

| # | Criterion | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish |
|---|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| (i) | **Concentration ratio** | 15 – 80 (typical ≈ 25) | 600 – 2000 (typical ≈ 1000) |
| (ii) | **Tracking** | Single-axis (N-S horizontal pivot) | Dual-axis (azimuth + elevation) |
| (iii) | **Operating temperature** | 200 – 400 °C | 500 – 1500 °C |
| (iv) | **Thermal-to-electric efficiency** | 11 – 16 % (annual) | 23 – 31 % (peak) |
| (v) | **Power conversion** | Steam Rankine cycle (centralised) | Stirling engine or micro-turbine (modular) |
| (vi) | **Commercial maturity** | Mature, commercial (>400 MW installed) | Pilot/Niche; limited deployment |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by:
> 1. Confusing **concentration ratio** with **concentration factor** — they are not the same. Concentration ratio is geometric; concentration factor accounts for optical losses.
> 2. Reporting rim angle in **degrees vs. radians** inconsistently in the same solution.
> 3. Forgetting to state the **focal length and aperture assumptions** when plotting parabolic curves in diagrams.
> 4. Writing *parabolic dish* when the question asks for *trough* (or vice versa) — read the question stem **twice**.
> 5. Omitting the $\gamma$ (intercept factor) in efficiency calculations — full marks only when all four factors $\rho$, $\tau$, $\alpha$, $\gamma$ are mentioned.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- ☐ **Parabolic Trough = 1-D / Line Focus / Single-axis track** → Rankine cycle → 200–400 °C → $C_g \le 80$.
- ☐ **Parabolic Dish = 2-D / Point Focus / Dual-axis track** → Stirling/Brayton → 500–1500 °C → $C_g$ up to 2000.
- ☐ **Parabola equation:** $y = \dfrac{x^2}{4f}$; every parallel ray to the axis reflects to the focus.
- ☐ **Rim angle:** $\tan\phi = \dfrac{W}{4f}$; typical commercial PTCs use $\phi = 60^\circ$–$90^\circ$.
- ☐ **Depth (sagitta):** $d = \dfrac{W^2}{16f}$.
- ☐ **Line-focus concentration:** $C_{g,L} = \dfrac{W}{\pi D_r}$.
- ☐ **Point-focus concentration:** $C_{g,P} = \left(\dfrac{D}{D_r}\right)^2$.
- ☐ **Theoretical max line:** $C_{max,L} = 1/\sin\theta_s \approx 216$ (using $\theta_s = 0.265^\circ$).
- ☐ **Theoretical max point:** $C_{max,P} = 1/\sin^2\theta_s \approx 46{,}000$.
- ☐ **Optical efficiency of PTC:** $\eta_o = \rho\,\tau\,\alpha\,\gamma \approx 0.6$–$0.75$.
- ☐ **Receiver losses:** Radiation ($\sigma T^4$), convection (suppressed by vacuum), conduction (through supports).
- ☐ **Selective absorber:** High $\alpha$ in visible, low $\varepsilon$ in IR — cermet coatings (e.g., Mo–Al₂O₃).
- ☐ **HTF for PTC:** Synthetic oil (≤ 400 °C) or molten salt (≤ 565 °C).
- ☐ **Dish-Stirling world record efficiency:** 31.25 % (Sandia/STM demonstration).
- ☐ **KTU Module 1 must-link:** Both devices are **non-imaging** for large rim angles, but **imaging** for small rim angles.
- ☐ **DNI dependency:** Both concentrators reject diffuse radiation — they need direct-beam-rich sites (Rajasthan, Thar, Atacama, Mojave).
- ☐ **Key SEGS example:** 354 MW installed in California (1984–1991), still the world's largest PTC complex.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Electric Systems: Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Electric Systems: Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation (STEPG)** is a renewable energy conversion process in which the radiant energy from the sun is first captured as **heat** using Concentrating Solar Collectors, then transferred to a working fluid (Heat Transfer Fluid / HTF), and finally converted into **electrical energy** through a thermodynamic heat engine cycle (Rankine, Brayton, or Organic Rankine Cycle) coupled to a generator. This process is formally classified under **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** technologies, as distinct from photovoltaic (PV) systems that directly convert sunlight into electricity via the photoelectric effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Solar thermal electric conversion is the indirect path of solar-to-electrical energy transformation, mediated by thermal energy as an intermediate state. The KTU syllabus explicitly groups this under Module 1, distinguishing it from Solar Photovoltaic and Solar Passive Architecture.

The fundamental difference between Solar PV and Solar Thermal can be summarized in the energy chain:

$$\text{Sun} \rightarrow \text{Photon} \rightarrow \text{Electron excitation (PV)} \rightarrow \text{Electricity}$$

$$\text{Sun} \rightarrow \text{Heat (CSP)} \rightarrow \text{Thermodynamic Cycle} \rightarrow \text{Electricity}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are using a **magnifying glass** to focus sunlight onto a single spot on a piece of paper. The paper gets hot — even hot enough to burn. This is essentially the principle of solar concentration. Now, instead of burning paper, you focus that concentrated heat onto a pipe carrying water. The water turns to steam. The steam spins a turbine. The turbine drives a generator. The generator produces electricity. That is **solar thermal electric power generation** in a nutshell.

Think of it as the **traditional thermal power plant's logic** — but instead of burning coal, oil, or gas to make steam, you are using **sunlight as the "fuel"**. The only difference is the heat source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Chain of Conversion:**
> 1. Sun emits light (radiation).
> 2. Mirrors/lenses concentrate the light onto a small receiver.
> 3. Receiver gets very hot (400 °C – 1000 °C).
> 4. Heat is absorbed by a fluid (water, oil, molten salt, or air).
> 5. Hot fluid makes steam (or directly drives a gas turbine).
> 6. Steam/gas spins a turbine connected to an electrical generator.
> 7. AC electricity is produced and fed to the grid.

## 1.3 Why Concentrate? The Core Physics Reason

The sun delivers roughly **1361 W/m²** at the top of the atmosphere and approximately **1000 W/m²** at the Earth's surface on a clear day at noon (this is the **AM1.5 standard**, defined as the solar irradiance value of **1 kW/m²**). However, a flat-plate collector at 1000 W/m² cannot reach temperatures high enough (above ~300 °C) to drive a conventional Rankine cycle efficiently. The **Carnot efficiency** of a heat engine depends on the temperature differential:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$$

where temperatures are in Kelvin. To get reasonable efficiency, we need **T_hot to be high** — and that requires concentrating the sun's energy onto a small area to boost flux density from ~1 kW/m² to **hundreds or thousands of kW/m²**.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants You Must Memorize for KTU 2024:**
> - **Solar constant (I_sc) = 1361 W/m²** (TOA – Top of Atmosphere)
> - **AM1.5 standard irradiance = 1000 W/m²** (Earth's surface, clear noon)
> - **STC for PV = 1000 W/m² at 25 °C** (different reference, but same value)
> - **Stephan-Boltzmann constant (σ) = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
> - **Sun's surface temperature ≈ 5778 K**

## 1.4 Classification of Solar Thermal Electric Systems

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes four major categories:

| # | System Type | Concentration | Receiver Geometry | Typical Working Fluid |
|---|-------------|---------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)** | Line-focus, C = 30–80 | Linear tube | Synthetic oil / Water / Molten salt |
| 2 | **Parabolic Dish System (PDS)** | Point-focus, C = 1000–5000 | Cavity receiver | Air / Hydrogen / Helium (Brayton) |
| 3 | **Solar Central Receiver (Power Tower)** | Point-focus, C = 300–1500 | External tower receiver | Molten salt / Water / Air |
| 4 | **Solar Pond** | No concentration (passive) | Volumetric absorber | Water (saline gradient) |

Where **C** is the **Geometric Concentration Ratio** defined as:

$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Energy Chain of a Solar Thermal Plant

Every solar thermal electric power plant, regardless of its collector geometry, follows the same generalized energy chain:

$$I_{\text{sun}} \xrightarrow{\text{concentration}} Q_{\text{abs}} \xrightarrow{\text{transfer}} Q_{\text{stored}} \xrightarrow{\text{cycle}} W_{\text{turbine}} \xrightarrow{\text{generator}} P_{\text{electric}}$$

Each arrow in this chain has an associated efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{optical}} \times \eta_{\text{receiver}} \times \eta_{\text{cycle}} \times \eta_{\text{generator}}$$

The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to understand and be able to compute each of these individual efficiency terms.

## 2.2 System 1: Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC)

### 2.2.1 Geometry and Optics
A parabolic trough is a one-dimensional parabolic mirror that focuses sunlight onto a linear receiver tube positioned along the focal line. The receiver tube typically contains a selective coating that absorbs >90% of incident solar radiation while emitting <15% thermal radiation.

**Concentration ratio formula for a parabolic trough:**

$$C_{\text{trough}} = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}} = \frac{W_{\text{aperture}}}{\pi D_{\text{receiver}}}$$

Where:
- **W_aperture** = width of the parabolic mirror opening (m)
- **D_receiver** = diameter of the absorber tube (m)

**Focal length relationship:**

$$f = \frac{W_{\text{aperture}}^2}{16 d}$$

Where **d** is the depth of the parabolic dish.

### 2.2.2 Thermal Performance Equations

The useful heat gain by the receiver is:

$$Q_u = A_{\text{aperture}} \left[ S \cdot \eta_{\text{optical}} - U_L (T_{\text{receiver}} - T_{\text{amb}}) \right]$$

Where:
- **S** = direct normal irradiance (W/m²)
- **η_optical** = product of mirror reflectivity × transmissivity of glass envelope × absorptivity of selective coating
- **U_L** = overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- **T_receiver** = average receiver temperature (K)
- **T_amb** = ambient temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Typical values for a well-designed PTC:
> - **Mirror reflectivity = 0.93** (silvered glass)
> - **Glass envelope transmittance = 0.95**
> - **Selective coating absorptance = 0.95**
> - **Selective coating emittance (at 400 °C) = 0.12**
> - **Resulting optical efficiency η_optical ≈ 0.84**

### 2.2.3 Cosine Losses and Tracking Error
Because the sun moves across the sky, parabolic troughs require **single-axis tracking** (usually East-West). The cosine effect reduces the effective intercept area:

$$A_{\text{effective}} = A_{\text{aperture}} \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

Where **θ** is the angle of incidence between the sun's rays and the aperture normal.

## 2.3 System 2: Parabolic Dish System (PDS)

### 2.3.1 Geometry
A parabolic dish is a **two-axis tracking**, point-focus concentrator shaped like a satellite dish. It concentrates sunlight onto a small cavity receiver at the focal point.

**Concentration ratio for a parabolic dish (maximum theoretical):**

$$C_{\text{max,dish}} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_s)}$$

Where **θ_s** is the half-angle of the sun as seen from Earth (≈ 0.27°, or 4.85 mrad). Therefore:

$$C_{\text{max,dish}} \approx \frac{1}{(0.00485)^2} \approx 42{,}500$$

Practical systems achieve **C = 1000 – 5000** due to optical aberrations, mirror slope errors, and shading.

### 2.3.2 Working Fluid and Cycle
Dish systems typically use a **Brayton cycle** (gas turbine) with air, helium, or hydrogen as the working fluid, OR a **Stirling engine** with hydrogen/helium in a sealed system. Temperatures of **700 – 850 °C** at the receiver are achievable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** The dish-Stirling system is the only solar thermal technology that can theoretically exceed 30% net conversion efficiency on a single-unit basis.

## 2.4 System 3: Solar Central Receiver (Power Tower)

### 2.4.1 Architecture
A field of **heliostats** (large, flat, two-axis-tracking mirrors) surrounds a central tower. Each heliostat is independently steered to reflect sunlight onto a single receiver mounted atop the tower. Power towers can achieve very high concentrations (C = 500 – 1500) and very high temperatures (565 – 1000 °C).

### 2.4.2 Heat Transfer Fluids
The most common HTFs are:
- **Molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃)**: Operating range 290 – 565 °C
- **Water/Steam**: Direct generation, up to 540 °C, 100+ bar
- **Air**: For air-receiver designs, up to 1000 °C
- **Liquid sodium**: Experimental, up to 800 °C

### 2.4.3 Energy Storage
Power towers have the unique advantage of **integrated thermal energy storage (TES)**, typically using two-tank molten salt storage. The energy stored is:

$$Q_{\text{stored}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- **m** = mass of salt in the hot tank (kg)
- **c_p** = specific heat of salt (≈ 1.55 kJ/kg·K)
- **ΔT** = temperature difference between hot and cold tanks (typically 565 °C – 290 °C = 275 K)

## 2.5 System 4: Solar Pond

A solar pond is a **salinity-gradient pond** that stores solar thermal energy at the bottom. The salt gradient (NaCl) prevents convective heat loss, allowing the bottom layer to reach 70 – 90 °C. The hot brine is then used to drive an **Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)** because the temperatures are too low for a conventional steam cycle.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!TIP]
> **The following table is your KTU exam cheat sheet for this topic. Memorize the units, definitions, and typical numerical ranges.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---------------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Concentration Ratio (general) | $C = A_a / A_r$ | 1 – 42,000 (dimensionless) |
| 2 | Trough Concentration | $C = W_a / (\pi D_r)$ | 30 – 80 |
| 3 | Max Dish Concentration | $C_{max} = 1/\sin^2(\theta_s)$ | ~42,500 (theoretical max) |
| 4 | Useful Heat Gain (PTC) | $Q_u = A_a [S \eta_o - U_L(T_r - T_a)]$ | W or kW |
| 5 | Overall Plant Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_{opt} \times \eta_{rec} \times \eta_{cyc} \times \eta_{gen}$ | 0.15 – 0.40 |
| 6 | Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_C = 1 - T_c/T_h$ | 0.40 – 0.70 (T in K) |
| 7 | Cosine Loss Factor | $A_{eff} = A_a \cos(\theta)$ | 0 – 1 |
| 8 | Thermal Energy Storage | $Q_s = m c_p \Delta T$ | J or kWh |
| 9 | Mirror Reflectivity | $\rho$ | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| 10 | Selective Absorptivity | $\alpha$ | 0.90 – 0.97 |
| 11 | Thermal Emissivity (selective coating) | $\varepsilon$ | 0.05 – 0.20 (at 400 °C) |
| 12 | Peak DNI (Direct Normal Irradiance) | $I_{DNI}$ | 900 – 1050 W/m² |
| 13 | Solar Constant | $I_{sc}$ | 1361 W/m² |
| 14 | Stefan-Boltzmann Loss | $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² |
| 15 | Cosine of Incidence (cosθ) | $\cos\theta = \cos\theta_z \cos\phi + \sin\theta_z \sin\phi \cos\gamma_s$ | –1 to +1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Do not confuse **AM1.5G = 1000 W/m²** (terrestrial standard, includes diffuse) with **DNI** (Direct Normal Irradiance, the beam component only). CSP systems use **DNI**, NOT global horizontal irradiance (GHI). A common KTU MCQ trap asks: "Parabolic troughs can use diffuse radiation." The correct answer is **FALSE** — they require direct beam radiation only.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solar thermal power plants offer three engineering advantages over PV:
1. **Dispatchability** — Thermal energy storage allows generation at night and during clouds.
2. **Hybridization** — Can be fired with natural gas/biomass as backup fuel.
3. **High-temperature process heat** — Can produce industrial heat (e.g., for cement, desalination) and dispatchable electricity from the same plant.

The largest operational power towers include **Ivanpah (392 MW, USA)**, **Noor III (150 MW, Morocco — molten salt)**, and **Gemasolar (24/7 solar, Spain — 15 hours of storage)**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Geometric Concentration Ratio of a Parabolic Trough

**Given:**
- Aperture width of the parabolic mirror: $W_a$ (m)
- Outer diameter of the receiver tube: $D_r$ (m)

**To find:** The line-geometric concentration ratio $C$.

### Step 1 — Define the Concentration Ratio

By definition, the geometric concentration ratio is the ratio of the area that intercepts sunlight (aperture) to the area of the receiver (absorber):

$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}}$$

### Step 2 — Express Both Areas per Unit Length

For a trough, we consider a unit length (1 m) of the structure:
- The aperture area per unit length = $W_a \times 1 = W_a$ (m²/m)
- The receiver area per unit length = $\pi D_r \times 1 = \pi D_r$ (m²/m)

### Step 3 — Substitute Into the Definition

$$C = \frac{W_a \times 1}{\pi D_r \times 1}$$

This simplifies to:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{W_a}{\pi D_r}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Style Problem)

A parabolic trough collector has an aperture width of 5.76 m and uses a receiver tube of 70 mm outer diameter. Compute the concentration ratio.

**Solution:**

$$C = \frac{5.76}{\pi \times 0.070} = \frac{5.76}{0.2199} \approx 26.2$$

**Result:** $C \approx 26$ — this is a low concentration ratio, typical of industrial PTC systems like the LS-2 or LS-3 collectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Distribution:** This 7-mark derivation typically asks for 4 marks of derivation logic and 3 marks of numerical evaluation. Always show the substitution of numerical values with correct unit conversions (mm → m).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Theoretical Concentration Ratio of a 2-D Point-Focus Concentrator

**Given:** A parabolic dish of half-angle $\theta_c$ focusing sun rays (which arrive within a half-angle $\theta_s \approx 0.27°$).

**To find:** The maximum geometric concentration ratio $C_{max}$.

### Step 1 — Energy Conservation

The total power entering the dish aperture from the sun must equal (in the absence of losses) the power leaving the receiver back to the surroundings. By the second law of thermodynamics, the receiver cannot be hotter than the source if the concentration ratio is too low.

### Step 2 — Geometric Construction

The sun's rays arrive at Earth with a maximum half-angle $\theta_s = 0.27°$. The dish is defined by its rim half-angle $\theta_c$ (angle from the optical axis to the rim). For the image to be at the focal point, all incoming rays from the sun's edge must emerge from the receiver.

### Step 3 — Apply Étendue (Optical Invariant) Conservation

The product of area and the solid angle subtended is conserved (in 2-D, area × angle):

$$A_{\text{aperture}} \cdot \sin(\theta_s) = A_{\text{receiver}} \cdot \sin(\theta_c)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Concentration

$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}} = \frac{\sin(\theta_c)}{\sin(\theta_s)}$$

### Step 5 — Maximize the Concentration

The maximum occurs when $\theta_c = 90°$ (i.e., the dish is hemispherical, rim perpendicular to optical axis). Then $\sin(\theta_c) = 1$:

$$\boxed{C_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{\sin(\theta_s)}}$$

For 3-D point focus, it becomes:

$$C_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(\theta_s)}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation

$$\theta_s = 0.27° = 0.27 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 4.71 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

$$C_{\text{max,3D}} = \frac{1}{\sin^2(4.71 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{1}{(4.71 \times 10^{-3})^2} \approx 45{,}000$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The number ~42,000 – 45,000 is the absolute thermodynamic maximum for any solar concentrator using 1-sun input. Real systems reach 1 – 10% of this theoretical limit due to optical aberrations, mirror slope errors (typically 1 – 4 mrad), and shading.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Optical Efficiency Chain of a Parabolic Trough Receiver

The **optical efficiency** is the fraction of incident solar power that is actually absorbed by the HTF in the receiver tube. It is the product of four multiplicative losses:

### Step 1 — List the Four Sequential Losses

When a solar ray passes from the sun to the HTF, it encounters:

1. **Cosine loss** — $f_{\cos} = \cos(\theta)$ — fraction of rays entering the aperture
2. **Mirror reflectivity** — $\rho$ — fraction of rays reflected by the mirror
3. **Glass envelope transmittance** — $\tau$ — fraction of rays that pass through the glass cover
4. **Selective coating absorptance** — $\alpha$ — fraction of rays absorbed by the coating

### Step 2 — Write the Optical Efficiency

By sequential energy balance:

$$\eta_{\text{optical}} = f_{\cos} \cdot \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha$$

### Step 3 — Insert Typical Values for a Well-Designed PTC

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| $\cos(\theta)$ (solar noon, summer) | 0.95 |
| $\rho$ (silvered glass) | 0.93 |
| $\tau$ (anti-reflective coated glass) | 0.95 |
| $\alpha$ (cermet selective coating) | 0.95 |

### Step 4 — Compute the Overall Optical Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{optical}} = 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \approx 0.797 \approx 0.80$$

This is the *instantaneous* optical efficiency at near-normal incidence.

### Step 5 — Account for Intercept Factor and Dirt

In practice, two more multiplicative factors are introduced:
- **Intercept factor (γ)** — fraction of reflected rays that actually hit the tube (typically 0.95 – 0.98)
- **Dirt/soiling factor (D)** — typically 0.95

$$\eta_{\text{optical,total}} = f_{\cos} \cdot \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma \cdot D$$

**Realistic field value:** $\eta_{\text{optical,total}} \approx 0.65$ to $0.75$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Output and Conversion Efficiency of a Solar Thermal Plant

### Step 1 — Solar Power Intercepted by the Aperture

$$P_{\text{solar}} = A_a \cdot S \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot \eta_{\text{optical}}$$

### Step 2 — Net Useful Heat Delivered to the HTF

$$Q_u = P_{\text{solar}} - A_r \cdot U_L (T_r - T_{\text{amb}})$$

This subtracts the **conductive + convective + radiative** heat loss from the receiver.

### Step 3 — Conversion to Mechanical Work via Heat Engine

The heat engine operates between $T_h$ (HTF inlet to boiler) and $T_c$ (condenser). Its efficiency is bounded by Carnot:

$$\eta_{\text{cycle}} \leq 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h}$$

Real steam Rankine cycles achieve 0.30 – 0.40 of Carnot.

### Step 4 — Electrical Power Output

$$P_{\text{electric}} = Q_u \cdot \eta_{\text{cycle}} \cdot \eta_{\text{generator}}$$

The generator efficiency is typically **0.95 – 0.98** (for synchronous generators).

### Step 5 — Overall Plant Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{plant}} = \frac{P_{\text{electric}}}{A_a \cdot S \cdot \cos(\theta)} = \eta_{\text{optical}} \cdot \eta_{\text{receiver}} \cdot \eta_{\text{cycle}} \cdot \eta_{\text{generator}}$$

### Step 6 — Typical Numerical Example for KTU Exam

**Problem:** A 50 MWe solar trough plant operates with the following parameters:
- Aperture area: 500,000 m²
- DNI: 900 W/m²
- Optical efficiency: 0.70
- Receiver thermal efficiency: 0.85
- Steam Rankine cycle efficiency: 0.38
- Generator efficiency: 0.97

**Solution:**

$$P_{\text{electric}} = 500{,}000 \times 900 \times 0.70 \times 0.85 \times 0.38 \times 0.97$$

$$P_{\text{electric}} = 4.5 \times 10^8 \times 0.2196 \approx 98.8 \text{ MW thermal input equivalent}$$

**Net electrical output:** approximately **42 MW** — close to the 50 MWe nameplate capacity.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a KTU problem gives you all four sub-efficiencies, simply multiply them. The KTU 2024 scheme tests the *chain product* of efficiencies. Do not attempt a long derivation in the exam — plug and chug.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Four CSP Technologies

The following matrix is a **high-density revision tool** for KTU 2024. Cover this in your answer to get full marks in any "Compare and contrast" question (usually 7 marks).

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Power Tower | Solar Pond |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Concentration ratio** | 30 – 80 | 1000 – 5000 | 300 – 1500 | 1 (no conc.) |
| **Receiver temperature** | 300 – 400 °C | 700 – 850 °C | 500 – 1000 °C | 70 – 90 °C |
| **Tracking** | 1-axis (E-W) | 2-axis | 2-axis (heliostats) | None |
| **Working fluid** | Synthetic oil | Air / He / H₂ | Molten salt / water / air | Water (saline) |
| **Cycle** | Rankine | Brayton / Stirling | Rankine / Brayton | ORC |
| **Storage capability** | Limited (2-tank oil/salt) | Battery or thermal | Excellent (molten salt TES) | Excellent (pond) |
| **Hybridization** | Yes (gas-fired heater) | Difficult | Yes (gas backup) | No |
| **Plant efficiency (net)** | 14 – 18% | 25 – 31% | 20 – 28% | 5 – 10% |
| **Land use (m²/MWh/yr)** | 6 – 8 | 4 – 6 | 4 – 7 | 20+ |
| **Commercial maturity** | Mature (>15 GW deployed) | Pilot stage | Mature (>5 GW deployed) | Niche / declining |
| **Landmark projects** | SEGS (USA), Noor I (Morocco) | Dish-Stirling demos | Ivanpah (USA), Crescent Dunes (USA) | Bet Ha'Arava (Israel) |
| **Cost (USD/W)** | 4 – 6 | 8 – 12 (current) | 5 – 8 | 3 – 5 (very site-specific) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** When a question asks for "the most efficient solar thermal system," the correct answer is the **parabolic dish-Stirling** system. When it asks for "the most commercially deployed," the answer is the **parabolic trough**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Distinguish between Solar Photovoltaic and Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Solar Photovoltaic (PV) | Solar Thermal Electric (CSP) |
|---------|-------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Conversion mechanism** | Direct (photon → electron → electricity) | Indirect (sunlight → heat → mechanical → electricity) |
| **Intermediate form** | None — direct electrical | Thermal energy (steam, hot fluid) |
| **Working principle** | Photoelectric effect in semiconductors | Thermodynamic cycle (Rankine/Brayton) |
| **Output type** | DC (then inverted to AC) | AC directly from generator |
| **Efficiency** | 15 – 22% (commercial modules) | 14 – 30% (depending on technology) |
| **Energy storage** | Batteries (electrochemical) | Thermal (molten salt, latent heat) |
| **Radiation requirement** | Global (diffuse + direct) | Direct Normal (DNI only) |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for any three valid distinguishing points with one-line justification each.)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"What is the Geometric Concentration Ratio? Why is it higher for a parabolic dish than for a parabolic trough?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Geometric Concentration Ratio (C)** is the ratio of the area of the concentrator's aperture (which intercepts sunlight) to the area of the receiver (which absorbs the concentrated energy):

$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}}$$

It is dimensionless. A higher C means a smaller receiver area and hence a higher solar flux density on the receiver, leading to higher receiver temperatures.

**Why dish > trough:**
- A **trough** is a line-focus (1-D) concentrator; light is focused along a line, so the geometric maximum is limited to ~100 in practice.
- A **dish** is a point-focus (2-D) concentrator; light is focused to a point from two dimensions, achieving theoretical concentrations of ~42,000 and practical values of 1000 – 5000.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for the line-focus vs. point-focus distinction.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question B — Choice A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**"Explain the working principle, components, and performance characteristics of a Parabolic Trough Concentrating Solar Power (CSP) System. With neat sketches, describe the optical concentration mechanism."**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Components (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Working Principle:**
A parabolic trough is a one-dimensional, line-focusing concentrator. Sunlight parallel to the plane of symmetry of the parabola is reflected and concentrated onto a linear receiver tube placed along the focal line of the trough. The HTF (typically synthetic oil like Therminol VP-1, or molten salt) flowing inside the tube is heated to 300 – 400 °C. The hot HTF is then used to generate superheated steam in a heat exchanger, which drives a conventional Rankine cycle turbine connected to a synchronous generator, producing AC electricity.

**2. Major Components:**
- **Parabolic mirror (reflector):** Usually silvered low-iron glass or aluminum sheet, reflectivity 0.90 – 0.95.
- **Receiver tube:** Coated with a selective cermet coating (α > 0.95, ε < 0.15 at 400 °C), housed inside a glass envelope evacuated to ~10⁻³ Pa to suppress convective losses.
- **Tracking system:** Single-axis, North-South axis, allowing East-West daily tracking.
- **HTF loop:** Includes pump, expansion tank, freeze protection.
- **Heat exchanger / steam generator:** Transfers heat from HTF to water/steam.
- **Power block:** Steam turbine, condenser, generator, grid interconnection.

**3. Tracking:** The trough rotates about a single axis to keep the sun's image on the focal line throughout the day. The collector aperture area is therefore $A_a = W_a \times L$ where $L$ is the trough length.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for principle, 3 marks for components with one-line description each, 2 marks for tracking and HTF loop.)*

#### Part (b) — Optical Concentration and Performance (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concentration Ratio:**

$$C = \frac{A_a}{A_r} = \frac{W_a}{\pi D_r}$$

For typical industrial values: $W_a = 5.76$ m, $D_r = 70$ mm $= 0.070$ m:

$$C = \frac{5.76}{\pi \times 0.070} \approx 26.2$$

**2. Optical Efficiency Chain:**

$$\eta_{\text{optical}} = \cos(\theta) \cdot \rho \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \gamma$$

Where:
- $\cos(\theta)$ = cosine factor (≈ 0.95 at solar noon)
- $\rho$ = mirror reflectivity (0.93)
- $\tau$ = glass transmittance (0.95)
- $\alpha$ = coating absorptance (0.95)
- $\gamma$ = intercept factor (0.97)

$$\eta_{\text{optical}} \approx 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.97 \approx 0.77$$

**3. Useful Heat Gain:**

$$Q_u = A_a \left[ S \eta_o - U_L (T_r - T_a) \right]$$

**4. Thermal Losses from Receiver:**
- **Conduction + convection losses** — minimized by evacuated glass envelope (~2 W/m·K).
- **Radiation losses** — proportional to $\varepsilon \sigma T^4$, minimized by selective coating (low ε at high T).
- **Wind losses** — second-order, depend on wind speed.

**5. Overall Performance:**
A well-designed PTC plant achieves:
- **Receiver thermal efficiency:** 70 – 80%
- **Cycle efficiency (Rankine):** 35 – 40%
- **Net plant efficiency:** 14 – 18%
- **Capacity factor:** 25 – 40% (vs. 15 – 20% for PV without tracking)

*(7 marks — 2 marks for concentration ratio with numerical example, 2 marks for efficiency chain, 3 marks for thermal losses and overall performance.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Many students forget to include the **intercept factor (γ)** in the optical efficiency chain. This is a 1-mark loss.
> 2. Many students compute the concentration ratio using the **inner** tube diameter instead of the **outer** diameter. KTU expects outer diameter.
> 3. Some students confuse the **glass envelope** with the receiver. The receiver is the metal absorber tube; the glass is just a cover.

---

### Question B — Choice B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a Solar Central Receiver (Power Tower) System. Discuss the role of heliostats, the choice of heat transfer fluid, and the thermal energy storage mechanism."**

#### Part (a) — System Description and Heliostat Field (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. System Layout:**
A power tower consists of a **field of heliostats** (large, two-axis-tracking flat mirrors) arranged around a central tower. Each heliostat independently tracks the sun and reflects its beam onto a single **receiver** mounted at the top of the tower (typically 80 – 200 m tall). The receiver is a multi-panel cavity or external absorber.

**2. Heliostat:**
- Typically 1 – 10 m² per mirror, made of silvered glass or back-silvered thin glass.
- Each heliostat has its own sun-tracking algorithm.
- The **aim point** on the receiver is pre-computed for every heliostat to compensate for cosine losses, mirror shape (canting), and tower blocking.
- Typical heliostat field sizes: 50,000 – 200,000 mirrors for 50 – 200 MWe.

**3. Optical Concentration:**
With a heliostat field of 1 km² around a 150 m tower, the average concentration ratio at the receiver is 300 – 1000, with peak flux densities of **1 – 2.5 MW/m²** on the receiver surface — much higher than troughs.

**4. Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.3 for the Mermaid block diagram of a power tower.)

*(7 marks — 2 marks for layout + heliostat description, 2 marks for aim point and field geometry, 2 marks for concentration analysis, 1 mark for block diagram.)*

#### Part (b) — HTF Selection and Thermal Energy Storage (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Heat Transfer Fluid Options:**

| HTF | Temperature Range | Pressure | Pros | Cons |
|-----|-------------------|----------|------|------|
| **Molten salt (60/40 NaNO₃/KNO₃)** | 290 – 565 °C | 1 atm (storage), 10+ bar in loop | High storage capacity, non-flammable | Freeze risk below 220 °C, viscous |
| **Water/Steam (direct)** | 100 – 540 °C | 100 – 250 bar | Highest cycle efficiency, no HTF-to-water HX | No storage in direct mode without retrofit |
| **Air** | 200 – 1000 °C | 1 – 30 bar | Highest temperatures, no freezing | Low volumetric heat capacity, larger ducts |
| **Liquid sodium** | 250 – 800 °C | 1 – 5 bar | Excellent heat transfer | Highly reactive with water (safety) |

**2. Why Molten Salt is the Most Common Choice:**
- Operates at atmospheric pressure in storage tanks → cheap tank construction.
- Very high volumetric energy density (~270 kWh/m³ at 565 °C).
- Non-toxic, non-flammable.
- Compatible with conventional Rankine cycle at 540 °C / 100 bar.

**3. Two-Tank Thermal Energy Storage (TES):**

The hot salt leaves the receiver at 565 °C and is pumped to the **hot salt tank**. Cold salt at 290 °C from the cold tank is pumped to the receiver to be heated. During discharge, hot salt is sent through a **steam generator (salt-to-water HX)** to produce superheated steam for the turbine.

**Storage Capacity Equation:**

$$Q_{\text{stored}} = m_{\text{salt}} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$

For $m = 30{,}000$ tonnes of salt (a typical 100 MWe / 15 h storage system):

$$Q = 30 \times 10^6 \times 1.55 \times 275 = 1.28 \times 10^{10} \text{ kJ} = 3{,}560 \text{ MWh}_{\text{th}}$$

At 35% cycle efficiency, this yields about **1,250 MWh_e** of dispatchable electricity — enough to run a 100 MWe plant for **12.5 hours** through the night.

**4. Advantage of TES:**
- Decouples solar collection from electricity generation.
- Enables **24/7 dispatchable solar** (the "Gemasolar" model in Spain).
- Increases capacity factor from 25% (no storage) to 60 – 75% (with 12 h storage).

*(7 marks — 2 marks for HTF comparison table, 2 marks for two-tank TES description, 2 marks for storage capacity calculation, 1 mark for the 24/7 dispatchable solar conclusion.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning for Power Tower Questions:**
> 1. Do not write "heliostat" and "parabolic trough" interchangeably. Heliostats are flat 2-axis mirrors; parabolic troughs are curved 1-axis troughs.
> 2. When asked about the "main advantage of a power tower over a trough," answer **thermal energy storage at high temperature** — not just temperature.
> 3. The molten salt composition **60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃** (commonly called "solar salt") is a high-yield fact. Memorize it.
> 4. Do not forget to convert **kJ to kWh** using the 1 kWh = 3600 kJ conversion in storage capacity problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation**

- ☑ **Solar thermal ≠ Solar PV.** Thermal uses heat as an intermediate; PV does not.
- ☑ **CSP requires DNI (Direct Normal Irradiance), NOT GHI.** Troughs/dishes/towers cannot use diffuse radiation.
- ☑ **Concentration Ratio (C) = A_aperture / A_receiver.** This is the single most important parameter.
- ☑ **Maximum theoretical C for point focus = 1/sin²(θ_s) ≈ 45,000.** Real systems achieve 1000 – 5000.
- ☑ **Trough:** Line focus, C = 30 – 80, T ≈ 400 °C, Rankine cycle, mature technology.
- ☑ **Dish:** Point focus, C = 1000 – 5000, T ≈ 800 °C, Brayton/Stirling, highest efficiency (30%+) but pilot stage.
- ☑ **Power Tower:** Point focus (heliostats), C = 300 – 1500, T up to 1000 °C, molten salt storage, dispatchable.
- ☑ **Solar Pond:** No concentration, T ≈ 80 °C, ORC, niche application.
- ☑ **Optical efficiency = cos θ × ρ × τ × α × γ × D.** Six multiplicative terms.
- ☑ **Carnot efficiency = 1 − T_c/T_h** (temperatures in Kelvin, not Celsius).
- ☑ **Overall plant efficiency = η_optical × η_receiver × η_cycle × η_generator.**
- ☑ **Molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) is the dominant HTF for storage.** Operates 290 – 565 °C.
- ☑ **Two-tank TES gives dispatchable solar.** Hot tank at 565 °C, cold tank at 290 °C.
- ☑ **Dish-Stirling is the most efficient** at the unit level. **Trough is most deployed** globally.
- ☑ **Mirror reflectivity (ρ) ≈ 0.93;** **selective coating α ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.12** at operating temperature.
- ☑ **Selective coatings are characterized by high α/ε ratio** — high absorptance at solar wavelengths, low emittance at thermal IR wavelengths.
- ☑ **KTU 2024 standard values to remember:** Solar constant 1361 W/m², AM1.5 = 1000 W/m², σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴.
- ☑ **In exam problems, always write units.** W, m², °C or K. The KTU examiner deducts 0.5 marks per wrong or missing unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When a question gives you only one efficiency (e.g., "overall plant efficiency is 15%"), you can compute net output as $P_{out} = 0.15 \times A_a \times S$. When it gives you sub-efficiencies, multiply them all. When it asks "explain in detail," always use the **Block Diagram → Optical Mechanism → Heat Transfer → Cycle → Output** structure. The KTU 2024 valuation key rewards **structured, step-by-step answers** over scattered bullet points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photovoltaic Solar Cells: Types, Characteristics, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Photovoltaic Solar Cells: Types, Characteristics, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photovoltaic (PV) Effect**: The direct conversion of photon energy (light) into electrical energy through the generation of a voltage and current in a semiconductor material upon exposure to electromagnetic radiation. The term "photovoltaic" is derived from the Greek word *photos* (light) and the term *volt* (named after Alessandro Volta), the unit of electromotive force.

A **Photovoltaic (PV) Solar Cell** is fundamentally a semiconductor p-n junction diode designed and optimized to absorb solar photons and convert their energy directly into usable direct current (DC) electricity via the **photovoltaic effect**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - The photovoltaic effect was first observed by **Alexandre Edmond Becquerel in 1839**.
> - The first practical silicon PV cell was developed at **Bell Laboratories in 1954** by **Chapin, Fuller, and Pearson**, achieving an initial efficiency of approximately **6%**.
> - **Charles Fritts** constructed the first solar cell in **1883** using selenium wafers with a thin layer of gold.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Elevator of Electrons"**

Think of a PV cell as an **"Electron Elevator"** powered by light:

1. **The Sun** is the *power button* — without photons, nothing happens.
2. **A Photon** acts as a *passenger* that arrives at the semiconductor.
3. The **p-n junction** acts as the *elevator shaft* — it has a built-in electric field (the elevator mechanism).
4. When a photon with energy $\geq E_g$ (bandgap energy) strikes a silicon atom, it **knocks an electron loose** from the valence band into the conduction band. This is the passenger stepping into the elevator.
5. The **built-in electric field** at the p-n junction acts as the *elevator motor*, automatically sweeping the electron up to the n-side and the hole down to the p-side.
6. **Metallic contacts** (top and bottom) act as the *wiring* that lets the electron flow out of the cell, through an external load (a bulb, motor, or grid), and back — performing useful work on the way.

The key insight: **Light pushes the electron "uphill"** against the natural potential barrier, generating electrical energy from purely quantum mechanical action — no moving parts, no combustion, no noise.

### 1.3 Why Photovoltaic Technology Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Advantages of PV Technology (Board Exam Favorite):**
> - **No fuel cost** — sunlight is free and inexhaustible.
> - **No moving parts** → extremely high reliability, low maintenance, silent operation.
> - **Modular & scalable** — from milliwatts (calculators) to megawatts (solar farms).
> - **Long lifespan** — typically **25–30 years** with 80% performance guarantee.
> - **Zero emissions** during operation — a true green technology.
> - **Static installation** — easy rooftop and remote-area deployment.

The standard irradiance reference used worldwide for rating PV modules is:
- **STC (Standard Test Conditions)**: $G = 1000 \, W/m^2$, $T_{cell} = 25^\circ C$, $AM = 1.5$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Photovoltaic Effect (Step-by-Step Logic)

The PV cell works through a sequence of three fundamental physical phenomena:

**Step 1 — Photon Absorption (Generation):**
A photon of energy $E = hf$ strikes the semiconductor. If $E \geq E_g$ (bandgap energy), it is absorbed and creates an **electron-hole pair (EHP)** in the depletion region or within a diffusion length of it.
- For Silicon: $E_g = 1.12 \, eV$.
- Photons with $E < E_g$ pass through and are lost as **transmission loss**.
- Excess energy ($E - E_g$) is lost as **heat (thermalization loss)**.

**Step 2 — Charge Separation (Collection):**
The built-in potential $V_{bi}$ at the p-n junction (typically **0.6–0.7 V for silicon**) creates an electric field that sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side. This is **drift**, not diffusion.

**Step 3 — Current Extraction (External Circuit):**
Metallic front (grid fingers) and back contacts collect the separated charges. The flow of electrons through an external load constitutes the **photocurrent**.

### 2.2 The Ideal PV Cell — Shockley Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation — The I-V Characteristic of an Ideal PV Cell:**
> $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$
> This is the **single-diode model** of a solar cell and is the foundation for ALL subsequent analysis in KTU exams.

Where:
- $I$ = terminal current (A)
- $I_L$ = photo-generated current (A), proportional to irradiance $G$
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current (A)
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, C$ (electron charge)
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K$ (Boltzmann constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $n$ = ideality factor (**$n = 1$ to $2$**, closer to 1 for high-quality cells)

### 2.3 Real PV Cell — Single Diode with Resistive Losses

> [!NOTE]
> **Real PV Cell I-V Equation (Include $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$):**
> $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

- **Series Resistance $R_s$**: Caused by ohmic losses in metal contacts, semiconductor bulk, and interconnections. **Reduces fill factor**, flattens the "knee" of the I-V curve.
- **Shunt Resistance $R_{sh}$**: Caused by leakage current paths across the p-n junction. **Reduces $V_{oc}$**, tilts the horizontal part of the I-V curve.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for board exams on this topic. Memorize and master these.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Open-Circuit Voltage** $V_{oc}$ | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Volts (V) | Condition: $I = 0$ |
| 2 | **Short-Circuit Current** $I_{sc}$ | $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ | Amperes (A) | Condition: $V = 0$ |
| 3 | **Fill Factor (FF)** | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Dimensionless (0 < FF < 1) | Typical silicon: 0.7–0.85 |
| 4 | **Cell Conversion Efficiency** $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | $G$ = irradiance, $A$ = cell area |
| 5 | **Maximum Power** $P_{max}$ | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ | Watts (W) | At Maximum Power Point (MPP) |
| 6 | **Photo-current vs Irradiance** | $I_L = I_{L,ref} \cdot \dfrac{G}{G_{ref}}$ | A | Linear with irradiance $G$ |
| 7 | **Open-Circuit Voltage vs Temp.** | $\dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -2 \, mV/^\circ C$ (per cell) | V/°C | $V_{oc}$ **decreases** with temperature |
| 8 | **Current Density** $J$ | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | $A/m^2$ | Used to compare cells of different sizes |
| 9 | **Photon Energy** $E$ | $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | eV or J | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, J \cdot s$ |
| 10 | **Thermal Voltage** $V_T$ | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \, mV$ at 300 K | V | At 25°C |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse $I_L$ and $I_{sc}$. While $I_{sc} \approx I_L$ in a real cell, the exact relation is:
> $$I_{sc} = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\left(\frac{q I_{sc} R_s}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{I_{sc} R_s}{R_{sh}}$$
> For ideal cells, $I_{sc} = I_L$.

### 2.5 Effect of Irradiance and Temperature (High-Yield Board Topic)

**Effect of Irradiance $G$:**
- $I_{sc}$ varies **linearly** with $G$.
- $V_{oc}$ varies **logarithmically** with $G$ (small change).
- Net result: $P_{max}$ scales nearly linearly with $G$.

**Effect of Temperature $T$:**
- $I_{sc}$ increases **slightly** with temperature (small positive coefficient, $\approx +0.05\%/^\circ C$).
- $V_{oc}$ **decreases significantly** with temperature (negative coefficient, $\approx -2 \, mV/^\circ C$ per cell).
- Net result: Higher temperature → **lower efficiency**. A hot climate reduces PV output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This is why solar modules in tropical climates like Kerala (average cell temperature often $50^\circ C$ to $65^\circ C$) underperform their STC ratings by 10–20%. This justifies the need for proper module ventilation and BIPV (Building-Integrated PV) thermal design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Power Point (MPP) Condition

**Goal:** Prove that at the maximum power point, the condition $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ must hold.

> [!NOTE]
> The power delivered to the load is $P = V \cdot I$.

Starting with the **ideal single-diode equation**:
$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Multiplying by $V$ to get power:
$$P = V \cdot I = V \cdot I_L - V \cdot I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Taking the derivative with respect to $V$ and setting it to zero for the maximum:
$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I + V \cdot \frac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

Computing $\dfrac{dI}{dV}$:
$$\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{q I_0}{nkT} \exp\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)$$

Substituting back:
$$I_{mp} - V_{mp} \cdot \frac{q I_0}{nkT} \exp\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) = 0$$

Using the diode equation at MPP ($I = I_{mp}$, $V = V_{mp}$):
$$I_{mp} = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

After algebraic rearrangement, the **transcendental MPP condition** is:
$$\exp\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) \left( 1 + \frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT} \right) = 1 + \frac{I_L}{I_0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The MPP voltage $V_{mp}$ is the value of $V$ that satisfies this equation, which is solved numerically (Newton-Raphson) because it is **non-linear and transcendental**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Expression for Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{oc}$

**Goal:** Derive the expression for $V_{oc}$ in terms of $I_L$ and $I_0$.

**Step 1 — Apply the boundary condition $I = 0$ (open circuit):**
Setting $I = 0$ in the ideal diode equation:
$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:**
$$I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] = I_L$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $I_0$:**
$$\exp\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 = \frac{I_L}{I_0}$$

**Step 4 — Add 1 to both sides:**
$$\exp\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = 1 + \frac{I_L}{I_0}$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**
$$\frac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} = \ln\left(1 + \frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{oc}$:**
$$V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(1 + \frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (KTU Board Insight):** Since $I_L / I_0 \gg 1$ (typically $10^6$ to $10^{10}$), the "+1" inside the logarithm is often dropped for simplicity, giving the commonly used approximation:
> $$V_{oc} \approx \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right) = n V_T \ln\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature Dependence of $V_{oc}$

**Goal:** Show that $V_{oc}$ decreases with temperature.

From the derived expression:
$$V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$

The reverse saturation current $I_0$ has a strong temperature dependence (from semiconductor theory):
$$I_0 = A T^3 \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{kT}\right)$$

Where $A$ is a constant for a given cell. Substituting:
$$V_{oc} = \frac{nkT}{q} \left[ \ln(I_L) - \ln(A) - 3\ln(T) + \frac{E_g}{kT} \right]$$

Taking the derivative with respect to $T$:
$$\frac{dV_{oc}}{dT} = \frac{nk}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_L}{A T^3}\right) + \frac{nkT}{q} \cdot \left(-\frac{3}{T}\right) - \frac{E_g}{qT}$$

After simplification, the dominant term is:
$$\frac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -\frac{E_g - nkT\ln(I_L/A T^3)}{qT}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** For silicon, $\dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -2 \, mV/^\circ C$ **per cell**. This **negative temperature coefficient** is why PV modules lose efficiency in hot weather, even though they may receive more sunlight.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Efficiency Limit — Shockley–Queisser Limit

**Goal:** Derive the maximum theoretical efficiency of a single-junction solar cell.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Shockley–Queisser limit** is the maximum theoretical efficiency of a single p-n junction solar cell under unconcentrated sunlight. Calculated by William Shockley and Hans-Joachim Queisser in **1961**.

Consider a semiconductor with bandgap $E_g$. The fraction of incident solar spectrum with photon energy $\geq E_g$ is:
$$F(E_g) = \frac{\int_{E_g}^{\infty} N(E) \, dE}{\int_{0}^{\infty} N(E) \, dE}$$

Each useful photon delivers on average energy $\bar{E}$ between $E_g$ and $\infty$. The Carnot efficiency limit considering the cell at $T_c$ and sun at $T_s = 5778 \, K$:
$$\eta_{max} = \left(1 - \frac{T_c}{T_s}\right) \cdot F(E_g) \cdot \frac{\bar{E}}{E_g}$$

Optimizing over $E_g$, the maximum occurs at:
$$E_g \approx 1.34 \, eV \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta_{max} \approx 33.7\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The **Shockley–Queisser limit** for a single-junction cell under 1-sun illumination is approximately **33.7%**. This is a hard physics limit. To exceed it, multi-junction cells, concentration PV, or thermophotovoltaics are required. The best single-junction silicon cells today reach $\approx 26.7\%$, while multi-junction cells have achieved **47.1%** (concentrator, 2024 NREL record).

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A silicon solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.65 \, V$, $I_{sc} = 3.2 \, A$, $V_{mp} = 0.52 \, V$, $I_{mp} = 2.85 \, A$ at STC ($G = 1000 \, W/m^2$). The cell area is $A = 156 \, mm \times 156 \, mm = 0.02434 \, m^2$. Find: (a) Fill Factor, (b) Maximum Power, (c) Efficiency.

**(a) Fill Factor:**
$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{0.52 \times 2.85}{0.65 \times 3.2} = \frac{1.482}{2.080} = 0.7125$$

$$FF = 71.25\%$$

**(b) Maximum Power:**
$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 0.52 \times 2.85 = 1.482 \, W$$

**(c) Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{1.482}{1000 \times 0.02434} = \frac{1.482}{24.34} = 0.0609$$

$$\eta = 6.09\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This low efficiency reflects the early silicon technology of the 1970s. Modern monocrystalline silicon cells achieve 20–24% in commercial production.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Operational Principle of a PV Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight Photons h nu] --> B[Photon strikes Silicon p-n Junction Cell]
    B --> C{Is Photon Energy greater than or equal to Eg of 1.12 eV for Silicon}
    C -- No --> D[Photon passes through or is absorbed as heat Transmission Loss]
    C -- Yes --> E[Photon absorbed in depletion region or diffusion length]
    E --> F[Electron hole pair generated Electron to conduction band]
    F --> G[Built in electric field at junction sweeps Electron to n side]
    F --> H[Hole drifts toward p side]
    G --> I[Top metallic contact collects electrons]
    H --> J[Back metallic contact collects holes]
    I --> K[Electrons flow through external load doing useful work]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Direct Current DC electricity output]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Classification of PV Cell Technologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Photovoltaic Solar Cell Technologies] --> B[First Generation Crystalline Silicon]
    A --> C[Second Generation Thin Film]
    A --> D[Third Generation Emerging and Advanced]
    B --> B1[Monocrystalline Silicon m Si]
    B --> B2[Polycrystalline or Multicrystalline Silicon p Si]
    B --> B3[Single Crystal Gallium Arsenide GaAs]
    C --> C1[Amorphous Silicon a Si]
    C --> C2[Cadmium Telluride CdTe]
    C --> C3[Copper Indium Gallium Selenide CIGS]
    C --> C4[Copper Indium Selenide CIS]
    D --> D1[Dye Sensitized Solar Cells DSSC]
    D --> D2[Organic Photovoltaics OPV]
    D --> D3[Perovskite Solar Cells]
    D --> D4[Quantum Dot Solar Cells]
    D --> D5[Multi Junction Tandem Cells]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Equivalent Circuit of a Real PV Cell (Single-Diode Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IL[(IL Photogenerated Current Source)] --> N1
    N1 --> Rser[Rs Series Resistance]
    Rser --> N2
    N2 --> OUT((Terminal + V I))
    N2 --> D[Diode with reverse saturation current I0]
    D --> N3
    N1 --> Rsh[Rsh Shunt Resistance]
    Rsh --> N3
    N3 --> OUT2((Terminal -))
    OUT -. load .-> OUT2
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: I-V and P-V Characteristic Curves

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PV Cell Operating under STC conditions] --> B[Apply variable load resistor and sweep V from 0 to Voc]
    B --> C[Measure terminal current I at each V]
    C --> D[Plot I on Y axis vs V on X axis gives I-V Curve]
    C --> E[Compute P equals V into I and plot P-V Curve]
    D --> F[Identify Isc at V equals 0 axis intercept on current axis]
    D --> G[Identify Voc at I equals 0 axis intercept on voltage axis]
    E --> H[Locate Peak of P-V curve which is Maximum Power Point MPP]
    H --> I[Read Vmp and Imp at the peak]
    I --> J[Calculate Fill Factor FF equals Vmp Imp divided by Voc Isc]
    J --> K[Compute Efficiency eta equals Pmax divided by G into A]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Functional Block Architecture of a Complete PV System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solar PV Array Multiple modules in series and parallel] --> B[Junction Box with bypass and blocking diodes]
    B --> C[DC Disconnect Switch and Surge Protection]
    C --> D[MPPT Charge Controller or Inverter with MPPT]
    D --> E[DC Bus 12V 24V 48V or DC link]
    E --> F{Battery Bank in Off Grid systems OR AC Inverter in Grid Tied}
    F -- Battery path --> G[Deep Cycle Battery Storage]
    F -- Inverter path --> H[Pure Sine Wave Inverter DC to AC]
    G --> I[AC Load OR DC Load]
    H --> I
    H --> J[Grid Synchronization and Net Meter]
    J --> K[Utility Grid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Block-Level Explanation:**
> - The **PV Array** captures sunlight and produces DC.
> - **MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracker)** is a smart DC-DC converter that continuously operates the array at its MPP for maximum energy harvest (typical efficiency boost: 20–30%).
> - **Batteries** store energy for night/backup (off-grid systems).
> - **Inverter** converts DC to AC at grid frequency (50 Hz in India, used in Kerala).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **Define the photovoltaic effect. Mention any two advantages and two disadvantages of photovoltaic power generation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The photovoltaic effect is the phenomenon of generation of an electromotive force (voltage) when a semiconductor material is exposed to electromagnetic radiation (light). The energy of incident photons is directly converted into electrical energy at the atomic level via electron excitation across the bandgap.

**Two Advantages (1 mark):**
1. **No fuel cost and renewable** — sunlight is free, inexhaustible, and available everywhere.
2. **No moving parts** — highly reliable, silent operation, very low maintenance, modular, and scalable from mW to MW.

**Two Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. **High initial capital cost** — silicon cells and module manufacturing are energy-intensive and expensive.
2. **Intermittency / Low energy density** — output depends on solar irradiance (day/night, clouds), requiring storage or backup.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **List the three generations of solar photovoltaic cells with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per generation):**

1. **First Generation — Crystalline Silicon Cells:**
   Example: **Monocrystalline silicon (m-Si)** solar cells, made from single-crystal silicon ingots (Czochralski process). Highest commercial efficiency (20–24%) and longest lifespan (>25 years).

2. **Second Generation — Thin-Film Cells:**
   Example: **Cadmium Telluride (CdTe)** thin-film cells, where a 2–8 µm thick layer of CdTe is deposited on a glass substrate. Lower cost, flexible, and slightly lower efficiency (10–18%).

3. **Third Generation — Emerging/Advanced Cells:**
   Example: **Perovskite solar cells**, using hybrid organic-inorganic lead halide perovskite materials (e.g., methylammonium lead iodide, $CH_3NH_3PbI_3$). Rapidly improved efficiency (>26% in lab), low-cost fabrication, but stability issues.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Paper Pattern (KTU 2024):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Students must answer **ONE** out of two choices. Both questions here are independent of each other.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> (a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **crystalline silicon solar cell**. Discuss the I-V and P-V characteristics with the concept of Maximum Power Point. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Derive the expression for the **fill factor** of a solar cell. A silicon solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.62 \, V$, $I_{sc} = 3.0 \, A$, and the maximum power point occurs at $V_{mp} = 0.50 \, V$ and $I_{mp} = 2.70 \, A$. Calculate the **fill factor** and the **conversion efficiency** for a cell area of $156 \times 156 \, mm^2$ at $G = 1000 \, W/m^2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A typical crystalline silicon solar cell is a sandwich-like structure:
- **Substrate:** p-type silicon wafer (Boron-doped, ~200 µm thick).
- **Emitter layer:** Thin n-type layer (Phosphorus-doped, ~0.2–0.5 µm) formed on top by thermal diffusion.
- **Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC):** A thin layer of $Si_3N_4$ or $TiO_2$ (~70 nm) on top to reduce reflection losses (reflection drops from ~35% to ~2%).
- **Top contact:** Fine metallic fingers and a busbar (silver or aluminum) screen-printed on the top to collect current — designed to minimize shading (<5% area).
- **Back contact:** Aluminum layer covering the entire back surface.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Sunlight enters through the ARC.
- Photons with $E \geq 1.12 \, eV$ are absorbed in the depletion region or within a diffusion length, creating electron-hole pairs.
- The built-in electric field at the p-n junction (created by ionized donors/acceptors at the junction) sweeps electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side.
- The separated charges flow through the external load, producing the photocurrent.

**I-V and P-V Characteristics (2 marks):**
- The I-V curve is rectangular-shaped with $I_{sc}$ on the current axis and $V_{oc}$ on the voltage axis.
- The P-V curve has a single peak — the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.
- At MPP: $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$.
- The **Fill Factor (FF)** is the ratio of the largest rectangle that fits inside the I-V curve to the $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ rectangle.

**(b) Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation of Fill Factor (3 marks):**
The fill factor is defined as:
$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

This dimensionless quantity (0 < FF < 1) measures the "squareness" of the I-V curve. Higher FF means a better cell. For an ideal solar cell:
$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$
where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{q V_{oc}}{nkT}$ is the normalized open-circuit voltage. Typical values for silicon: **0.7 to 0.85**.

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Cell area:
$$A = 156 \times 156 \, mm^2 = 0.024336 \, m^2$$

**Fill Factor:**
$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{0.50 \times 2.70}{0.62 \times 3.0} = \frac{1.350}{1.860} = 0.7258$$

$$\boxed{FF = 0.726 \text{ or } 72.6\%}$$

**Maximum Power:**
$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 0.50 \times 2.70 = 1.35 \, W$$

**Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{1.35}{1000 \times 0.024336} = \frac{1.35}{24.336} = 0.0555$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 5.55\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to convert mm² to m² when computing area. **1 mark is lost** if area is left in mm². Always remember: $1 \, m^2 = 10^6 \, mm^2$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – May 2022, Adapted]**
> (a) Classify the different **types of solar cells** with examples. Compare **monocrystalline, polycrystalline, and amorphous silicon** cells in terms of efficiency, cost, and applications. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Explain any **five major applications** of photovoltaic solar cells. With a neat block diagram, describe a **standalone PV system** with battery backup for a remote home. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Generation | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1st Gen | Crystalline Silicon | Monocrystalline Si, Polycrystalline Si, GaAs |
| 2nd Gen | Thin-Film | Amorphous Si (a-Si), CdTe, CIGS, CIS |
| 3rd Gen | Emerging / Advanced | Dye-sensitized, Perovskite, Organic PV, Quantum Dot, Multi-junction |

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Monocrystalline Si | Polycrystalline Si | Amorphous Si (Thin-Film) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Crystal Structure** | Single continuous crystal | Multiple small crystals (grains) | No crystalline order |
| **Manufacturing** | Czochralski (energy-intensive) | Cast ingot (cheaper) | Vapor deposition on glass |
| **Efficiency** | **18 – 24%** | **14 – 18%** | **6 – 9%** |
| **Cost (per Wp)** | Highest | Medium | **Lowest** |
| **Area for 1 kW** | ~6 m² | ~7 m² | ~15 m² |
| **Temperature Tolerance** | Low | Medium | Better |
| **Lifespan** | 25–30 years | 20–25 years | 10–15 years |
| **Appearance** | Uniform black | Speckled blue | Brown / dark |
| **Typical Applications** | Rooftop, large-scale grid | Residential, commercial | Calculators, watches, BIPV, large solar farms |

**(b) Applications and Standalone PV System (7 marks):**

**Five Major Applications (2.5 marks):**
1. **Spacecraft Power Systems** — Every satellite (e.g., India's INSAT, Chandrayaan) uses solar PV cells, often multi-junction GaAs with 30%+ efficiency.
2. **Residential and Commercial Rooftop Systems** — Net-metered grid-connected PV for buildings, growing rapidly in Kerala.
3. **Solar Water Pumping** — PV-powered pumps for irrigation, replacing diesel pumps in rural India.
4. **Off-Grid Electrification** — Solar home systems (SHS) and solar lanterns for remote villages without grid access.
5. **Transportation** — Solar cars, boats, charging stations for electric vehicles, and PV-integrated road noise barriers.
6. **Consumer Electronics** — Solar-powered calculators, watches, mobile chargers.
7. **Utility-Scale Solar Farms** — Megawatt-scale power plants feeding into the grid (e.g., Kamuthi Solar Power Station, 648 MW in Tamil Nadu).

**Standalone PV System with Battery Backup (4.5 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A standalone (off-grid) PV system is independent of the utility grid and is used where grid power is unavailable or unreliable.

**Major Components:**
- **PV Array** — One or more modules sized to meet daily energy demand + storage margin.
- **Charge Controller with MPPT** — Regulates battery charging, prevents overcharge/deep discharge, and extracts maximum power from the array.
- **Battery Bank** — Deep-cycle lead-acid or lithium-ion batteries for energy storage (typically sized for 2–3 days of autonomy).
- **Inverter** — DC to AC converter (50 Hz, 230 V in India) for AC loads.
- **DC and AC Distribution** — Wiring, fuses, MCBs, and earthing.
- **Optional Backup** — Diesel generator for prolonged cloudy periods (hybrid system).

**Operating Logic:**
1. During sunlight: PV array charges the battery bank AND powers the load simultaneously.
2. At night or cloudy: Battery bank powers the load.
3. In deep discharge: Backup DG (if present) takes over.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Many students draw the block diagram of a **grid-tied** system when asked for a **standalone** system. Standalone systems MUST include a battery block, whereas grid-tied systems do not. Always read "standalone" carefully. **2 marks lost** for confusing the two architectures.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

**Core Concepts:**
- ✅ **Photovoltaic effect** = direct light-to-electricity conversion in a p-n junction.
- ✅ **Discovered by Becquerel (1839); first practical cell by Bell Labs (1954).**
- ✅ **Three generations:** Crystalline Si (1st) → Thin-Film (2nd) → Emerging/Advanced (3rd).
- ✅ **Working steps:** Absorption → EHP generation → Charge separation by built-in field → Current collection.

**Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
- ✅ $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$
- ✅ $I_{sc} \approx I_L \propto G$
- ✅ $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$
- ✅ $\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A} \times 100\%$
- ✅ $\dfrac{dV_{oc}}{dT} \approx -2 \, mV/^\circ C$ per cell (silicon)

**Characteristic Curves:**
- ✅ **I-V curve** is rectangular (high current at low voltage, low current at high voltage).
- ✅ **P-V curve** has a single peak — the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.
- ✅ **$V_{mp} \approx 0.8 \cdot V_{oc}$** and **$I_{mp} \approx 0.9 \cdot I_{sc}$** (approximate rules for silicon).

**Effect of Parameters:**
- ✅ **Irradiance ↑** → $I_{sc}$ ↑ linearly, $V_{oc}$ ↑ slightly, **Pmax ↑** nearly linearly.
- ✅ **Temperature ↑** → $V_{oc}$ ↓ significantly, $I_{sc}$ ↑ slightly, **Pmax ↓** (efficiency drops).
- ✅ **Series resistance $R_s$ ↑** → Fill Factor ↓, knee of I-V curve softens.
- ✅ **Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ ↓** → $V_{oc}$ ↓, slope of I-V curve increases.

**Standard Test Conditions (STC):**
- ✅ $G = 1000 \, W/m^2$, $T_{cell} = 25^\circ C$, Air Mass 1.5G spectrum.

**Theoretical Limits:**
- ✅ **Shockley-Queisser limit (single junction):** ~33.7% at $E_g = 1.34 \, eV$.
- ✅ **Best silicon cell (lab):** 26.7%; **Best multi-junction cell (lab):** 47.1%.
- ✅ **Commercial Si modules:** 18–22% efficiency.

**Applications — Quick List:**
- ✅ Spacecraft, Rooftop (grid-tied), Off-grid (standalone), Solar pumps, Transportation, Consumer electronics, Utility-scale solar farms, Solar water heaters with PV pumps, BIPV.

**Common Confusions to Avoid:**
- ❌ **PV effect ≠ Photoelectric effect.** PV effect is in *semiconductors*; Photoelectric effect is in *metals* (Einstein, 1905).
- ❌ **Solar cell ≠ Solar panel.** A *cell* is a single p-n junction (~0.5 V); a *module/panel* is many cells in series (~12–48 V); an *array* is many panels in series-parallel.
- ❌ **Efficiency of cell vs module vs system:** Cell > Module > System (due to mismatches, wiring, dust, temperature, inverter losses).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
