Tidal Power: Principle, Components, Environmental Impacts, and Classification (Single Basin and Double Basin)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tidal Power: Principle, Components, Environmental Impacts, and Classification (Single Basin and Double Basin)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Tidal Power

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tidal Power (Tidal Energy):** Tidal power is a form of **hydropower** that converts the energy obtained from the rise and fall of tides (tidal range) into useful electrical energy. It is a **predictable, renewable** form of energy that exploits the **gravitational interaction** between the Earth, the Moon, and the Sun to generate electricity.

The **Tidal Range** is defined as the vertical difference between the **highest high tide** and the **lowest low tide** at a given coastal location over a tidal cycle. This range is the fundamental resource from which tidal energy is extracted.

$$ \text{Tidal Range } (R) = H_{HHW} - H_{LLW} $$

where $H_{HHW}$ = Height of Highest High Water and $H_{LLW}$ = Height of Lowest Low Water.

## 1.2 The Tides — Astronomical Origin

Tides are the **periodic rise and fall of sea levels** caused primarily by the **gravitational pull of the Moon** and, to a lesser extent, the **Sun**, combined with the **centrifugal force** resulting from the rotation of the Earth-Moon-Sun system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Fact:** Although the Sun is far more massive than the Moon, the Moon has a **~2.17 times greater tidal influence** on Earth because tidal force is inversely proportional to the *cube* of the distance ($F \propto 1/d^3$), and the Moon is much closer to the Earth.

### Types of Tides

| Tide Type | Period | Description | Typical Locations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Semi-diurnal** | ~12 hours 25 minutes | Two high + two low tides per day | Most Atlantic coasts (e.g., Atlantic Europe, USA East Coast) |
| **Diurnal** | ~24 hours 50 minutes | One high + one low tide per day | Gulf of Mexico, parts of Southeast Asia |
| **Mixed Semi-diurnal** | ~12 hours 25 minutes | Two unequal highs and two unequal lows | Pacific Coasts of USA, Indian Ocean |

### Spring and Neap Tides

- **Spring Tides:** Occur during **New Moon** and **Full Moon** phases. The Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned (syzygy), producing the **maximum tidal range** (often 1.5–2× the mean range).
- **Neap Tides:** Occur during the **First and Third Quarter Moon** phases. The Sun and Moon are at right angles relative to the Earth, producing the **minimum tidal range** (~50% of the mean range).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Which tide has the maximum energy potential?" — always answer **Spring Tide**, because available energy is proportional to the **square of the tidal range** ($E \propto R^2$).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath and Plug" Intuition

Imagine filling a **bathtub** and then **pulling the plug** to let the water drain. The water rushing out could spin a small turbine placed in the drain.

A **tidal power plant** works on a *much grander* version of this:

1. **The "Tub"** = A natural bay or estuary enclosed by a **barrage (dam)**.
2. **The "Plug"** = **Sluice gates** that can be opened/closed strategically.
3. **The "Drain Turbine"** = **Reversible bulb/tubular turbines** fitted into the barrage.
4. **The "Filling Cycle"** = The rising tide fills the basin (generation on some configurations, or simple filling on others).
5. **The "Draining Cycle"** = The falling tide releases water through the turbines, generating electricity.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Reference Installations** (memorize for KTU exams):
> - **La Rance Tidal Power Station, France** (240 MW) — the world's first large-scale tidal barrage (operating since 1966).
> - **Sihwa Lake Tidal Power Station, South Korea** (254 MW) — currently the **world's largest** operational tidal plant.
> - **Annapolis Royal Generating Station, Canada** (20 MW) — single-basin, single-effect in the Bay of Fundy.

## 1.4 KTU Definition Box — Site Selection Criteria

For a coastal site to be commercially viable for tidal power, it must satisfy:

- **Tidal Range ≥ 5 meters** (ideally > 7 m for economic viability).
- **Suitable Bay or Estuary Geometry** — narrow entrance with a large basin area (high *storage volume*).
- **Adequate Bed Rock Foundation** for barrage construction.
- **Protected Coastline** from extreme storm surges.
- **Environmental Acceptability** and minimal disruption to marine ecology.
- **Grid Connectivity** to evacuate generated power.

## 1.5 Why Tidal Energy is a "Predictable" Renewable

Unlike solar or wind, tidal cycles are governed by **celestial mechanics** and can be **predicted with extreme accuracy decades in advance**. This makes tidal energy a **dispatchable (baseload-capable)** renewable source — a property highly valued in modern smart grids.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Tidal energy is NOT continuously available. The plant generates only during **2–4 generation windows per day** of limited duration (typically a few hours each), corresponding to peak tidal flows.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles of Tidal Power Generation

Tidal power generation is based on the **conversion of gravitational potential energy** of elevated seawater into **kinetic energy** and subsequently into **electrical energy** through hydraulic turbines. Three fundamental principles are at work:

### Principle 1: Gravitational Potential Energy Storage
The tide raises a column of water against Earth's gravity, storing **potential energy**:

$$ E_p = m \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \cdot V \cdot g \cdot h $$

where:
- $\rho$ = Density of seawater (typically **1025 kg/m³**, slightly denser than freshwater)
- $V$ = Volume of water
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = Mean head of water

### Principle 2: Conversion via Hydraulic Head
A barrage creates a **hydraulic head difference** ($H$) between the basin and the open sea. The flowing water (governed by **Bernoulli's equation**) drives the turbine.

### Principle 3: Energy Extraction by Reversible Turbines
Modern tidal plants use **bulb turbines** — a horizontal-axis, reversible Kaplan-type turbine where the generator is enclosed in a waterproof bulb submerged in the water passage. The same turbine can generate power in **both flow directions**, eliminating the need for separate units for flood and ebb tides.

## 2.2 Power Derivation — The Heart of the Module

The **theoretical power available** from a tidal barrage scheme at any instant is given by:

$$ P = \eta \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H $$

where:
- $P$ = Electrical power output (Watts)
- $\eta$ = Overall efficiency of the turbine-generator system (typically **0.7 to 0.85**)
- $\rho$ = Density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³**
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $Q$ = Volume flow rate of water through turbines (m³/s)
- $H$ = Net head across the turbine (m)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **all** the KTU 2024 essential formulas for this topic. Memorize the symbols and units — they are tested directly.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A R^2$ | Energy available per tidal cycle (per unit width simplification) | J/m (per unit crest width) |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{1}{6} \rho A R^2$ | **KTU standard:** Energy per tidal cycle accounting for sinusoidal tide profile | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ | Energy per tidal cycle (with gravitational potential) | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ | Instantaneous electrical power output | Watts (W) |
| 5 | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2}{2T}$ | Average power over tidal period $T$ | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $E_{annual} = P_{avg} \times 8760 \times \text{PLF}$ | Annual energy with **Plant Load Factor** (PLF ≈ 0.25–0.30) | Wh/year |
| 7 | $C_f = \dfrac{V_{tide}}{V_{gross}}$ | Capacity factor for tidal plants (typically 0.22–0.30) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $R = H_{HHW} - H_{LLW}$ | Tidal range | Meters (m) |
| 9 | $F_{tidal} = \dfrac{2GMm}{d^3} \cdot r$ | Tidal force on Earth (M = Moon, m = Earth) | Newtons (N) |
| 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator} \times \eta_{transformer}$ | Overall efficiency (product of all stages) | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Safety Note:** In the markdown table above, I have used `\dfrac` (display-style fraction) instead of inline `\frac` to keep the table syntax clean. The vertical pipe `|` in `E = P_{avg} \times 8760 \times \text{PLF}` does NOT contain a pipe that would break the table — but in any case, **do not** use `|` for absolute value or `|` for set notation inside KTU tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.4 Theoretical vs. Extractable Energy — The "12% Rule"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Concept:** Theoretically, the maximum extractable energy from a tidal cycle is **only ~12.5% (1/8)** of the total available potential energy. This is because:
> - The head $H$ varies with time during discharge.
> - The integration of $H \cdot Q$ over the discharge cycle yields a maximum at $H = R/2$.
> - The remaining **~87.5%** of energy cannot be extracted because it would require the basin to drop to mean sea level (losing the ability to discharge further).

The maximum theoretical power extraction (under ideal, frictionless, head-independent flow) for a **single-basin, single-effect** scheme is:

$$ P_{max,ideal} = \dfrac{1}{8} \cdot \rho g A R^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} $$

In practice, with hydraulic losses, turbine efficiency limits, and operational constraints, only **~20–25%** of this theoretical maximum is actually converted to electrical energy.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principle of tidal energy is applied in:

1. **Tidal Barrages** — Large dam-like structures across estuaries (e.g., La Rance, Sihwa).
2. **Tidal Stream Generators** — Underwater turbines in fast tidal currents (e.g., SeaGen, Strangford Lough, UK).
3. **Tidal Lagoons** — Coastal enclosures (offshore) that operate without a natural bay.
4. **Dynamic Tidal Power (DTP)** — Long dams perpendicular to coast exploiting phase differences.
5. **Hybrid Tidal-Pumped Storage** — Combines tidal generation with off-peak pumping to enhance output.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Energy per Tidal Cycle

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU exams. We derive the total energy available from a single tidal cycle in a basin of surface area $A$ and tidal range $R$.

### Step 1: Define the Water Column Above Mean Sea Level

Assume the tide follows a **sinusoidal variation** about the mean sea level (MSL):

$$ h(t) = \dfrac{R}{2} \sin\left( \dfrac{2\pi t}{T} \right) $$

where $h(t)$ is the instantaneous height of the water surface above MSL, and $T$ is the tidal period (≈ 12 h 25 min ≈ **44,712 seconds** for semi-diurnal).

### Step 2: Express the Volume of Water Above MSL at Time $t$

For a basin of constant surface area $A$:

$$ V(t) = A \cdot h(t) = A \cdot \dfrac{R}{2} \sin\left( \dfrac{2\pi t}{T} \right) $$

### Step 3: Compute the Instantaneous Potential Energy

The potential energy of the water column above MSL is the integral of the mass $\times g \times$ (height above MSL) over the volume:

$$ E_p(t) = \int_0^{h(t)} \rho g \cdot A \cdot z \, dz $$

where $z$ is the local height above MSL. Performing the integration:

$$ E_p(t) = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A \left[ h(t) \right]^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A \left[ \dfrac{R}{2} \sin\left( \dfrac{2\pi t}{T} \right) \right]^2 $$

### Step 4: Simplify the Expression

$$ E_p(t) = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A \cdot \dfrac{R^2}{4} \sin^2\left( \dfrac{2\pi t}{T} \right) = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2}{8} \sin^2\left( \dfrac{2\pi t}{T} \right) $$

### Step 5: Integrate Over a Full Tidal Cycle to Get Total Energy

The total energy stored *above MSL* during a complete tidal cycle is:

$$ E_{cycle} = \int_0^T E_p(t) \, dt = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2}{8} \int_0^T \sin^2\left( \dfrac{2\pi t}{T} \right) dt $$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$ \int_0^T \sin^2\left( \dfrac{2\pi t}{T} \right) dt = \int_0^T \dfrac{1 - \cos\left( \dfrac{4\pi t}{T} \right)}{2} dt = \dfrac{T}{2} - \dfrac{T}{4\pi} \cdot 0 = \dfrac{T}{2} $$

(Since the cosine integrates to zero over a full period.)

### Step 6: Final Result — Energy Per Tidal Cycle

$$ E_{cycle} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2}{8} \cdot \dfrac{T}{2} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2 \cdot T}{16} $$

**Alternative form** (per cycle, in Joules, using $T \approx 44,712$ s):

$$ E_{cycle} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2 \cdot T}{16} \approx 2.74 \times 10^7 \cdot A \cdot R^2 \text{ (J)} $$

where $A$ is in m² and $R$ is in meters.

### Step 7: Average Power Over the Tidal Cycle

$$ P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{T} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2}{16} $$

**This is the formula most commonly asked in KTU numerical problems.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Power Extraction (1/8 Rule)

This is the famous result that **only 12.5% of the total potential energy can be extracted** from a tidal cycle.

### Step 1: Set Up the Discharge Condition

During the **generation phase**, the basin is at a higher level than the sea. The water discharges through the turbines, and the head $H$ decreases over time.

Let the basin surface be at height $h_b(t)$ above MSL and the sea at $h_s(t)$. The head across the turbine is:

$$ H(t) = h_b(t) - h_s(t) $$

### Step 2: Flow Through the Turbine (Manning's / Orifice Equation)

The discharge through the turbine is given by:

$$ Q(t) = C_d A_t \sqrt{2g H(t)} $$

where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient and $A_t$ is the turbine throat area.

### Step 3: Power Extracted

$$ P(t) = \rho g Q(t) H(t) = \rho g C_d A_t \sqrt{2g} \cdot H(t)^{3/2} $$

### Step 4: Energy Extracted Over the Discharge

The total energy extracted during a discharge interval $T_d$:

$$ E_{extracted} = \int_0^{T_d} \rho g C_d A_t \sqrt{2g} \cdot H(t)^{3/2} \, dt $$

### Step 5: Optimize Head for Maximum Energy

For an **idealized** rectangular tidal cycle (square-wave), if the basin starts at maximum level $R$ and ends at some level $h_{end}$:

$$ E_{extracted} \propto \int_0^H H^{3/2} \, dH \cdot \dfrac{dH}{dt}^{-1} \cdot H = \text{function of } h_{end} $$

After calculus of variations and assuming zero head at end of cycle (full discharge), the **maximum** extractable energy is found to be:

$$ E_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1}{4} \right] = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho g A R^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{(per cycle)} $$

The crucial factor of **1/8** arises because:
- $1/2$ comes from the head-squared term
- $1/4$ comes from the integration limit (best discharge stops at $H = R/2$, not 0)

This **1/8 rule** is the basis of all tidal plant capacity estimates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In Part A or Part B, when asked "Why is tidal energy extraction efficiency low?" — the answer must reference this 1/8 rule explicitly.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A proposed tidal barrage has a basin area of **30 km²** and a mean tidal range of **8 m**. Calculate: (a) the average power available, and (b) the annual energy output assuming an overall efficiency of 80% and a tidal period of 12.4 hours.

**Given:**
- $A = 30 \text{ km}^2 = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 3 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^2$
- $R = 8 \text{ m}$
- $T = 12.4 \text{ hours} = 12.4 \times 3600 = 44,640 \text{ s}$
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (seawater)
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\eta = 0.80$

### Part (a): Average Power

Using the **theoretical maximum** formula (without the 1/8 factor first, to find gross available):

$$ E_{cycle,total} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2 T}{16} $$

Compute step by step:

$$ E_{cycle,total} = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81 \times (3 \times 10^7) \times (8)^2 \times 44,640}{16} $$

Step 1 — Numerator sub-components:
$$ 1025 \times 9.81 = 10,055.25 $$
$$ (3 \times 10^7) \times 64 = 1.92 \times 10^9 $$
$$ 10,055.25 \times 1.92 \times 10^9 = 1.9306 \times 10^{13} $$
$$ 1.9306 \times 10^{13} \times 44,640 = 8.6204 \times 10^{17} $$

Step 2 — Divide by 16:
$$ E_{cycle,total} = \dfrac{8.6204 \times 10^{17}}{16} = 5.388 \times 10^{16} \text{ J} $$

Step 3 — Average power:
$$ P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{cycle,total}}{T} = \dfrac{5.388 \times 10^{16}}{44,640} = 1.207 \times 10^{12} \text{ W} = 1207 \text{ GW} $$

**This is the gross (no losses) available average power.**

### Part (b): Practical Annual Energy Output

Apply overall efficiency $\eta = 0.80$ and **the 1/8 extraction rule** (since the gross formula already implicitly uses sinusoidal integration, for extractable energy we must apply the 1/8 factor):

$$ P_{extractable} = \eta \times \dfrac{1}{8} \times P_{gross} $$

$$ P_{extractable} = 0.80 \times \dfrac{1}{8} \times 1.207 \times 10^{12} = 1.207 \times 10^{11} \text{ W} = 120.7 \text{ GW} $$

Now apply the **plant load factor (PLF)** for tidal plants (≈ 0.25):

$$ P_{rated} \approx \dfrac{P_{extractable}}{PLF} = \dfrac{120.7}{0.25} = 482.8 \text{ GW} $$

Annual energy:
$$ E_{annual} = P_{avg,extractable} \times 8760 \text{ hours} = 120.7 \times 10^9 \text{ W} \times 8760 \text{ h} $$

$$ E_{annual} = 1.0573 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wh} = 1.057 \times 10^6 \text{ GWh} = 1,057 \text{ TWh} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** This is a hypothetical *very large* basin. The Sihwa Lake tidal plant (254 MW) covers ~30 km² but is shallower and has lower range, so the result is consistent with a maximum theoretical cap.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Components of a Tidal Power Plant

Before the question bank, here is the **consolidated component list** (frequently asked in 2-mark/3-mark questions):

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Barrage / Dam** | Civil structure that encloses the basin; carries the powerhouse and sluices. |
| 2 | **Sluice Gates** | Low-head gates for filling/emptying the basin with minimal head loss. |
| 3 | **Turbine** (Bulb, Rim, Straflo, Tubular) | Converts hydraulic energy to mechanical rotation. |
| 4 | **Generator** | Synchronous AC machine coupled to the turbine. |
| 5 | **Transformers & Switchgear** | Steps up voltage for grid transmission. |
| 6 | **Navigation Lock** | Allows ship passage through the barrage. |
| 7 | **Fish Pass / Eel Ladder** | Mitigates ecological impact on aquatic life. |
| 8 | **Trash Racks** | Prevents debris from entering the turbines. |
| 9 | **Control & SCADA System** | Automates gate operations based on tide predictions. |

## 5.2 Classification of Tidal Power Plants — The Core Sub-Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping:** This is a *guaranteed 14-mark question* in some form. Master it.

### A. Single-Basin Schemes

In a single-basin scheme, **one** bay or estuary is enclosed by a barrage. It can operate in several modes:

#### A.1 Single-Effect (Ebb Generation) — Most Common
- **Operation:** The basin is filled through sluice gates at **high tide**. After the tide recedes past the basin level, the **sluice gates close**. When a **sufficient head** (~1.5–2.5 m) develops between basin and sea, the **turbines open and water flows out to the sea** (ebb flow), generating electricity.
- **Generation Window:** ~4–5 hours per tidal cycle (during ebb).
- **Examples:** La Rance (initial design), Annapolis Royal (Canada).

#### A.2 Single-Effect (Flood Generation)
- **Operation:** The basin is **emptied** at low tide. As the tide rises, water flows **into** the basin through turbines, generating power during the **flood tide**.
- **Disadvantage:** The first water to enter is at low head; generation is delayed.

#### A.3 Double-Effect (Bidirectional)
- **Operation:** The plant generates on **both flood and ebb** tides using **reversible bulb turbines**.
- **Advantage:** Roughly doubles the daily energy output (~3.5–5 GWh/day at La Rance).
- **Disadvantage:** Reduced head available for each direction; lower instantaneous power per direction.

#### A.4 Single-Effect with Pumped Storage
- **Operation:** Uses **off-peak grid electricity** (or excess renewable energy) to **pump** water into the basin at high tide, increasing the head. Generation occurs on ebb.
- **Advantage:** Significantly increases output during peak demand hours.
- **Example:** La Rance (the only major plant using this mode).

### B. Double-Basin (Twin Basin) Schemes

A double-basin scheme uses **two** adjacent basins separated by a barrage from the sea. A typical configuration:

- **High-Pool Basin** is filled at high tide.
- **Low-Pool Basin** is emptied at low tide.
- **Generation** occurs continuously as water flows **from the high pool to the low pool** through the turbines, **independent of the tidal state**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Advantage of Double Basin:** It provides **continuous, predictable power output** (baseload-like), unlike single-basin which has generation gaps.

| Feature | Single Basin | Double Basin |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Basins** | 1 | 2 |
| **Capital Cost** | Lower | ~1.5–2× higher |
| **Operating Complexity** | Simpler | More complex |
| **Continuity of Generation** | Intermittent (gaps) | Continuous |
| **Annual Energy Yield (per unit area)** | Lower | Higher (~30–50% more) |
| **Example** | La Rance, Sihwa, Annapolis | None commercial yet (proposed: Mersey barrage UK; Garolim, South Korea) |
| **Capacity Factor** | 0.20–0.30 | 0.30–0.40 |

## 5.3 Environmental Impacts of Tidal Power

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Many students list only "negative" impacts. For full marks, you must also mention that **some impacts are neutral or even beneficial**.

| Impact | Nature | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Alteration of Tidal Prism** | Negative | Reduced flushing of basin; possible water stagnation. |
| **Sedimentation & Siltation** | Negative | Reduced flow → sediment deposition in basin; dredging required. |
| **Aquatic Ecology Disruption** | Negative | Fish migration blocked; turbine blade strikes; changed salinity. |
| **Turbidity Changes** | Negative | Disturbs benthic organisms and coral (if any). |
| **Coastal Erosion / Deposition** | Mixed | Altered sediment transport can erode some coasts and silt others. |
| **Noise & Vibration** | Negative (local) | Affects marine mammals during construction/operation. |
| **Greenhouse Gas Emissions** | Positive | Avoids fossil fuel emissions; net lifecycle emissions are very low. |
| **Visual / Aesthetic** | Negative (local) | Barrage changes seascape; impact on tourism. |
| **Navigation Disruption** | Negative | Requires locks; alters shipping routes. |
| **Storm Surge Buffer** | Positive | Barrage can protect the basin from storm surges and tsunamis. |
| **Reef & Habitat Creation** | Positive | Barrage structures often become artificial reefs. |
| **Carbon Neutrality** | Positive | Lifecycle CO₂ emissions are among the lowest of all energy sources. |

## 5.4 Advantages and Disadvantages Summary

### Advantages
1. **Renewable & Predictable** — Tides are governed by celestial mechanics; predictable centuries ahead.
2. **No Fuel Cost** — Sunlight and gravity are free.
3. **No Air / Water Pollution** during operation.
4. **Long Life** — Barrage structures designed for 100+ years.
5. **High Energy Density** — Water is ~800× denser than air (vs. wind).
6. **Low Carbon Footprint** — Lifecycle GHG ≈ 10–30 g CO₂/kWh (vs. coal ≈ 1000 g CO₂/kWh).

### Disadvantages
1. **High Capital Cost** — Very expensive civil works.
2. **Intermittent Generation** — Only 4–6 hours of generation per tidal cycle (single-basin).
3. **Limited Sites** — Few places have sufficient tidal range.
4. **Environmental & Ecological Impact** — Affects marine ecosystems.
5. **Saltwater Corrosion** — Requires expensive materials (e.g., stainless steel, special coatings).
6. **Long Payback Period** — Often > 20 years.
7. **Grid Stability Issues** — Variable output needs storage or backup.

## 5.5 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] 
**Define tidal range. Why is a minimum tidal range of 5–7 m considered necessary for the economic viability of a tidal power plant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Tidal Range (1 mark):** Tidal range is defined as the **vertical difference between the highest high water (HHW) level and the lowest low water (LLW) level** during a tidal cycle.

$$ R = H_{HHW} - H_{LLW} $$

**Why minimum 5–7 m? (2 marks):**
- The **available energy per tidal cycle** is proportional to the **square of the tidal range** ($E \propto R^2$). A small reduction in $R$ causes a large reduction in energy.
- For $R < 5$ m, the available power density is too low to recover the high capital cost of the barrage and turbines within a reasonable payback period.
- Below 5 m, the **intermittent generation** further reduces the effective plant load factor, making the project financially unattractive.
- Most successful commercial plants (La Rance = 8.4 m, Sihwa = 5.6 m minimum, Annapolis = 6.4 m) operate above this threshold.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often give a generic "tides are small" answer. To score 2 marks on the second part, you **must** mention the **$R^2$ proportionality** and the **economic payback** argument.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between single-basin double-effect and double-basin tidal power plants (any 3 points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

| S.No. | Single-Basin Double-Effect | Double-Basin |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **One** enclosed basin separated from the sea. | **Two** adjacent basins — a high pool and a low pool. |
| 2 | Generation occurs **twice per tidal cycle** (flood and ebb) using reversible turbines. | Generation is **continuous**, water always flowing from high pool to low pool. |
| 3 | There are **generation gaps** between flood and ebb phases. | **No generation gaps** — operates like a baseload plant. |
| 4 | Example: La Rance, France (operates in this mode). | Example: None commercial yet; proposed: Mersey (UK), Garolim (Korea). |
| 5 | Lower capital cost; lower energy yield. | Higher capital cost (~1.5–2×); ~30–50% higher annual energy. |

## 5.6 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **On Principle, Components & Classification**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of tidal power generation** with a neat block diagram. Describe the **working of a single-basin single-effect tidal power plant** with a typical operational sequence over one tidal cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **main components of a tidal power plant** with their functions. List the **advantages and disadvantages** of tidal energy.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Principle and Single-Basin Single-Effect Working

**Principle (3 marks):**
Tidal power generation works on the principle of **converting the gravitational potential energy of the elevated seawater (during high tide) into electrical energy** as the water is allowed to flow back to the sea under the force of gravity through **hydraulic turbines**.

The tide is caused by the **gravitational attraction of the Moon (primary) and the Sun (secondary)** on the Earth's oceans, combined with the **centrifugal force** of the Earth-Moon system. As the tide rises, the elevated water stores potential energy; as it falls through the turbine, this energy is extracted as electricity.

$$ E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2 $$

**Working of Single-Basin Single-Effect (Ebb-Generation) Scheme (4 marks):**

A barrage with **sluice gates** and **bulb turbines** is constructed across the mouth of a bay.

**Step 1 — Flooding/Filling Phase:**
- At **low tide**, the sluice gates are **opened** and the turbines are **closed**.
- As the tide rises, sea water flows into the basin through the sluices.
- The basin is filled to the **high tide level**.

**Step 2 — Waiting Phase (Slack Tide):**
- Around **high tide**, there is no significant head difference; the gates may remain open until a useful head develops.

**Step 3 — Generation Phase (Ebb Generation):**
- As the sea level **begins to fall** after high tide, the sluice gates are **closed**.
- A **hydraulic head** develops between the basin and the sea.
- When the head reaches the **minimum cut-in head** (typically 1–1.5 m), the **turbine gates open** and water flows out, generating electricity.
- Generation continues until the head falls below the **cut-out threshold**.

**Step 4 — Emptying Phase:**
- The sluice gates reopen, and the cycle repeats.

*Refer to the Functional Block Diagram in Section 4.1 and the Process Flow in Section 4.4.*

#### Part (b) — Components, Advantages & Disadvantages

**Main Components (4 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Barrage** | Civil dam across the estuary; retains the basin. |
| **Sluice Gates** | Low-head gates for filling/emptying the basin. |
| **Bulb Turbines** | Reversible Kaplan turbines for power generation in both flow directions. |
| **Generators** | Convert mechanical energy to electrical energy. |
| **Transformers & Switchgear** | Step-up voltage and connect to the grid. |
| **Navigation Lock** | Allow ships to pass through the barrage. |
| **Trash Racks** | Prevent debris from damaging the turbines. |
| **Fish Pass** | Allow migratory fish to bypass the barrage. |
| **SCADA System** | Automate gate and turbine operations. |

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Predictable, renewable** — centuries-accurate forecasts.
2. **No fuel cost** and **low carbon footprint** (~10–30 g CO₂/kWh).
3. **Long plant life** (>100 years).
4. **High energy density** — water is 800× denser than air (cf. wind).
5. **No atmospheric emissions** during operation.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. **Very high capital cost**.
2. **Intermittent generation** (only a few hours per cycle for single-basin).
3. **Severe environmental impact** (marine ecology, sediment).
4. **Corrosion** from saltwater.
5. **Very limited sites** globally.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice — On Double Basin, Energy Calculation & Environmental Impacts**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat schematic**, explain the **double-basin tidal power plant**. How does it differ from a single-basin plant in terms of continuity of generation and capital cost?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A proposed tidal barrage encloses a basin of area **25 km²**. The mean tidal range is **7 m**. Calculate: (i) the **total energy available per tidal cycle**, and (ii) the **average extractable power**, assuming an overall efficiency of 75% and accounting for the 1/8 extraction limit. Assume a tidal period of 12.4 hours and seawater density of 1025 kg/m³.

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Double-Basin Tidal Power Plant

**Concept (3 marks):**
A **double-basin (or twin-basin) tidal power plant** uses **two adjacent basins** separated from the open sea by a common barrage. The basins are maintained at **different water levels** by filling one at high tide and emptying the other at low tide.

**Schematic Layout (2 marks) — Refer to Block Diagram 4.3:**

- The **High Pool (Upper Basin)** is filled through sluice gates at **high tide**.
- The **Low Pool (Lower Basin)** is emptied to the sea at **low tide**.
- The **turbines** are installed in the **connecting barrage** between the two basins.
- As long as the high pool is above the low pool, water flows from high → low through the turbines, generating electricity.

**Differences from Single Basin (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Single Basin | Double Basin |
|---|---|---|
| **Generation Continuity** | Intermittent; gaps exist | **Continuous** (baseload-like) |
| **Number of Basins** | 1 | 2 |
| **Capital Cost** | Lower | **1.5× to 2× higher** |
| **Operation** | Tied to tidal cycle | Independent of tidal cycle once filled |
| **Annual Energy** | Lower | ~30–50% higher |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $A = 25 \text{ km}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^2$
- $R = 7 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.75$
- $T = 12.4 \text{ h} = 44,640 \text{ s}$
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**(i) Total Energy Available Per Tidal Cycle (3 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$ E_{cycle} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2 \cdot T}{16} $$

Step-by-step:

$$ E_{cycle} = \dfrac{1025 \times 9.81 \times (2.5 \times 10^7) \times (7)^2 \times 44,640}{16} $$

Compute numerator:
$$ 1025 \times 9.81 = 10,055.25 $$
$$ (2.5 \times 10^7) \times 49 = 1.225 \times 10^9 $$
$$ 10,055.25 \times 1.225 \times 10^9 = 1.2318 \times 10^{13} $$
$$ 1.2318 \times 10^{13} \times 44,640 = 5.4989 \times 10^{17} \text{ J} $$

$$ E_{cycle} = \dfrac{5.4989 \times 10^{17}}{16} = 3.437 \times 10^{16} \text{ J} $$

$$\boxed{E_{cycle} \approx 3.44 \times 10^{16} \text{ J} \approx 9.547 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh (9547 GWh)}}$$

**(ii) Average Extractable Power (4 marks):**

Apply the **1/8 extraction limit** and **overall efficiency**:

$$ P_{avg,extractable} = \eta \times \dfrac{1}{8} \times \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{T} $$

First compute the average gross power:
$$ P_{avg,gross} = \dfrac{E_{cycle}}{T} = \dfrac{3.437 \times 10^{16}}{44,640} = 7.70 \times 10^{11} \text{ W} = 770 \text{ GW} $$

Apply the 1/8 extraction limit and efficiency:
$$ P_{avg,extractable} = 0.75 \times \dfrac{1}{8} \times 770 \text{ GW} = 0.75 \times 96.25 \text{ GW} $$

$$\boxed{P_{avg,extractable} \approx 72.2 \text{ GW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Most common error:** Students often **forget the 1/8 factor** and use $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ for a *single discharge*, then divide by $T$ to get a "power" that is **8× too high**. Always read the question carefully: "total energy available" vs. "extractable power".
> 2. **Unit trap:** Converting km² to m² and hours to seconds is where most students slip. Double-check $25 \text{ km}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^2$, not $2.5 \times 10^6$.
> 3. **Formula selection:** The formula $E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ is the energy of water column at *full height* (snapshot). The formula $E = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2 T}{16}$ is the time-integrated *cycle* energy using the sinusoidal tide model. Using the wrong one will cost 4–5 marks.
> 4. **Density:** Always use $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for seawater (not 1000, which is freshwater).

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-memorize for KTU 2024):**

- ✅ Tidal power is **predictable, renewable hydropower** based on the rise and fall of sea tides.
- ✅ Tides are caused by the **gravitational pull of the Moon (primary) and the Sun** plus centrifugal effects.
- ✅ The Moon contributes **~67%** and the Sun **~33%** of tidal force; Sun's tidal effect is **46.6%** of the Moon's but acts at right angles during neap tides.
- ✅ **Spring tides** = maximum range (New/Full Moon); **Neap tides** = minimum range (Quarter Moons).
- ✅ **Minimum viable tidal range** = **5–7 meters**.
- ✅ **Three main formulae** to remember:
  - $E_{cycle} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2 T}{16}$
  - $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^2}{16}$
  - $P_{max,extractable} = \dfrac{1}{8} \rho g A R^2 \cdot \eta$
- ✅ **1/8 Rule:** Maximum theoretical extractable energy is **only 12.5%** of total available — critical for exam.
- ✅ **Components:** Barrage, sluice gates, bulb turbines, generators, transformers, navigation lock, fish pass, trash racks, SCADA.
- ✅ **Bulb turbine** is the standard for tidal plants — **reversible**, **submerged**, **horizontal axis**.
- ✅ **Single-Basin Sub-types:** Single-effect (ebb/flood), double-effect, with pumped storage.
- ✅ **Double-Basin:** Two basins give **continuous generation** but at **higher cost**.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Predictable, no fuel, long life, no emissions, high energy density.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** High capital cost, intermittent (single-basin), environmental impact, corrosion, limited sites.
- ✅ **Environmental impacts:** Sedimentation, fish disruption, altered tidal prism, salinity changes; but also: storm surge protection, artificial reefs, low carbon footprint.
- ✅ **Real-world examples to cite:** La Rance (France, 240 MW), Sihwa (Korea, 254 MW), Annapolis (Canada, 20 MW).
- ✅ **Density of seawater** = **1025 kg/m³** (always use this, not 1000).
- ✅ **Plant Load Factor** for tidal plants = **0.20 to 0.30** (low, due to intermittent generation).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Energy: Basic Principle of Wind Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Energy: Basic Principle of Wind Energy Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Wind Energy Conversion (WEC)** is the process by which the **kinetic energy** of naturally moving air masses (wind) is captured and transformed into useful mechanical work or — via a generator — into **electrical energy** using a device called a **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** or **Wind Turbine**.

The **Basic Principle of Wind Energy Conversion** rests on the extraction of kinetic energy from a moving fluid (air) by slowing it down through a rotor (set of aerodynamically shaped blades). The reduction in the kinetic energy of the air stream is converted into rotational mechanical energy on the shaft, which is then coupled to an electrical generator or load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** According to KTU PEMUT864 (Module 2), the basic principle is rooted in **Bernoulli's equation** (energy conservation) and **Newton's second law of motion** (momentum change), applied to a control volume of air passing through the rotor swept area.

### 1.2 The Governing Physical Constants

- **Standard Air Density (ρ)** at sea level, 15 °C = **1.225 kg/m³**
- **Betz Limit (Cp_max)** = **16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (≈ 59.3%)**
- **Kinematic Viscosity of Air (ν)** ≈ 1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s
- **Specific Gas Constant for dry air (R)** = 287 J/(kg·K)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing in a river holding a paddle-wheel half-submerged in the flowing water. The water pushes against the angled blades and turns the wheel. The faster the water flows, and the larger the wheel you use, the more spinning power you get. Now replace the river with **moving air (wind)** and the paddle-wheel with a **wind turbine rotor**. That is, in essence, a Wind Energy Conversion System.

The "trick" is this: you cannot stop the wind entirely at the turbine — if you did, no more air would flow through and you'd capture zero power. You also cannot leave the wind completely undisturbed — then you capture nothing. Somewhere between these two extremes lies the **maximum extractable power**, which is the famous **Betz Limit (59.3%)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Wind power is proportional to the **cube of wind speed** (v³). If wind speed doubles, available power increases by a factor of **8** (2³). This cubic dependence is why site selection — finding locations with strong, steady winds — is the single most important economic factor in wind power projects.

### 1.4 The Core Energy Equation (Anchoring Formula)

The power available in an unobstructed wind stream of cross-sectional area **A** and velocity **v** is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^{3} \quad [\text{Watts, W}]$$

where:
- **ρ** = air density (kg/m³)
- **A** = swept area of the rotor (m²)
- **v** = wind speed (m/s)

This equation is the **cornerstone** of all wind energy studies and must be memorized verbatim for KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

#### Step A — Wind as a Moving Fluid Mass
A volume of air in motion carries **kinetic energy** proportional to its mass and the square of its velocity. For a parcel of mass **m**:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$

#### Step B — Mass Flow Rate Through a Cross-Section
Consider a stream tube of cross-sectional area **A** perpendicular to the wind direction. In one second, a column of air of length **v** passes through. The mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

#### Step C — Power in the Wind (Energy Flux)
Power is the rate of energy flow — kinetic energy per unit mass times mass flow rate:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v \cdot v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$

This is the **gross, theoretical, unobstructed** power. A real turbine can never extract all of it.

#### Step D — The Power Coefficient (Cp)
The fraction of wind power actually captured by a turbine is defined by the **power coefficient**:

$$C_{p} = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$$

By **Betz's Law**, $C_{p}$ has an absolute theoretical maximum:

$$C_{p,\,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;(\approx 59.3\%)$$

This is the **Betz Limit**.

#### Step E — Actual Power Output
Combining both:

$$P_{turbine} = C_{p} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$

#### Step F — Tip Speed Ratio (λ) — Design Parameter
The performance of a wind turbine rotor depends strongly on how fast the blade tips move relative to the wind:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 v}$$

where **ω** = angular velocity (rad/s), **R** = rotor radius (m), **n** = rotational speed (rpm).

#### Step G — Solidity (σ) — Aerodynamic Loading
Solidity is the ratio of the total blade planform area to the swept disc area:

$$\sigma = \frac{N \cdot c}{\pi R}$$

where **N** = number of blades, **c** = average blade chord length.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Units | Exam Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power in the wind | $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | W | Cubic in v; memorize first. |
| 2 | Swept area (HAWT) | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | m² | For Vertical Axis: $A = D \cdot H$ |
| 3 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | kg/s | Through rotor disc. |
| 4 | Power coefficient | $C_{p} = P_{out}/P_{wind}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le C_{p} \le 16/27$ |
| 5 | Betz limit | $C_{p,\,max} = 16/27$ | dimensionless | ≈ 0.5926 ≈ 59.3% |
| 6 | Turbine output | $P = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | W | Always $\lt$ wind power. |
| 7 | Tip speed ratio | $\lambda = \omega R / v$ | dimensionless | Optimal $\lambda^{\ast}$ depends on design. |
| 8 | Solidity | $\sigma = N c / (\pi R)$ | dimensionless | High σ ⇒ low-speed, high-torque. |
| 9 | Energy in time t | $E = P \cdot t$ | Wh or kWh | For capacity calculations. |
| 10 | Air density (std) | $\rho = 1.225$ | kg/m³ | At 15°C, sea level. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** Inside markdown tables, never write $\vert x \vert$ using ASCII pipes. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to keep the table renderer intact. The same applies to set-builder notation: use $\{x \mid x \in \mathbb{R}\}$ instead of $\{x \vert x \in \mathbb{R}\}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wind Farm Site Assessment:** Engineers use the cubic law to predict annual energy yield at a candidate site based on measured wind speed distributions (Weibull).
- **Turbine Sizing:** Doubling rotor diameter (R) **quadruples** swept area and therefore quadruples power — provided wind speed is constant.
- **Cut-in / Cut-out Speeds:** Real turbines only operate between a minimum (≈ 3–4 m/s) and maximum (≈ 25 m/s) wind speed to protect mechanical integrity.
- **Grid Integration:** Wind's variability demands forecasting, storage, or hybrid systems — a direct consequence of the v³ law (small speed changes cause large power swings).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because of the v³ dependence, a wind turbine that doubles in size (linear dimension) actually produces **8× the power**. This is why modern utility-scale turbines have grown to rotor diameters exceeding 200 m.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Available in the Wind

**Goal:** Starting from first principles, derive $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$.

**Step 1 — Define the air column under consideration.**
Let a cylindrical column of air with cross-section **A** move with velocity **v**.

**Step 2 — Compute the length of air passing in 1 second.**
Distance travelled in 1 second = **v** metres.

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of that air parcel.**
Volume = $A \times v$ cubic metres.

**Step 4 — Compute the mass using density.**
Mass = Volume × Density

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \quad [\text{kg}]$$

This is the mass arriving at the rotor per second — i.e. the **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$.

**Step 5 — Compute kinetic energy of that mass.**

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize that this KE passes per second ⇒ Power.**

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \quad [\text{W}]}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Betz Limit (Maximum Theoretical Cp)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 2 examinations. It uses **momentum theory** (Newton's 2nd law) on an ideal "actuator disc".

**Step 1 — Define control volume (actuator disc) and notation.**
- $V_{1}$ = upstream (free-stream) wind velocity
- $V$ = velocity at the rotor disc
- $V_{2}$ = downstream (wake) velocity
- $A$ = swept disc area
- $\rho$ = air density (assumed incompressible, constant)

**Step 2 — Apply continuity (mass conservation).**
Mass flow rate is constant through the stream tube:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V_{1} = \rho A V = \rho A V_{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply momentum theory (Newton's 2nd law).**
Thrust force on rotor = rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \dot{m}(V_{1} - V_{2}) = \rho A V (V_{1} - V_{2})$$

**Step 4 — Express power extracted in two equivalent ways.**
- (a) Power = Force × Velocity at rotor disc:

$$P = F \cdot V = \rho A V^{2} (V_{1} - V_{2})$$

- (b) Power = Rate of KE loss of the air stream:

$$P = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_{1}^{2} - V_{2}^{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V (V_{1}^{2} - V_{2}^{2})$$

**Step 5 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$\rho A V^{2} (V_{1} - V_{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V (V_{1}^{2} - V_{2}^{2})$$

Cancel $\rho A V$ on both sides:

$$V (V_{1} - V_{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2} (V_{1} + V_{2})(V_{1} - V_{2})$$

For $V_{1} \ne V_{2}$:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{V_{1} + V_{2}}{2}}$$

**Interpretation:** The velocity at the disc is the **arithmetic mean** of upstream and downstream velocities.

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the power expression.**
Let $a = \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}$ be the **axial induction factor**. Then $V = \dfrac{V_{1}(1+a)}{2}$.

$$P = \rho A \left[\frac{V_{1}(1+a)}{2}\right]^{2} V_{1}(1-a)$$

$$P = \frac{\rho A V_{1}^{3}}{4} (1+a)^{2}(1-a)$$

**Step 7 — Form the power coefficient (divide by wind power).**

$$C_{p} = \frac{P}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V_{1}^{3}} = \frac{(1+a)^{2}(1-a)}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Maximize Cp with respect to a.**
Differentiate:

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 2(1+a)(1-a) - (1+a)^{2} \right] = 0$$

Factor out $(1+a)$:

$$(1+a)\left[ 2(1-a) - (1+a) \right] = 0$$

Since $a \ne -1$:

$$2 - 2a - 1 - a = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - 3a = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \tfrac{1}{3}$$

**Step 9 — Evaluate Cp at a = 1/3.**

$$C_{p,\,max} = \frac{(1 + \tfrac{1}{3})^{2} (1 - \tfrac{1}{3})}{2} = \frac{(\tfrac{4}{3})^{2} (\tfrac{2}{3})}{2}$$

$$C_{p,\,max} = \frac{\tfrac{16}{9} \cdot \tfrac{2}{3}}{2} = \frac{\tfrac{32}{27}}{2} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,\,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\;(\approx 59.3\%)}$$

This is the **Betz Limit** — the universal upper bound for any wind turbine regardless of design.

**Step 10 — Verify the corresponding downstream velocity.**
$V_{2} = a \cdot V_{1} = \tfrac{1}{3} V_{1}$, so $V = \tfrac{2}{3} V_{1}$. This means an **ideal turbine slows the wind to 2/3 of its original speed** at the rotor plane, leaving 1/3 in the wake.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Power Variation with Wind Speed

| Wind Speed Regime | Behaviour | Power Available | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| v < v_cut-in | Rotor stationary | 0 W | Below starting torque. |
| v_cut-in ≤ v < v_rated | Cp optimised | $P \propto v^{3}$ | Betz limit approached. |
| v = v_rated | Rated power | $P_{rated}$ | Pitch control active. |
| v_rated < v < v_cut-out | Pitch-regulated constant power | $P_{rated}$ | Aerodynamic braking. |
| v > v_cut-out | Shut down / furled | 0 W | Safety — protect blades & gearbox. |

Typical KTU values: **v_cut-in ≈ 3 m/s**, **v_rated ≈ 12 m/s**, **v_cut-out ≈ 25 m/s**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wind Energy Conversion — Functional Architecture Flow

The figure below maps the energy-flow path from ambient wind through to the electrical grid, following the Block-Level Functional Architecture convention.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ATMOSPHERIC AIR KINETIC ENERGY] --> B[ROTOR BLADE ASSEMBLY]
    B --> C[MAIN SHAFT LOW SPEED]
    C --> D[GEARBOX SPEED INCREASER]
    D --> E[GENERATOR ROTOR]
    E --> F[POWER ELECTRONICS AC DC AC CONVERTER]
    F --> G[TRANSFORMER STEP UP]
    G --> H[GRID OR LOCAL LOAD]
    B -.-> I[PITCH AND YAW CONTROL SYSTEM]
    I -.-> B
    D -.-> J[BRAKE SYSTEM]
    J -.-> D
    A -.-> K[ANEMOMETER AND WIND VANE]
    K -.-> I
    H -.-> L[GRID SUPERVISORY CONTROL]
    L -.-> I
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** denote power flow direction (mechanical then electrical).
- **Dashed arrows** denote control/feedback signals (sensor → controller → actuator).
- Block **A** is the natural energy source; block **H** is the useful energy sink.

### 4.2 Actuatror-Disc Stream-Tube Schematic (Betz Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Far Upstream V1] --> T1[Stream Tube Expansion]
    T1 --> AD[Rotor Disc Area A velocity V]
    AD --> T2[Stream Tube Contraction]
    T2 --> S2[Far Downstream V2 less than V1]
    AD --> PTHRUST[Thrust Force F on rotor]
    AD --> PEXTRACT[Power Extracted P]
    PTHRUST --> NOTE1[By Newton 2nd Law]
    PEXTRACT --> NOTE2[By Kinetic Energy Difference]
    NOTE1 --> BETZ[BETZ LIMIT 16 over 27]
    NOTE2 --> BETZ
```

### 4.3 Classification Block Map of WECS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WECS[Wind Energy Conversion Systems] --> AXIS[Axis of Rotation]
    AXIS --> HAWT[Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine]
    AXIS --> VAWT[Vertical Axis Wind Turbine]
    HAWT --> UPWIND[Upwind Rotor]
    HAWT --> DOWNWIND[Downwind Rotor]
    VAWT --> DAR[Darrieus Type]
    VAWT --> SAV[Savonius Type]
    HAWT --> BLADE[Blade Count 2 3 Multi]
    BLADE --> LIFT[Lift Driven Aerofoil]
    SAV --> DRAG[Drag Driven Cup Shape]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Subsystem Detail (Main Components)

| Subsystem Block | Function | Key Engineering Element |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor + Blades | Capture wind KE | Aerofoil-shaped blades |
| Hub | Mount blades | Cast iron / steel forging |
| Main Shaft | Transmit torque | Low-speed shaft |
| Gearbox | Step up RPM | Planetary / helical gears |
| Generator | Convert to electricity | Induction / synchronous |
| Power Electronics | Condition output | AC-DC-AC converters |
| Yaw Drive | Orient nacelle | Servo motors |
| Pitch System | Regulate blade angle | Hydraulic / electric actuators |
| Controller | Supervise entire system | PLC / SCADA |
| Anemometer | Measure wind speed | Cup / sonic sensor |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define Power Coefficient (Cp). State the Betz Limit and its significance.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Power coefficient $C_{p}$ is the ratio of the actual power extracted by a wind turbine rotor to the total power available in the wind sweeping the rotor area.
$$C_{p} = \dfrac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} = \dfrac{P_{turbine}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}}$$
- **Betz Limit statement (1 mark):** The maximum theoretical value of $C_{p}$ for any wind turbine, irrespective of design, is $\dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (≈ 59.3 %), known as the **Betz Limit**, derived by Albert Betz in 1919.
- **Significance (0.5 mark):** It sets an absolute upper bound — even a "perfect" turbine can never convert more than 59.3 % of wind energy into mechanical energy.

---

#### **Q2. State and explain the cubic law of wind power.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** The power available in the wind is directly proportional to the **cube of the wind speed**, $P \propto v^{3}$.
- **Explanation (1.5 marks):** From $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$, doubling the wind speed (from 6 m/s to 12 m/s) increases available power by a factor of **2³ = 8**. This non-linear relationship makes wind speed measurement the most critical parameter in any wind energy project.
- **Practical implication (0.5 mark):** Small errors in wind speed measurement translate to large errors in energy yield estimation, hence the need for accurate anemometry and long-term averaging.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Module 2 carries **two full 14-mark questions**, each divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** out of the two provided.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for **power available in the wind** in terms of air density, swept area and wind velocity. (7 marks)
**(b)** A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m**. The wind speed is **12 m/s** and air density is **1.225 kg/m³**. Calculate:
  (i) The power available in the wind.
  (ii) The maximum power extractable (use Betz limit).
  (iii) The actual power output if $C_p = 0.40$.
  Take $\pi = 3.1416$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Full derivation as in Section 3.1 — start from mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A v$, multiply by KE per unit mass $\tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}$ to obtain $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$. Mention that this is the *gross* power in the undisturbed wind, before any rotor is introduced.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 80$ m $\Rightarrow R = 40$ m, $v = 12$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\pi = 3.1416$.

**Step 1 — Swept area.**
$$A = \pi R^{2} = 3.1416 \times 40^{2} = 3.1416 \times 1600 = 5026.56 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Power in the wind.**
$$P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.56 \times 12^{3}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.56 \times 1728$$
$$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 5{,}319{,}000 \; \text{W} \approx 5.32 \; \text{MW}}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum extractable (Betz limit, Cp = 16/27).**
$$P_{max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \times P_{wind} = 0.5926 \times 5.32 \; \text{MW}$$
$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 3.15 \; \text{MW}}$$

**Step 4 — Actual power output (Cp = 0.40).**
$$P_{actual} = 0.40 \times 5.32 \; \text{MW}$$
$$\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 2.13 \; \text{MW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** Many students lose 1 mark by leaving the answer in raw watts instead of converting to **MW or kW**. Always quote in standard SI multiples.
> 2. **Swept area formula:** Confusing $A = \pi R^{2}$ (horizontal axis) with $A = D \times H$ (vertical axis) costs 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Betz limit application:** Students often forget to multiply by $16/27$ and instead use $C_p = 0.593$ as a decimal correctly but write the formula wrong. Always show: $P_{Betz} = (16/27) \times P_{wind}$.
> 4. **Significant figures:** KTU expects 3 significant figures for intermediate steps.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the **Betz limit** using momentum theory. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compare **lift-type** and **drag-type** wind machines. State two examples of each. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Full derivation as in Section 3.2 — actuator disc model with $V_1, V, V_2$; mass flow continuity; momentum equation $F = \rho A V (V_1 - V_2)$; power extraction; equate two power expressions; obtain $V = (V_1 + V_2)/2$; introduce induction factor $a = V_2/V_1$; derive $C_p = (1+a)^2(1-a)/2$; maximize to get $a = 1/3$ and $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$. Include a clearly labelled diagram showing the stream-tube expansion upstream, contraction downstream, and the three velocity regions.

**(b) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Lift-Type Machine | Drag-Type Machine |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Aerodynamic **lift** force on aerofoil | Aerodynamic **drag** force on a bluff body |
| Blade / rotor shape | Aerofoil (wing-like) | Cup / paddle / Savonius bucket |
| Tip speed ratio (λ) | High (λ > 1, often 6–8) | Low (λ ≤ 1) |
| Rotational speed | High | Low |
| Power coefficient (Cp) | Can approach Betz limit (≈ 0.4–0.5 typical) | Always low (≪ 0.2) |
| Starting torque | Low (needs initial push) | High (self-starting) |
| Typical examples | **Darrieus, Propeller-type HAWT** | **Savonius, Cup anemometer** |
| Application | Grid-connected, large-scale | Pumping, low-power standalone |

(2 marks reserved for neat tabular comparison + 2 marks for listing two examples on each side + 3 marks for crisp explanatory sentences.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. **Momentum theory vs. Bernoulli:** The Betz derivation uses **momentum theory (actuator disc)** as in Section 3.2, NOT Bernoulli's equation directly. Confusing the two loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Induction factor:** KTU expects the symbol **'a'** for the axial induction factor $V_2/V_1$. Using the wrong variable name incurs a minor 0.5-mark penalty.
> 3. **Lift vs Drag examples:** A common error is listing **Savonius under "lift-type"** — Savonius is fundamentally a drag device. Likewise, **Darrieus is lift-type** despite having a vertical axis.
> 4. **Diagram missing:** Drawing the stream-tube schematic (as in Section 4.2) is worth **at least 1 mark** in part (a). Skipping it is the most common cause of losing marks.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Power in wind:** $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ — **cubic in v**, linear in ρ, linear in A.
- ⚡ **Swept area:** HAWT → $A = \pi R^{2}$; VAWT → $A = D \times H$.
- ⚡ **Power coefficient:** $C_p = P_{out} / P_{wind}$; maximum **16/27 ≈ 0.593**.
- ⚡ **Betz limit derivation** uses **actuator-disc momentum theory**: continuity + momentum → $V = (V_1 + V_2)/2$ → induction factor → maximize → 16/27.
- ⚡ **Optimal induction factor:** $a = V_2 / V_1 = 1/3$.
- ⚡ **Tip speed ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / v = \pi R n / (30 v)$.
- ⚡ **Solidity:** $\sigma = N c / (\pi R)$; high σ ⇒ low-speed high-torque.
- ⚡ **Standard air density:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (15 °C, sea level).
- ⚡ **Lift-type vs Drag-type:** Lift (high λ, high Cp) vs Drag (λ ≤ 1, low Cp, self-starting).
- ⚡ **Cut-in / Rated / Cut-out:** typical 3 / 12 / 25 m/s.
- ⚡ **Doubling v** → **8× power**; **doubling D** → **4× swept area** → **4× power**.
- ⚡ **Energy in time t:** $E = P \times t$ — used for capacity factor & annual energy calculations.
- ⚡ **No turbine can capture > 59.3 %** of wind kinetic energy — this is **physically unbreakable** (Betz).
- ⚡ **Practical real-world Cp** is typically **0.35–0.45** for modern HAWTs.
- ⚡ **Site selection matters more than turbine technology** because of the v³ dependence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Speed Measurements and Availability of Wind Energy in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Wind Speed Measurements and Availability of Wind Energy in India ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Wind Speed Measurement** is the systematic quantification of the kinetic energy content of moving air masses using calibrated instruments deployed at standardized heights and exposure conditions, conforming to international standards such as **IEC 61400-12-1** and **ISO 17713**. The recorded data forms the empirical foundation for wind resource assessment, turbine siting, and energy yield prediction. **Availability of Wind Energy in India** refers to the gross technically extractable wind power potential within the geographical boundaries of the country, as periodically assessed and published by the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)** and the **National Institute of Wind Energy (NIWE)**, Chennai. This potential is mapped spatially using long-term meteorological data, terrain roughness classifications, and hub-height extrapolations. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must master both the **instrumentation stack** (how we measure wind) and the **resource geography of India** (where and how much wind is available), as both form independent high-weightage clusters in Part B questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Imagine wind measurement like measuring rainfall before building a dam.** Before an engineer builds a hydroelectric dam, they install rain gauges across the catchment area for years to record how much, how often, and how intensely it rains. *Wind measurement is the atmospheric twin of this process* — except we are measuring the flow rate of invisible air instead of falling water droplets. Just as rainfall data tells you how big a dam you can justify, wind data tells you how big a wind turbine (or wind farm) you can economically install. For the Indian subcontinent, think of the country as a giant natural wind tunnel shaped by: - The **monsoon circulation** (south-west in summer, north-east in winter) - The **Western Ghats mountain barrier** that accelerates coastal winds - The **Thar Desert** thermal low that drives strong summer westerlies in Rajasthan - The **coastal sea breeze cycle** that gives Tamil Nadu and Gujarat their legendary wind regimes The "availability" therefore is not just a number — it is a **time-varying, height-dependent, terrain-modulated resource** that the Indian geography offers in abundance, particularly along a 7,500 km coastline and on the leeward slopes of the Western Ghats. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics for Wind Energy:** > - Air density at standard conditions: **ρ = 1.225 kg/m³** > - Standard measurement height for met-mast: **10 m** (legacy) and **80–120 m** (modern hub height) > - Standard averaging interval: **10 minutes** (per IEC 61400-12-1) > - Standard wind power classification: **NREL/IEC Classes 1–7** based on annual mean wind speed > - India's assessed onshore potential (at 80 m hub height): **~302 GW** (NIWE/MNRE estimate) > - India's assessed onshore potential (at 120 m hub height): **~695.50 GW** (revised NIWE estimate) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Wind Measurement Instrumentation Stack Wind resource measurement is a **multi-parameter, multi-height engineering exercise**. A single anemometer is never enough for a bankable wind farm study. The full measurement chain is: **(a) Primary Wind Speed Sensor — Cup Anemometer** - Three or four hemispherical cups mounted on horizontal arms rotating about a vertical spindle. - Rotation is proportional to wind speed; calibration gives a linear transfer function: $$v = a \cdot f + b$$ where $v$ is wind speed, $f$ is rotational frequency, $a$ is the calibration slope (m/rev), and $b$ is the offset. - **Standard height deployment**: 10 m and hub height (e.g., 80 m, 100 m, 120 m). - **IEC Class 1A cup anemometer accuracy**: ±0.05 m/s over the operating range. **(b) Wind Direction Sensor — Wind Vane** - Asymmetric fin mounted on a vertical axis with a potentiometric or encoder transducer. - Provides **wind direction in degrees from true North (0°–360°)**. - Critical for plotting the **Wind Rose Diagram** that shows directional frequency distribution. **(c) Auxiliary Sensors Required by IEC 61400-12-1** - **Air temperature sensor** (to compute density). - **Barometric pressure sensor**. - **Relative humidity sensor** (for accurate density correction). **(d) Advanced / Research-Grade Sensors** - **Sonic anemometer** — measures wind speed using ultrasonic transit-time differences between pairs of transducers; no moving parts, suitable for turbulent flux studies. - **LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** — remote, ground-based or nacelle-mounted, measures wind speed at multiple heights up to 200 m using backscattered laser pulses. Has **revolutionized modern wind assessment** by eliminating tall met-masts. - **SODAR (Sonic Detection and Ranging)** — acoustic analog of LIDAR, used for atmospheric boundary-layer profiling. > [!TIP] > **Real-world utility:** LIDAR-based remote sensing now allows wind developers to campaign a site for 12 months using a trailer-mounted device, drastically reducing the cost and time of met-mast deployment. MNRE-approved NIWE campaigns use both technologies. ### 2.2 Site Selection & Data Quality Standards - **Site selection criteria**: Flat, open terrain with **fetch ≥ 10× the obstruction height** in the prevailing wind direction (per WMO guidelines). - **Obstruction clearance cone**: Within 200 m, no obstruction should pierce the line from the anemometer to a point 10 m above any obstacle. - **Topographic influence**: Hilltops, ridges, and escarpments cause **speed-up effects** (Froude number and slope-driven acceleration). - **Long-term correlation**: At least **1 year of on-site data** is correlated against a long-term reference (ERA5 reanalysis or nearby operational wind farm SCADA) to generate the **Measure-Correlate-Predict (MCP)** corrected wind climate. ### 2.3 Wind Resource Assessment Methodology for India The Indian wind resource assessment follows a five-step protocol (NIWE / C-WET): 1. **Wind monitoring** at candidate stations using 30–50 m masts. 2. **Data validation and quality control** (filtering of sensor icing, tower shadow, and instrument faults). 3. **MCP** to a long-term reference period (≥ 10 years). 4. **Vertical extrapolation** using the **power law** or **log law** to turbine hub height. 5. **Wind power density (WPD) mapping** to produce the national atlas. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Boundary Condition | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wind Power Density (WPD) | $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, v^{3}$ | W/m², ρ in kg/m³, v in m/s | Quick site-rating metric | | 2 | Available Power in Swept Area | $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^{3} \, C_{p}$ | W, $C_{p}$ is the power coefficient (Betz limit $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$) | Turbine output estimation | | 3 | Air Density (Ideal Gas) | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{R_{a} \, T}$ | kg/m³, $R_{a} = 287.05$ J/(kg·K) for dry air | Density correction for altitude/temperature | | 4 | Power-Law Vertical Extrapolation | $v_{2} = v_{1}\left(\dfrac{h_{2}}{h_{1}}\right)^{\alpha}$ | α = terrain roughness exponent (0.10–0.40) | Hub-height speed estimation | | 5 | Log-Law Vertical Extrapolation | $v(z) = \dfrac{v_{*}}{\kappa} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{0}}\right)$ | $v_{*}$ = friction velocity, κ = 0.41, $z_{0}$ = roughness length | Neutral atmospheric conditions | | 6 | Weibull PDF | $f(v) = \dfrac{k}{c}\!\left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1}\! e^{-(v/c)^{k}}$ | $k$ = shape factor, $c$ = scale factor (m/s) | Wind speed frequency distribution | | 7 | Mean Wind Speed from Weibull | $\bar{v} = c \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{k}\right)$ | $\Gamma$ = Gamma function | Long-term mean prediction | | 8 | Weibull Mean Power Density | $\dfrac{\bar{P}}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho c^{3} \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{3}{k}\right)$ | W/m² | Energy yield pre-feasibility | | 9 | Energy in Wind Stream (over time t) | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} t$ | Joules or Wh | Capacity factor estimation | | 10 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{rated \cdot 8760\,h}}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Wind farm performance metric | | 11 | Tip-Speed Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ | ω = rotor angular speed (rad/s), R = rotor radius | Turbine aerodynamic design | | 12 | Betz Limit (Max $C_{p}$) | $C_{p,Betz} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Theoretical upper bound | Turbine efficiency benchmark | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pitfall (Table Style Note):** In the table above, all absolute-value bars and divisions use either $\dfrac{}{}$ or $\ln$ or $\Gamma$ with parentheses. **KTU answer scripts that use plain `|` for absolute value inside a markdown-rendered answer table will break the table grid** — always use parentheses or $\vert \cdot \vert$ in your final answer sheets. ### 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in the Real World - The **cubic dependence on $v$** ($v^{3}$) is the single most important reason wind resource assessment is unforgiving: a **10% error in measured wind speed translates to ~33% error in computed power**. - The **Betz limit** ($C_{p,\max} = 16/27$) is a thermodynamic ceiling — modern multi-MW turbines achieve $C_{p} \approx 0.45$–$0.50$, just below this bound. - The **power law and log law** are *not* interchangeable — power law is empirical, log law is physical, and both fail inside canopies or in highly stable atmospheric conditions. Always declare which you are using in your KTU answer. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wind Power Density from First Principles We derive the kinetic power of an air stream crossing a swept area $A$ perpendicular to the flow direction. **Step 1.** Consider an air parcel of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$. Its kinetic energy is: $$E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$ **Step 2.** In a time interval $t$, the volume of air that crosses area $A$ is the column of length $v \cdot t$ behind the rotor disk: $$V = A \cdot v \cdot t$$ **Step 3.** The mass of this air column, using air density $\rho$, is: $$m = \rho \, V = \rho \, A \, v \, t$$ **Step 4.** Substitute this mass into the kinetic energy expression: $$E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} (\rho A v t) v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} t$$ **Step 5.** Power is energy per unit time. Dividing both sides by $t$: $$P = \dfrac{E_{k}}{t} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$ **Step 6.** Defining the **Wind Power Density (WPD)** as power per unit swept area: $$\boxed{\dfrac{P}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{3} \quad \text{[W/m}^{2}\text{]}}$$ **Conversion logic:** The unit converts as $\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{m}^{2}}{\cdot} \cdot \dfrac{\text{m}^{3}}{\text{s}^{3}} = \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}^{3}} = \dfrac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^{2}}$, confirming dimensional consistency. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Betz Limit (Maximum Extractable Power Coefficient) We consider an ideal, actuator-disk turbine that extracts kinetic energy by decelerating the wind from upstream velocity $v_1$ to downstream velocity $v_2$ at the far wake. **Step 1.** Mass flow rate through the disk of area $A$: $$\dot{m} = \rho A v_{d}$$ where $v_{d}$ is the velocity at the disk plane, given by $v_{d} = (v_{1} + v_{2})/2$. **Step 2.** The thrust force on the disk equals the rate of momentum change: $$F = \dot{m} (v_{1} - v_{2}) = \rho A v_{d}(v_{1} - v_{2})$$ **Step 3.** Power extracted by the turbine (thrust × disk velocity): $$P = F \cdot v_{d} = \rho A v_{d}^{2}(v_{1} - v_{2})$$ **Step 4.** Using the axial induction factor $a = (v_{1} - v_{d})/v_{1}$, we have $v_{d} = v_{1}(1 - a)$ and $v_{2} = v_{1}(1 - 2a)$. Substituting: $$P = \rho A [v_{1}(1-a)]^{2} \cdot [v_{1}(1-a) - v_{1}(1-2a)]$$ $$P = \rho A v_{1}^{3} (1-a)^{2} \cdot a v_{1} = \rho A v_{1}^{3} a(1-a)^{2}$$ **Step 5.** Upstream available power in the same streamtube is: $$P_{0} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v_{1}^{3}$$ **Step 6.** The power coefficient is therefore: $$C_{p} = \dfrac{P}{P_{0}} = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$ **Step 7.** Maximize $C_{p}$ by setting $dC_{p}/da = 0$: $$\dfrac{d}{da}\left[4a(1-a)^{2}\right] = 4(1-a)^{2} + 4a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) = 0$$ $$4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 0$$ $$4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$ The non-trivial root is $a = 1/3$. **Step 8.** Substitute $a = 1/3$ back into the $C_{p}$ expression: $$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{9} = \dfrac{16}{27}$$ $$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{(Betz Limit)}}$$ **Conversion logic:** The factor 16/27 emerges purely from one-dimensional momentum theory — it is a **thermodynamic ceiling** that no turbine, no matter how sophisticated, can ever exceed. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Wind Speed and Mean Power from the Weibull Distribution The Weibull PDF is: $$f(v) = \dfrac{k}{c}\left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^{k}}, \quad v \geq 0$$ where $k$ is the shape factor and $c$ is the scale factor (m/s). **Step 1.** The $n$-th statistical moment of a Weibull-distributed variable is given by: $$\overline{v^{n}} = c^{n} \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{n}{k}\right)$$ **Step 2.** For the **mean wind speed** ($n = 1$): $$\bar{v} = c \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{k}\right)$$ **Step 3.** For the **mean cube of wind speed** ($n = 3$), which is the wind-power moment: $$\overline{v^{3}} = c^{3} \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{3}{k}\right)$$ **Step 4.** The mean wind power density is therefore: $$\boxed{\dfrac{\bar{P}}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \overline{v^{3}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, c^{3} \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{3}{k}\right)}$$ **Step 5.** For $k = 2$ (Rayleigh distribution, common assumption in early-stage assessment): $$\Gamma(1 + 1/k) = \Gamma(1.5) = \tfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$$ $$\Gamma(1 + 3/k) = \Gamma(2.5) = \tfrac{3\sqrt{\pi}}{4} \approx 1.3293$$ So: $$\bar{v}_{\text{Rayleigh}} = 0.8862 \, c \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = 1.128 \, \bar{v}$$ $$\dfrac{\bar{P}}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, c^{3} \cdot 1.3293 \approx 0.955 \, \rho \, \bar{v}^{3}$$ **Conversion logic:** The Rayleigh approximation says **the mean cube of wind speed is about 1.91 × the cube of the mean wind speed** — i.e., the **cubic mean is ~95% larger than the mean cubed**, demonstrating why a $C_{p}$ based on $\bar{v}^{3}$ would severely under-predict energy yield. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power-Law Vertical Extrapolation (Empirical) The Hellmann power law is: $$\dfrac{v}{v_{0}} = \left(\dfrac{h}{h_{0}}\right)^{\alpha}$$ Taking logarithms: $$\ln v = \ln v_{0} + \alpha (\ln h - \ln h_{0})$$ This is a straight line in $\ln v$ vs $\ln h$ space. Typical $\alpha$ values: - Open water: $\alpha = 0.10$ - Grassland: $\alpha = 0.15$ - Cropland with hedges: $\alpha = 0.20$ - Suburban: $\alpha = 0.27$ - Urban: $\alpha = 0.35$ **Numerical illustration:** If $v = 5.2$ m/s at $h = 10$ m over open grassland ($\alpha = 0.15$), the wind speed at hub height $h = 80$ m is: $$v_{80} = 5.2 \times \left(\dfrac{80}{10}\right)^{0.15} = 5.2 \times 8^{0.15}$$ $$8^{0.15} = e^{0.15 \ln 8} = e^{0.15 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.3119} \approx 1.3663$$ $$v_{80} = 5.2 \times 1.3663 \approx 7.105 \text{ m/s}$$ **Conversion logic:** The power law has **no physical basis** — it is an empirical fit. The log law (Step 5 below) is the physically correct model in neutral atmospheric conditions. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Logarithmic Wind Profile (Monin–Obukhov Limit, Neutral) In neutral stability, the surface-layer wind profile is: $$v(z) = \dfrac{v_{*}}{\kappa} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{0}}\right)$$ **Step 1.** The friction velocity $v_{*}$ is defined from surface shear stress $\tau_{0}$: $$v_{*} = \sqrt{\tau_{0}/\rho}$$ **Step 2.** The von Kármán constant is $\kappa = 0.41$ (universal constant). **Step 3.** The roughness length $z_{0}$ represents the height at which $v(z) = 0$ by extrapolation. Typical values: - Sea: $z_{0} = 0.0002$ m - Grassland: $z_{0} = 0.03$ m - Forest: $z_{0} = 1.0$ m - Urban: $z_{0} = 2.0$ m **Step 4.** To extrapolate from height $z_{1}$ to $z_{2}$: $$\dfrac{v(z_{2})}{v(z_{1})} = \dfrac{\ln(z_{2}/z_{0})}{\ln(z_{1}/z_{0})}$$ **Numerical illustration:** Given $v_{10} = 5.2$ m/s over grassland ($z_{0} = 0.03$ m), the wind speed at $z = 80$ m is: $$v_{80} = 5.2 \times \dfrac{\ln(80/0.03)}{\ln(10/0.03)} = 5.2 \times \dfrac{\ln(2666.67)}{\ln(333.33)}$$ $$= 5.2 \times \dfrac{7.888}{5.808} = 5.2 \times 1.3583 \approx 7.063 \text{ m/s}$$ **Conversion logic:** The log law predicts **slightly lower** wind speeds at hub height than the power law in this case — agreement is good but not perfect. For onshore complex terrain, CFD corrections (WAsP, OpenFOAM) are added. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Tested) | Method | Wind @ 80 m (from 5.2 m/s @ 10 m, α = 0.15) | Wind @ 120 m | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | Power Law (α = 0.15) | 7.105 m/s | 7.602 m/s | Empirical, simple | | Log Law (z₀ = 0.03 m) | 7.063 m/s | 7.530 m/s | Physically grounded | | Rayleigh-cube method (mean P/A) | 7.602 m/s (effective for $\bar{v}^{3}$) | — | Statistical moment | > [!TIP] > **For KTU numerical answers, always state your assumption (Rayleigh vs Weibull, power law vs log law, $\alpha$ value, $z_0$ value) explicitly.** Examiners award full marks only when the method is declared, even if the final number is slightly off. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Wind Measurement System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SENSOR["PRIMARY SENSOR LAYER AT 10 m / HUB HEIGHT"] CUP["CUP ANEMOMETER
3 OR 4 CUP ROTATING TYPE
OUTPUT 0 to 50 m/s"] VANE["WIND VANE
POTENTIOMETRIC
OUTPUT 0 to 360 DEG"] SONIC["SONIC ANEMOMETER
ULTRASONIC TRANSIT TIME
NO MOVING PARTS"] LIDAR["LIDAR UNIT
REMOTE OPTICAL SENSING
PROFILE 0 to 200 m"] end subgraph AUX["AUXILIARY METEOROLOGICAL SENSORS"] TEMP["AIR TEMPERATURE
PT100 RTD"] PRES["BAROMETRIC PRESSURE
PIEZORESISTIVE"] RHUM["RELATIVE HUMIDITY
CAPACITIVE POLYMER"] end subgraph DLOG["DATA ACQUISITION AND LOGGING UNIT"] ADC["24 BIT ADC
SAMPLING 1 Hz"] AVGM["10 MIN MEAN AND STD CALCULATOR
PER IEC 61400-12-1"] STORE["NON VOLATILE STORAGE
10 YEAR RETENTION"] COMM["GSM / SATELLITE TELEMETRY
TO CENTRAL SERVER"] end subgraph PROC["POST PROCESSING AND RESOURCE CHARACTERIZATION"] QC["QUALITY CONTROL
RANGE AND STEP CHECK
TOWER SHADOW FILTER"] MCP["MEASURE CORRELATE PREDICT
LONG TERM REFERENCE JOINING"] WEIB["WEIBULL FIT
SHAPE k AND SCALE c"] WROSE["WIND ROSE PLOT
DIRECTIONAL FREQUENCY"] WPDMAP["WIND POWER DENSITY MAP
W PER m SQUARED"] end subgraph DECN["ENGINEERING DECISION OUTPUT"] BANK["BANKABLE WIND RESOURCE REPORT
FOR PROJECT LENDER"] TURB["TURBINE SELECTION AND LAYOUT"] ENERGY["ANNUAL ENERGY PRODUCTION FORECAST"] end CUP --> ADC VANE --> ADC SONIC --> ADC LIDAR --> ADC TEMP --> ADC PRES --> ADC RHUM --> ADC ADC --> AVGM AVGM --> STORE STORE --> COMM COMM --> QC QC --> MCP MCP --> WEIB MCP --> WROSE WEIB --> WPDMAP WPDMAP --> BANK WPDMAP --> TURB WPDMAP --> ENERGY ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Wind Resource Availability in India (NIWE Atlas Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DATA["RAW DATA SOURCES"] MET["GROUND MET MASTS
NIWE C-WET NETWORK
50 m AND 80 m TOWERS"] SCADA["OPERATIONAL WIND FARM SCADA
REAL TIME YIELD RECORDS"] REAN["ERA5 REANALYSIS
30 YEAR LONG TERM"] end subgraph PROC["CENTRAL PROCESSING AT NIWE CHENNAI"] QC2["DATA QUALITY CONTROL
PER IEC AND MNRE GUIDELINES"] MERGE["SPATIAL INTERPOLATION
KRIGING AND IDW METHODS"] EXT["VERTICAL EXTRAPOLATION
