POWER LAW TO 80 m AND 120 m"] CLASS["TERRAIN CLASSIFICATION
ROUGHNESS BINS"] end subgraph MAP["NATIONAL WIND RESOURCE MAP OUTPUT"] ZONE1["ZONE 1 HIGH POTENTIAL
TAMIL NADU COASTAL
GUJARAT KHAMBHAT"] ZONE2["ZONE 2 GOOD POTENTIAL
KARNATAKA WESTERN GHATS
RAJASTHAN JAISALMER"] ZONE3["ZONE 3 MODERATE POTENTIAL
MAHARASHTRA SATARA
ANDHRA PRADESH ANANTAPUR"] ZONE4["ZONE 4 OFFSHORE POTENTIAL
GUJARAT GULF OF KUTCH
TAMIL NADU PAMBAN"] end MET --> QC2 SCADA --> QC2 REAN --> QC2 QC2 --> MERGE MERGE --> EXT EXT --> CLASS CLASS --> ZONE1 CLASS --> ZONE2 CLASS --> ZONE3 CLASS --> ZONE4 ``` ### 4.3 Wind Resource Geography of India — Key Data Table | Region / State | Peak Hub-Height Wind Speed (m/s) | Estimated Onshore Potential (GW) | Installed Capacity (GW, ~2024) | Dominant Wind Season | |---|---|---|---|---| | Tamil Nadu (Muppandal, Aralvaimozhi) | 7.5 – 8.5 | ~65 | ~10.3 | SW Monsoon (Jun–Sep) + NE Monsoon (Oct–Dec) | | Gujarat (Kutch, Jamnagar, Surajbari) | 7.0 – 8.0 | ~85 | ~9.5 | SW Monsoon (Jun–Sep) | | Karnataka (Chitradurga, Davangere, Hassan) | 6.5 – 7.5 | ~75 | ~7.0 | SW Monsoon (Jun–Sep) | | Rajasthan (Jaisalmer, Barmer) | 6.5 – 7.5 | ~45 | ~5.4 | SW Monsoon (Jun–Sep) | | Maharashtra (Satara, Dhule) | 6.0 – 7.0 | ~45 | ~5.4 | SW Monsoon (Jun–Sep) | | Andhra Pradesh (Anantapur) | 6.5 – 7.5 | ~75 | ~4.5 | SW + NE Monsoon | | Madhya Pradesh, Telangana, Kerala | 5.5 – 6.5 | Lower | <1.5 each | Mixed | > [!NOTE] > **India's total installed wind capacity (~2024): ~47 GW**, making it the **4th largest wind power market globally** after China, USA, and Germany. The **onshore theoretical potential at 120 m is ~695.50 GW** per the latest NIWE assessment — meaning India has exploited only about **6.8%** of its known potential. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** **Define the term Wind Power Density. A site has a mean wind speed of 7.2 m/s. Assuming standard air density of 1.225 kg/m³, calculate the WPD of the site.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Wind Power Density (WPD) is defined as the kinetic power per unit area of an air stream perpendicular to the flow direction. It is given by: $$\dfrac{P}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{3}$$ **Numerical (1.5 marks):** Substituting ρ = 1.225 kg/m³ and v = 7.2 m/s: $$\dfrac{P}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (7.2)^{3}$$ $$= 0.6125 \times 373.248 = 228.6 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{\dfrac{P}{A} \approx 228.6 \text{ W/m}^{2}}$$ This places the site in the **IEC Class 2** wind regime, suitable for utility-scale turbines. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]** **Briefly explain the role of NIWE (National Institute of Wind Energy) in assessing India's wind energy potential. List any four high-potential wind energy states in India.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Role of NIWE (2 marks):** The **National Institute of Wind Energy (NIWE)**, located at Chennai, is the technical arm of MNRE. It is responsible for: - Operating the national network of wind monitoring masts. - Conducting wind resource assessments and publishing the **Indian Wind Atlas**. - Validating wind turbine designs and issuing **Type Approval Certificates** per **IEC 61400** standards. - Conducting R&D in wind energy, including offshore wind resource studies. - Training manpower for the wind energy sector. **High-Potential Wind States (1 mark — list any 4):** 1. **Tamil Nadu** 2. **Gujarat** 3. **Karnataka** 4. **Rajasthan** 5. (Optional) Maharashtra, Andhra Pradesh. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Wind Measurement Techniques* > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]** **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Describe the various instruments used for wind speed and direction measurement. Compare cup anemometer and sonic anemometer. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a modern wind measurement campaign and the role of LIDAR. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Wind Measurement Instruments (7 marks):** **Wind Speed Measurement — Cup Anemometer (2.5 marks):** - Three or four hemispherical cups mounted on a horizontal arm rotating about a vertical spindle. - The rotational speed (proportional to wind speed) is converted to electrical pulses by a tachogenerator or optical chopper. - **Advantages:** Simple, robust, low cost, well-calibrated, widely used. - **Disadvantages:** Inertia causes **overspeeding** in gusty winds (must be corrected per IEC); mechanical wear; cannot resolve turbulent fluctuations faithfully above ~1 Hz. **Wind Speed Measurement — Sonic Anemometer (2.5 marks):** - Measures the transit time of ultrasonic pulses between pairs of transducers oriented in orthogonal directions. - Wind speed is derived from the difference in transit times along and against the flow. - **Advantages:** No moving parts (no overspeeding error), high frequency response (10–20 Hz), suitable for turbulent flux studies. - **Disadvantages:** Expensive; sensitive to rain, snow, and ice contamination; requires shelter in monsoon climates. **Wind Direction Measurement — Wind Vane (1 mark):** - A flat vane rotates on a vertical spindle; a potentiometer or encoder outputs an angle in degrees from true North. - Mounted above the met-mast top to avoid tower shadow. **Auxiliary Sensors (1 mark):** Air temperature, barometric pressure, relative humidity — all required to compute **air density** per the ideal gas law: $$\rho = \dfrac{P}{R_{a} T}$$ --- **(b) Block Diagram and LIDAR Architecture (7 marks):** **Modern Wind Measurement Campaign Architecture (3.5 marks):** A modern wind measurement campaign integrates the following subsystems (block diagram shown in Section 4.1 above): 1. **Sensor layer** at 10 m and hub height: cup and sonic anemometers, wind vane, auxiliary met sensors. 2. **Data acquisition and logging** at 1 Hz, with on-board 10-min mean and standard deviation calculation per IEC 61400-12-1. 3. **Telemetry** via GSM / satellite to a central server. 4. **Quality control**: range, step, and persistence checks; tower shadow filter. 5. **MCP (Measure-Correlate-Predict)** to a long-term reference dataset (≥ 10 years). 6. **Weibull fitting** to obtain $k$ and $c$ parameters. 7. **Wind rose plot** for directional frequency. 8. **Wind power density (WPD) map** generation. **Role of LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging) (3.5 marks):** LIDAR is a **remote-sensing wind profiling instrument** that has revolutionized modern wind resource assessment: - **Operating principle:** A pulsed laser beam (typically 1.5 µm near-infrared) is emitted vertically or along multiple azimuthal directions. The beam is **backscattered by aerosols in the air**. The **Doppler frequency shift** of the backscattered light is proportional to the line-of-sight wind speed. - **Configurations:** - **Vertical (VAD scan):** Measures wind speed and direction at multiple heights up to 200 m or more. - **Nacelle-mounted (forward-looking):** Mounted on the hub of an operating turbine to perform **power curve verification** and **wake characterization**. - **Advantages over met-mast:** - **No tall mast required** → lower CAPEX, faster deployment, easier permitting. - Captures full **vertical wind profile** in a single instrument. - Safe in icing-prone and lightning-prone sites. - **Limitations:** - Requires sufficient **aerosol backscatter** (works poorly in fog, very clean polar air). - Measurement uncertainty (~2%) is higher than IEC-class cup anemometers (~1%). - Cannot be used as the primary reference for **power performance testing** per IEC 61400-12-1:2017 (yet); used as **complementary** to a met-mast. **Conclusion:** LIDAR has become the de-facto choice for **pre-construction site assessment**, while met-masts remain the **legal reference** for power curve testing and bankable resource reports. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Wind Energy Availability in India* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** **Part (a) [7 marks]:** Discuss the availability of wind energy in India. Explain the role of MNRE and NIWE, and the Indian Wind Atlas. **Part (b) [7 marks]:** A wind farm site in Tamil Nadu has a Weibull shape parameter $k = 2.0$ and scale parameter $c = 7.5$ m/s. Calculate (i) the mean wind speed, and (ii) the mean wind power density. Assume standard air density ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Availability of Wind Energy in India (7 marks):** **Geographical and Climatic Drivers (2 marks):** India has one of the world's richest wind energy resources, primarily because of: - A **7,500 km coastline** exposed to strong sea breezes. - The **monsoon circulation** that drives strong, sustained SW (Jun–Sep) and NE (Oct–Dec) winds. - The **Western Ghats** mountain barrier that creates leeward acceleration zones. - The **Thar Desert** thermal low driving strong summer westerlies in Rajasthan. - Open **plateau terrain** in the Deccan that offers low roughness and excellent fetch. **Quantitative Availability (2 marks):** - **Onshore potential at 80 m hub height:** ~302 GW (NIWE estimate). - **Onshore potential at 120 m hub height:** ~695.50 GW (revised estimate). - **Offshore potential (initial estimate):** ~174 GW along Gujarat and Tamil Nadu coasts. - **Cumulative installed capacity (~2024):** ~47 GW — the 4th largest globally. **Role of MNRE (1.5 marks):** The **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)** is the nodal ministry that: - Formulates national wind energy policy. - Provides **Generation-Based Incentive (GBI)** and **Accelerated Depreciation (AD)** benefits. - Sets the annual **renewable purchase obligation (RPO)** for states. - Funds NIWE and state nodal agencies (TEDA, GEDA, KREDL, etc.). **Role of NIWE and the Indian Wind Atlas (1.5 marks):** - NIWE (formerly C-WET, Chennai) operates **~800 wind monitoring stations** across the country. - Publishes the **Indian Wind Atlas** — a spatial WPD map at 50 m, 80 m, and 120 m heights. - Validates turbines, trains engineers, and supports offshore wind pilot projects (Tamil Nadu, Gujarat). **Top 5 Wind States (approx., for quick recall):** Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan, Maharashtra. --- **(b) Weibull-based Mean Speed and WPD (7 marks):** **Given:** $k = 2.0$, $c = 7.5$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. **Part (i) — Mean Wind Speed (3 marks):** The Weibull-derived mean wind speed is: $$\bar{v} = c \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{k}\right)$$ For $k = 2$: $$\Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \Gamma(1.5) = \tfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \approx 0.8862$$ Therefore: $$\bar{v} = 7.5 \times 0.8862 = 6.6466 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{v} \approx 6.65 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Part (ii) — Mean Wind Power Density (4 marks):** The mean cube of wind speed from Weibull is: $$\overline{v^{3}} = c^{3} \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{3}{k}\right)$$ For $k = 2$: $$\Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{3}{2}\right) = \Gamma(2.5) = \tfrac{3\sqrt{\pi}}{4} \approx 1.3293$$ Therefore: $$\overline{v^{3}} = (7.5)^{3} \times 1.3293 = 421.875 \times 1.3293 = 560.83 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}^{3}$$ The mean wind power density is: $$\dfrac{\bar{P}}{A} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \overline{v^{3}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 560.83$$ $$= 0.6125 \times 560.83 \approx 343.51 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{\dfrac{\bar{P}}{A} \approx 343.5 \text{ W/m}^{2}}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** This WPD value is well above the **300 W/m²** threshold for a **"good" wind regime** (IEC Class 2 to 3), confirming the suitability of the Tamil Nadu site for utility-scale wind power generation with modern 2–3 MW turbines. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Gamma function values must be correct.** A common student error is to use $\Gamma(1.5) = 1.5$ (linear confusion). The correct value is $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.8862$. Forgetting this loses 1 full mark. > 2. **WPD uses $\bar{v}^{3}$, not $\bar{v}^{3}$.** Always compute the **mean of the cube** using the Weibull moment formula. Using $(\bar{v})^{3} = (6.65)^{3} = 294.1$ m³/s³ instead of $\overline{v^{3}} = 560.83$ m³/s³ is a **fatal under-prediction** that loses 2 marks. > 3. **Units must be carried through:** ρ in kg/m³, $c$ in m/s, WPD in W/m². Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units. > 4. **Cup anemometer overspeeding error** is a **theoretical** 3% bias that IEC corrections remove. If asked to compute WPD from a "naive" cup reading, add a one-line note about the correction. > 5. **Betz limit explanation** must include the **momentum balance with axial induction factor $a = 1/3$** — vague "flow deceleration" answers lose 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Wind Power Density (WPD)** = $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^{3}$ (W/m²) — remember the **cubic dependence**. - [ ] **Betz Limit** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ — derived using axial induction factor $a = 1/3$. - [ ] **Cup anemometer** is the legacy standard; **sonic anemometer** is research-grade; **LIDAR** is the modern remote-sensing workhorse. - [ ] **IEC 61400-12-1** mandates 10-min averaging of wind data. - [ ] **Power Law** $v_{2} = v_{1}(h_{2}/h_{1})^{\alpha}$ is empirical; **Log Law** $v(z) = (v_{*}/\kappa) \ln(z/z_{0})$ is physical. - [ ] **Weibull distribution** is the standard for wind speed frequency; $k$ = shape (typical 1.5–2.5), $c$ = scale (m/s). - [ ] **Mean Weibull speed** = $c \, \Gamma(1 + 1/k)$; **mean Weibull cube** = $c^{3} \, \Gamma(1 + 3/k)$. - [ ] **For $k = 2$ (Rayleigh):** $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2$; $\Gamma(2.5) = 3\sqrt{\pi}/4$. - [ ] **Air density formula** $\rho = P/(R_{a} T)$ with $R_{a} = 287.05$ J/(kg·K). - [ ] **NIWE Chennai** = nodal technical body for wind energy in India. - [ ] **MNRE** = nodal policy ministry; offers GBI and AD benefits. - [ ] **India's onshore potential:** ~302 GW @ 80 m; ~695.50 GW @ 120 m (revised). - [ ] **Installed capacity (India, 2024):** ~47 GW (4th globally). - [ ] **Top 5 wind states:** Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan, Maharashtra. - [ ] **LIDAR limitations:** requires aerosol backscatter; cannot replace met-mast for power performance testing as primary reference. - [ ] **Tower shadow correction** is mandatory for cup anemometer data from sectors within ±60° of the boom. - [ ] **MCP (Measure-Correlate-Predict)** is required to extend short-term measurements to long-term climatology. - [ ] **Wind rose** is a polar histogram of wind direction frequency, often combined with directional WPD. - [ ] **Capacity Factor** $CF = E_{actual} / E_{rated \cdot 8760h}$ — typical Indian onshore $CF \approx 0.22$–$0.32$. - [ ] **WPD classification:** > 500 W/m² = excellent, 300–500 = good, 200–300 = fair, < 200 = poor. - [ ] **Gamma function values to memorize:** $\Gamma(1) = 1$; $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2$; $\Gamma(2.5) = 3\sqrt{\pi}/4$.

topics.title: Basic Components of Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Basic Components of Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) # Module 2 — Basic Components of Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an integrated electromechanical assembly designed to intercept the **kinetic energy** present in moving air masses (wind) and convert it sequentially into **rotational mechanical energy**, then into **usable electrical energy** through an electromagnetic generator. It functions entirely on the principle of aerodynamic lift/drag forces exerted by the wind stream on the rotor blades, governed by the laws of fluid dynamics and Bernoulli's principle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of PEMUT864, the WECS is analyzed as a *system*, not just a turbine. Students must be able to enumerate, identify, and explain the function of every physical and electrical subsystem — from the blade tip to the grid interface. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Windmill Meets Generator" Picture Imagine a giant **ceiling fan running in reverse**: instead of using electricity to spin blades that push air, the *incoming air itself spins the blades*, and that rotation is mechanically routed to turn a small copper-and-magnet machine at the back which *generates* electricity. Every component in a WECS exists either to **capture wind**, **transmit rotation**, **control orientation/speed**, or **convert rotation to electricity**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Air density (ρ)** at sea level: **1.225 kg/m³** - **Betz coefficient (Cₚ, max)** theoretical upper limit: **16/27 ≈ 0.593 (59.3%)** - **Cut-in wind speed** (typical): **3–4 m/s** - **Rated wind speed** (typical): **12–15 m/s** - **Cut-out wind speed** (safety): **25 m/s** - **Survival wind speed**: **60–70 m/s** > [!NOTE] > These numerical thresholds are *board-favourite* values. Memorize them — they appear frequently in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 System-Level Decomposition A horizontal-axis WECS (the dominant utility-scale design) is best decomposed into **four functional blocks**: 1. **Aerodynamic Capture Subsystem** — Rotor blades + Hub 2. **Mechanical Transmission Subsystem** — Low-speed shaft + Gearbox + High-speed shaft 3. **Electrical Conversion Subsystem** — Generator + Power electronics + Transformer 4. **Control, Safety & Support Subsystem** — Yaw system, Pitch system, Brake, Anemometer, Controller, Tower ### 2.2 Component-by-Component Functional Analysis #### A. Aerodynamic Capture Subsystem | Component | Function | Engineering Rationale | |---|---|---| | **Rotor Blades** | Convert kinetic energy of wind into rotational torque | Shaped as airfoils; lift force drives rotation | | **Hub** | Mounts blades to the main shaft; pitch mechanism housing | Distributes mechanical load evenly | | **Spinner / Nose Cone** | Streamlines hub aerodynamically | Reduces drag, protects hub internals | #### B. Mechanical Transmission Subsystem | Component | Function | Engineering Rationale | |---|---|---| | **Low-Speed Shaft (Rotor Shaft)** | Carries torque from hub to gearbox | Operates at ~15–20 rpm; high torque, low speed | | **Gearbox (Speed Increaser)** | Steps up rotational speed to generator-compatible rpm | Generators need ~1500 rpm (grid sync); ratio typically 1:75 to 1:100 | | **High-Speed Shaft** | Couples gearbox to generator | High rpm, lower torque | #### C. Electrical Conversion Subsystem | Component | Function | Engineering Rationale | |---|---|---| | **Generator** | Converts mechanical rotation to electrical AC | Usually DFIG (Doubly-Fed Induction Generator) or PMSG (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator) | | **Power Electronics Converter** | Rectifies/regulates frequency and voltage | AC-DC-AC conversion for variable-speed operation | | **Transformer** | Steps up voltage for grid transmission | Grid requires high voltage (e.g., 33 kV, 132 kV) | #### D. Control, Safety & Support Subsystem | Component | Function | Engineering Rationale | |---|---|---| | **Yaw Motor & Mechanism** | Rotates nacelle to face wind direction | Maximizes capture; misaligned rotor loses power | | **Pitch Control System** | Adjusts blade pitch angle (β) | Limits power above rated wind speed; feathering in storms | | **Anemometer** | Measures wind speed at hub height | Feeds signal to controller for cut-in/cut-out decisions | | **Wind Vane** | Measures wind direction | Works with yaw system | | **Controller (SCADA-based)** | Brain of the turbine | Monitors, protects, optimizes | | **Mechanical/Aerodynamic Brake** | Stops rotor in emergencies | Disc brake on high-speed shaft; aerodynamic stall braking | | **Tower** | Elevates rotor into stronger, less turbulent wind | Power ∝ v³, so hub height matters enormously | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Aerodynamic Foundation | Formula | LaTeX | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | Power in wind | $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$ | Kinetic flux through swept area A | | Power extracted by turbine | $P_{\text{mech}} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$ | Actual mechanical output | | Power coefficient | $C_{p} = \dfrac{P_{\text{mech}}}{P_{\text{wind}}}$ | Efficiency of conversion | | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ | Rotor tip speed / wind speed | | Betz Limit | $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Theoretical upper bound | | Swept area | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | For horizontal-axis rotor | | Generator rpm (after gearbox) | $N_{g} = G \cdot N_{r}$ | G = gearbox ratio | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** All mathematical separators above use `\dfrac` and `\tfrac` rather than vertical bars to remain cleanly inside the markdown table row. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility WECS components underpin: - **Utility-scale wind farms** (5 MW+ turbines feeding national grids). - **Distributed/off-grid systems** (small turbines for telecom towers, rural electrification). - **Hybrid renewable systems** (Wind-PV-Diesel hybrids for islands — relevant to Kerala's Lakshadweep and hilly tribal hamlets). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Power Available in Wind **Premise:** A fluid mass of density $\rho$ moving with velocity $v$ through a cross-sectional area $A$ possesses kinetic energy. **Step 1 — Mass flow rate through area A:** The mass of air passing per second is density times volumetric flow: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$$ where $A$ is the rotor swept area (m²), $v$ is the undisturbed upstream wind speed (m/s), and $\rho$ is air density (kg/m³). **Step 2 — Kinetic energy per unit mass:** For a parcel of air in motion: $$\text{KE per kg} = \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Power (rate of kinetic energy flux):** Multiplying the mass flow rate by the specific kinetic energy: $$P_{\text{wind}} = \dot{m} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}$$ Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho A v$: $$\boxed{P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}}$$ **Conversion Logic:** This equation confirms the **cubic relationship** between wind speed and available power — doubling wind speed yields **8× the power**, which is why KTU examiners emphasize site selection with care. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Mechanical Power Extracted by Turbine **Premise:** A real turbine cannot capture 100% of the kinetic flux. The fraction captured is the power coefficient $C_p$. **Step 1 — Define $C_p$:** $$C_{p} = \frac{P_{\text{mech}}}{P_{\text{wind}}}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply:** $$P_{\text{mech}} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$$ **Step 3 — Express in terms of swept area $A = \pi R^{2}$:** $$P_{\text{mech}} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,\pi R^{2}\,v^{3}$$ **Conversion Logic:** $C_p$ depends on **Tip Speed Ratio (λ)** and **Blade Pitch Angle (β)**. The optimal operating point maximizes $C_p$. --- ## 3.3 Tip Speed Ratio and Gearbox Ratio Relationship **Step 1 — Define TSR:** $$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v}$$ **Step 2 — Express rotational speed in rpm:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N_{r}}{60}$$ **Step 3 — Substituting:** $$\lambda = \frac{2\pi N_{r} R}{60\,v}$$ **Step 4 — Generator speed after gearbox:** The gearbox has ratio $G = N_g / N_r$, hence: $$N_{g} = G \cdot N_{r}$$ **Conversion Logic:** At $v = 12$ m/s with $R = 40$ m and $\lambda_{\text{opt}} = 7$, the rotor rpm is ~20 rpm; the gearbox lifts this to ~1500 rpm for grid-synchronous generators. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Interdependency | Subsystem | Feeds Into | Failure Mode | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | Blades | Hub | Erosion, lightning | Lightning rod, leading-edge protection | | Gearbox | High-speed shaft | Gear wear, overheating | Oil cooling, vibration sensors | | Generator | Transformer | Insulation breakdown | Temperature sensors, SCADA trips | | Yaw system | Nacelle orientation | Cable twist | Cable twist counters, swivel joint | | Controller | All subsystems | Sensor failure | Redundant sensors, watchdog timers | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Horizontal-Axis WECS ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[WIND STREAM
Kinetic Energy Source] --> A2[ROTOR BLADES
3-blade airfoil array] A2 --> A3[HUB
Connects blades to shaft] A3 --> A4[LOW-SPEED SHAFT
~15 to 20 rpm] A4 --> A5[GEARBOX
Speed increaser 1 to 90] A5 --> A6[HIGH-SPEED SHAFT
~1500 rpm] A6 --> A7[GENERATOR
DFIG or PMSG] A7 --> A8[POWER ELECTRONICS
AC-DC-AC Converter] A8 --> A9[STEP-UP TRANSFORMER
0.69 kV to 33 kV] A9 --> A10[GRID INTERFACE
Substation] B1[ANEMOMETER] --> B5[CONTROLLER SCADA] B2[WIND VANE] --> B5 B3[YAW DRIVE] --> B5 B4[PITCH ACTUATORS] --> B5 B5 -.Control Signals.-> A2 B5 -.Control Signals.-> B3 B5 -.Control Signals.-> B4 B5 -.Control Signals.-> A8 C1[BRAKE SYSTEM] -.Emergency Stop.-> A6 C2[TOWER] --- A3 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic of Subsystem Grouping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CAPTURE[Aerodynamic Capture] D1[Blades] D2[Hub] D3[Nose Cone] end subgraph MECH[Mechanical Transmission] E1[Low Speed Shaft] E2[Gearbox] E3[High Speed Shaft] end subgraph ELEC[Electrical Conversion] F1[Generator] F2[Power Electronics] F3[Transformer] F4[Grid] end subgraph CTRL[Control and Safety] G1[Anemometer] G2[Wind Vane] G3[Yaw Motor] G4[Pitch System] G5[Brake] G6[SCADA Controller] end D1 --> D2 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 G6 --> G3 G6 --> G4 G6 --> G5 G6 --> F2 G1 --> G6 G2 --> G6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** Complex aerodynamic hardware cannot be cleanly drawn with text alone. Functional block diagrams convey *signal flow*, *control loops*, and *subsystem boundaries* — exactly what KTU examiners reward in 14-mark descriptive answers. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **List any six basic components of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS).** **Model Answer:** The six basic components of a horizontal-axis WECS are: 1. **Rotor blades** – capture wind kinetic energy via aerodynamic lift. 2. **Hub** – mounts the blades and connects them to the low-speed shaft. 3. **Gearbox** – increases the low rotor rpm (~15–20) to the high rpm (~1500) needed by the generator. 4. **Generator** – converts mechanical rotation into electrical energy (commonly DFIG or PMSG). 5. **Yaw mechanism** – rotates the nacelle so the rotor faces the wind. 6. **Controller (with anemometer)** – monitors wind conditions, governs cut-in/cut-out, pitch, and yaw. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the function of the yaw mechanism in a WECS?** **Model Answer:** The **yaw mechanism** is a motor-driven orientation system that rotates the **nacelle** about the vertical tower axis so that the rotor disc always faces the incoming wind direction. This ensures **maximum energy capture** and prevents lateral loads on the blades. It works in conjunction with the **wind vane** and **controller**, which provide continuous feedback. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic components of a Wind Energy Conversion System.** **Model Solution:** A Wind Energy Conversion System converts the **kinetic energy of wind** into **electrical energy** through a series of mechanical and electrical stages. The major components, grouped by function, are: **1. Aerodynamic Capture Subsystem:** - **Rotor Blades:** Designed as airfoil cross-sections. Wind flowing over them produces a net lift force, generating torque. Modern turbines use **3 blades** to balance cost, vibration, and capture. - **Hub:** The central mounting that holds the blades and transmits their combined torque to the low-speed shaft. - **Spinner/Nose Cone:** Reduces drag at the hub front. **2. Mechanical Transmission Subsystem:** - **Low-Speed Shaft:** Turns at the rotor's low rpm (~15–20), carrying very high torque. - **Gearbox:** Steps up rotational speed (typically ratio 1:75 to 1:100) to generator-compatible rpm. - **High-Speed Shaft:** Drives the generator at high rpm, lower torque. **3. Electrical Conversion Subsystem:** - **Generator:** Usually a **Doubly-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG)** or **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG)**. - **Power Electronics Converter:** Rectifier-inverter assembly enabling **variable-speed** operation. - **Step-up Transformer:** Elevates voltage to grid levels (e.g., 33 kV). **4. Control, Safety & Support Subsystem:** - **Yaw Motor + Wind Vane:** Rotates nacelle into the wind. - **Pitch Control:** Adjusts blade pitch angle β to regulate power. - **Anemometer:** Measures wind speed. - **Controller (SCADA):** Acts as the brain; issues cut-in/cut-out, pitch, yaw commands. - **Brake System:** Mechanical disc + aerodynamic braking for emergency stops. - **Tower:** Elevates the rotor into stronger, more laminar wind. **Block Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 above. --- **(b) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the power available in wind and explain why the Betz limit is 16/27.** **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** Consider wind of velocity $v$ passing through the rotor swept area $A$. **Step 1 — Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m} = \rho A v$$ **Step 2 — Kinetic energy per unit mass:** $$\text{KE}_{\text{per kg}} = \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Power in the wind (rate of KE flux):** $$P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$ **Step 4 — Practical extraction:** A real turbine extracts only a fraction $C_p$: $$P_{\text{mech}} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$ **Step 5 — Betz Limit:** Using momentum theory for an ideal actuator disc, Betz showed that the **maximum possible $C_p$** is: $$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$ **Why 16/27?** - As wind approaches the disc, it must **decelerate** — but it cannot be brought to zero (then air would pile up indefinitely). - The **optimum axial induction factor** $a = 1/3$ maximizes the kinetic-to-mechanical energy transfer. - Plugging $a = 1/3$ into the momentum-theory power expression yields $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2 = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot (\tfrac{2}{3})^2 = \tfrac{16}{27}$. This is the theoretical maximum — **no turbine can exceed 59.3% aerodynamic efficiency**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the role of the gearbox, pitch control system, and braking system in a WECS.** **Model Solution:** **1. Gearbox:** The rotor operates at a slow speed (~15–20 rpm) while grid-synchronous generators require ~1500 rpm (or 1800 rpm at 60 Hz). The **gearbox**, typically a multi-stage planetary/helical assembly, steps up the speed by a factor of ~75 to ~100. Without the gearbox, the generator would produce very low-frequency, unusable electricity. The trade-off is mechanical wear, noise, and the need for oil lubrication and vibration monitoring. **2. Pitch Control System:** Each blade is mounted on a **pitch bearing**, allowing it to rotate about its own long axis. **Pitch actuators** (hydraulic or electric) change the **blade pitch angle β**: - **Below rated wind speed:** β is set to the **aerodynamic optimum** (low β) for maximum capture. - **Above rated wind speed:** β is increased to **shed excess lift**, thereby **limiting power output** to the rated value. - **Cut-out / emergency:** β is rotated to **90° (feathered)**, presenting the blade flat to the wind — aerodynamic braking. **3. Braking System:** Two complementary braking methods are used: - **Mechanical (disc) brake:** Mounted on the high-speed shaft, hydraulically actuated. Used for parking and emergency stops. - **Aerodynamic (stall/pitch) brake:** Achieved by feathering the blades to ~90°; this is the *primary* safety mechanism because it requires no power — a fail-safe feature. Together, these systems ensure safe operation across the full wind-speed envelope. --- **(b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the working of a horizontal-axis wind turbine and discuss the function of the tower.** **Model Solution:** **Working of HAWT:** 1. Wind strikes the rotor blades at hub height. 2. The **aerodynamic lift** on the airfoil-shaped blades produces torque, rotating the rotor. 3. The **hub** transmits this torque to the **low-speed shaft**. 4. The **gearbox** increases the rpm to generator-compatible values. 5. The **generator** converts rotational mechanical energy into electrical energy. 6. The **power electronics** condition the variable-frequency AC into grid-quality AC. 7. The **transformer** steps up the voltage for transmission. 8. The **controller**, using inputs from the anemometer and wind vane, governs **yaw alignment** and **pitch angle** to optimize capture and protect the machine. **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1. **Function of the Tower:** - **Elevation:** Wind speed increases with height (power law profile $v(h) = v_{0}(h/h_{0})^{\alpha}$). A taller tower accesses stronger, steadier wind. - **Power Impact:** Since $P \propto v^{3}$, even a small increase in wind speed yields substantial gains. - **Structural Support:** Bears the entire static + dynamic load of nacelle + rotor. - **Types:** Lattice tower (cheaper), tubular steel tower (stiffer, preferred for large turbines), and concrete tower (newer hybrid designs). A typical 2 MW turbine uses a **tubular steel tower** of height ~80–100 m with hub diameter ~4 m at the top. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in WECS questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the cubic relation** $P \propto v^{3}$ — examiners allocate full 2 marks for explicitly stating this. > 2. **Confusing gearbox direction** — gearboxes *increase* speed (reduce torque); many students write the reverse. > 3. **Skipping the Betz limit derivation** — merely *stating* 16/27 is insufficient; show $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$ at $a = 1/3$. > 4. **No block diagram** — in 7-mark descriptive parts, a block diagram is *mandatory*. Missing it costs 2 marks. > 5. **Mixing up yaw vs. pitch** — yaw rotates the *nacelle*; pitch rotates each *blade* on its own axis. > 6. **Omitting cut-in / rated / cut-out wind speeds** — these are compulsory in any "WECS basics" answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ WECS = **Aerodynamic Capture + Mechanical Transmission + Electrical Conversion + Control & Safety**. - ✅ Rotor blades are **airfoils**; lift force creates torque. - ✅ Hub connects **3 blades** to the **low-speed shaft**. - ✅ Gearbox steps up rpm from **~15 to ~1500**. - ✅ Generator: typically **DFIG or PMSG**. - ✅ Yaw system orients nacelle to wind; **pitch system** regulates blade angle. - ✅ Anemometer + Wind Vane + **SCADA Controller** form the control triad. - ✅ **Brakes:** mechanical disc (on high-speed shaft) + aerodynamic feathering (fail-safe). - ✅ **Tower** height matters because $P_{\text{wind}} \propto v^{3}$. - ✅ $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$, $C_p \le \tfrac{16}{27}$, $\lambda = \omega R / v$. - ✅ **Cut-in (~3–4 m/s), Rated (~12–15 m/s), Cut-out (~25 m/s)** — memorize these three thresholds. - ✅ Always include a **block diagram** in 7+ mark answers.

topics.title: Classification of WECS: Horizontal Axis (Single, Double, and Multiblade) and Vertical Axis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of WECS: Horizontal Axis (Single, Double, and Multiblade) and Vertical Axis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an integrated mechanical-electrical apparatus that converts the **kinetic energy of moving air (wind)** into useful mechanical work, which is subsequently transformed into electrical energy via a generator. Classification of WECS is principally based on the **orientation of the rotor shaft** relative to the ground (and hence the wind direction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> According to the PEMUT864 syllabus, WECS are broadly classified into:
> 1. **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT)** — Rotor shaft is **parallel** to the ground/horizontal plane; blades rotate about a horizontal axis.
> 2. **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT)** — Rotor shaft is **perpendicular** to the ground; blades rotate about a vertical axis.

### 1.2 Sub-Classification Tree

| Class | Sub-Type | Blade Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| **HAWT** | Single-Blade | 1 blade + counterweight |
| **HAWT** | Double-Blade | 2 blades diametrically opposite |
| **HAWT** | Multi-Blade | 3 blades (most common) |
| **VAWT** | Savonius | S-shaped / drag-based |
| **VAWT** | Darrieus | Egg-beater / lift-based |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine holding a **bamboo stick (horizontal)** with a paper pinwheel attached, and you run forward — wind hits the blades head-on, and the pinwheel spins around the horizontal stick. That is a **HAWT** (like most modern wind farms you see in Muppandal, Tamil Nadu or Kanjikode, Kerala).
>
> Now imagine fixing the pinwheel on a **vertical pole** (like a ceiling fan mounted sideways on a pole) — wind blowing from any horizontal direction makes it spin. That is a **VAWT**. It does not need to "turn its face" toward the wind.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Definitions)

- **Rotor Diameter (D)** — Diameter of the swept area, in **metres (m)**.
- **Swept Area (A)** — Area of the circular disc traced by the blades; $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, in **m²**.
- **Hub Height (h)** — Height of the rotor centre above ground, in **m**.
- **Rated Power** — Maximum continuous electrical output under design wind speed, in **kW or MW**.
- **Cut-in, Rated, Cut-out Wind Speeds** — Typically **3–4 m/s**, **12–15 m/s**, **25 m/s** respectively.
- **Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$)** — Ratio of blade tip speed to free wind speed; dimensionless.
- **Solidity ($\sigma$)** — Ratio of total blade chord area to swept area; dimensionless.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Power-in-Wind Equation

The available **power in the wind** flowing through a swept area $A$ at velocity $v$ is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \, \rho \, A \, v^{3}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15 °C
- $A$ = swept area in **m²**
- $v$ = free-stream wind velocity in **m/s**
- $P_{wind}$ is in **watts (W)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $v^3$ matters:** Doubling wind speed gives **8× the power**. This cubic dependence is why site selection (high-wind zones) is critical in WECS engineering.

### 2.2 Power Extracted by Turbine

A turbine can extract only a fraction of $P_{wind}$:

$$P_{turbine} = C_{p} \, P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \, C_{p} \, \rho \, A \, v^{3}$$

where:
- $C_{p}$ = **power coefficient** (efficiency of energy extraction)
- **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ (59.3%)

### 2.3 Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi D \, n}{v}$$

where:
- $\omega$ = angular velocity in **rad/s**
- $R$ = rotor radius in **m** ($R = D/2$)
- $n$ = rotational speed in **revolutions per second (rps)**
- $v$ = wind velocity in **m/s**

### 2.4 Solidity ($\sigma$)

$$\sigma = \frac{N \, c}{\pi D} = \frac{N \, c}{2 \pi R}$$

where:
- $N$ = number of blades
- $c$ = blade chord length in **m**

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power in wind | $P_{w} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | W | Cubic in $v$ |
| 2 | Power extracted | $P = C_{p}\, P_{w}$ | W | $C_{p} \le 16/27$ |
| 3 | Betz limit | $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | — | Theoretical max |
| 4 | Swept area | $A = \pi D^{2}/4$ | m² | For HAWT |
| 5 | Tip speed ratio | $\lambda = \omega R / v$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Solidity | $\sigma = Nc/(\pi D)$ | — | High $\sigma \Rightarrow$ drag type |
| 7 | Torque | $T = P/\omega$ | N·m | — |
| 8 | Thrust | $F_{T} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{2} C_{T}$ | N | $C_{T} \approx 0.8$–$1.0$ |
| 9 | Rotor RPM | $N = 60 v \lambda / (\pi D)$ | rpm | For design |
| 10 | Specific speed | $n_{s} = n\sqrt{P}/(h)^{5/4}$ | — | For VAWT sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas govern **turbine selection, blade design, generator sizing, and gearbox ratio** calculations in any KTU numerical on WECS performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power-in-Wind from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define mass flow rate.**
Consider a control volume of air passing perpendicularly through swept area $A$ at velocity $v$ per unit time.

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, v \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

**Step 2 — Express kinetic energy per unit mass.**
For a fluid element of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy per second (i.e., Power).**
Substituting $\dot{m}$ for $m$ per unit time:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \, \dot{m} \, v^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Betz Limit ($C_{p,\max} = 16/27$)

Let upstream wind velocity = $v_{1}$, downstream velocity = $v_{2}$, and velocity at the rotor disc = $v$.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation:**
$$\rho A_{1} v_{1} = \rho A v = \rho A_{2} v_{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli and momentum theorem (axial direction):**
$$\text{Force on disc} = \dot{m}(v_{1} - v_{2}) = \rho A v (v_{1} - v_{2})$$

**Step 3 — Power extracted by disc = Force × disc velocity:**
$$P = F \cdot v = \rho A v^{2} (v_{1} - v_{2})$$

**Step 4 — Velocity at rotor (by continuity and momentum balance):**
$$v = \frac{v_{1} + v_{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute (4) into (3):**
$$P = \rho A \left(\frac{v_{1}+v_{2}}{2}\right)^{2} (v_{1} - v_{2})$$

**Step 6 — Define induction factor $a = (v_{1} - v)/v_{1}$ so that $v_{2} = v_{1}(1 - 2a)$ and $v = v_{1}(1-a)$:**
$$P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v_{1}^{3} \cdot 4a(1-a)^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Define power coefficient:**
$$C_{p} = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$

**Step 8 — Differentiate w.r.t. $a$ and equate to zero:**
$$\frac{d C_{p}}{d a} = 4[(1-a)^{2} - 2a(1-a)] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

Non-trivial root: $a = 1/3$.

**Step 9 — Substitute $a = 1/3$:**
$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\; (59.3\%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** No turbine — no matter how well designed — can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in the wind. The remaining ~41% must stay in the wake for downstream turbines.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HAWT vs VAWT

| Parameter | HAWT (Single/2/3-blade) | VAWT (Savonius / Darrieus) |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor axis | Horizontal (parallel to ground) | Vertical (perpendicular to ground) |
| Wind direction dependence | Requires **yaw mechanism** to face wind | Omni-directional (no yaw needed) |
| Typical $\lambda$ | High (5–10) | Low (0.3–1.5) |
| Typical $C_{p}$ | 0.35 – 0.45 | 0.15 – 0.35 |
| Starting torque | Low (except multi-blade) | High (Savonius) |
| Hub height | Tall tower needed | Generator at ground level |
| Maintenance | At hub height (difficult) | Easy (ground level) |
| Cyclic stress on blades | High (gravity cyclic load) | Low (no gravity cycle) |
| Suitable sites | High-wind, open terrain | Urban, rooftop, turbulent wind |
| Capacity range | kW to **10+ MW** | kW to ~hundreds of kW |
| Cost per kW | Lower (commercial scale) | Higher (small scale) |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: HAWT Sub-Types

| Feature | Single Blade | Double Blade | Multi-Blade (3-blade) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of blades ($N$) | 1 | 2 | 3 (or more) |
| Counterweight | **Required** | Not required | Not required |
| Solidity $\sigma$ | Very low (≈ 0.02) | Low (≈ 0.04) | Moderate (≈ 0.05–0.1) |
| Tip speed ratio $\lambda$ | Very high (10–15) | High (7–10) | Medium (5–7) |
| Starting torque | **Very poor** | Poor | Good |
| Aerodynamic efficiency | High (high $C_p$ at design $\lambda$) | High | Moderate-High |
| Cost | Lower (less material) | Moderate | Higher |
| Vibration | High (imbalance) | Moderate | Low (balanced) |
| Application | Large MW machines, prototype | Medium-large MW | **Most commercial utility-scale** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why multi-blade (3-blade) dominates the commercial market:** It offers the **best compromise** between aerodynamic efficiency, smooth torque output, low vibration, and acceptable starting torque — ideal for utility-scale grid-connected wind farms.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: VAWT Sub-Types

| Feature | Savonius | Darrieus |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | **Drag** (pressure differential) | **Lift** (aerodynamic foil) |
| Blade shape | S-shaped / bucket | Airfoil (NACA 0018 etc.) |
| Tip speed ratio $\lambda$ | 0.3 – 0.7 | 1.0 – 6.0 |
| Power coefficient $C_p$ | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| Self-starting | **Yes** (excellent) | **No** (requires push) |
| Efficiency | Low | High |
| Structural stress | Low | High (centrifugal on curved blades) |
| Application | Anemometers, water pumping | Roof-top, distributed generation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: WECS Master Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["WECS Classification"]
    node2["By Axis Orientation"]
    node3["HAWT Horizontal Axis"]
    node4["VAWT Vertical Axis"]

    node5["Single Blade HAWT"]
    node6["Double Blade HAWT"]
    node7["Multi Blade HAWT"]

    node8["Savonius VAWT"]
    node9["Darrieus VAWT"]
    node10["H Rotor VAWT"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node3 --> node5
    node3 --> node6
    node3 --> node7
    node4 --> node8
    node4 --> node9
    node4 --> node10
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: HAWT Sub-Type Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Free Wind V"]
    nodeB["Rotor Blades"]
    nodeC["Main Shaft"]
    nodeD["Gearbox"]
    nodeE["Generator"]
    nodeF["Controller and Yaw System"]

    nodeA -- Kinetic Energy --> nodeB
    nodeB -- Mechanical Rotation --> nodeC
    nodeC -- High Torque Low RPM --> nodeD
    nodeD -- High RPM Low Torque --> nodeE
    nodeE -- Electrical Output --> nodeG["Grid"]
    nodeF -- Feedback Control --> nodeB
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: VAWT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeW["Omni Directional Wind"]
    nodeS["Vertical Rotor S or H Shape"]
    nodeV["Vertical Shaft"]
    nodeG1["Gearbox at Ground"]
    nodeG2["Generator at Ground"]
    nodeG3["Output"]

    nodeW --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeG1
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2
    nodeG2 --> nodeG3
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: HAWT Sub-Type Selection Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeQ1["Need High Cp and Low Material Cost"]
    nodeQ2["Need Balance of Cost and Performance"]
    nodeQ3["Need Smooth Torque and Low Vibration"]

    nodeA1["Single Blade HAWT"]
    nodeA2["Double Blade HAWT"]
    nodeA3["Three Blade HAWT"]

    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeA1
    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeA2
    nodeQ3 -- Yes --> nodeA3
    nodeQ3 -- Large Multi Blade Water Pumping --> nodeA4["Many Blade HAWT Low Speed High Torque"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid schematics depict functional architecture and decision logic rather than geometric drawing. For geometric blade shapes (Savonius S-curve, Darrieus troposkein), refer to standard renewable energy textbooks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Classify Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS) based on the orientation of the rotor axis. Mention two advantages of Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT) over Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT).**

**Model Answer:**

WECS are classified into two main categories based on rotor axis orientation:

1. **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT):** The rotor shaft is parallel to the ground and perpendicular to the wind direction. Examples: single-blade, double-blade, three-blade (multi-blade) turbines.

2. **Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT):** The rotor shaft is perpendicular to the ground and parallel to the wind direction. Examples: Savonius and Darrieus rotors.

**Advantages of VAWT over HAWT:**
- **Omni-directional operation** — VAWTs accept wind from any horizontal direction without requiring a yaw mechanism.
- **Simplified installation and maintenance** — the generator, gearbox, and other heavy components are mounted at ground level, eliminating the need for tall tower climbing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** A 3-mark answer should mention the classification AND at least 2 advantages clearly. Avoid vague statements like "easy to use" — be specific.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) and Solidity. State their typical ranges for a high-speed HAWT and a low-speed multi-blade water-pumping windmill.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR, $\lambda$):** The ratio of the linear velocity of the blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity.
$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v}$$

- **Solidity ($\sigma$):** The ratio of the total blade chord area to the swept area of the rotor.
$$\sigma = \frac{N \, c}{\pi D}$$

**Typical ranges:**

| Turbine Type | $\lambda$ | $\sigma$ |
|---|---|---|
| High-speed HAWT (3-blade, utility-scale) | 5 – 8 | 0.03 – 0.08 (low) |
| Low-speed multi-blade water-pumping | 1 – 2 | 0.3 – 0.8 (high) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "high-speed" with "high TSR" — actually high-speed turbines have **high TSR** but **low solidity**, since fewer/thinner blades cut through air faster.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, classify the various types of Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) based on the number of blades and describe the relative merits and demerits of each type. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a Darrieus Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT) with a suitable diagram. Compare its performance with the Savonius VAWT. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Classification of HAWT based on number of blades:**

| Type | Configuration | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-blade HAWT** | 1 blade + counterweight on opposite hub | Lowest blade material cost; highest TSR possible; very high $C_p$ at design point | Severe imbalance; very high vibration; poor starting torque; needs heavy counterweight |
| **Double-blade HAWT** | 2 blades, diametrically opposite | Reduced material compared to 3-blade; good $C_p$ | Slight visual "flicker"; moderate vibration |
| **Multi-blade (3-blade)** | 3 blades at 120° spacing | **Best balance**; smooth torque; low vibration; aesthetically preferred | Highest blade cost; slightly lower peak $C_p$ than 2-blade |

**Multi-blade (>3 blades):** Used in **low-speed, high-torque applications** like water-pumping windmills (American Farm Windmill). They have **high solidity**, low TSR, and high starting torque.

**(b) Darrieus VAWT — Construction and Working:**

**Construction:** It consists of **two or three curved airfoil blades** (typically NACA 0018 or 0015 profile) attached to a **vertical shaft**. The blades trace a **troposkein curve** so that centrifugal and aerodynamic stresses remain nearly constant along the blade length.

**Working:** As wind blows across the blades, each blade experiences an **aerodynamic lift force**. Since the blades move in a circular path, the lift force has a tangential component at every azimuthal position that drives the rotor. The Darrieus is a **purely lift-driven** device and cannot self-start; it requires an external motor push or a Savonius stage for starting.

**Comparison with Savonius:**

| Parameter | Savonius | Darrieus |
|---|---|---|
| Driving mechanism | Drag | Lift |
| Tip speed ratio $\lambda$ | 0.3 – 0.7 | 1.0 – 6.0 |
| Power coefficient $C_p$ | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| Self-starting | **Yes** | **No** |
| Efficiency | Low | Higher |
| Blade stress | Low | High (centrifugal) |
| Typical use | Anemometers, small pumps | Rooftop, distributed gen |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for power available in wind. A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m**, operating at a wind speed of **12 m/s**. Air density is **1.226 kg/m³**. Calculate (i) the power in the wind, (ii) the maximum extractable power at Betz limit, and (iii) the actual power output if $C_p = 0.40$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the construction and working of a **Savonius VAWT** and a **multi-blade (water-pumping) HAWT**. Compare their typical performance parameters. **(7 Marks)**

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Derivation and Numerical:**

**Derivation (steps):**
- Mass flow rate through area $A$: $\dot{m} = \rho A v$
- Kinetic energy per unit mass = $v^{2}/2$
- Power in wind per unit time:
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}v^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$$

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ m $\Rightarrow R = 40$ m
- $v = 12$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.226$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Swept area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^{2}}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Power in wind:**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}(1.226)(5026.55)(12)^{3}$$

Compute $12^{3} = 1728$.

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.226 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.226 \times 8,685,878.4$$

$$P_{wind} = 5.32 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum extractable power (Betz limit, $C_p = 16/27$):**
$$P_{Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \times P_{wind} = 0.5926 \times 5.32 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_{Betz} \approx 3.15 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 — Actual power at $C_p = 0.40$:**
$$P_{actual} = 0.40 \times 5.32 \text{ MW} = 2.13 \text{ MW}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Swept area | $A$ | 5026.55 m² |
| Power in wind | $P_{wind}$ | 5.32 MW |
| Betz-limit power | $P_{Betz}$ | 3.15 MW |
| Actual power ($C_p = 0.4$) | $P_{actual}$ | 2.13 MW |

**(b) Savonius VAWT vs Multi-Blade HAWT:**

**Savonius VAWT:**
- **Construction:** Two (or more) half-cylindrical "S-shaped" buckets mounted on a vertical shaft. The concave and convex surfaces facing the wind produce a pressure differential.
- **Working:** It operates on the **principle of drag**. The concave bucket experiences higher drag than the convex one, producing a net torque that rotates the shaft. It has **high starting torque** and self-starts at very low wind speeds (≈ 2–3 m/s).
- **Performance:** $\lambda \approx 0.3$–$0.7$; $C_p \approx 0.15$–$0.25$; suited for **anemometers, ventilation fans, small pumps**.

**Multi-Blade HAWT (Water-Pumping):**
- **Construction:** 12–24 flat metal blades mounted on a horizontal hub atop a tall lattice tower. A tail vane keeps the rotor facing the wind.
- **Working:** Designed for **high torque at low RPM**, directly coupled to a reciprocating pump via a crank mechanism. Operates at low TSR but high solidity.
- **Performance:** $\lambda \approx 1$–$2$; $C_p \approx 0.20$–$0.30$; suited for **rural water lifting, battery charging**.

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Savonius VAWT | Multi-Blade HAWT |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Drag | Lift + Drag |
| TSR $\lambda$ | 0.3 – 0.7 | 1 – 2 |
| Solidity $\sigma$ | ~1 | 0.3 – 0.8 |
| $C_p$ | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| Self-starting | Excellent | Good |
| Tower height | Low | Tall |
| Application | Anemometry, micro-generation | Water pumping |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not skip derivation steps** — losing 1–2 marks on a 7-mark derivation question is common when students write the final formula without showing the logical flow.
> - **Do not confuse "power in wind" with "power extracted."** Always state the **Betz limit** explicitly when discussing $C_p$.
> - **Units! Units! Units!** — KTU examiners deduct heavily if wind speed is left in km/h instead of m/s. Always convert before substituting.
> - **For HAWT sub-types:** Many students forget the **counterweight** in single-blade HAWT — always mention it for full marks.
> - **For VAWT:** A common error is calling Darrieus "self-starting" — it is **NOT** self-starting (Savonius is).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ WECS = Wind Energy Conversion System; classified into **HAWT** (shaft horizontal) and **VAWT** (shaft vertical).
- ✅ HAWT sub-types: **Single-blade** (with counterweight), **Double-blade** (2 blades), **Multi-blade** (3-blade commercial; many-blade for water-pumping).
- ✅ VAWT sub-types: **Savonius** (drag, self-starting), **Darrieus** (lift, NOT self-starting), **H-rotor** (straight-bladed Darrieus).
- ✅ **Power in wind:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ — cubic in wind speed.
- ✅ **Betz Limit:** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ (theoretically, no turbine can exceed this).
- ✅ **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / v$ — high for high-speed HAWT (5–10), low for VAWT (0.3–6).
- ✅ **Solidity:** $\sigma = Nc/(\pi D)$ — high solidity → high starting torque, low speed; low solidity → high speed, low starting torque.
- ✅ **VAWT advantages:** omni-directional, no yaw, ground-level maintenance.
- ✅ **VAWT disadvantages:** lower $C_p$, pulsating torque (Darrieus), less commercially proven.
- ✅ **Single-blade HAWT** → highest TSR and lowest cost but poor self-starting and severe imbalance.
- ✅ **Three-blade HAWT** → **industry standard** for utility-scale wind farms (best balance of efficiency, smoothness, cost).
- ✅ **Multi-blade (>3) HAWT** → low-speed, high-torque; used in **water-pumping** applications (American farm windmill).
- ✅ **Savonius** is the only VAWT that **self-starts** reliably; **Darrieus** must be push-started.
- ✅ **Constant values to memorise:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³; Betz coefficient = 16/27.
- ✅ **Standard wind speed categories:** Cut-in ≈ 3–4 m/s; Rated ≈ 12–15 m/s; Cut-out ≈ 25 m/s.
- ✅ **Examiners love numericals on $P_{wind}$, Betz power, and TSR** — practice at least 3 such problems before the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction
Photosynthesis Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Photosynthesis Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Photosynthesis** is the biophysical and biochemical process by which phototrophic organisms (green plants, algae, cyanobacteria, and certain bacteria) capture **electromagnetic radiation in the visible spectrum (400 nm – 700 nm)** and convert it into chemical energy stored in organic compounds (primarily carbohydrates), using carbon dioxide and water as primary reactants.

The generalized stoichiometric representation is:

$$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Light energy}, \, Chlorophyll} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$

In thermodynamic terms, photosynthesis is a **non-spontaneous (endergonic) uphill redox reaction** with a standard Gibbs free energy change of:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = +2870 \text{ kJ/mol of glucose}$$

This energy is supplied exclusively by photons absorbed by antenna pigments (chlorophyll-a, chlorophyll-b, carotenoids, and phycobilins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEMUT864, Module 3):**
> Photosynthesis is the *foundational bioconversion process* that governs the formation of **biomass** — the precursor to all solid, liquid, and gaseous biofuels (bioethanol, biodiesel, biogas, and producer gas). Every biofuel engineering calculation begins with this equation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a leaf as a **biological solar panel with a built-in battery factory**:

- **Solar Panel Function** → Chloroplast thylakoid membranes absorb sunlight, just like PV cells absorb photons.
- **Wiring** → Electron transport chains (Photosystem II → Cytochrome b₆f → Photosystem I) transport excited electrons, similar to electrical wiring.
- **Battery Factory** → The Calvin cycle (in the stroma) uses the electrical "current" (ATP and NADPH) to "manufacture" glucose — the stored chemical battery.
- **Green Color** → Chlorophyll reflects green light (~500–570 nm) and absorbs blue (~430 nm) and red (~660 nm) wavelengths, which is precisely why plants appear green and why solar spectrum matching is critical for efficient photosynthesis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> **Solar constant at Earth's surface (AM 1.5): G_sc ≈ 1367 W/m²**
> **Average photosynthetically active radiation (PAR): ~45% of total solar flux**
> **Maximum theoretical photosynthetic efficiency: ~11%** (limited by thermodynamics and photochemistry).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Functional Stages of Photosynthesis

Photosynthesis is universally divided into two sequential, spatially separated stages:

#### **Stage I — Light-Dependent Reactions (Photophosphorylation)**

Occurs in the **thylakoid membranes** of chloroplasts.

- **Step 1 — Photon Capture:** Photons strike the **P680 reaction center** of **Photosystem II (PS II)**, exciting electrons to a higher energy state.
- **Step 2 — Water Splitting (Photolysis):** PS II extracts electrons from water via the **oxygen-evolving complex (OEC)**:

$$2H_2O \xrightarrow{4 \, h\nu, \, Mn_4CaO_5 \, cluster} O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$$

- **Step 3 — Electron Transport:** Excited electrons travel along the **Z-scheme** through plastoquinone (PQ), the **cytochrome b₆f complex**, and plastocyanin (PC) to **Photosystem I (PS I)**.
- **Step 4 — Re-excitation at PS I (P700):** A second photon re-energizes the electron, which is then passed to **ferredoxin (Fd)** and finally to **NADP⁺ reductase**, producing **NADPH**.
- **Step 5 — Chemiosmotic ATP Synthesis:** Proton gradient ($\Delta \mu_{H^+}$) across the thylakoid membrane drives **ATP synthase**, generating **ATP** from ADP + Pi (photophosphorylation).

#### **Stage II — Light-Independent Reactions (Calvin–Benson Cycle)**

Occurs in the **stroma** of chloroplasts. Despite the name, it *requires* ATP and NADPH from Stage I.

- **Carbon Fixation:** The enzyme **RuBisCO** (Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase) catalyzes the attachment of CO₂ to Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP).
- **Reduction Phase:** 3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA) is reduced to glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P) using ATP and NADPH.
- **Regeneration Phase:** G3P is partly recycled to regenerate RuBP using additional ATP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Yield of the Calvin Cycle (per 6 CO₂ molecules fixed):**
> **1 molecule of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) + 18 ATP consumed + 12 NADPH consumed**

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Units / Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall Photosynthetic Reaction | $6CO_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$ | Stoichiometric |
| 2 | Standard Gibbs Free Energy | $\Delta G^{\circ} = +2870$ | kJ/mol |
| 3 | Quantum Requirement (minimum photons per O₂) | $\Phi \geq 8$ | photons / O₂ molecule |
| 4 | Quantum Yield | $\Phi = \dfrac{n_{O_2}}{n_{h\nu}}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Photosynthetic Efficiency | $\eta_{photo} = \dfrac{E_{chem,\,stored}}{E_{solar,\,incident}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | PAR Fraction | $f_{PAR} \approx 0.45$ of total solar | dimensionless |
| 7 | Energy Content of Dry Biomass | $HCV \approx 17$ to $20$ | MJ/kg |
| 8 | Energy Stored in 1 mole Glucose | $E_{glucose} = \Delta G^{\circ} \approx 2870$ | kJ/mol |
| 9 | Energetic Value per O₂ evolved | $E_{O_2} \approx \dfrac{2870}{6} \approx 478.3$ | kJ/mol O₂ |
| 10 | RuBisCO Turnover Number | $k_{cat} \approx 3$ to $10$ | s⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Photosynthetic efficiency directly determines the **biomass yield per hectare per year** — critical for sizing **biogas plants**, **bioethanol distilleries**, and **biodiesel feedstock farms**.
> - The **Z-scheme** inspires modern **dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs)** and **artificial photosynthesis** reactors for hydrogen production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Theoretical Maximum Photosynthetic Efficiency

The maximum theoretical efficiency is bounded by **four sequential loss factors** (the "Four-Limit Model" by Bolton & Hall, 1979 — widely used in KTU numericals).

Let $E_{solar}$ be the total incident solar energy and $E_{chem}$ be the chemical energy stored in glucose.

**Step 1 — Photon Energy Constraint (Energy Limit):**
Only photons with energy $E \geq E_g$ (the band-gap equivalent of the reaction) are useful. The fraction of usable photons in the solar spectrum yields:

$$\eta_1 = 0.36 \quad (\text{approx. } 36\%)$$

**Step 2 — Quantum Conversion Limit:**
Each photon drives at most one electron through PS II and PS I, requiring $\geq 8$ photons per O₂ molecule (4 per photosystem). This gives:

$$\eta_2 = 0.32 \quad (\text{approx. } 32\%)$$

**Step 3 — Biochemical Free Energy Limit:**
Not all absorbed energy is stored as glucose free energy. The ratio of useful chemical energy to absorbed photon energy gives:

$$\eta_3 \approx 0.40 \quad (\text{approx. } 40\%)$$

**Step 4 — Respiration & Maintenance Loss:**
Plants consume ~20–40% of fixed carbon in mitochondrial respiration, reducing net storage:

$$\eta_4 \approx 0.65 \quad (\text{approx. } 65\% \text{ retained})$$

**Combined Theoretical Maximum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max,\,theoretical} &= \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \eta_4 \\
&= 0.36 \times 0.32 \times 0.40 \times 0.65 \\
&= 0.029952 \\
&\approx 3.0\% \text{ to } 4.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

(With optimistic assumptions, theoretical ceiling reaches **~11%**, but practical field efficiencies are only **0.5% to 2%**.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Energy Stored per Mole of CO₂ Fixed

Starting from the Gibbs free energy of glucose formation:

**Step 1:** The standard free energy change for the overall reaction:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{rxn} = +2870 \text{ kJ/mol glucose}$$

**Step 2:** Per mole of CO₂ fixed (since 6 moles CO₂ are consumed):

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{CO_2} = \frac{+2870}{6}$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{CO_2} = +478.33 \text{ kJ/mol CO}_2$$

**Step 3:** Converting to energy per unit mass of CO₂ (M = 44 g/mol):

$$E_{CO_2} = \frac{478.33 \text{ kJ}}{44 \text{ g}} = 10.87 \text{ kJ/g CO}_2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{CO_2} &= 10.87 \text{ kJ/g} \\
&= 10{,}870 \text{ kJ/kg CO}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Every **1 kg of atmospheric CO₂** captured by photosynthesis stores approximately **10.87 MJ** of chemical energy — a key metric for **carbon-capture biofuel economics**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Biomass Energy Yield from Photosynthesis

**Given:**
- Daily PAR (Photosynthetically Active Radiation): $I_{PAR} = 500 \text{ W/m}^2$
- Cultivation duration: $t = 12$ hours
- Average photosynthetic efficiency (C₃ plant, field): $\eta = 1.5\%$

**Step 1 — Total incident PAR energy per m² per day:**

$$E_{inc} = I_{PAR} \times t = 500 \text{ W/m}^2 \times (12 \times 3600 \text{ s})$$

$$E_{inc} = 500 \times 43200 = 2.16 \times 10^7 \text{ J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Chemical energy stored:**

$$E_{stored} = \eta \times E_{inc} = 0.015 \times 2.16 \times 10^7$$

$$E_{stored} = 3.24 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m}^2/\text{day} = 0.324 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent dry biomass produced (using HCV = 18 MJ/kg):**

$$m_{biomass} = \frac{E_{stored}}{HCV} = \frac{0.324 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}}{18 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

$$m_{biomass} = 0.018 \text{ kg/m}^2/\text{day} = 18 \text{ g/m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Annual Yield (per hectare):**

$$Y_{annual} = 0.018 \times 365 \times 10{,}000 \approx 65{,}700 \text{ kg/ha/year}$$

$$\boxed{Y_{annual} \approx 65.7 \text{ tonnes dry biomass per hectare per year}}$$

This figure is consistent with high-yield energy crops like **Napier grass** or **sugarcane**, validating the derivation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Photosynthetic Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sunlight Photons 400 to 700 nm] --> B[Photosystem II P680]
    A2[CO2 from atmosphere] --> D[Calvin Cycle Stroma]
    A3[H2O from roots] --> B
    B -->|Photolysis| B1[O2 released to atmosphere]
    B -->|Electron transport via PQ and Cyt b6f| C[Photosystem I P700]
    C -->|Fd and NADP reductase| C1[NADPH produced]
    B -->|Proton pumping| E[ATP Synthase]
    E --> E1[ATP produced]
    C1 --> D
    E1 --> D
    D --> F[G3P Glyceraldehyde 3 phosphate]
    F --> F1[Glucose C6H12O6 stored as biomass]
    F --> F2[Regeneration of RuBP]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Z-Scheme — Electron Flow & Energy Levels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P680[PS II P680 ground state] -->|Photon hν1| P680e[P680 excited]
    P680e -->|Electron| PQ[Plastoquinone pool]
    PQ --> CB6F[Cytochrome b6f complex]
    CB6F --> PC[Plastocyanin]
    PC --> P700[PS I P700 ground state]
    P700 -->|Photon hν2| P700e[P700 excited]
    P700e --> FD[Ferredoxin]
    FD --> NADPR[NADP+ Reductase]
    NADPR --> NADPH[NADPH final acceptor]
    H2O[Water H2O] -->|Photolysis by OEC| P680
    P680 --> O2[Oxygen released]
    style P680 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P700 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style NADPH fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Energy Conversion Chain for Biofuels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SOL[Solar Radiation 1367 W per m2] --> PHOT[Photosynthesis in chloroplasts]
    PHOT -->|Efficiency 0.5 to 2 percent| BIO[Biomass formation]
    BIO -->|Thermochemical| BG[Biogas via anaerobic digestion]
    BIO -->|Biochemical| BE[Bioethanol via fermentation]
    BIO -->|Chemical| BD[Biodiesel via transesterification]
    BIO -->|Direct combustion| HP[Heat and Power]
    BG --> EL1[Electricity generation]
    BE --> TR1[Transport fuel]
    BD --> TR2[Transport fuel]
    HP --> EL2[Industrial process heat]
    style SOL fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PHOT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BIO fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

Define photosynthesis. Write the balanced stoichiometric equation and state the standard Gibbs free energy change.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Photosynthesis is the biochemical process by which green plants, algae, and cyanobacteria convert light energy into chemical energy stored in organic compounds (carbohydrates), using CO₂ and H₂O.

$$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Light, Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$

The standard Gibbs free energy change is:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = +2870 \text{ kJ/mol of glucose}$$

This positive $\Delta G^{\circ}$ confirms that the reaction is **endergonic** (non-spontaneous) and requires continuous light energy input. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for $\Delta G^{\circ}$ value.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

Differentiate between the light-dependent and light-independent reactions of photosynthesis. Mention their locations.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Light-Dependent Reactions | Light-Independent Reactions |
|---|---|---|
| Alternative Name | Photophosphorylation | Calvin–Benson Cycle |
| Location | Thylakoid membrane | Stroma |
| Requires Light? | Yes (directly) | No (but requires ATP/NADPH) |
| Main Products | ATP, NADPH, O₂ | Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) |
| Key Enzyme | ATP Synthase | RuBisCO |

(3 marks distributed: 1 for location, 1 for products, 1 for any two distinguishing features.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam Pattern – 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Z-scheme of electron transport** in photosynthesis with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of Photosystem II, Photosystem I, the cytochrome b₆f complex, and water photolysis.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A field of Napier grass receives an average PAR of **480 W/m²** for **10 hours/day**. Given the field photosynthetic efficiency is **1.8%** and biomass calorific value is **17 MJ/kg**, compute:
- (i) The daily chemical energy stored per m².
- (ii) The annual dry biomass yield in tonnes per hectare.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) — Z-Scheme Explanation [7 Marks]:**

The Z-scheme represents the **photosynthetic electron transport chain**, named for its zigzag redox potential profile.

**Step 1 — Photosystem II (P680):** [1 mark]
PS II absorbs red light (~680 nm), exciting electrons from its reaction center P680 to a higher energy level. The water-splitting **oxygen-evolving complex (Mn₄CaO₅ cluster)** extracts electrons from H₂O:

$$2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$$

**Step 2 — Electron Transport to PS I:** [2 marks]
Electrons travel through **plastoquinone (PQ)** → **cytochrome b₆f complex** (which pumps protons into the thylakoid lumen) → **plastocyanin (PC)** to PS I.

**Step 3 — Photosystem I (P700):** [1 mark]
PS I re-excites the electron using another photon (~700 nm). The electron reduces **ferredoxin (Fd)**.

**Step 4 — NADPH Formation:** [1 mark]
Ferredoxin transfers electrons to **NADP⁺ reductase**, generating **NADPH**:

$$NADP^+ + H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow NADPH$$

**Step 5 — ATP Synthesis:** [1 mark]
The proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane drives **ATP synthase**, producing ATP from ADP + Pi (chemiosmotic phosphorylation).

**Step 6 — Diagrammatic Block Representation:** [1 mark]
(Refer to Mermaid Z-scheme in Section 4 — student should sketch P680 → PQ → Cyt b₆f → PC → P700 → Fd → NADP⁺).

---

**(b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $I_{PAR} = 480 \text{ W/m}^2$, $t = 10 \text{ hr/day}$, $\eta = 1.8\% = 0.018$, $HCV = 17 \text{ MJ/kg}$.

**(i) Daily chemical energy stored per m²:**

$$E_{inc} = I_{PAR} \times t = 480 \times (10 \times 3600) = 480 \times 36000 = 1.728 \times 10^7 \text{ J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

$$E_{stored} = \eta \times E_{inc} = 0.018 \times 1.728 \times 10^7 = 3.1104 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{E_{stored} = 0.3104 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

**(ii) Annual dry biomass yield per hectare:**

Daily biomass per m²:

$$m_{daily} = \frac{E_{stored}}{HCV} = \frac{0.3104 \text{ MJ}}{17 \text{ MJ/kg}} = 0.01826 \text{ kg/m}^2/\text{day}$$

Annual biomass per hectare (1 ha = 10,000 m², 365 days):

$$Y_{annual} = 0.01826 \times 365 \times 10{,}000$$

$$\boxed{Y_{annual} \approx 66{,}649 \text{ kg/ha/year} \approx 66.65 \text{ tonnes/ha/year}} \quad \text{[4 marks]}$$

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam Pattern – 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat flowchart, describe the **Calvin Cycle (C₃ pathway)**. State the role of RuBisCO and calculate the net ATP and NADPH consumed per molecule of glucose synthesized.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The standard free energy change for photosynthesis is +2870 kJ/mol glucose. If **12 moles of photons** (each of energy **176 kJ/mol**) are required per mole of glucose, calculate:
- (i) The energy supplied by photons.
- (ii) The overall photosynthetic efficiency based on free energy conversion.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) — Calvin Cycle Description [7 Marks]:**

The Calvin Cycle (discovered by Melvin Calvin, Nobel Prize 1961) occurs in the **stroma** and consists of three phases:

**Phase 1 — Carbon Fixation [2 marks]:**
CO₂ attaches to **Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP, a 5-C sugar)** via the enzyme **RuBisCO**, forming an unstable 6-C intermediate that immediately splits into two molecules of **3-phosphoglycerate (3-PGA)**.

**Phase 2 — Reduction [2 marks]:**
3-PGA is phosphorylated by ATP → 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate (1,3-BPG), which is then reduced by NADPH → **glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P)**.

**Phase 3 — Regeneration of RuBP [2 marks]:**
5 out of every 6 G3P molecules (each 3-C) are rearranged using additional ATP to regenerate 3 molecules of RuBP. The remaining 1 G3P exits the cycle as net product → ultimately forms glucose.

**Net Stoichiometry (per 1 glucose = 6 CO₂ fixed): [1 mark]**

$$6CO_2 + 18ATP + 12NADPH \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 18ADP + 18P_i + 12NADP^+$$

$$\boxed{18 \text{ ATP and } 12 \text{ NADPH consumed per glucose molecule}}$$

---

**(b) — Free Energy Efficiency [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = +2870 \text{ kJ/mol glucose}$, $n_{h\nu} = 12$ mol photons/mol glucose, $E_{photon} = 176 \text{ kJ/mol photons}$.

**(i) Total photon energy supplied:**

$$E_{photon,\,total} = n_{h\nu} \times E_{photon} = 12 \times 176$$

$$\boxed{E_{photon,\,total} = 2112 \text{ kJ/mol glucose}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

**(ii) Photosynthetic efficiency (free energy basis):**

$$\eta_{FE} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{glucose}}{E_{photon,\,total}} \times 100 = \frac{2870}{2112} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FE} \approx 135.88\%} \quad \text{[4 marks]}$$

**Interpretation:** The value **>100%** is physically impossible for a single-pass reaction and indicates that the simplified assumption (12 photons) underestimates the true photon requirement. The thermodynamically consistent minimum is **8 photons/O₂ × 6 O₂ = 48 photons per glucose**, giving:

$$\eta_{FE,\,realistic} = \frac{2870}{48 \times 176} \times 100 = \frac{2870}{8448} \times 100 \approx 33.97\%$$

This aligns with the quantum conversion limit (~32%) from Section 3.1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Glucose Stoichiometry:** Students often write $6CO_2 + 6H_2O \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$ as $C_6H_{12}O_6$ rather than remembering it represents glucose — losing 0.5 mark for sloppy notation.
> 2. **Unit Errors in PAR Calculations:** Forgetting to convert hours → seconds ($t = 10 \text{ hr} = 36{,}000 \text{ s}$). Always multiply by 3600.
> 3. **RuBisCO Spelling:** Frequently misspelled as "Rubisco" or "Rubisco". Correct full form: **Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase**. Loss of 0.5 mark if abbreviated without expansion.
> 4. **ATP/NADPH Numbers:** Mixing up the Calvin cycle totals — **18 ATP + 12 NADPH per glucose**, not per CO₂.
> 5. **Efficiency >100% Trap:** As shown in Q-B(b), failing to comment on the physical impossibility of >100% efficiency signals weak conceptual understanding.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Photosynthesis converts solar energy to chemical energy** stored in glucose ($\Delta G^{\circ} = +2870 \text{ kJ/mol}$).
- ✅ **Overall equation:** $6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{light} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$
- ✅ **Two stages:** Light reactions (thylakoid) + Calvin cycle (stroma).
