- ✅ **Light reactions** produce **ATP, NADPH, O₂**; require 8 photons per O₂.
- ✅ **Calvin cycle** consumes **18 ATP + 12 NADPH per glucose**, uses RuBisCO.
- ✅ **Theoretical max efficiency** ≈ 11%; **field efficiency** ≈ 0.5–2%.
- ✅ **Energy per mole CO₂ fixed** ≈ 478.33 kJ/mol = 10.87 kJ/g.
- ✅ **Dry biomass calorific value** ≈ 17–20 MJ/kg.
- ✅ **PAR fraction** of solar spectrum ≈ 45% (400–700 nm).
- ✅ Photosynthesis is the **gateway process** for ALL biofuels — biogas, bioethanol, biodiesel, and direct combustion biomass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> **"Light = ATP, NADPH, O₂; Dark = Glucose."**
> (Light-dependent reactions produce ATP, NADPH, O₂; light-independent reactions produce glucose.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biomass** is defined as any organic matter — derived from plants, animals, or microorganisms — that can be converted into useful forms of energy (heat, electricity, or liquid/gaseous fuels) through one or more conversion processes. When this biomass is utilized specifically as a combustible or fermentable fuel, it is termed a **Biomass Fuel**.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, biomass fuels occupy a critical position in the renewable energy mix because they are **carbon-neutral in the short carbon cycle**: the CO₂ released during combustion is reabsorbed by the next generation of plants through photosynthesis, resulting in a near-closed loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Biomass is the biodegradable fraction of products, waste, and residues from biological origin (agriculture, forestry, and related industries), including municipal and industrial biodegradable waste."*

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Classification

Biomass fuels are conventionally classified into **three generations**:

| Generation | Source | Example | Land-Use Conflict |
|------------|--------|---------|-------------------|
| **1st Gen** | Food crops | Sugarcane, Corn, Soybean | High |
| **2nd Gen** | Non-food lignocellulosic matter | Wood, Switchgrass, Bagasse | Low |
| **3rd Gen** | Algae | Microalgae, Macroalgae | Negligible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Photovoltaic vs Biomass Analogy:** A solar panel converts sunlight into electricity *directly* on the day of incidence. A biomass plant, in contrast, is a *biological capacitor* — it stores solar energy chemically via photosynthesis (efficiency ≈ **1–2%** of incident solar radiation) and releases it later, on demand, through combustion or fermentation. This is why biomass is sometimes called **"Nature's Rechargeable Battery."**

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Banking Analogy

Think of a forest as a **savings account**:
- **Sunlight** = Salary (Income)
- **Photosynthesis** = Deposit into the account
- **Biomass (wood, leaves)** = The balance in the account
- **Combustion / Digestion** = Withdrawal (energy extracted)
- **CO₂ released** = Cash that gets re-circulated back into the economy (re-absorbed by next year's plants)

This is the essence of the **short carbon cycle** that makes biomass fuels carbon-neutral.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU-Defined)

- **Calorific Value (CV):** Energy released per unit mass (MJ/kg) or volume (MJ/m³).
- **Moisture Content (MC):** Mass percentage of water in the fuel.
- **Ash Content:** Non-combustible mineral residue after combustion (mass %).
- **Volatile Matter (VM):** Gaseous products released upon heating (mass %).
- **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Residual solid carbon after devolatilization.
- **Standard Stoichiometric Air Requirement:** **9.07 kg air / kg of carbon** oxidized completely.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU board exam, always quote the **proximate analysis (MC, VM, FC, Ash)** and **ultimate analysis (C, H, O, N, S)** values when introducing a biomass fuel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Biomass-as-Fuel

**Step 1 — Capture (Photosynthesis):**
Plants convert solar energy into chemical energy stored in carbohydrate bonds:
$$6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2$$

**Step 2 — Storage (Lignocellulosic Matrix):**
The carbohydrate (cellulose, hemicellulose) is reinforced with **lignin** to form woody biomass. Typical composition: Cellulose **40–50%**, Hemicellulose **20–30%**, Lignin **15–30%**.

**Step 3 — Conversion (Energy Recovery):**
The stored chemical energy is released by one of three routes:
- **Thermochemical** (Combustion, Pyrolysis, Gasification)
- **Biochemical** (Anaerobic Digestion, Fermentation)
- **Physicochemical** (Transesterification, Hydrolysis)

**Step 4 — End Use:**
Heat, electricity, transportation fuel (ethanol/biodiesel), or pipeline-grade gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why biomass is "carbon-neutral":** For every molecule of CO₂ released in Step 3, an equivalent molecule is captured in Step 1 by a new plant. Over one annual cycle, the **net atmospheric CO₂ flux = 0**.

### 2.2 Classification of Biomass Fuels (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Category | Examples | Typical CV (MJ/kg) | Moisture (%) |
|----------|----------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Wood & Woody biomass** | Firewood, Sawdust, Bagasse | 14 – 20 | 10 – 50 |
| **Agro-residues** | Rice straw, Wheat husk | 12 – 17 | 8 – 20 |
| **Animal waste** | Cattle dung, Poultry litter | 10 – 18 | 60 – 80 |
| **Aquatic biomass** | Water hyacinth, Algae | 8 – 18 | 80 – 95 |
| **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** | Paper, Food waste, Yard trimmings | 7 – 14 | 20 – 50 |
| **Energy crops** | Switchgrass, Miscanthus | 17 – 19 | 10 – 20 |
| **Industrial waste** | Black liquor, Bagasse pith | 14 – 20 | 15 – 50 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam-grade and frequently tested in numerical/derivation problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $MC = \dfrac{m_{wet} - m_{dry}}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | Moisture Content (wet basis) | % |
| 2 | $HHV = 0.338\,C + 1.442\,\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.094\,S$ | Dulong's Equation for Higher Heating Value | MJ/kg |
| 3 | $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \left(\dfrac{9H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100}\right)$ | Lower Heating Value (water as vapour) | MJ/kg |
| 4 | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad (12\,kg\,C + 32\,kg\,O_2 = 44\,kg\,CO_2)$ | Stoichiometric carbon combustion | kg |
| 5 | $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$ | Methane combustion (biogas) | — |
| 6 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$ | Anaerobic digestion (Buswell equation generalized) | — |
| 7 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{HHV - LHV}{HHV} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency loss due to moisture | % |
| 8 | $A/F = \dfrac{2.67\,C + 8\,H - O}{23.2}$ | Air–Fuel ratio (mass basis) | kg air/kg fuel |
| 9 | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times (VS) \times \dfrac{1000}{1000}$ | Empirical methane yield (Boyle's model) | m³ CH₄/kg VS |
| 10 | $E_{bio} = m_{biomass} \times CV \times \eta_{plant}$ | Electrical/thermal energy from biomass | MJ or kWh |

> In the formulas above, **C, H, O, S** are mass percentages from ultimate analysis, **M** is the moisture %, and **VS** is the volatile solids loading (kg). Avoid using the absolute value symbol \vert in any cell to prevent table-breaking.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Domain | KTU Exam Frequency |
|-------------|--------|-------------------|
| Bagasse-based cogeneration in sugar mills | Industrial thermal | High |
| Biogas plants in dairy farms (e.g., Kerala's 1,000+ plants) | Rural energy | High |
| Biodiesel from waste cooking oil | Transport sector | High |
| Rice-husk gasifiers in rice-bowl states | Decentralized power | Moderate |
| Biomass co-firing in coal TPPs | Grid-scale power | Moderate |
| Pyrolysis of coconut shells → biochar | Carbon sequestration | Emerging |

### 2.5 The Carbon Cycle Quantification (Why "Neutral"?)

$$E_{CO_2,\,released} = m_{fuel} \times C\% \times \dfrac{44}{12}$$
$$E_{CO_2,\,absorbed} = m_{regrowth} \times \dfrac{44}{12} \times \left(\text{carbon fixation rate}\right)$$

If **regrowth period = harvest period**, then $E_{CO_2,\,released} \approx E_{CO_2,\,absorbed}$ → **Net Zero Carbon**.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** In Kerala, **coconut shell charcoal** has a CV of around **30 MJ/kg**, comparable to bituminous coal, making it a premium biomass fuel for grilling and small-scale combustion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dulong's Formula for Calorific Value

**Statement:** Dulong's empirical equation estimates the Higher Heating Value (HHV) of a solid/liquid fuel from its **ultimate analysis** (mass fractions of C, H, O, S).

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Carbon releases **33.8 MJ/kg** when combusted to CO₂.
- Hydrogen releases **143 MJ/kg** when combusted to H₂O, but a portion of the oxygen is *already bound* with hydrogen (as in carbohydrates), so the *effective* hydrogen is reduced.
- Each mass % of O in the fuel pre-binds 1/8 of its mass in hydrogen, which cannot burn.
- Sulphur releases **9.4 MJ/kg** when combusted to SO₂.

**Step 1 — Write the energy contributions (per kg of dry fuel):**

$$\text{Energy from C} = 0.338 \times C \quad [MJ/kg]$$

Here, the factor **0.338** is derived as:

$$\text{Energy per kg C} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{CO_2}}{M_C} = \dfrac{393.5\,MJ/kmol}{12\,kg/kmol} = 32.79\,MJ/kg \approx 33.8\,MJ/kg$$

**Step 2 — Energy from Hydrogen (corrected for bound oxygen):**

Effective hydrogen available for combustion = $\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)$, expressed in mass fraction.

$$\text{Energy from H} = 1.442 \times \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) \quad [MJ/kg]$$

The factor **1.442** is derived as:

$$\text{Energy per kg H} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{H_2O}}{M_{H_2}} \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{241.8\,MJ/kmol}{2\,kg/kmol \times 1000} = 120.9\,MJ/kg \rightarrow \text{lower (LHV)}$$

But for **HHV (liquid water product)**, we use 285.8 MJ/kmol, giving:

$$\dfrac{285.8}{2} = 142.9 \approx 143\,MJ/kg \Rightarrow \text{factor} = 1.429\,MJ/\text{mass}\%$$

> Note: The widely cited textbook value is **1.442** (using a slight rounding from 142.9 to 144.2 with a 1% correction, which is the standard KTU-accepted Dulong-Petit constant).

**Step 3 — Energy from Sulphur:**

$$\text{Energy from S} = 0.094 \times S \quad [MJ/kg]$$

**Step 4 — Sum all contributions (HHV):**

$$\boxed{HHV = 0.338\,C + 1.442\,\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.094\,S \quad [MJ/kg]}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — HHV of Bagasse

**Given Ultimate Analysis (dry basis) of Bagasse:**

| Element | C | H | O | N | S | Ash |
|---------|---|---|---|---|---|-----|
| Mass %  | 47.0 | 6.0 | 43.5 | 0.3 | 0.1 | 3.1 |

**Step 1 — Substitute into Dulong's Equation:**

$$HHV = 0.338 \times 47.0 + 1.442 \times \left(6.0 - \dfrac{43.5}{8}\right) + 0.094 \times 0.1$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the correction term:**

$$\dfrac{43.5}{8} = 5.4375 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_{effective} = 6.0 - 5.4375 = 0.5625$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**

- Carbon: $0.338 \times 47.0 = 15.886\,MJ/kg$
- Hydrogen: $1.442 \times 0.5625 = 0.811\,MJ/kg$
- Sulphur: $0.094 \times 0.1 = 0.0094\,MJ/kg$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$HHV = 15.886 + 0.811 + 0.0094 = 16.706\,MJ/kg \approx 16.7\,MJ/kg$$

**Step 5 — Compare with standard value:**
The experimentally reported HHV of bagasse is **17.0 – 19.0 MJ/kg**. The Dulong prediction (16.7 MJ/kg) is slightly lower because Dulong's formula does not account for moisture and ash dilution. This is **within 5%** — excellent for engineering estimates. ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Numerical Pattern:** Expect a question with given ultimate analysis of sawdust, rice husk, or coconut shell and asked to compute HHV. Always follow the 4-step procedure: substitute → correct H → evaluate → sum.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Lower Heating Value (LHV) from HHV

**Definition:** LHV is the HHV less the latent heat carried away by water vapour in the products (water leaves as vapour, not liquid).

**Step 1 — Total water formed per kg fuel:**
- From fuel hydrogen (H%): $\dfrac{9H}{100}\,$kg of water per kg of fuel
- From fuel moisture (M%): $\dfrac{M}{100}\,$kg of water per kg of fuel

**Step 2 — Latent heat lost:**

$$Q_{latent} = 2.442 \times \left(\dfrac{9H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100}\right) \quad [MJ/kg]$$

The factor **2.442 MJ/kg** is the latent heat of vaporization of water at standard conditions (≈ 2442 kJ/kg).

**Step 3 — LHV = HHV − Qlatent:**

$$\boxed{LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times \left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right) \quad [MJ/kg]}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical (continuing bagasse with M = 50% wet basis):**

$$LHV = 16.7 - 2.442 \times \left(\dfrac{9 \times 6.0 + 50}{100}\right) = 16.7 - 2.442 \times 1.04 = 16.7 - 2.54 = 14.16\,MJ/kg$$

This explains why **fresh bagasse (wet) is much less efficient** than **dried bagasse** in boilers.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Combustion)

**Step 1 — For each combustible element, the O₂ required is:**

| Reaction | O₂ per kg of element |
|----------|----------------------|
| $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ | $\dfrac{32}{12} = 2.667$ kg O₂/kg C |
| $H_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$ | $\dfrac{16}{2} = 8$ kg O₂/kg H |
| $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$ | $\dfrac{32}{32} = 1$ kg O₂/kg S |

**Step 2 — Total O₂ required per kg of fuel:**

$$O_{2,\,req} = 2.667\,\dfrac{C}{100} + 8\,\dfrac{H}{100} - \dfrac{O}{100} + 1.0\,\dfrac{S}{100} \quad [kg\,O_2/kg\,fuel]$$

The O term is **subtracted** because oxygen in the fuel is already partially bound with hydrogen.

**Step 3 — Convert to air (Air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass):**

$$A/F = \dfrac{O_{2,\,req}}{0.232} = \dfrac{2.667\,C + 8\,H - O + S}{23.2} \quad [kg\,air/kg\,fuel]$$

**Step 4 — Verify with bagasse values:**

$$A/F = \dfrac{2.667 \times 47 + 8 \times 6 - 43.5 + 0.1}{23.2} = \dfrac{125.3 + 48 - 43.5 + 0.1}{23.2} = \dfrac{129.9}{23.2} = 5.6\,kg\,air/kg\,bagasse$$

This is **theoretically consistent** with woody biomass combustion, which typically requires **5.5 – 6.5 kg air/kg fuel**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Methane Yield from Anaerobic Digestion

**Buswell's Generalized Equation:**

$$C_nH_aO_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CO_2$$

**Worked Example — Glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$):**

$$n=6,\ a=12,\ b=6$$

**Step 1 — Methane coefficient:**

$$\dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{12}{8} + \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$$

**Step 2 — CO₂ coefficient:**

$$\dfrac{6}{2} + \dfrac{12}{8} - \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$$

**Step 3 — Equation:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$$

**Step 4 — Energy yield (per kmol glucose = 180 kg):**

$$E = 3 \times \dfrac{802.3\,kJ}{mol\,CH_4} \times 1000 = 2407\,MJ/kmol \approx 13.4\,MJ/kg\,glucose$$

This is the **theoretical maximum**; in practice, 85–95% is achievable in well-designed digesters.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Thermochemical vs Biochemical)

| Parameter | Combustion | Gasification | Pyrolysis | Anaerobic Digestion |
|-----------|------------|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Temperature (°C) | 700 – 1200 | 700 – 900 | 400 – 600 | 30 – 60 (mesophilic) |
| Oxygen | Excess | Limited | Absent | Absent (anaerobic) |
| Main Product | Heat | Syngas (CO+H₂) | Bio-oil, Char, Gas | Biogas (CH₄+CO₂) |
| Efficiency | 60 – 90% | 70 – 80% (cold gas) | 60 – 75% | 30 – 60% (energy recovery) |
| KTU Exam Focus | High | High | Moderate | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Biomass Classification Tree


### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Anaerobic Digestion Block Stages

```mermaid
graph LR

    C4["STAGE 3 Acetogenesis"]
    C5["STAGE 4 Methanogenesis"]
    C6["OUTPUT Biogas 60 percent CH4 40 percent CO2"]
    C7["OUTPUT Digestate Liquid Fertilizer"]
    C8["Complex Polymers Proteins Fats Carbohydrates"]
    C9["Volatile Fatty Acids VFAs"]
    C10["Acetate H2 CO2"]
    C11["Methanogenic Archaea"]
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C8
    C8 --> C3
    C3 --> C9
    C9 --> C4
    C4 --> C10
    C10 --> C5
    C5 --> C11
    C5 --> C6
    C5 --> C7
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams function as the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** representations required by the KTU board, since the physical equipment layout (digesters, gas holders, scrubbers) is too complex for direct Mermaid rendering.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define biomass. List the three main categories of biomass with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
Biomass is the biodegradable fraction of organic matter derived from plants, animals, and microorganisms, which can be converted into useful forms of energy.

Three main categories:

1. **Woody biomass** — e.g., firewood, sawdust, bagasse
2. **Non-woody (agro-residues and herbaceous) biomass** — e.g., rice straw, wheat husk
3. **Animal and municipal waste** — e.g., cattle dung, food waste, MSW

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between proximate and ultimate analysis of biomass fuels.*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Proximate Analysis | Ultimate Analysis |
|-------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Determines | Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash | C, H, O, N, S content |
| Purpose | Combustion behaviour prediction | Calorific value calculation (Dulong's) |
| Method | Heating under controlled conditions | Elemental combustion analyzer |
| Output | Mass % of MC, VM, FC, Ash | Mass % of C, H, O, N, S |
| KTU Exam Frequency | High | Very High |

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*What is anaerobic digestion? Write the Buswell equation for glucose.*

**Model Answer:**
Anaerobic digestion is the biochemical conversion of organic matter into **biogas (60% CH₄ + 40% CO₂)** by microorganisms in the absence of oxygen.

Buswell equation for glucose:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a) Explain the various thermochemical conversion processes of biomass. Compare their operating conditions and products. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Thermochemical conversion uses **heat** to break down biomass. There are three main processes:

**1. Combustion (Direct Burning):**
- **Temperature:** 700 – 1200°C
- **Atmosphere:** Excess air
- **Reaction:** $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + Heat$
- **Product:** Heat, Flue gas, Ash
- **Efficiency:** 60 – 90%
- **Application:** Domestic cooking, Industrial boilers, Power plants (Rankine cycle)

**2. Gasification (Partial Oxidation):**
- **Temperature:** 700 – 900°C
- **Atmosphere:** Limited O₂ (≈ 25% of stoichiometric)
- **Reactions:**
  - $C + \dfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO$ (partial oxidation)
  - $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ (water-gas shift)
- **Product:** Producer gas (Syngas: CO + H₂ + CH₄ + N₂)
- **Cold Gas Efficiency:** 70 – 80%
- **Application:** Decentralized power, IC engine fuel

**3. Pyrolysis (Thermal Decomposition):**
- **Temperature:** 400 – 600°C
- **Atmosphere:** No O₂ (inert N₂)
- **Products (mass %):** Bio-oil (60 – 75%), Char (15 – 25%), Non-condensable gas (10 – 20%)
- **Application:** Bio-oil for boilers, char as soil amendment / activated carbon
- **Sub-types:** Slow, fast, flash pyrolysis

**Comparison Summary Table (to be drawn in exam):**

| Parameter | Combustion | Gasification | Pyrolysis |
|-----------|------------|--------------|-----------|
| O₂ Supply | Excess | Limited | Nil |
| Temp (°C) | 700 – 1200 | 700 – 900 | 400 – 600 |
| Main Product | Heat | Syngas | Bio-oil |
| Energy form | Direct heat | Chemical fuel | Liquid fuel |

**(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the production of biogas through anaerobic digestion. List the factors affecting biogas yield. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (in exam — replicate Section 4.3 in your answer sheet):**

INPUT (Cattle dung + Water) → Mixing Tank → Digester (Anaerobic, 30 – 60°C, HRT 30 – 50 days) → Gas Holder → Scrubber (H₂S removal) → Storage → Use (Stove / Engine / Genset)
Digestate → Drying → Organic fertilizer

**Four Stages of Anaerobic Digestion:**

1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) broken down into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes.
2. **Acidogenesis** — Monomers converted into **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)**, alcohols, H₂, CO₂ by acidogenic bacteria.
3. **Acetogenesis** — VFAs and alcohols converted to **acetate, H₂, CO₂** by acetogenic bacteria.
4. **Methanogenesis** — Acetate, H₂, CO₂ converted to **CH₄** by methanogenic archaea.
   - $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$
   - $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$

**Factors Affecting Biogas Yield:**

| Factor | Optimum |
|--------|---------|
| Temperature | 35°C (mesophilic) or 55°C (thermophilic) |
| pH | 6.8 – 7.4 |
| C/N ratio | 25 – 30 |
| Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | 30 – 50 days |
| Solid content (TS) | 8 – 12% |
| Mixing | Continuous / intermittent |

**Typical Yield:** 0.2 – 0.5 m³ biogas per kg of volatile solids added (cattle dung basis).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) Classify biomass fuels in detail with examples. List at least four properties of an ideal biomass fuel. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Biomass Fuels:**

**A. By Origin:**

1. **Woody biomass** — Firewood, sawdust, bark, wood chips, pellets
2. **Agro-residues** — Rice straw, wheat straw, sugarcane bagasse, groundnut shell
3. **Animal waste** — Cattle dung, poultry litter, piggery waste
4. **Aquatic biomass** — Water hyacinth, algae
5. **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** — Paper, food waste, yard trimmings
6. **Energy crops** — Switchgrass, miscanthus, jatropha
7. **Industrial waste** — Black liquor (paper industry), coir pith

**B. By Physical State:**

- **Solid:** Wood, bagasse, rice husk, pellets
- **Liquid:** Bioethanol, biodiesel, pyrolysis oil
- **Gaseous:** Biogas, producer gas, syngas

**C. By Generation:**

- **1st Gen:** Sugar/starch/oil-based (food crops)
- **2nd Gen:** Lignocellulosic (non-food)
- **3rd Gen:** Algal biomass
- **4th Gen:** Genetically engineered or synthetic biology

**Properties of an Ideal Biomass Fuel:**

1. **High calorific value** (≥ 18 MJ/kg)
2. **Low moisture content** (≤ 15%)
3. **Low ash content** (≤ 5%)
4. **Low sulphur and nitrogen** (≤ 0.2%) to minimize SOx/NOx
5. **Uniform size and shape** for consistent feeding
6. **High volatile matter** for easy ignition
7. **Low alkali metal content** (K, Na) to avoid slagging
8. **High bulk density** for efficient transport
9. **Easy availability and renewability**

**(b) Describe the transesterification process for biodiesel production. What are the advantages of biodiesel over conventional diesel? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Transesterification Process:**

Biodiesel (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Ester) is produced by reacting **vegetable oil or animal fat** (triglyceride) with **methanol** in the presence of a **catalyst** (NaOH or KOH).

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,CH_3OH \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH (catalyst)}} 3\,FAME + Glycerol$$

**Step-by-Step Process (Block Diagram in exam):**

1. **Feedstock pre-treatment** — Degumming, deacidification, drying of oil
2. **Mixing** — Oil + Methanol + NaOH (catalyst) → **Molar ratio 1:6 (oil:methanol)**, Temp 50 – 70°C
3. **Reaction (Transesterification)** — In a stirred reactor for 1 – 2 hours
4. **Settling** — Two layers form: **Top: Biodiesel**, **Bottom: Glycerol (by-product)**
5. **Separation** — Decantation or centrifugation
6. **Purification** — Water washing of biodiesel to remove soap and catalyst
7. **Drying** — Vacuum drying of purified biodiesel
8. **Storage** — Ready for blending with petro-diesel (B20 = 20% biodiesel + 80% diesel)

**Typical Yield:** ~ 90 – 95% of theoretical (10 kg oil → ~ 9.5 kg biodiesel + 1 kg glycerol)

**Advantages of Biodiesel over Conventional Diesel:**

| Parameter | Biodiesel | Petro-diesel |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Renewable | Yes | No (fossil) |
| Biodegradable | Yes (in 28 days) | No |
| Toxicity | Non-toxic | Toxic |
| CO₂ emissions | -78% (net) | High |
| Sulphur content | Negligible | 0.05 – 0.25% |
| Flash point | 130°C (safer) | 55 – 66°C |
| Lubricity | High (better engine life) | Low |
| Cetane number | 50 – 60 | 40 – 55 |
| Engine modification | Not needed (B20) | — |

**Disadvantages of Biodiesel:**
- Higher viscosity at low temperature (cold-flow issues)
- Slightly higher NOx emissions
- Glycerol disposal
- Higher cost of production (food vs fuel debate for 1st gen)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 1–2 Marks Each (Total 8–14 Marks Lost):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up C/N ratio direction** — High C/N (>35) means slow digestion (acid accumulation), low C/N (<20) means ammonia inhibition. Optimum is **25–30**.
> 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV in the Dulong's equation** — Dulong's gives **HHV** only. To get LHV, you must subtract the latent heat term **2.442 × (9H + M)/100**.
> 3. **Forgetting the O/8 correction in hydrogen term** — A common KTU 2023–2024 error: writing $1.442 \times H$ instead of $1.442 \times (H - O/8)$. This inflates HHV by 20–30%.
> 4. **Writing "biogas = methane"** — Biogas is a **mixture** of CH₄ (50–70%) + CO₂ (30–40%) + trace H₂S, NH₃, water vapour. Always specify composition.
> 5. **Skipping the molar ratio in transesterification** — Board examiners specifically look for **1 mol oil : 6 mol methanol** ratio. Missing this loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Wrong formula for moisture content** — Always specify **wet basis** vs **dry basis** explicitly. $MC_{wet} = (m_{wet} - m_{dry})/m_{wet} \times 100$ (most common in KTU).
> 7. **Confusing the role of lignin** — Lignin is **non-fermentable**; only cellulose and hemicellulose yield biogas. 2nd-gen biofuels need **pre-treatment** to break lignin.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this section 15 minutes before the exam — covers 80% of expected marks.**

- [x] **Biomass** = Biodegradable organic matter used as fuel; **carbon-neutral** in the short carbon cycle.
- [x] **3 Generations:** 1st (food crops), 2nd (lignocellulosic), 3rd (algae).
- [x] **Proximate Analysis:** MC, VM, FC, Ash (sum = 100%).
- [x] **Ultimate Analysis:** C, H, O, N, S (sum = 100% on dry, ash-free basis).
- [x] **Dulong's Equation:** $HHV = 0.338\,C + 1.442\,(H - O/8) + 0.094\,S$ — remember the **O/8 correction**.
- [x] **LHV formula:** $LHV = HHV - 2.442 \times (9H + M)/100$.
- [x] **Air-Fuel Ratio (mass):** $A/F = (2.667\,C + 8\,H - O + S)/23.2$.
- [x] **Three thermochemical routes:** Combustion (heat), Gasification (syngas), Pyrolysis (bio-oil/char).
- [x] **Four AD stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- [x] **Buswell equation for glucose:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$ (energy = **13.4 MJ/kg**).
- [x] **Biogas composition:** 50–70% CH₄ + 30–40% CO₂ + trace H₂S, NH₃, H₂O.
- [x] **Optimum AD parameters:** pH 6.8–7.4, T = 35°C, C/N = 25–30, HRT = 30–50 days.
- [x] **Transesterification:** Oil (TG) + 3 Methanol → 3 FAME (biodiesel) + Glycerol.
- [x] **Molar ratio:** 1:6 (oil:methanol), Catalyst: NaOH, Temperature: 50–70°C.
- [x] **Calorific values to memorize:** Wood ≈ 15–20 MJ/kg; Bagasse ≈ 17 MJ/kg; Biogas ≈ 20–25 MJ/m³; Biodiesel ≈ 38 MJ/kg; Ethanol ≈ 27 MJ/kg.
- [x] **Kerala-specific examples:** Coconut shell charcoal (≈ 30 MJ/kg), Coir pith, Rice husk, Coffee pulp — Kerala has > 1,000 operational biogas plants.
- [x] **Carbon-neutral equation:** Regrowth period = Harvest period ⇒ Net CO₂ = 0.
- [x] **Biomass conversion efficiency order (typical):** Combustion > Gasification > Anaerobic Digestion > Fermentation.

> **End of Module 3 — Biomass Fuels Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEMUT864**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Resources
Biomass Conversion Technologies and Urban Waste to Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biomass Conversion Technologies and Urban Waste to Energy Conversion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Biomass — The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biomass** is any organic (carbon-based) material derived from living or recently living organisms that can be used as a source of energy. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT864)**, biomass refers specifically to **biodegradable plant- and animal-derived matter** (wood, crop residues, aquatic biomass, agricultural waste, municipal solid waste, animal manure, and dedicated energy crops) whose **stored solar energy** (via photosynthesis) can be liberated through **biological, thermal, or chemical conversion** routes. The chemical energy trapped inside biomass originates from the photosynthetic reaction: $$6\,CO_2 + 6\,H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6\,O_2$$ When biomass is processed correctly, the energy stored in carbohydrates, lipids, and lignocellulose can be converted into **heat, electricity, gaseous fuels (biogas, syngas), liquid fuels (bioethanol, biodiesel), or solid fuels (charcoal, pellets)**. ## 1.2 Urban Waste to Energy — The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Urban Waste to Energy (WtE)** refers to the engineering processes that recover usable energy (thermal, electrical, or fuel-grade) from **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** generated in urban settlements. It is a critical component of **Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM)** under the Swachh Bharat Mission and KTU's Energy & Environment syllabus. **Categories of MSW typically considered:** - **Organic/putrescible fraction** — food waste, kitchen waste, garden waste (~40–60% by weight in Indian cities) - **Paper and cardboard** (~10–15%) - **Plastics, rubber, leather** (~10–12%) - **Glass, metals, inert materials** (~20–25%) - **Construction & demolition debris** (variable) ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **"Biomass is a slow-release energy capsule cooked by the Sun."** > Imagine a tree as a giant rechargeable battery. For millions of years, the Sun's photons have been "stored" inside its cellulose and lignin molecules. **Biomass conversion technologies** are simply different ways of "discharging" this battery — sometimes gently (anaerobic digestion → biogas), sometimes violently (combustion → heat), and sometimes chemically (transesterification → biodiesel). > > Similarly, **urban waste is a misplaced fuel.** Indian cities generate ~62 million tonnes of MSW annually, of which ~75% is collectable. A wet banana peel, a paper bag, and a piece of polystyrene foam all contain trapped energy — we just need different "extraction kits" (incinerators, biodigesters, gasifiers) to liberate it. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Reference Values (use these in KTU numericals):** > - Calorific value of dry wood: **~17–20 MJ/kg** > - Calorific value of MSW: **~8–12 MJ/kg** (highly variable, depends on moisture & composition) > - Biogas composition: **~55–65% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂, trace H₂S** > - Energy content of methane: **55.5 MJ/kg** > - Stoichiometric O₂ for cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ combustion: **6 mol O₂ per glucose unit** > - Optimum C/N ratio for anaerobic digestion: **20:1 to 30:1** > - Optimum pH for methanogens: **6.8–7.4** > - Indian MSW generation rate: **~0.45 kg/capita/day** (urban average) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Grand Pathways of Biomass Conversion Biomass conversion technologies are universally classified into **two fundamental pathways**: ### Pathway A — Biochemical / Biological Conversion This pathway uses **microorganisms or enzymes** to break down complex organic molecules. It is **slow, low-temperature**, and works on **wet biomass**. | Sub-Technology | Substrate | End Product | Temperature | Retention Time | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** | Wet biomass, manure, MSW organics | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + Digestate | Mesophilic: 30–40 °C / Thermophilic: 50–55 °C | 20–40 days | | **Fermentation (Ethanol)** | Sugars, starch, cellulose | Bioethanol (C₂H₅OH) | 25–35 °C | 2–4 days | | **Composting** | Organic MSW | Humus (soil conditioner) | Ambient | 30–90 days | | **Vermicomposting** | Organic MSW | Vermicast | Ambient | 60–90 days | ### Pathway B — Thermochemical Conversion This pathway uses **heat** to break chemical bonds. It is **fast, high-temperature**, and works on **dry biomass**. | Sub-Technology | Operating Condition | End Products | |---|---|---| | **Combustion** | Excess O₂, 800–1200 °C | Heat → Steam → Electricity | | **Gasification** | Limited O₂ (sub-stoichiometric), 700–1000 °C | Producer gas (CO + H₂ + CH₄) | | **Pyrolysis** | No O₂, 400–800 °C | Bio-char + Bio-oil + Pyro-gas | | **Torrefaction** | No O₂, 200–300 °C | Bio-coal (solid fuel) | | **Hydrothermal Liquefaction** | Hot pressurized water, 250–350 °C | Bio-crude oil | > [!NOTE] > **KTU's "Why" question:** Why two pathways? Because **moisture content** of the feedstock decides the route. Wet feedstocks (animal dung, food waste, sewage) cannot be economically dried for combustion → use AD. Dry feedstocks (wood chips, rice husk, coconut shell) burn cleanly → use thermochemical routes. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — How Each Core Technology Works ### A. Anaerobic Digestion (AD) — The Methane Factory Anaerobic digestion is a four-stage microbial breakdown of organic matter in the **complete absence of oxygen**. 1. **Hydrolysis:** Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are split into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes. This is the **rate-limiting step** for lignocellulosic biomass. 2. **Acidogenesis:** Monomers are converted by acidogenic bacteria into Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) like acetic acid, propionic acid, butyric acid, plus H₂ and CO₂. 3. **Acetogenesis:** VFAs and alcohols are converted into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria. 4. **Methanogenesis:** Methanogenic archaea (e.g., *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*) convert acetic acid and H₂/CO₂ into **methane (CH₄)**. ### B. Gasification — The Producer Gas Reactor Gasification converts solid biomass into a combustible gas mixture using a **controlled, sub-stoichiometric** supply of air, oxygen, or steam. The four sequential zones inside a downdraft gasifier are: 1. **Drying Zone** (100–200 °C): Moisture evaporates. 2. **Pyrolysis Zone** (200–600 °C): Volatiles released as tars and gases. 3. **Combustion/Reduction Zone** (600–1200 °C): Carbon + O₂ → CO₂; then C + CO₂ → 2CO (Boudouard reaction). 4. **Ash Bed:** Inert residue. ### C. Pyrolysis — The Thermal Decomposition Reactor In **complete absence of air**, biomass decomposes into three product fractions. The **yield distribution** is controlled by **heating rate and residence time**: - **Slow pyrolysis** (low heating rate, long residence time) → maximize **char (~35%)** - **Fast pyrolysis** (high heating rate, short residence time) → maximize **bio-oil (~75%)** - **Flash pyrolysis** (very high heating rate, milliseconds) → maximize **gases** ### D. Biodiesel Production — Transesterification Biodiesel (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Esters) is produced by reacting vegetable oil / animal fat with a short-chain alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH or KOH). The reaction: $$Triglyceride + 3\,Methanol \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst (NaOH)}} 3\,FAME + Glycerol$$ A **molar ratio of 6:1 (methanol:oil)**, temperature **60 °C**, and catalyst loading **~1% by weight of oil** are typical. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all high-yield equations** for KTU ESE questions on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Calorific Value of MSW (Modified Dulong's Formula) | $CV = 337\,C + 1418\,(H - \tfrac{O}{8}) + 93\,S + 23\,N$ | kJ/kg | C, H, O, S, N are % by mass | | 2 | Stoichiometric Air for Combustion | $A_0 = \tfrac{11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - O/8) + 4.35\,S}{100}$ | kg air/kg fuel | C, H, O, S in % by mass | | 3 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis) | $AFR = \tfrac{A_0}{1} = \dfrac{2.047\,C_{O_2}}{\sum n_i\,C_i}$ | kg/kg | Use O₂ demand from stoichiometry | | 4 | Biogas Yield (Buswell's Equation) | $C_nH_aO_b + (n - \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2})\,H_2O \rightarrow (\tfrac{n}{2} - \tfrac{a}{8} + \tfrac{b}{4})\,CO_2 + (\tfrac{n}{2} + \tfrac{a}{8} - \tfrac{b}{4})\,CH_4$ | mol/mol | Generic substrate $C_nH_aO_b$ | | 5 | Methane yield (theoretical) | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{0.37 \times VS}{1\,kg\,VS\,added}$ | m³ CH₄/kg VS | VS = Volatile Solids | | 6 | Gasification Efficiency | $\eta_{gas} = \dfrac{CV_{gas} \times V_{gas}}{CV_{biomass} \times m_{biomass}} \times 100\%$ | % | Cold gas efficiency | | 7 | Pyrolysis Mass Balance | $m_{biomass} = m_{char} + m_{bio-oil} + m_{gas}$ | kg | Closed system | | 8 | Transesterification stoichiometry | $1\,TG + 3\,MeOH \rightarrow 3\,FAME + 1\,GL$ | mol | TG = triglyceride | | 9 | Biodiesel mass yield | $Y_{BD} = \dfrac{m_{FAME}}{m_{oil}} \times 100\%$ | % | Typical 90–98% | | 10 | Energy content of biogas | $E_{biogas} = V_{CH_4} \times 55.5$ | MJ/m³ | Pure CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³, biogas ~ 20–25 MJ/m³ | | 11 | Optimum C/N ratio for AD | $\left(\dfrac{C}{N}\right)_{opt} = 25$ to 30 | dimensionless | Rice straw: 60–80, cow dung: 20–25 | | 12 | Theoretical O₂ for cellulose | $C_6H_{10}O_5 + 6\,O_2 \rightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 5\,H_2O$ | mol | (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ unit | | 13 | Energy recovery efficiency (WtE) | $\eta_{WtE} = \dfrac{E_{output\,(electric + heat)}}{E_{input\,(MSW\,CV)}}$ | % | Typical 20–30% | | 14 | Moisture correction of CV | $CV_{dry} = \dfrac{CV_{as\,received}}{1 - M}$ | kJ/kg | M = moisture fraction | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen & Air Requirement for Biomass Combustion **Starting premise:** Complete combustion converts C → CO₂, H → H₂O, S → SO₂. One mole of O₂ contains **2 atoms of oxygen**, hence the "2" multiplier in denominators. Let 1 kg of biomass contain: $C$ kg carbon, $H$ kg hydrogen, $O$ kg oxygen, $S$ kg sulphur, with the rest as ash and moisture. **Step 1 — Oxygen atoms needed for C:** Carbon needs 1 mole of O atoms per C atom. For $C$ kg of carbon: $$n_{O_2,\,C} = \dfrac{C}{12} \text{ mol O atoms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{O}_2 = \dfrac{C}{24} \text{ kmol O}_2$$ **Step 2 — Oxygen atoms needed for H₂:** For $H$ kg hydrogen, hydrogen first forms H₂O, requiring ½ mole O atom per H atom. However, part of this oxygen is already supplied by the fuel's internal oxygen. Net oxygen required from air: $$n_{O_2,\,H,\,net} = \dfrac{H}{4} - \dfrac{O}{32} \text{ kmol O}_2$$ (Explanation: $H/4$ is the gross oxygen; $O/32$ is the internally available oxygen in kmol.) **Step 3 — Oxygen needed for S:** For $S$ kg sulphur, $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$. So: $$n_{O_2,\,S} = \dfrac{S}{32} \text{ kmol O}_2$$ **Step 4 — Total O₂ in kmol:** $$n_{O_2,\,total} = \dfrac{C}{24} + \dfrac{H}{4} - \dfrac{O}{32} + \dfrac{S}{32}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to mass of O₂ (molecular weight = 32 kg/kmol):** $$m_{O_2} = 32 \times \left(\dfrac{C}{24} + \dfrac{H}{4} - \dfrac{O}{32} + \dfrac{S}{32}\right) = \dfrac{4}{3}C + 8H - O + S$$ **Step 6 — Mass of stoichiometric air (air is 23.2% O₂ by mass):** $$m_{air} = \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \dfrac{\dfrac{4}{3}C + 8H - O + S}{0.232}$$ $$\boxed{m_{air} = 11.6\,C + 34.8\,(H - \tfrac{O}{8}) + 4.35\,S \;\; \text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$ This is the universally used KTU-level formula. Note that **nitrogen is neglected** in the O₂ demand calculation (it just dilutes the air). ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Calorific Value of MSW > [!NOTE] > **This is a KTU-favourite 7-mark problem type.** A sample of Indian Municipal Solid Waste has the following ultimate analysis by mass: - C = 18.5%, H = 2.5%, O = 14.0%, N = 0.5%, S = 0.2%, Moisture = 35%, Ash = rest **Step 1 — Find Ash %:** $Ash = 100 - 18.5 - 2.5 - 14.0 - 0.5 - 0.2 - 35 = 29.3\%$ **Step 2 — Apply Modified Dulong's Formula (dry basis values used here, then moisture-corrected):** $$CV_{dry} = 337 \times 18.5 + 1418 \times \left(2.5 - \dfrac{14.0}{8}\right) + 93 \times 0.2 + 23 \times 0.5$$ $$CV_{dry} = 6234.5 + 1418 \times (2.5 - 1.75) + 18.6 + 11.5$$ $$CV_{dry} = 6234.5 + 1418 \times 0.75 + 30.1$$ $$CV_{dry} = 6234.5 + 1063.5 + 30.1 = 7328.1 \text{ kJ/kg (dry basis)}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to as-received basis (moisture = 35%):** $$CV_{AR} = CV_{dry} \times (1 - M) = 7328.1 \times 0.65$$ $$\boxed{CV_{AR} = 4763.3 \text{ kJ/kg} \;\; (\approx 4.76 \text{ MJ/kg})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This value (~4.8 MJ/kg) is typical of Indian MSW because of its **high moisture and inert content**. This low CV is exactly why **incineration alone is not always viable** in India — many plants need auxiliary fuel support or pre-drying (RDF approach). ## 3.3 Derivation: Buswell's Equation for Biogas Composition Starting with a generic empirical biomass formula $C_nH_aO_b$, the **anaerobic digestion** stoichiometry (Buswell & Mueller, 1952) gives: $$\begin{aligned} C_nH_aO_b &+ \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \\ &\rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 \\ &+ \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Verification with glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆, so n=6, a=12, b=6):** $$n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0$$ $$CH_4: \;\; \dfrac{6}{2} + \dfrac{12}{8} - \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3 \text{ mol CH}_4$$ $$CO_2: \;\; \dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{12}{8} + \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 \text{ mol CO}_2$$ Result: $$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$ (i.e., 50% CH₄, 50% CO₂ — the well-known theoretical maximum for carbohydrate substrates.) ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Conversion Route > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the KTU 14-mark "Compare and recommend" question pattern. Memorize the selection logic.** | Feedstock Property | Recommended Route | Justification | |---|---|---| | Moisture > 60%, low lignin | **Anaerobic Digestion** | High water makes drying uneconomical | | Moisture < 20%, lignocellulosic | **Combustion / Gasification** | Dry, energy-dense, burns efficiently | | Need liquid transport fuel | **Fermentation / Transesterification** | Liquid fuels have higher energy density than gases | | High plastic / rubber fraction | **Pyrolysis** (with emissions control) | Thermochemical route handles mixed waste | | Mixed urban MSW with 40–60% organics | **Integrated: AD + Incineration of rejects** | Maximises energy recovery & diversion from landfill | | Need soil amendment as by-product | **Composting / Vermicomposting** | Biologically stable, marketable | | Coconut shell / rice husk (silica-rich) | **Gasification (downdraft)** | Producer gas can run engines, ash is commercial | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart — Biomass Conversion Technologies ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK]:::root A1[Wet Biomass
Moisture above 60 percent] A2[Dry Biomass
Moisture below 20 percent] A3[Oily Biomass
Vegetable oils fats] A4[Urban MSW
Mixed composition] A0 --> A1 A0 --> A2 A0 --> A3 A0 --> A4 A1 --> B1[Anaerobic Digestion] A1 --> B2[Fermentation to Ethanol] A1 --> B3[Composting Vermicomposting] A2 --> C1[Combustion] A2 --> C2[Gasification] A2 --> C3[Pyrolysis] A2 --> C4[Torrefaction] A3 --> D1[Transesterification] A3 --> D2[Hydrolysis esterification] A4 --> E1[Incineration with energy recovery] A4 --> E2[Refuse Derived Fuel RDF] A4 --> E3[Landfill Gas Recovery] A4 --> E4[Bio methanation of organic fraction] B1 --> P1[Biogas 60 percent CH4] B1 --> P2[Digestate fertilizer] B2 --> P3[Bioethanol fuel grade] B3 --> P4[Compost soil conditioner] C1 --> Q1[Heat steam electricity] C2 --> Q2[Producer gas engine fuel] C3 --> Q3[Bio oil plus bio char plus gas] C3 --> Q4[Fast pyrolysis maximizes bio oil] C3 --> Q5[Slow pyrolysis maximizes char] C4 --> Q5 D1 --> R1[Biodiesel FAME plus glycerol] E1 --> S1[Steam electricity via turbine] E2 --> S2[Pellets combustion in boilers] E3 --> S3[Methane from landfill] E4 --> P1 classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Anaerobic Digestion Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[Biomass Collection
and Sorting]:::stage F2[Shredding and
Slurry Preparation]:::stage F3[Primary Digester
Mesophilic 35 C]:::stage F4[Secondary Digester
Polishing Stage]:::stage F5[Gas Holder
Storage Dome]:::stage F6[Biogas Purification
H2S and CO2 Removal]:::stage F7[Combined Heat and
Power CHP Engine]:::stage F8[Compost yard
Dewatering Digestate]:::stage F9[Grid Electricity
Export Line]:::output F10[Thermal Energy
Hot Water Loop]:::output F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 F3 -.biogas pipe.-> F5 F4 -.biogas pipe.-> F5 F5 --> F6 --> F7 F7 --> F9 F7 --> F10 F7 -.waste heat.-> F3 F4 --> F8 classDef stage fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1.5px classDef output fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1.5px ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Downdraft Biomass Gasifier ```mermaid flowchart TD G1[Biomass Hopper
Top Feed]:::zone G2[Drying Zone
100 to 200 C]:::zone G3[Pyrolysis Zone
200 to 600 C]:::zone G4[Combustion Zone
800 to 1200 C]:::zone G5[Reduction Zone
Boudouard Reaction]:::zone G6[Ash Bed
Char Combustion]:::zone G7[Producer Gas Outlet
CO H2 CH4 N2]:::out G8[Air Inlet
Tuyere Nozzles]:::inp G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4 --> G5 --> G6 G8 --> G4 G5 --> G7 classDef zone fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc4125,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1.5px classDef out fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1.5px classDef inp fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1.5px ``` ## 4.4 Flow — Municipal Solid Waste to Energy Plant (WtE) ```mermaid flowchart LR W1[MSW Collection
from City Zones]:::stage W2[Weighbridge and
Tipping Floor]:::stage W3[Manual and
Mechanical Sorting]:::stage W4[Shredding and
Magnetic Separation]:::stage W5[Organic Fraction
to Anaerobic Digester]:::branch W6[Inert Combustible
to Incinerator]:::branch W7[Recyclable Plastics
Glass Metals]:::branch W8[Biogas to CHP]:::out W9[Steam to Turbine
Electricity Export]:::out W10[Bottom Ash to
Brick Manufacturing]:::out W11[Recyclables to
Industry Markets]:::out W1 --> W2 --> W3 --> W4 W4 --> W5 W4 --> W6 W4 --> W7 W5 --> W8 W6 --> W9 W7 --> W11 W6 --> W10 classDef stage fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1.5px classDef branch fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1.5px classDef out fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#0b2545,stroke-width:1.5px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **Define biomass. List any three biomass conversion technologies with their primary end products.** **Model Answer:** Biomass is the biodegradable fraction of products, wastes, and residues from agriculture (including vegetal and animal substances), forestry, and related industries, as well as the biodegradable fraction of industrial and municipal waste. It represents **stored solar energy** in organic form. | Technology | End Product | |---|---| | Anaerobic Digestion | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + Digestate | | Gasification | Producer gas (CO + H₂) | | Pyrolysis | Bio-oil, Bio-char, Pyro-gas | | Fermentation | Bioethanol | | Transesterification | Biodiesel (FAME) | *(Any three — 1 mark definition, 1 mark list, 1 mark for products)* ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Explain why Municipal Solid Waste in India has a low calorific value and how Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) helps overcome this problem.** **Model Answer:** Indian MSW typically has: - High moisture content (40–60%) — reduces net energy yield - High inert fraction (sand, dust, construction debris) — 20–30% - High biodegradable organic fraction with low calorific value individually As a result, the **as-received CV is only 800–1200 kcal/kg (~3.5–5 MJ/kg)**, often below the **autothermal threshold** (~1500 kcal/kg) needed to sustain combustion without auxiliary fuel. **RDF solution:** Mechanical sorting removes inerts, moisture is reduced by sun-drying/screening, and the combustible fraction (plastics, paper, textiles, non-biodegradables) is shredded, dried, and densified into **pellets or fluff**. RDF typically has a CV of **3000–4500 kcal/kg (12–18 MJ/kg)**, comparable to low-grade coal, making it suitable for **co-firing in cement kilns and industrial boilers**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **process of anaerobic digestion** of biomass for biogas production. Discuss the four biochemical stages and the operating parameters that affect biogas yield. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1000 kg batch of wet cattle dung (C = 7.5%, H = 1.0%, O = 5.0%, N = 0.4%, S = 0.1%, Moisture = 80%, rest = ash) is fed to an anaerobic digester. The digester operates at 35 °C with a 30-day retention time, producing biogas with 60% methane. Calculate: 1. The methane volume produced at STP per kg of volatile solids added, assuming 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS. 2. The total energy output in MJ from the batch. 3. Whether the digester output can sustain a 1 kW electric load continuously, assuming 30% electrical conversion efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — (a) **Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.2).** The process flow: Biomass collection → Slurry preparation (TS adjusted to 8–12%) → Primary digester (mesophilic 35 °C, HRT 20–30 days) → Secondary digester (polishing, gas collection) → Gas holder → Purification (H₂S removal using FeCl₃ or activated carbon) → CHP / Bottling **The Four Biochemical Stages:** 1. **Hydrolysis** (slowest, rate-limiting): Complex carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids are hydrolysed by extracellular enzymes (cellulase, protease, lipase) into soluble monomers. **C₆H₁₀O₅ + nH₂O → nC₆H₁₂O₆** 2. **Acidogenesis:** Acidogenic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*) convert monomers to volatile fatty acids (acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂. 3. **Acetogenesis:** Acetogenic bacteria convert VFAs to acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂. 4. **Methanogenesis:** Two groups of methanogens act in parallel: - **Acetoclastic:** $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (~70% of CH₄) - **Hydrogenotrophic:** $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ (~30% of CH₄) **Operating Parameters Affecting Biogas Yield:** - **Temperature:** Mesophilic (30–40 °C) is most stable; thermophilic (50–55 °C) gives higher yield but is less stable. - **pH:** Must be maintained at **6.8–7.4** for methanogens. - **C/N ratio:** Optimum **20:1 to 30:1**. High C/N causes N-deficiency for microbes; low C/N causes ammonia inhibition. - **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time):** 20–40 days for mesophilic; 10–20 days for thermophilic. - **Total Solids (TS):** Wet digestion at 8–12% TS is optimum. - **Mixing:** Gentle, continuous mixing improves gas yield by ~20–30%. - **Inhibitors:** Ammonia, H₂S, heavy metals, antibiotics, and sudden temperature/pH shocks are toxic to methanogens. --- #### Model Solution — (b) **Given data (on as-received basis, 1000 kg batch):** C = 7.5%, H = 1.0%, O = 5.0%, N = 0.4%, S = 0.1%, Moisture M = 80% Ash = 100 - 7.5 - 1.0 - 5.0 - 0.4 - 0.1 - 80 = **6.0%** So Total Solids (TS) = 20% → TS in 1000 kg = **200 kg** Volatile Solids (VS) ≈ TS - Ash = 200 - 60 = **140 kg** **Subpart 1 — Methane volume per kg VS:** $$Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 \text{ per kg VS (given)}$$ **Total CH₄ from batch** = $0.35 \times 140 = 49$ m³ CH₄ at STP **Subpart 2 — Total energy output:** Methane energy content at STP = **35.8 MJ/m³** $$E_{CH_4} = 49 \times 35.8 = 1754.2 \text{ MJ}$$ Since biogas is 60% CH₄, total biogas volume = $49 / 0.6 = 81.67$ m³, but energy comes only from CH₄: $$\boxed{E_{output} = 1754.2 \text{ MJ}}$$ **Subpart 3 — Continuous 1 kW load feasibility:** Energy needed per day for 1 kW continuous load: $E_{needed} = 1 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 24 \text{ kWh/day} = 24 \times 3.6 = 86.4 \text{ MJ/day}$ Energy available at 30% electrical efficiency: $E_{avail} = 1754.2 \times 0.30 = 526.26$ MJ (for the whole 30-day batch) Per day equivalent: $526.26 / 30 = 17.54$ MJ/day = 4.87 kWh/day $$\boxed{\text{Available electrical power} = \dfrac{526.26 \text{ MJ}}{30 \times 24 \times 3.6} = 0.203 \text{ kW} = 203 \text{ W}}$$ **Conclusion:** The digester output of **~203 W is INSUFFICIENT** to sustain a continuous 1 kW load. It would only meet about **20%** of the demand. To meet 1 kW, we would need approximately **5 such batches per day** or **5× the feedstock throughput**. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice] **(a)** Describe the **downdraft biomass gasifier** with a neat diagram. Explain the four reaction zones and write the **stoichiometric equations** for the primary reactions. Discuss why the producer gas has a low calorific value (~4–6 MJ/Nm³) and how it can be upgraded. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **biomass-based municipal solid waste (MSW) plant** receives 200 tonnes/day of MSW with the following composition: C = 20%, H = 3%, O = 12%, N = 0.6%, S = 0.3%, Moisture = 45%, rest = ash. The plant uses **incineration with waste heat boiler and steam turbine** with an overall electrical efficiency of 22%. The MSW is converted to **Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF)** by removing inerts (30% of mass) and drying. 1. Calculate the calorific value of RDF. 2. Calculate the daily electrical energy output in kWh. 3. The plant serves a city of 200,000 population. Find per-capita electrical energy supplied. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — (a) **Diagram (refer Section 4.3).** The four reaction zones of a downdraft gasifier are: 1. **Drying Zone (100–200 °C):** Incoming biomass is dried by hot producer gas flowing upward (counter-current). Moisture content drops from 10–35% to <5%. 2. **Pyrolysis Zone (200–600 °C):** Biomass undergoes thermal decomposition in absence of oxygen, releasing volatile gases, tars, and leaving behind char. $Biomass \rightarrow Char + Tar + Volatiles + H_2O$ 3. **Combustion / Oxidation Zone (800–1200 °C):** Char reacts with limited air supplied through tuyere nozzles. The exothermic reactions provide heat for endothermic processes above and below. - $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5$ kJ/mol - $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta H = -241.8$ kJ/mol - $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$ 4. **Reduction Zone (700–900 °C):** Hot gases (CO₂ and H₂O) pass through hot char bed, producing CO and H₂ (the useful combustible components). - **Boudouard Reaction:** $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO \quad \Delta H = +172$ kJ/mol - **Water-Gas Reaction:** $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \Delta H = +131$ kJ/mol - **Methanation (minor):** $C + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_4$ **Why producer gas has low calorific value:** Producer gas from air-blown gasifiers is diluted with **~45–55% N₂** from the air. The typical composition is: - CO: 18–22% - H₂: 12–18% - CH₄: 2–4% - CO₂: 9–12% - N₂: 45–55% Calculated CV: $(0.20 \times 12.6) + (0.15 \times 10.8) + (0.03 \times 35.8) = 2.52 + 1.62 + 1.07 = 5.21$ MJ/Nm³ **Upgrading methods:** - Use **pure O₂** instead of air (eliminates N₂ dilution) → CV rises to 10–15 MJ/Nm³. - **Steam gasification** → adds H₂, raises CV. - **Hot gas cleanup** (cyclone, ceramic filter) to remove tars. - **Catalytic reforming** of tars and methane to CO + H₂. - **Pressure swing adsorption** to remove CO₂. --- #### Model Solution — (b) **Given:** 200 TPD of MSW, ultimate analysis on as-received basis: C=20, H=3, O=12, N=0.6, S=0.3, M=45%, Ash = 100 - 20 - 3 - 12 - 0.6 - 0.3 - 45 = **19.1%** **Subpart 1 — Calorific Value of RDF:** **Step A:** Calculate CV of as-received MSW using Dulong's formula (in kcal/kg for ease, then convert): $CV_{AR} = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\,(H - O/8) + 2240\,S\right] \text{ kcal/kg}$ $$CV_{AR} = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080 \times 20 + 34500 \times (3 - 12/8) + 2240 \times 0.3\right]$$ $$CV_{AR} = 80.8 \times 20 + 345 \times (3 - 1.5) + 22.4 \times 0.3$$ $$CV_{AR} = 1616 + 517.5 + 6.72 = 2140.22 \text{ kcal/kg} \approx 8.96 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ **Step B:** RDF preparation removes **30% inerts** and reduces moisture. Assume final moisture in RDF is **15%**. - MSW per day = 200 TPD - Inerts removed = 30% → Combustible feed = 200 × 0.7 = 140 TPD - Original moisture in 200 T = 90 T (45% of 200) - Original dry mass in combustible fraction = 200 × 0.7 × 0.55 = 77 T (inerts already excluded, moisture from combustible) - Ash in original MSW = 19.1% × 200 = 38.2 T, but ~30% of inerts are ash + non-combustibles; the effective ash in the combustible feed (after RDF processing) is reduced to **~10%** of combustible mass. For simplicity, after RDF processing (inerts removed, dried to 15% M): - Combustible dry mass in RDF ≈ 77 T - New moisture fraction = 15% → New wet RDF mass = 77 / 0.85 ≈ **90.6 TPD** - Ash in RDF ≈ 10% of RDF → 9.06 T CV of dry combustible fraction (ignoring ash effect on heating value per kg combustible): $$CV_{RDF,\,dry\,combustible} = 8.96 \text{ MJ/kg (unchanged)}$$ CV of RDF as fired (including ash and moisture): $$CV_{RDF,\,AR} = CV_{dry\,comb} \times (1 - M - A) = 8.96 \times (1 - 0.15 - 0.10) = 8.96 \times 0.75$$ $$\boxed{CV_{RDF} = 6.72 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$ **Subpart 2 — Daily electrical energy output:** Energy input per day = 90.6 TPD × 6.72 MJ/kg = 90600 × 6.72 = **608,832 MJ/day** Electrical output at 22% efficiency: $E_{elec} = 0.22 \times 608832 = 133,943$ MJ/day Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ): $E_{elec} = 133943 / 3.6$ $$\boxed{E_{elec} = 37,206 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 37.2 \text{ MWh/day}}$$ **Subpart 3 — Per capita supply:** $$\text{Per capita} = \dfrac{37206 \text{ kWh/day}}{200000 \text{ persons}} = 0.186 \text{ kWh/person/day}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Per capita supply} = 0.186 \text{ kWh per person per day}}$$ **Interpretation:** This is about 5.6 kWh/month per person — modest, suitable for **street lighting and water pumping** for a residential colony, but insufficient to power a household's full electrical needs (which would be ~150–250 kWh/month per family in India). A typical 1.5 MW WtE plant in India is sized to handle 500–700 TPD of MSW. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Biomass Conversion Questions:** > 1. **Confusing pyrolysis zones with gasifier zones.** Pyrolysis has NO combustion zone; gasifier has FOUR zones (drying, pyrolysis, combustion, reduction). Many students write only 3 zones for gasifier — **lose 2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to convert m³ of CH₄ to MJ correctly.** Methane's energy density is **35.8 MJ/m³ at STP** (NOT 55.5 MJ/kg — that's mass-basis). Using the wrong one is a **3-mark trap**. > 3. **Not specifying the basis of CV.** Always state whether the CV is on **dry basis, dry-ash-free (DAF) basis, or as-received basis**. Mixing them in a numerical = **2-mark penalty**. > 4. **Ignoring moisture correction.** Indian MSW has 40–60% moisture. Students who compute CV and forget to multiply by $(1 - M)$ get **3 marks deducted**. > 5. **Writing transesterification without stoichiometry.** Always show: TG + 3MeOH → 3FAME + Glycerol. Just saying "oil + methanol → biodiesel" gets **partial credit only**. > 6. **Misspelling methanogenic archaea as "bacteria".** They are **archaea** (domain Archaea), not bacteria. Examiners are strict on this **nomenclature error** (1 mark). > 7. **In Biodiesel questions:** Many students forget the **molar ratio (6:1 methanol:oil)** and **temperature (60 °C)** specifications. Include them for full marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Must Memorize for KTU ESE** ### 1. Classification (Block-level) - **Biochemical route (wet):** Anaerobic Digestion, Fermentation, Composting - **Thermochemical route (dry):** Combustion, Gasification, Pyrolysis, Torrefaction - **Chemical route (oily):** Transesterification (biodiesel) ### 2. Anaerobic Digestion — 4 Stages (in order) - **H**ydrolysis → **A**cidogenesis → **A**cetogenesis → **M**ethanogenesis - Mnemonic: **"HAAM"** (Haam se biogas banta hai!) - Optimum: **pH 6.8–7.4**, C/N **20–30**, T = 30–55 °C ### 3. Gasifier — 4 Zones - **D**rying → **P**yrolysis → **C**ombustion → **R**eduction - Mnemonic: **"DPCR"** = Downdraft Process Combustion Reduction - Two key reactions: **Boudouard (C + CO₂ → 2CO)** & **Water-gas (C + H₂O → CO + H₂)** ### 4. Pyrolysis Yields - Slow → max **char** (35%) - Fast → max **bio-oil** (75%) - Flash → max **gas** ### 5. Key Numbers (Recall Instantly) - CV of dry wood = **17–20 MJ/kg** - CV of MSW (Indian) = **3.5–6 MJ/kg** - CV of RDF = **12–18 MJ/kg** - CH₄ in biogas = **55–65%** - Energy of CH₄ (volumetric) = **35.8 MJ/m³** - Energy of CH₄ (mass) = **55.5 MJ/kg** - Optimum C/N for AD = **25:1** - Air composition = **23.2% O₂ by mass, 21% by volume** ### 6. The 7 Critical Equations 1. Dulong's CV formula 2. Stoichiometric air = $11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S$ 3. Buswell's stoichiometry for $C_nH_aO_b$ 4. $\eta_{gas} = CV_{gas} \times V_{gas} / (CV_{biomass} \times m_{biomass})$ 5. $Y_{CH_4} \approx 0.35$ m³/kg VS (operational average) 6. $E_{biogas} = V_{CH_4} \times 35.8$ MJ 7. $m_{air} = m_{O_2} / 0.232$ ### 7. Urban Waste to Energy — 4 Pathways - **Incineration** (with WtE boiler) — direct thermal - **RDF** — densified fuel for industry - **Landfill Gas (LFG)** — passive recovery - **Biomethanation** — controlled AD of organic fraction ### 8. Decision Rule of Thumb - **High moisture → AD or fermentation** - **High lignin + dry → combustion / gasification** - **Vegetable oil → transesterification (biodiesel)** - **Mixed urban waste → integrated WtE (AD + RDF + incineration)** > **End of Module 3 Notes — Biomass Conversion Technologies & Urban Waste to Energy Conversion. Best of luck for your KTU ESE!**

topics.title: Biomass Gasification and Biomass to Ethanol Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Gasification and Biomass to Ethanol Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biomass Gasification — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass Gasification** is a thermo-chemical conversion process in which solid biomass (or any carbonaceous feedstock) is partially oxidized at sub-stoichiometric air supply, typically at temperatures between **600 °C and 1200 °C**, to produce a combustible gaseous fuel known as **Producer Gas** (also called *Synthesis Gas* or *Syngas* when used for chemical synthesis).

The general simplified stoichiometric expression for complete gasification of cellulose (the structural backbone of biomass) with sub-stoichiometric oxygen is:

$$C_6H_{10}O_5 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 6CO + 5H_2$$

The product gas is a low-to-medium calorific value mixture primarily composed of **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Hydrogen (H₂)**, **Methane (CH₄)**, **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**, **Nitrogen (N₂)**, and water vapor. The calorific value of producer gas typically ranges from **4 MJ/Nm³ to 7 MJ/Nm³** depending on the gasifier type and feedstock moisture content.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smoldering Campfire vs. The Flaming Bonfire**
> Imagine a campfire. If you blow gently on glowing red charcoal embers, the wood does **not** burst into bright yellow flames. Instead, the embers turn a brighter orange-red, a hot gas is released, and the wood slowly turns to ash. That hot, flammable smoke you see is essentially **producer gas being generated**. Now imagine blowing that gas through a pipe into a separate chamber where it cleanly combusts — that is exactly what a gasifier does! Instead of letting the biomass burn directly, the gasifier *tricks* the chemistry by starving it of oxygen, forcing the wood to release its energy in a gaseous form that can be cleaned and used in engines, turbines, or chemical reactors.

The **key insight** is that a normal fire wastes energy as uncontrolled heat and light, while a gasifier captures the chemical energy of biomass into a controllable, transportable gas.

## 1.2 Biomass to Ethanol Production — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomass to Ethanol (Bioethanol) Production** is a biochemical/thermochemical conversion process in which the carbohydrate content of biomass (sugars, starches, or cellulose/hemicellulose) is transformed into **ethyl alcohol (C₂H₅OH)** through fermentation by microorganisms (typically *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) or, in advanced routes, via gasification followed by catalytic synthesis of ethanol from syngas.

The classical biochemical fermentation reaction is:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2 + \text{Heat}$$

The **theoretical maximum yield** of ethanol from glucose is **0.511 g ethanol per gram of glucose consumed**, equivalent to approximately **51.1 % by mass**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Bread Dough, Beer, and Whiskey**
> Think about how bread rises. The yeast eats the sugar in the dough, releases CO₂ (which creates bubbles that make the bread fluffy), and produces a tiny amount of alcohol that evaporates during baking. The exact same biochemical process — yeast consuming sugar and producing alcohol + CO₂ — is the heart of ethanol production. In a biofuel plant, however, we don't let the CO₂ escape; we *capture* the alcohol, filter it, and concentrate it through distillation (essentially the same process used to make whiskey, but for fuel instead of drinking). The challenge with biomass-to-ethanol is that most plant matter is *not* sugar — it is locked inside tough cellulose and hemicellulose fibers, so we must first "unlock" the sugars using a pre-treatment step before yeast can work.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Indicators to Memorize for KTU Exams**
> - **Calorific Value of Producer Gas**: 4 – 7 MJ/Nm³
> - **Equivalence Ratio (ER)**: 0.20 – 0.40 for gasification
> - **Gasification Temperature**: 600 °C – 1200 °C
> - **Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose**: 0.511 g/g
> - **Energy Content of Ethanol**: ~29.7 MJ/kg (HHV)
> - **Density of Ethanol**: 789 kg/m³
> - **Auto-ignition Temperature of Ethanol**: 363 °C

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Biomass Gasification

Gasification is governed by a sequence of overlapping reactions, all of which occur in a single gasifier chamber but at different zones (drying, pyrolysis, oxidation/combustion, and reduction). The dominant reactions are:

### 2.1.1 Drying Zone (Zone I) — Physical Process

$$Biomass_{(wet)} \rightarrow Biomass_{(dry)} + H_2O_{(vapor)}$$

This is an **endothermic** physical moisture removal step occurring at temperatures **< 200 °C**. No chemical reaction takes place here, but it consumes a substantial portion of the reactor's enthalpy, especially for feedstocks with high moisture content.

### 2.1.2 Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone (Zone II) — Thermal Decomposition

At temperatures between **200 °C and 600 °C**, biomass decomposes in the absence of oxygen (or with limited oxygen) into three products: **char (solid carbon)**, **tar (condensable vapors)**, and **pyrolysis gas (non-condensable)**.

$$Biomass \rightarrow Char + Tar + \text{Gas (CO, H}_2\text{, CO}_2\text{, CH}_4\text{, C}_2\text{H}_4\text{)}$$

### 2.1.3 Oxidation / Combustion Zone (Zone III) — Exothermic Reactions

Here, the sub-stoichiometric oxygen reacts with char and volatiles, providing the heat required to sustain the endothermic reduction reactions downstream.

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$C + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO \quad \Delta H = -111.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta H = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### 2.1.4 Reduction Zone (Zone IV) — Endothermic Reactions

This is the **heart of gasification** — the hot combustion products react with char to form combustible CO and H₂.

$$C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{Boudouard Reaction})$$

$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.4 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{Water-Gas Reaction})$$

$$C + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{Methanation Reaction})$$

$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \text{ kJ/mol} \quad (\text{Water-Gas Shift})$$

## 2.2 Classification of Gasifiers

Gasifiers are categorized primarily by the **direction of air/oxidant flow** relative to the biomass movement. The four principal types are:

| Type | Airflow Direction | Tar Content | Typical CV (MJ/Nm³) | Best Application |
|------|-------------------|-------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Updraft (Counter-current)** | Upward, opposite to biomass | **Very High** | 5 – 6 | Direct firing, drying |
| **Downdraft (Co-current)** | Downward, parallel to biomass | **Low** | 4 – 5 | IC engines, small power |
| **Fluidized Bed** | Upward at high velocity | Medium | 4 – 6 | Large-scale, uniform feed |
| **Entrained Flow** | Concurrent, very fast | Very Low | 8 – 10 | Pulverized feedstock |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Downdraft is the Engineering Sweet Spot for Engines:** Downdraft gasifiers have the lowest tar content because the producer gas must pass *through* the hot oxidation zone before exiting. Tar is essentially vaporized biomass that didn't fully crack; in a downdraft, the high temperature (>800 °C) cracks the tars into lighter gases. Updraft gasifiers have high thermal efficiency but produce so much tar that the gas cannot be used directly in engines without extensive cleaning.

## 2.3 Key Performance Parameters

### 2.3.1 Equivalence Ratio (ER)

$$ER = \frac{\text{Actual Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR}_{st})}$$

- **ER = 1.0** → Complete combustion (no gasification)
- **ER < 1.0** → Sub-stoichiometric oxygen → Gasification
- **ER = 0** → Pure pyrolysis
- **Typical Gasification ER = 0.20 – 0.40**

### 2.3.2 Gasification Efficiency (Cold Gas Efficiency, CGE)

$$\eta_{CGE} = \frac{\dot{m}_{gas} \times LHV_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{biomass} \times LHV_{biomass}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{gas}$ = mass flow rate of producer gas
- $\dot{m}_{biomass}$ = mass flow rate of dry biomass feed
- $LHV$ = Lower Heating Value (because water in the gas is not condensed)

### 2.3.3 Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE)

$$\eta_{CCE} = \frac{\text{Mass of Carbon in Producer Gas}}{\text{Mass of Carbon in Biomass Feed}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Card — Memorize Before Exam**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|-------------------|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio | $ER = AFR_{actual} / AFR_{stoich}$ | 0.20 – 0.40 (gasification) |
| 2 | Cold Gas Efficiency | $\eta_{CGE} = \frac{\dot{m}_g \cdot LHV_g}{\dot{m}_b \cdot LHV_b}$ | 60 – 80 % |
| 3 | Carbon Conversion Efficiency | $\eta_{CCE} = \frac{C_{gas}}{C_{feed}} \times 100$ | 70 – 95 % |
| 4 | Air Requirement | $M_{air} = \frac{0.0692 \cdot MC + 2.64 \cdot MH + 0.45 \cdot MO}{0.21}$ | kg/kg biomass |
| 5 | Producer Gas Yield | $Y_g = \frac{\text{Volume of gas}}{\text{Mass of dry biomass}}$ | 2 – 4 Nm³/kg |
| 6 | Ethanol Stoichiometric Yield | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \to 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ | 0.511 g/g glucose |
| 7 | Fermentation Efficiency | $\eta_{ferm} = \frac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | 85 – 95 % |
| 8 | Distillation Energy | $E_{dist} \approx 2.5 - 3.0$ MJ/kg ethanol |  |
| 9 | Heating Value of Ethanol | $LHV_{ethanol} = 26.9$ MJ/kg, HHV = 29.7 MJ/kg | |
| 10 | Gasification Temperature Zones | Drying < 200 °C, Pyrolysis 200–600 °C, Oxidation 800–1200 °C | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Gasification efficiency is the *single most important* metric when selecting a gasifier for a combined heat and power (CHP) plant. For grid-tied electricity generation, CGE > 75 % is required for commercial viability. Below 60 %, the project is economically unviable.

## 2.5 Biomass to Ethanol — Two Routes

### Route A: Biochemical (Sugar/Starch Fermentation)

1. **Feedstock Preparation** → Sugarcane, sugar beet, sweet sorghum, corn, cassava
2. **Sugar Extraction / Starch Hydrolysis** → Juice pressing or enzymatic starch breakdown
3. **Fermentation** → *S. cerevisiae* converts C6 sugars to ethanol
4. **Distillation** → Separates ethanol (95 % azeotrope) from water
5. **Dehydration** → Molecular sieves produce anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %+)

### Route B: Lignocellulosic (Second-Generation) — More Complex

1. **Pretreatment** → Physical (milling), chemical (dilute acid, alkali), or biological to break down lignin
2. **Hydrolysis** → Enzymatic breakdown of cellulose to glucose (C6) and hemicellulose to xylose (C5)
3. **Fermentation** → Separate C6 and C5 fermentation (C5 sugars require genetically modified organisms)
4. **Distillation & Dehydration** → As above

### Route C: Thermochemical (Syngas to Ethanol)

1. **Gasification** → Biomass to syngas (CO + H₂)
2. **Catalytic Synthesis** → Syngas fermented or catalytically converted to ethanol using Rh-based catalysts
3. **Purification** → Distillation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the World Wants Lignocellulosic Ethanol:** First-generation ethanol (from corn or sugarcane) competes with food production. Second-generation ethanol uses agricultural waste, wood chips, or dedicated energy crops (switchgrass, miscanthus) — it does **not** compete with food. This is the *only* sustainable long-term pathway for bioethanol, and it is a guaranteed KTU exam topic.

## 2.6 Mass & Energy Balance Highlights

### Stoichiometric Ethanol Production from Glucose

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$$

**Molecular weights:**
- Glucose: 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180 g/mol
- Ethanol: 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 46 g/mol
- CO₂: 12 + 2(16) = 44 g/mol

**Mass yield:** $\frac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.5111$ g ethanol per g glucose

This 51.1 % is the **theoretical maximum**. Industrial yields range from 85 % to 95 % of this theoretical value due to:
- Cell viability losses
- Side-product formation (glycerol, acetic acid)
- Incomplete sugar conversion

$$\text{Actual Yield} = 0.5111 \times 0.90 \approx 0.460 \text{ g ethanol / g glucose (practical)}$$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility Snapshot**
> - **Producer Gas Applications**: Rural electrification via gasifier-IC engine sets (5 kW – 1 MW), direct industrial heating, syngas for Fischer-Tropsch diesel, methanol synthesis, hydrogen production, and Combined Cycle gas turbine plants.
> - **Bioethanol Applications**: Gasoline blending (E10, E20, E85), feedstock for **ETBE** (Ethyl Tertiary Butyl Ether) which replaces MTBE as a clean octane booster, feedstock for **bio-ethylene** (which produces bio-based plastics).
> - **Environmental Impact**: Producer gas displaces fossil fuels in process industries; bioethanol reduces net CO₂ emissions by ~60 % compared to gasoline on a well-to-wheel basis.
