> **Stage 6 — Cathode Reduction & By-product Removal (How):** Electrons, protons, and oxygen recombine at the cathode to form **water** (and sometimes $\text{CO}_2$ if hydrocarbon fuels are used). The water is drained, while heat is removed by a coolant loop.
> **Why:** Continuous removal of products is essential to prevent concentration polarization and maintain cell voltage.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following high-yield table is the **single most important reference** for solving Part B numerical questions on fuel cells.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Standard Units |
|---|---------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $E^{\circ} = \dfrac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Theoretical open-circuit voltage from Gibbs free energy | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \times 100$ | Theoretical (thermodynamic) efficiency | % |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{voltaic}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^{\circ}} \times 100$ | Voltage efficiency (accounts for irreversibilities) | % |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{faradaic}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{actual}}}{I_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Current / Faradaic efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out, electrical}}}{P_{\text{in, chemical}}} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\dot{n} \cdot \Delta H}$ | Practical conversion efficiency | % |
| 6 | $P_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ} \cdot I - I^2 \cdot R_{\text{int}}$ | Net electrical power output (W) | Watts |
| 7 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz relation (links efficiency to temperature) | J/mol |
| 8 | $E_{T} = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$ | Nernst equation (temperature & pressure corrected E.M.F.) | Volts |
| 9 | $\text{LHV efficiency} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\text{LHV of } \text{H}_2} \approx 94.5\%$ | Used when water leaves as vapor (most realistic for vehicles) | % |
| 10 | $\text{Energy} = \dot{n}_{\text{fuel}} \times \Delta H$ | Chemical input power from molar flow rate | W |

**Mnemonic:** *"Gives Voltage Has Power"* — **G** (ΔG), **V** (E°), **H** (ΔH), **P** (Power).

## 2.3 Why Fuel Cells Beat Heat Engines — Real-World Engineering Utility

Conventional thermal power plants (Rankine, Brayton cycles) are bound by the **Carnot efficiency limit**:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$$

A fuel cell **bypasses the heat step entirely** — chemical energy is converted *directly* to electrical energy. This gives three engineering advantages:

1. **No moving parts** → silent operation, near-zero wear, low maintenance.
2. **No combustion** → negligible $\text{NO}_x$, $\text{SO}_x$, particulate emissions.
3. **Scalable & modular** → power stacks can be paralleled for kW to MW applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked *"Why is fuel cell efficiency higher than a heat engine?"*, the answer must explicitly invoke the fact that fuel cells are **not limited by the Carnot cycle** because they convert energy via an electrochemical pathway rather than thermal pathway.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Gibbs Free Energy to Open-Circuit Voltage

**Given:**
- Half-cell reaction: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at 25 °C
- $n = 2$ electrons transferred per mole of $\text{H}_2$
- $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$

**To Derive:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$

**Step 1 — Free Energy and Electrical Work Equivalence**
The maximum useful electrical work obtainable from a chemical reaction equals the negative of the change in Gibbs free energy.

$$W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 2 — Express Electrical Work in terms of Charge and Voltage**
Electrical work is the product of charge transported and the potential difference.

$$W_{\text{elec}} = Q \cdot E^{\circ} = (nF) \cdot E^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\Delta G &= nF E^{\circ} \\
\Rightarrow \quad E^{\circ} &= \dfrac{-\Delta G}{nF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ} &= \dfrac{-(-237{,}200 \text{ J/mol})}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}} \\
&= \dfrac{237{,}200}{192{,}970} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1.229 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each individual hydrogen–oxygen cell delivers approximately **1.229 V** at standard conditions. To obtain higher voltages (e.g., 200 V for a vehicle traction bus), multiple cells are stacked in **series** to form a **fuel cell stack**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Efficiency from First Principles

**Given:**
- $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (HHV)
- Same half-cell reaction as above.

**Step 1 — Define Thermodynamic Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\text{Useful (electrical) energy}}{\text{Total chemical energy input}} = \dfrac{\vert \Delta G \vert}{\vert \Delta H \vert} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (HHV Basis)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{th, HHV}} &= \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} \times 100\% \\
&\approx \mathbf{83.0\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — LHV Basis (water exits as vapor, $\Delta H_{\text{LHV}} = 241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{th, LHV}} &= \dfrac{237.2}{241.8} \times 100\% \\
&\approx \mathbf{94.5\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The HHV basis assumes product water condenses to liquid (recovering latent heat). The LHV basis is used when water escapes as steam (typical automotive exhaust condition) and gives the highest *theoretical* ceiling — higher than any practical heat engine.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Practical Efficiency Using Nernst Voltage Drop

**Given:**
- $T = 353 \text{ K}$ (80 °C), $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1.5 \text{ atm}$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21 \text{ atm}$ (air)
- Measured operating cell voltage $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.70 \text{ V}$ at load current $I = 50 \text{ A}$
- $\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} = 0.001 \text{ mol/s}$

**Step 1 — Nernst Open-Circuit Voltage at given T and P**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_T &= E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{H}_2} \cdot P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{1}\right) \\
&= 1.229 + \dfrac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} \ln\left(1.5 \times \sqrt{0.21}\right) \\
&= 1.229 + (0.01520) \ln(0.687) \\
&= 1.229 + (0.01520)(-0.375) \\
&\approx 1.223 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Electrical Output Power**

$$P_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I = 0.70 \times 50 = 35 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Chemical Input Power (HHV)**

$$P_{\text{in}} = \dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} \cdot \Delta H^{\circ} = 0.001 \times 285{,}800 = 285.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Overall Practical Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{overall}} &= \dfrac{35}{285.8} \times 100\% \\
&\approx \mathbf{12.25\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{voltage}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.70}{1.223} \times 100\% \approx 57.2\%$$

**Interpretation:** Real fuel cells lose ~43% of their theoretical voltage to **activation polarization** (sluggish reaction kinetics), **ohmic polarization** (electrolyte resistance), and **concentration polarization** (mass transport limitations). This is the single most important reason practical efficiencies sit between **40% and 60%**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Fuel Cells by Electrolyte

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Mandatory Classification Table**

| Feature | **PEMFC** | **AFC** | **PAFC** | **MCFC** | **SOFC** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrolyte** | Proton Exchange Membrane | Aqueous KOH | Liquid $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ | Molten $\text{Li}_2\text{CO}_3$/$\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$ | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) |
| **Mobile Ion** | $\text{H}^+$ | $\text{OH}^-$ | $\text{H}^+$ | $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ | $\text{O}^{2-}$ |
| **Operating Temp.** | 50–100 °C | 65–220 °C | 150–220 °C | 600–700 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| **Electrical Eff.** | 40–60% | 50–60% | 40–50% | 45–55% | 50–60% |
| **Cogeneration Eff.** | 70–90% | ~80% | ~85% | ~85% | ~85% |
| **Catalyst** | Platinum | Platinum / Nickel | Platinum | Nickel (no precious metal) | Perovskite (no precious metal) |
| **Primary Fuel** | $\text{H}_2$ (pure) | $\text{H}_2$ (pure) | $\text{H}_2$ / reformed | $\text{H}_2$, $\text{CH}_4}$, CO | $\text{H}_2$, $\text{CH}_4}$, CO |
| **Key Applications** | Automobiles (cars, buses), portable, space | Space (Apollo, Shuttle), military | Stationary power (200 kW units) | Large stationary, industrial | Large stationary, hybrid cycles |

**Memory Aid:** *"PAMPS" — Proton–Alkali–Molten–Phosphoric–Solid* — read left to right with increasing operating temperature.

---

## 3.5 Classification by Operating Temperature

1. **Low-Temperature Fuel Cells** (50–250 °C): PEMFC, AFC, PAFC.
2. **Intermediate-Temperature Fuel Cells** (~500 °C): Limited commercial use.
3. **High-Temperature Fuel Cells** (600–1000 °C): MCFC, SOFC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why temperature matters:** High-temperature cells do **not** need precious metal catalysts, can directly use hydrocarbon fuels (internal reforming), and have higher tolerance to CO poisoning. Low-temperature cells, however, start faster and use cheaper, more common materials.

## 3.6 Conversion Efficiency — Comparative Snapshot

| Fuel Cell Type | Practical Electrical Efficiency | With Heat Recovery (CHP) |
|---|---|---|
| PEMFC | 40–60% | 70–90% |
| AFC | 50–60% | ~80% |
| PAFC | 40–50% | ~85% |
| MCFC | 45–55% | ~85% |
| SOFC | 50–60% | ~85% |
| **Internal Combustion Engine (Reference)** | **25–35%** | **80–90%** |
| **Coal Thermal Power (Reference)** | **33–42%** | **—** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that **every fuel cell type beats the IC engine** in raw electrical efficiency. This is a frequently asked 3-mark comparative question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic H₂–O₂ Fuel Cell

The following Mermaid diagram captures the functional flow of a single fuel cell, including all sub-components and the direction of mass, ion, and electron transport.

```mermaid
graph LR
    H2Tank[H2 Fuel Supply] -->|Pressurized H2 Gas| Anode
    AirIntake[Air or O2 Supply] -->|Pressurized Oxidant| Cathode
    Anode[Anode Catalyst Layer - Pt or Ni] -->|Oxidation: H2 to 2H+ plus 2e-| Electrolyte
    Electrolyte[Electrolyte - PEM, KOH, H3PO4, Molten Carbonate, or YSZ] -->|Ion Migration H+, OH-, CO3 2-, or O2-| Cathode
    Cathode[Cathode Catalyst Layer - Pt or Ni] -->|Reduction forms H2O| WaterOut[Water and Heat Byproduct]
    Anode -->|Electron Flow via External Wire| ExternalLoad[External Load - Motor, Light, Grid]
    ExternalLoad -->|Return Current Path| Cathode
    CoolingLoop[Coolant Manifold] -.->|Heat Removal| Electrolyte
    WaterOut -.->|Latent Heat Recovery CHP| CoolingLoop
```

**How to read this diagram for the KTU exam:**
- Follow the **solid arrows** to trace the electrochemical path (mass and electron flow).
- The **dotted arrows** denote the auxiliary thermal loop used for Combined Heat and Power (CHP).
- The **External Load** block is where useful electrical work is extracted.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fuel Cell Stack and BOP

```mermaid
graph TD
    Reformer[Fuel Processor / Reformer] -->|Purified H2| StackIn
    AirComp[Air Compressor and Humidifier] -->|Filtered O2| StackIn
    StackIn[FC Stack Inlet Manifold] --> Cell1[Cell 1 - 1.2 V]
    Cell1 --> Cell2[Cell 2 - 1.2 V]
    Cell2 --> Cell3[Cell N - 1.2 V]
    Cell3 --> StackOut[FC Stack Outlet Manifold]
    StackOut -->|Excess H2 Recycle| Reformer
    StackOut -->|Exhaust H2O and Heat| BoP
    BoP[Balance of Plant BOP] -->|Cooling| Radiator[Heat Exchanger]
    BoP -->|Power Conditioning| Inverter[DC to AC Inverter]
    Inverter --> Grid[AC Load or Grid]
    ControlUnit[Electronic Control Unit ECU] -.->|Regulates Flow, T, P| Reformer
    ControlUnit -.->|Modulates Current| StackIn
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever asked to *"draw the block diagram of a fuel cell system"*, students must include the **Balance of Plant (BoP)** — compressor, humidifier, heat exchanger, inverter, and ECU — not just the bare cell. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for missing BoP.

---

## 4.3 Voltage–Current (V–I) Polarization Curve

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Open Circuit Voltage E0 approx 1.23 V] -->|Activation Polarization - steep drop| B[Ohmic Region - linear drop]
    B -->|Concentration Polarization - sharp drop| C[Limiting Current Density]
    A --- D[Activation Loss Region]
    B --- E[Ohmic Loss Region]
    C --- F[Mass Transport Loss Region]
```

**Reading aid:** The **operating point** of a fuel cell is the "knee" between the ohmic and concentration regions. Designers operate there to maximize the product $V \times I$ (power density).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a fuel cell. Mention any two advantages of a fuel cell over a conventional battery.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A fuel cell is an **electrochemical device** that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (e.g., hydrogen) and an oxidant (e.g., oxygen) **directly into electrical energy**, with water and heat as by-products, as long as reactants are supplied.

**Two advantages over a conventional battery:**
1. **Continuous operation** — A fuel cell does not require recharging; refueling with hydrogen restores operation instantly, whereas a battery needs hours to recharge.
2. **Higher energy density** — Fuel cells offer energy densities of **500–1000 Wh/kg** versus ~150–250 Wh/kg for Li-ion batteries, making them superior for long-range transport and heavy-load applications.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks advantages. Use bullet points; KTU examiners award partial credit per correct bullet.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: List any three types of fuel cells based on the electrolyte used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Based on electrolyte, fuel cells are classified as:
1. **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** — uses a solid polymer membrane.
2. **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)** — uses a solid ceramic electrolyte (YSZ).
3. **Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)** — uses molten lithium/potassium carbonate.

*(Other valid answers: AFC, PAFC — 1 mark per correct type, max 3.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) and (b) each carrying **7 marks**. Choose either **Question A** or **Question B** below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Explain the principle of operation of a Hydrogen-Oxygen fuel cell with neat sketches and relevant equations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell is an electrochemical device that directly converts the chemical energy of $\text{H}_2$ and $\text{O}_2$ into DC electricity using an electrolyte that selectively conducts ions.

**Step 2 — Construction (2 marks):**
- Two porous electrodes — **anode** and **cathode** — coated with a catalyst (Pt).
- An **electrolyte** sandwiched between them.
- External circuit connecting the electrodes through a load.

**Step 3 — Working with Half-Cell Reactions (3 marks):**

At the **anode**, hydrogen is oxidized:

$$\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-\quad (E^{\circ} = 0 \text{ V})$$

Protons migrate through the electrolyte; electrons travel through the external circuit.

At the **cathode**, oxygen is reduced:

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}\quad (E^{\circ} = 1.23 \text{ V})$$

**Net Reaction:**

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

**Step 4 — Sketches (1 mark):**
*(Draw the standard anode–electrolyte–cathode arrangement with arrows showing ion flow through electrolyte and electron flow through external load.)*

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the theoretical efficiency of a fuel cell and explain why it is independent of the Carnot limit. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derivation (4 marks):**

The maximum useful electrical work obtained equals the negative of Gibbs free energy change.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{elec}} &= -\Delta G = nFE^{\circ} \\
\text{Electrical power produced per mole} &= nFE^{\circ} \\
\text{Total chemical energy input per mole} &= \vert \Delta H \vert \\
\eta_{\text{th}} &= \dfrac{\text{Useful electrical energy}}{\text{Total chemical energy}} \\
&= \dfrac{nFE^{\circ}}{\vert \Delta H \vert} = \dfrac{\vert \Delta G \vert}{\vert \Delta H \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\text{H}_2$–$\text{O}_2$ cell:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{th, HHV}} &= \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} \times 100\% \approx 83\% \\
\eta_{\text{th, LHV}} &= \dfrac{237.2}{241.8} \times 100\% \approx 94.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Independence from Carnot Limit (3 marks):**
The Carnot limit applies only to **heat engines** that operate on a thermal cycle ($Q \rightarrow W_{\text{mech}}$). Fuel cells are **not heat engines** — they convert chemical free energy **directly** into electrical energy via an isothermal electrochemical reaction. There is **no intermediate thermal energy conversion step**, so the Second Law restriction via Carnot does not apply. This is the fundamental reason fuel cells can theoretically exceed 80% electrical efficiency, far above any practical heat engine.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Classify fuel cells based on electrolyte and operating temperature. Compare any two types in terms of efficiency, operating temperature, and applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Classification by Electrolyte (3 marks):**
1. PEMFC — Proton Exchange Membrane
2. AFC — Alkaline Fuel Cell
3. PAFC — Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell
4. MCFC — Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell
5. SOFC — Solid Oxide Fuel Cell

**Step 2 — Classification by Temperature (2 marks):**
- Low temperature (50–250 °C): PEMFC, AFC, PAFC
- High temperature (600–1000 °C): MCFC, SOFC

**Step 3 — Comparison of PEMFC vs SOFC (2 marks):**

| Parameter | PEMFC | SOFC |
|---|---|---|
| Electrolyte | Polymer membrane | YSZ (solid ceramic) |
| Operating T | 50–100 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| Electrical Efficiency | 40–60% | 50–60% |
| Catalyst | Platinum (costly) | Nickel / perovskite (cheap) |
| Fuel | Pure $\text{H}_2$ | $\text{H}_2$, $\text{CH}_4$, CO |
| Start-up | Fast (seconds) | Slow (hours) |
| Applications | Automobiles, portable | Large stationary, hybrid power plants |

---

**(b) A fuel cell uses $\text{H}_2$ as fuel with an enthalpy of combustion $\Delta H = -286 \text{ kJ/mol}$. It delivers a constant voltage of 0.75 V at a current of 100 A. Calculate: (i) the Gibbs free energy change, (ii) the theoretical efficiency, (iii) the actual efficiency, and (iv) the voltage efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\Delta H = -286 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$, $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.75 \text{ V}$, $I = 100 \text{ A}$

**Step (i) Gibbs Free Energy Change (2 marks):**

For theoretical OCV $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G &= -nFE^{\circ} \\
&= -2 \times 96{,}485 \times 1.229 \\
&= -237{,}200 \text{ J/mol} \\
&\approx \mathbf{-237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) Theoretical Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\vert \Delta G \vert}{\vert \Delta H \vert} = \dfrac{237.2}{286} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{82.94\%}
$$

**Step (iii) Actual Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

Molar flow rate of $\text{H}_2$ from current:

$$
\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{I}{nF} = \dfrac{100}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 5.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}
$$

Electrical output power:

$$P_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I = 0.75 \times 100 = 75 \text{ W}$$

Chemical input power:

$$P_{\text{in}} = \dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} \cdot \vert \Delta H \vert = 5.18 \times 10^{-4} \times 286{,}000 = 148.15 \text{ W}$$

$$
\eta_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{75}{148.15} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{50.63\%}
$$

**Step (iv) Voltage Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{voltage}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^{\circ}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.75}{1.229} \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{61.03\%}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- $\Delta G = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$
- $\eta_{\text{th}} = 82.94\%$
- $\eta_{\text{actual}} = 50.63\%$
- $\eta_{\text{voltage}} = 61.03\%$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Fuel Cell Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing HHV and LHV** — Students frequently mix the two bases for efficiency calculation. KTU examiners explicitly specify which one. Default to HHV unless the question says "water leaves as vapor".
> 2. **Forgetting the minus sign in ΔG** — ΔG for the $\text{H}_2$–$\text{O}_2$ reaction is *negative* (exergonic). Using $\Delta G = +237.2$ in the formula $E^{\circ} = -\Delta G / nF$ yields a wrong sign for voltage. Always carry the minus.
> 3. **Omitting the Balance of Plant (BoP)** in block diagrams — A bare cell diagram without humidifier, compressor, inverter, and ECU loses **at least 2 marks**.
> 4. **Wrong catalyst for SOFC/MCFC** — Writing "platinum catalyst" for SOFC/MCFC is incorrect. These use **nickel** (MCFC anode) or **perovskite** (SOFC cathode).
> 5. **Not stating $n = 2$ explicitly** — KTU board markers often give only **0.5 marks** for the half-cell reaction if $n$ is not numerically stated.
> 6. **Mixing up HHV and LHV values** — Standard HHV of $\text{H}_2$ = 285.8 kJ/mol; LHV = 241.8 kJ/mol. Using the wrong one yields ~13% error in theoretical efficiency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fuel Cells (KTU Module 4):**

- ☐ **Definition:** Electrochemical device converting fuel + oxidant → electricity + water + heat (continuously supplied).
- ☐ **Half-cell reactions:** Anode oxidation of $\text{H}_2$; cathode reduction of $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$; net produces $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- ☐ **Theoretical OCV:** $E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V}$ per cell at STP.
- ☐ **Theoretical efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{th}} = \vert \Delta G / \Delta H \vert = 83\%$ (HHV), $94.5\%$ (LHV).
- ☐ **Five Types by Electrolyte:** PEMFC, AFC, PAFC, MCFC, SOFC — remember **PAMPS**.
- ☐ **Temperature groups:** Low (50–250 °C) vs High (600–1000 °C).
- ☐ **Practical efficiency range:** 40–60% electrical; 70–90% with CHP.
- ☐ **Key advantage:** Not bounded by Carnot limit (no thermal step).
- ☐ **Applications triad:** Transportation (cars, buses), Stationary (power plants, CHP), Portable (laptops, military).
- ☐ **Polarization losses:** Activation, Ohmic, Concentration — each reduces $V_{\text{cell}}$ below $E^{\circ}$.
- ☐ **Voltage efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{voltage}} = V_{\text{cell}} / E^{\circ}$.
- ☐ **Faraday relation:** $\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2} = I / (nF)$.
- ☐ **Nernst equation:** Use when temperature/pressure deviate from STP.
- ☐ **Catalysts:** Pt for PEMFC/AFC/PAFC; Ni for MCFC; perovskite for SOFC.
- ☐ **Space legacy:** AFCs powered Apollo and Space Shuttle missions.
- ☐ **BoP components:** Compressor, humidifier, heat exchanger, inverter, ECU.

> **End of Module 4 — Fuel Cells Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen Energy: Production (Electrolysis and Thermochemical Methods), Storage, and Utilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT864 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SOURCES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen Energy: Production (Electrolysis and Thermochemical Methods), Storage, and Utilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Hydrogen Energy** refers to the chemical energy stored within molecular hydrogen ($\text{H}_2$) that can be released controllably through combustion or electrochemical oxidation (in a fuel cell), making hydrogen a *secondary energy carrier* rather than a primary energy source. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, hydrogen energy encompasses the **production**, **storage**, and **utilization** of $\text{H}_2$ derived from both renewable and non-renewable feedstocks, with an emphasis on the *hydrogen economy* framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Board Expectation):** Hydrogen is the **lightest, cleanest, and most energy-dense (by mass)** chemical fuel. Its gravimetric energy density is **142 MJ/kg (HHV)**, but its volumetric energy density is extremely low (≈ 10.8 MJ/m³ at STP), which is the central engineering challenge driving hydrogen storage research.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Battery of Molecules"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of hydrogen as a **"molecular battery."** A lithium-ion battery stores electricity as chemical energy and releases it as electricity. Hydrogen does the same — it is *manufactured* (charged) by spending electricity or heat, transported as a gas/liquid, and *discharged* later in a fuel cell to regenerate electricity with only **water as exhaust**.

Just as you must plug a battery into a charger, you must "charge" hydrogen by splitting water ($\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$). When the energy is needed, you "discharge" it by recombining hydrogen with oxygen in a fuel cell.

### 1.3 Physical & Chemical Properties of Hydrogen

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Molecular weight | **2.016 g/mol** |
| Density at STP (gas) | **0.0899 kg/m³** |
| Density (liquid, −253°C) | **70.8 kg/m³** |
| Boiling point | **20.27 K (−252.87 °C)** |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **142 MJ/kg** |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **120 MJ/kg** |
| Flammability range in air | **4 % – 75 % (by volume)** |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **585 °C** |
| Flame speed in air | ≈ 3 m/s |
| Diffusion coefficient in air | **6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** (fastest of all gases) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Hydrogen has the **highest energy-to-mass ratio** of any fuel (≈ 3× gasoline), but the **lowest energy-to-volume ratio** in gaseous form. This single trade-off defines the entire field of hydrogen storage engineering.

### 1.4 The Hydrogen Economy — Conceptual Framework

The **hydrogen economy** is the integrated system of:

1. **Primary Energy Source** (solar, wind, nuclear, fossil).
2. **Production** (electrolysis, thermochemical, reforming).
3. **Storage & Transport** (compressed, liquid, hydrides, pipelines).
4. **Utilization** (fuel cells, combustion engines, turbines, industrial feedstock).

$$\text{Hydrogen Economy} = \text{Production} \to \text{Storage} \to \text{Distribution} \to \text{End-Use}$$

### 1.5 Hydrogen Production — Color Coding (Industry Standard Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Knowledge:** Students must be able to identify hydrogen production pathways by their "color codes," as this is frequently asked in Part A questions.

- **Grey Hydrogen** — From natural gas (SMR); produces CO₂.
- **Brown Hydrogen** — From coal gasification; high CO₂ emissions.
- **Blue Hydrogen** — Grey/Brown + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS).
- **Green Hydrogen** — From renewable-powered electrolysis; **zero emissions**.
- **Pink Hydrogen** — From nuclear-powered electrolysis.
- **Yellow Hydrogen** — From grid electricity (mixed source).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Production Method 1 — Electrolysis of Water

Electrolysis is the process of splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using direct electric current, governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**.

$$\text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \xrightarrow{\text{Electricity}} \text{H}_{2(g)} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_{2(g)}$$

The **standard enthalpy change** at 25 °C and 1 atm is:

$$\Delta H^\circ = +285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The **Gibbs free energy change** is:

$$\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The difference $\Delta H - \Delta G = T\Delta S = 48.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$ corresponds to the **reversible thermal energy** absorbed from the surroundings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The **thermodynamic minimum cell voltage** (reversible voltage) is $E_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = \mathbf{1.23 \text{ V}}$. The **thermoneutral voltage** is $E_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{nF} = \mathbf{1.48 \text{ V}}$. Any practical electrolyzer must operate above 1.23 V (ideal) but ideally near 1.48 V (thermodynamically neutral operation).

#### 2.1.1 Types of Electrolyzers (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Electrolyte | Operating T | Efficiency | Cell Voltage | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Alkaline Electrolyzer (ALK)** | KOH / NaOH (liquid, 25–30 %) | 60 – 90 °C | 60 – 70 % | 1.8 – 2.4 V | Mature, cheap; uses Ni electrodes |
| **PEM Electrolyzer** | Polymer membrane (Nafion™) | 50 – 80 °C | 70 – 85 % | 1.8 – 2.2 V | Compact, fast response, high purity |
| **Solid Oxide Electrolyzer (SOEC)** | $\text{Y}_2\text{O}_3$-stabilized $\text{ZrO}_2$ | 700 – 850 °C | 75 – 90 % | 1.0 – 1.5 V | High efficiency, uses waste heat |

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step logic — Why SOEC has highest efficiency:**
> 1. High temperature supplies part of the energy as heat (cheaper than electricity).
> 2. Thermodynamic voltage decreases with temperature (Nernst equation).
> 3. Electrode kinetics improve dramatically at high T, reducing overpotentials.
> 4. Result: more electrical energy is converted to chemical energy per joule of input.

#### 2.1.2 Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (Production Rate)

The **mass** of hydrogen produced is:

$$m_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{\text{H}_2}}{n \cdot F}$$

The **volumetric flow rate** at STP:

$$\dot{V}_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot R \cdot T_{STP}}{n \cdot F \cdot P_{STP}}$$

where $n = 2$ electrons per $\text{H}_2$ molecule.

### 2.2 Production Method 2 — Thermochemical Methods

Thermochemical hydrogen production uses high-temperature heat (from nuclear or solar concentrators) to break water into hydrogen and oxygen through a closed loop of chemical reactions, with **all reagents regenerated**.

#### 2.2.1 Direct Thermolysis (One-step Thermal Decomposition)

$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{T > 2500\,°\text{C}} \text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why direct thermolysis is impractical:** Temperatures above 2500 °C are required, which demand exotic materials and cause rapid $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ recombination. This is why **cyclic thermochemical processes** were invented.

#### 2.2.2 Thermochemical Water-Splitting Cycles

These cycles split water through 2 or more chemical reactions with **all intermediate chemicals recycled**, requiring much lower peak temperatures (800–900 °C).

##### (a) Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) Cycle — *General Atomics Cycle*

The S–I cycle consists of three reactions:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bunsen Reaction:} \quad & \text{I}_2 + \text{SO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \to 2\text{HI} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \\[4pt]
\text{Sulfuric Acid Decomposition:} \quad & \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \xrightarrow{\sim 850\,°\text{C}} \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \\[4pt]
\text{Hydriodic Acid Decomposition:} \quad & 2\text{HI} \xrightarrow{\sim 300\,°\text{C}} \text{H}_2 + \text{I}_2
\end{aligned}$$

##### (b) Copper–Chlorine (Cu–Cl) Cycle

The Cu–Cl cycle is a **low-temperature** (≈ 550 °C) thermochemical cycle with 4–5 steps, attractive for coupling with Generation IV nuclear reactors:

$$\begin{aligned}
& 2\text{CuCl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{Cu}_2\text{OCl}_2 + 2\text{HCl} \quad (\approx 400\,°\text{C}) \\[2pt]
& \text{Cu}_2\text{OCl}_2 \to 2\text{CuCl} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \quad (\approx 500\,°\text{C}) \\[2pt]
& 2\text{CuCl} + 2\text{HCl} \to 2\text{CuCl}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (\approx 450\,°\text{C})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step logic — Why thermochemical cycles are preferred over direct thermolysis:**
> 1. Maximum temperature is reduced from 2500 °C to **≈ 850 °C (S–I)** or **550 °C (Cu–Cl)**.
> 2. Closed-loop chemistry means reagents ($\text{I}_2$, $\text{SO}_2$, $\text{CuCl}$, etc.) are continuously recycled — only $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is consumed and only $\text{H}_2$ and $\text{O}_2$ are produced.
> 3. The cycle can be heat-driven (nuclear or concentrated solar), giving **zero direct carbon emissions**.

### 2.3 Other Production Pathways (Concise Coverage)

| Method | Reaction Summary | Source of Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$ | Natural gas (thermal) |
| Partial Oxidation | $\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2$ | Fossil (thermal) |
| Coal Gasification | $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$ | Coal (thermal) |
| Biomass Gasification | Biomass + $\text{H}_2\text{O} \to$ syngas ($\text{CO} + \text{H}_2$) | Biomass (renewable) |
| Photoelectrochemical (PEC) | Sunlight + semiconductor + water | Solar (direct) |
| Biological (algae/bioreactors) | Enzymatic splitting of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Solar (biological) |

### 2.4 Hydrogen Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Storage is the **biggest engineering challenge** in the hydrogen economy. Four main strategies exist — students must know the energy density, advantages, and limitations of each.

| Storage Method | Volumetric Density | Gravimetric Density | Key Engineering Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compressed Gas (700 bar)** | ≈ 40 g/L | ≈ 4.5 wt% | High-pressure tank cost & safety |
| **Liquid $\text{H}_2$ (cryogenic, −253°C)** | ≈ 71 g/L | ≈ 6.0 wt% | Boil-off losses (≈ 0.1 %/day) |
| **Metal Hydrides** ($\text{LaNi}_5$, TiFe) | ≈ 100 g/L | 1 – 2 wt% | Heavy, slow kinetics |
| **Underground (salt caverns)** | Variable | Variable | Site-specific geology |
| **Liquid Organic $\text{H}_2$ Carriers (LOHC)** | ≈ 60 g/L | ≈ 6 – 7 wt% | Catalytic release temperature |

#### Energy Required to Compress Hydrogen

For isothermal compression of an ideal gas from $P_1$ to $P_2$:

$$W_{iso} = nRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

For adiabatic compression with polytropic index $k = 1.4$:

$$W_{adi} = \dfrac{nRT_1}{k - 1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Compressing $\text{H}_2$ to **700 bar** consumes roughly **10 – 15 %** of its LHV. This single fact illustrates why storage is the dominant cost & energy-loss element of the hydrogen value chain.

### 2.5 Hydrogen Utilization

| End Use | Application Domain | Key Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| **PEM / SOFC Fuel Cells** | Vehicles, stationary power | $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$ |
| **Internal Combustion Engines** | Modified gasoline/diesel engines | Combustion of $\text{H}_2$ |
| **Gas Turbines** | Repowered natural gas turbines | Combustion of $\text{H}_2$ in modified GT |
| **Ammonia Synthesis** | Haber–Bosch process | $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \to 2\text{NH}_3$ |
| **Steel & Refining** | Direct Reduced Iron (DRI) | $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{H}_2 \to 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| **Rocket Propellant** | Space launch vehicles | Combustion of $\text{LH}_2$ + $\text{LO}_2$ |

> **CRITICAL — Exam Quick-Reference Table:** All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU 2024 Part B numerical questions. Memorize units carefully.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G^\circ = nFE_{rev}$ | Gibbs free energy, electrons, Faraday constant, reversible voltage | kJ/mol, —, C/mol, V |
| 2 | $\Delta H^\circ = nFE_{tn}$ | Enthalpy, thermoneutral voltage | kJ/mol, V |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reversible Cell Voltage from Thermodynamics

**Given:** $\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ for the water-splitting reaction at 25 °C and 1 atm.

**Find:** The theoretical minimum cell voltage $E_{rev}$.

**Step 1 — Recall the fundamental thermodynamic relationship.**

The maximum reversible electrical work obtainable from a chemical reaction equals the negative of the Gibbs free energy change:

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Relate electrical work to charge and voltage.**

For $n$ moles of electrons transferred at cell voltage $E$:

$$W_{elec} = n \cdot F \cdot E$$

where:
- $n$ = number of electrons per mole of $\text{H}_2$ produced = **2**
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96 485 C/mol**
- $E$ = cell voltage (V)

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{elec,max} &= -\Delta G^\circ \\[4pt]
n \cdot F \cdot E_{rev} &= -\Delta G^\circ
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E_{rev}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{rev} &= \dfrac{-\Delta G^\circ}{nF} \\[6pt]
E_{rev} &= \dfrac{-(-237.2 \times 10^3 \text{ J/mol})}{(2)(96\,485 \text{ C/mol})} \\[6pt]
E_{rev} &= \dfrac{237\,200}{192\,970} \\[6pt]
E_{rev} &= 1.229 \text{ V} \;\approx\; \mathbf{1.23 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering meaning:** No matter how efficient your electrolyzer is designed, **you can never split water with less than 1.23 V** applied per cell. Real cells must exceed this to overcome kinetic and ohmic overpotentials.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis (Volumetric Form)

**Statement:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is proportional to the charge passed.

**Step 1 — Start with Faraday's mass law.**

$$m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where $Q = I \cdot t$ is the total charge in Coulombs.

**Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law to convert mass to volume at STP.**

For $\text{H}_2$ at STP ($T = 273.15$ K, $P = 101\,325$ Pa):

$$PV = n_{H_2} R T \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \dfrac{m}{M} \cdot RT/P$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two.**

Substituting $n_{H_2} = \dfrac{m}{M}$:

$$V = \dfrac{mRT}{MP}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Faraday's mass into the volume equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= \dfrac{1}{P} \cdot RT \cdot \left(\dfrac{ItM}{nFM}\right) \\[6pt]
V &= \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot R \cdot T}{n \cdot F \cdot P}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to volumetric flow rate** (divide by time $t$):

$$\dot{V}_{\text{H}_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot R \cdot T_{STP}}{n \cdot F \cdot P_{STP}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation example.**

For $I = 1000$ A, $n = 2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{H}_2} &= \dfrac{(1000)(8.314)(273.15)}{(2)(96\,485)(101\,325)} \\[6pt]
\dot{V}_{\text{H}_2} &= \dfrac{2.270 \times 10^6}{1.955 \times 10^{10}} \\[6pt]
\dot{V}_{\text{H}_2} &= 1.161 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\[6pt]
\dot{V}_{\text{H}_2} &\approx \mathbf{0.418 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Electrolyzer Efficiency from First Principles

**Definition:** Electrolyzer efficiency (energy basis) is the ratio of chemical energy output to electrical energy input.

**Step 1 — Chemical energy output (Hydrogen HHV).**

$$E_{out} = m_{\text{H}_2} \cdot \text{HHV}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical energy input.**

$$E_{in} = V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Form the efficiency ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{m_{\text{H}_2} \cdot \text{HHV}}{V_{cell} \cdot I \cdot t} \\[6pt]
\eta &= \dfrac{\text{HHV} \cdot M_{\text{H}_2}}{n \cdot F \cdot V_{cell}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check at $V_{cell} = 2.0$ V:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{(142 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg})(2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol})}{(2)(96\,485)(2.0)} \\[6pt]
\eta &= \dfrac{286\,272}{385\,940} \\[6pt]
\eta &= 0.742 \;=\; \mathbf{74.2\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison to ideal (1.23 V):**

$$\eta_{ideal} = \dfrac{(142 \times 10^6)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(2)(96\,485)(1.23)} = \dfrac{286\,272}{237\,473} = \mathbf{1.205} \;(\text{or 100\%})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The 1.23 V result equals 100 % by definition. Any practical cell operating at 1.8 – 2.4 V gives **50 – 75 %** efficiency. This 25–50 % loss is the "cost" of overpotentials and ohmic resistance.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermoneutral Voltage

**Step 1 — By definition, thermoneutral voltage corresponds to $\Delta H$.**

$$E_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{nF}$$

**Step 2 — Insert values.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{tn} &= \dfrac{285.8 \times 10^3 \text{ J/mol}}{(2)(96\,485 \text{ C/mol})} \\[6pt]
E_{tn} &= \dfrac{285\,800}{192\,970} \\[6pt]
E_{tn} &= 1.481 \text{ V} \;\approx\; \mathbf{1.48 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Physical meaning.**

- Operating at $V < 1.48$ V → cell absorbs heat from surroundings (endothermic operation).
- Operating at $V = 1.48$ V → cell is thermoneutral (no net heat exchange).
- Operating at $V > 1.48$ V → cell generates heat (excess losses dissipated).

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Adiabatic Compression Work for Hydrogen Storage

**Step 5.1 — For an ideal gas undergoing polytropic compression:**

$$W = \dfrac{nRT_1}{k - 1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} - 1\right]$$

**Step 5.2 — For diatomic hydrogen** ($k = 1.4$): $(k-1)/k = 0.4/1.4 = 0.2857$.

**Step 5.3 — Numerical example:** Compress 1 kg of $\text{H}_2$ from 1 bar to 700 bar starting at $T_1 = 298$ K.

Moles of $\text{H}_2$:
$$n = \dfrac{1000 \text{ g}}{2.016 \text{ g/mol}} = 496 \text{ mol}$$

Pressure ratio:
$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = 700$$

Temperature ratio rise:
$$\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k-1)/k} = 700^{0.2857}$$

**Step 5.4 — Compute** $700^{0.2857}$:

$$\ln(700^{0.2857}) = 0.2857 \cdot \ln(700) = 0.2857 \cdot 6.551 = 1.872$$

$$e^{1.872} = 6.498$$

**Step 5.5 — Compute work.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{(496)(8.314)(298)}{0.4} \cdot (6.498 - 1) \\[6pt]
W &= \dfrac{1\,228\,344}{0.4} \cdot 5.498 \\[6pt]
W &= 3\,070\,860 \cdot 5.498 \\[6pt]
W &= 1.689 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = \mathbf{16.89 \text{ MJ}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5.6 — Express as % of stored energy** (1 kg $\text{H}_2$ LHV = 120 MJ):

$$\dfrac{16.89}{120} = 0.141 = \mathbf{14.1\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Compressing hydrogen to 700 bar costs about **14 % of its LHV**. This is *only the theoretical minimum*; real compressors waste more, making the practical loss closer to 15 – 18 %.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Electrolyzer vs. Thermochemical vs. SMR

| Criterion | Electrolysis (PEM) | Thermochemical (S–I) | Steam Methane Reforming |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Electricity | High-temp heat + chem. | Natural gas + heat |
| Operating temperature | 50 – 80 °C | 850 °C (peak) | 700 – 900 °C |
| Carbon emissions | **Zero** (with renewable) | **Zero** | **9 – 12 kg CO₂ / kg H₂** |
| Efficiency (HHV) | 70 – 85 % | 40 – 55 % | 70 – 85 % |
| Commercial maturity | **Mature & scaling** | Pilot stage | **Mature** (≈ 96 % global) |
| Cost (USD/kg) | 3 – 8 | 5 – 15 (projected) | 1 – 3 |
| Feedstock | Water + renewable power | Water + nuclear/Solar | Natural gas + water |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Functional Architecture of a PEM Electrolyzer System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RES[Renewable Energy Source\nSolar PV or Wind] -->|DC Power| RECT[AC DC Converter\nand Rectifier]
    RECT -->|Regulated DC| STACK[PEM Electrolyzer Stack\n50 to 80 degree C]
    WATER[Deionized Water Supply] -->|H2O feed| STACK
    STACK -->|H2 gas and water vapor| SEP1[Gas Liquid Separator H2 side]
    STACK -->|O2 gas and water vapor| SEP2[Gas Liquid Separator O2 side]
    SEP1 -->|Wet H2| DRY1[Drying Unit\nPalladium membrane]
    DRY1 -->|Dry H2 99.99 percent| COMP[Compressor\nto 350 or 700 bar]
    COMP -->|Pressurized H2| TANK[High Pressure Storage Tank]
    SEP2 -->|O2 vent| VENT[Oxygen Vent or Capture]
    TANK -->|H2 supply| FC[PEM Fuel Cell\nor End Use]
    FC -->|DC electricity| LOAD[Electric Load\nVehicle or Grid]
    FC -->|Pure H2O| WATER2[Water Recycle Loop]
    WATER2 -->|Return| WATER
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Renewable electricity is rectified to DC, fed into a PEM stack where water is split. The hydrogen side is dried, compressed, and stored. Oxygen is vented. On demand, dry hydrogen enters a fuel cell to produce electricity + pure water, which is recycled.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Flow of a Sulfur–Iodine Thermochemical Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HT[High Temperature Heat Source\nNuclear or Concentrated Solar\n800 to 900 degree C] -->|Process heat| SR[Sulfuric Acid Decomposition Reactor\nH2SO4 to SO2 plus H2O plus 0.5 O2]
    SR -->|SO2 plus H2O| BR[Bunsen Reaction Section\nI2 plus SO2 plus 2 H2O to 2 HI plus H2SO4]
    HI_LOOP[Hydriodic Acid Phase] -->|2 HI| HI_DEC[HI Decomposition Reactor\n300 degree C\n2 HI to H2 plus I2]
    BR -->|2 HI| HI_DEC
    BR -->|H2SO4| SR
    HI_DEC -->|H2 product| H2OUT[Pure Hydrogen Output]
    HI_DEC -->|I2 recycle| BR
    SR -->|O2 product| O2OUT[Pure Oxygen Output]
    H2_OUT_STORAGE[Compressed or Liquid H2 Storage] --> H2OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** All reagents ($\text{I}_2$ and $\text{SO}_2$) are *recycled* in a closed loop. Only $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ enters and only $\text{H}_2$ + $\text{O}_2$ leave. The peak temperature of 850 °C is far below the 2500 °C needed for direct thermolysis.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "Green Hydrogen." State any two advantages of using hydrogen as a fuel compared to gasoline.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Green hydrogen is the hydrogen produced by **electrolysis of water using electricity generated from renewable sources** (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal). It is characterized by **near-zero lifecycle carbon emissions** (typically < 1 kg CO₂e/kg H₂), in contrast to grey hydrogen (≈ 9–12 kg CO₂/kg H₂) produced from natural gas.

**Two advantages over gasoline (1 mark):**
1. **Zero carbon emissions** at point of use — only $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is exhausted in a fuel cell.
2. **Higher gravimetric energy density** — hydrogen has 142 MJ/kg (HHV) vs. gasoline's ≈ 44 MJ/kg (≈ 3.2× higher).
3. *Bonus:* Renewable-source-compatible, enabling long-duration energy storage.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three methods of hydrogen storage and mention one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per method with limitation):**

| # | Method | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Compressed gas (700 bar)** | High tank cost, safety concerns, **≈ 14 % of LHV consumed in compression** |
| 2 | **Liquid hydrogen (cryogenic)** | **Boil-off loss** of ≈ 0.1 – 0.4 % per day; high liquefaction energy (~30 % of LHV) |
| 3 | **Metal hydrides** ($\text{LaNi}_5$) | Low gravimetric density (1 – 2 wt%), heavy systems, slow absorption kinetics |

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)** process for hydrogen production with a neat block diagram. State the principal chemical reactions involved and discuss the **environmental concerns** of SMR-based hydrogen.
> **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the construction and working principle of a **PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) Electrolyzer**. Compare its performance with the **alkaline electrolyzer** in terms of efficiency, current density, and purity. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — Steam Methane Reforming (7 marks)**

**1. Process Description (3 marks):**
Steam Methane Reforming is the dominant industrial method for hydrogen production (≈ 96 % of global supply). Natural gas ($\text{CH}_4$) reacts with **steam at 700 – 900 °C** over a **nickel-based catalyst** to produce a synthesis gas (syngas) mixture of $\text{H}_2$, $\text{CO}$, and $\text{CO}_2$. The CO is then converted in a water-gas shift reactor to produce additional $\text{H}_2$.

**2. Principal Reactions (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reforming:} \quad & \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}) \\[3pt]
\text{Water Gas Shift:} \quad & \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (\Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol})
\end{aligned}$$

**Overall:** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2$ (requires 1 mol methane per 4 mol $\text{H}_2$).

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark — verbal description):**
$\text{CH}_4$ (desulfurizer) → Reformer (700–900 °C, Ni catalyst) → Water-Gas Shift Reactor (200–400 °C) → Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) unit → Pure $\text{H}_2$ (99.99 %).

**4. Environmental Concerns (1 mark):**
- Emits **9 – 12 kg CO₂ per kg $\text{H}_2$** produced.
- Releases **trace $\text{NO}_x$** from high-temperature combustion in the reformer furnace.
- Methane leakage upstream can make lifecycle GHG emissions comparable to coal-based hydrogen.

---

**Model Solution (b) — PEM Electrolyzer vs. Alkaline (7 marks)**

**1. Construction of PEM Electrolyzer (3 marks):**
- **Anode:** Titanium substrate coated with iridium/ruthenium oxide catalyst (oxygen evolution).
- **Cathode:** Carbon paper coated with platinum catalyst (hydrogen evolution).
- **Membrane:** **Nafion™** (perfluorinated sulfonic acid polymer) — conducts $\text{H}^+$ ions.
- **Bipolar plates:** Titanium with platinum coating, distribute water and current.
- **Operating temperature:** 50 – 80 °C; **Pressure:** up to 70 bar differential.

**2. Working Principle (2 marks):**
Deionized water is fed to the anode, where it is oxidized:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \to \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

Protons migrate through the Nafion membrane to the cathode; electrons travel through the external circuit. At the cathode:

$$2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \to \text{H}_2$$

**3. Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | PEM Electrolyzer | Alkaline Electrolyzer |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency (HHV) | 70 – 85 % | 60 – 70 % |
| Current density | **1 – 2 A/cm² (high)** | 0.2 – 0.6 A/cm² |
| H₂ purity | **> 99.99 %** | 99.5 – 99.9 % |
| Dynamic response | **Fast (seconds)** | Slow (minutes) |
| Electrolyte | Solid polymer (Nafion) | Liquid KOH 25 – 30 % |
| Capital cost | Higher (Pt catalyst) | Lower (Ni electrodes) |
| Operating pressure | 30 – 70 bar | Atmospheric |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Alt Choice]**
> **(a)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) thermochemical cycle** for hydrogen production. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** over direct water electrolysis. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **PEM electrolyzer** operates at 2.0 V and 1000 A. Calculate the:
>   (i) mass of $\text{H}_2$ produced per hour,
>   (ii) volume of $\text{H}_2$ produced per hour at STP,
>   (iii) electrical energy consumed per kg of $\text{H}_2$ produced,
>   (iv) electrolyzer efficiency (using HHV = 142 MJ/kg). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — S–I Cycle (7 marks)**

**1. Process Description with Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to the S–I cycle diagram in Section 4.2. The S–I cycle decomposes water in **three coupled reactions** at progressively lower temperatures, using only heat and water as inputs, with **all other reagents recycled internally**.

**2. Reactions (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Bunsen (exothermic, 20–120 °C):} \quad & \text{I}_2 + \text{SO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \to 2\text{HI} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \\[3pt]
\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ decomposition (endothermic, 850 °C):} \quad & \text{H}_2\text\text{SO}_4 \to \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \\[3pt]
\text{HI decomposition (endothermic, 300–450 °C):} \quad & 2\text{HI} \to \text{H}_2 + \text{I}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**3. Advantages over Electrolysis (1 mark):**
- Uses **high-temperature heat directly** (potentially from nuclear or solar concentrators) — bypasses the **Carnot-limited** electrical generation step, giving higher overall energy efficiency (≈ 50 – 60 %).
- **No precious metal catalysts** required; can use nuclear-grade heat (700 – 900 °C).

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Requires handling of **corrosive $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ and HI** at high temperatures.
- **Materials challenge** at 850 °C (sulfuric acid decomposer).
- **Low overall efficiency** (≈ 40 – 55 %) due to thermal losses; currently at pilot stage only.

---

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $V_{cell} = 2.0$ V
- $I = 1000$ A
- $t = 1$ hr = 3600 s
- $n = 2$ electrons
- $F = 96\,485$ C/mol
- $M_{\text{H}_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T_{STP} = 273.15$ K, $P_{STP} = 101\,325$ Pa
- HHV = 142 MJ/kg

**Step (i) — Mass of $\text{H}_2$ per hour (2 marks):**

$$Q = I \cdot t = 1000 \times 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ C}$$

$$m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{nF} = \dfrac{(3.6 \times 10^6)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(2)(96\,485)} = \dfrac{7257.6}{192\,970} = 0.0376 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{H}_2} = 0.0376 \text{ kg/hr} = 37.6 \text{ g/hr}}$$

**Step (ii) — Volume at STP (1 mark):**

$$V = \dfrac{m \cdot R \cdot T_{STP}}{M \cdot P_{STP}} = \dfrac{(0.0376)(8.314)(273.15)}{(2.016 \times 10^{-3})(101\,325)}$$

$$V = \dfrac{85.4}{0.2043} = 418.0 \text{ L} = 0.418 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{H}_2} = 0.418 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}}$$

**Step (iii) — Electrical energy per kg of $\text{H}_2$ (2 marks):**

$$E_{elec} = V \cdot I \cdot t = (2.0)(1000)(3600) = 7.2 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 7.2 \text{ MJ per 0.0376 kg}$$

$$E_{per\,kg} = \dfrac{7.2 \text{ MJ}}{0.0376 \text{ kg}} = 191.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{E_{elec} = 191.5 \text{ MJ/kg H}_2}$$

**Step (iv) — Electrolyzer Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{HHV of H}_2}{E_{elec}} = \dfrac{142}{191.5} = 0.7415$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 74.15\%}$$

**Validation check:** Using $\eta = \dfrac{\text{HHV} \cdot M}{nF V} = \dfrac{(142 \times 10^6)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(2)(96\,485)(2.0)} = \dfrac{286\,272}{385\,940} = 0.7418$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** in Faraday's law problems: forgetting to convert current (A) × time (s) to Coulombs. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV**: HHV = 142 MJ/kg; LHV = 120 MJ/kg. Examiner will deduct if you quote the wrong one for efficiency. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 3. **Sign convention in thermoneutral voltage**: Students often write $E_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ instead of $\dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}$. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> 4. **Not specifying STP** in volumetric calculations — examiner will not award full credit.
> 5. **In SMR questions, students forget the water-gas shift reactor** — they describe only the reformer. Always mention the **two-stage** process: reforming *and* WGS. **Loss: 1 mark**.
> 6. **In thermochemical cycle questions**, students forget to mention that reagents are **recycled in a closed loop**. This is the *defining* feature of thermochemical cycles. **Loss: 1 mark**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Month KTU Exam Revision — Master These 12 Bullets:**

- **Hydrogen is a *secondary* energy carrier, not a primary source** — it must be produced from water, fossil fuels, or biomass.
- **HHV = 142 MJ/kg, LHV = 120 MJ/kg** — memorize both; examiner will test which to use.
- **Reversible voltage = 1.23 V; Thermoneutral voltage = 1.48 V** — these are *the* most tested numbers.
- **Faraday's mass law**: $m = \dfrac{ItM}{nF}$ — $n=2$ for hydrogen; $F = 96\,485$ C/mol.
- **Green hydrogen = renewable electricity + water electrolysis**; **Blue = SMR + CCS**; **Grey = SMR alone**.
- **PEM electrolyzer = 70-85 % efficiency, fast response, high purity**; **Alkaline = cheaper, slower**; **SOEC = highest efficiency but 700-850 °C**.
- **S–I cycle** uses 850 °C peak; **Cu–Cl cycle** uses 550 °C peak (better for Gen-IV nuclear).
- **Storage challenge**: 14 % of LHV lost compressing $\text{H}_2$ to 700 bar; cryogenic storage loses ≈ 0.1 %/day to boil-off.
- **Metal hydrides** ($\text{LaNi}_5$, $\text{MgH}_2$) are safe and compact but heavy (1 – 2 wt%).
- **SMR produces 9-12 kg CO₂/kg H₂** — the reason green hydrogen is preferred.
- **Fuel cell reaction**: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$.
- **Hydrogen has the highest flame speed and widest flammability range** of all fuels — *both a performance and safety concern*.
